solutions.team_name: VerdictAI — Criterion-Aware Tender Evaluation with Explainable Verdicts and Institutional Memory
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


P pavilion
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
VerdictAI — Criterion-Aware Tender Evaluation with Explainable Verdicts and Institutional Memory
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 21, 2026 12:39 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Clear doc link:  Click here (https://docs.google.com/document/d/1rv_D1L1mRCIiGX_YgDw-YVoOlp00xZvNHiQLambEW2I/edit?usp=sharing)  (https://docs.google.com/document/d/1rv_D1L1mRCIiGX_YgDw-YVoOlp00xZvNHiQLambEW2I/edit?usp=sharing) THE PROBLEMThree structural failures no checklist fixes:No institutional memory — "similar works" is interpreted differently by every evaluator. No record of how that phrase was resolved in prior tenders from the same department.No reproducible trace — a CVC complaint cannot be answered with a criterion-level record linking each verdict to a specific document, page, and value. Committee minutes are not an audit trail.No protection against silent errors — misread turnover figures, expired certificates, wrong measurement periods propagate to the final evaluation undetected. ARCHITECTURE: FIVE LAYERSL1 — Document Intelligence 5-step pre-processing before OCR: (1) Deskew via OpenCV Hough — corrects 2–15° scanner/phone rotation. (2) Sauvola adaptive binarisation — handles phone-camera gradients that global Otsu cannot. (3) Rubber stamp isolation + text recovery — red-channel separation, connected-component masking, colour-channel decomposition recovers text under stamps. A stamp over a CA certificate turnover figure is a daily occurrence in Indian procurement. (4) Non-local means denoising. (5) ESRGAN super-resolution to 300 DPI.Dual-engine OCR: Tesseract 5 (LSTM — English, Devanagari) + PaddleOCR v3 (tables, mixed-script, Kannada/Tamil) in parallel. Word-level confidence arbitration. Page-level confidence = length-weighted mean → directly drives routing. Degraded documents route to HITL, never auto-committed. Output: (page, bbox, text, ocr_confidence, source_format) per word.L2 — Effective Tender Specification (ETS) Builder The tender is a version-controlled object: base NIT + corrigenda applied chronologically. Each criterion carries originating document, clause reference, amendment history. No evaluation begins until the officer confirms the assembled ETS.Five criterion types — each evaluated differently: • numeric_threshold (Turnover ≥ ₹5 Cr): Deterministic. GFR override: NOT permitted. • categorical_presence (Valid GST): Document + validity date. GFR override: NOT permitted. • temporal_recency (3 similar works, last 5 yrs): Deterministic + CPM similarity. GFR override: NOT permitted. • composite (Turnover + experience + cert.): Decomposed sub-criteria. GFR override: per sub-criterion. • qualitative_assessment (Adequate technical capacity): LLM + CPM precedents. GFR override: Permitted.Schema review gate enforced by state machine — errors caught once, not propagated to all bidders. Output: Approved ETS — type, threshold, measurement period, evidence types, GFR_override_permitted, provenance chain.L3 — Evidence Extractor Per (bidder × criterion), type-specific search — not generic retrieval: • numeric_threshold: Table-aware parsing, extracts value + fiscal year + unit. Flags consolidated vs. standalone account ambiguity. • categorical_presence: Certificate search with regulatory synonyms. Routes to HITL if validity unreadable — does not assume invalidity. • temporal_recency: Project value, completion date, description → CPM similarity model. • qualitative_assessment: Relevant passages + CPM precedents, same department + category. • Entity mismatch detection: Fuzzy-matches company name from every financial document against registered bidder name. Parent-company financial substitution is a documented fraud vector — mismatch → mandatory HITL regardless of confidence. Output: (value, source_doc, page, bbox, ocr_conf, extraction_conf, entity_match_flag).L4 — Evaluation Engine + Confidence Router Type-specific logic: • numeric_threshold: extraction_conf < 0.75 → REVIEW regardless of comparison result. • categorical_presence: Found + current → PASS. Found + expired → FAIL. Unreadable date → REVIEW. Not found → REVIEW (officer confirms absence before disqualification — never auto-FAIL). • qualitative_assessment: Any LLM FAIL → HITL regardless of confidence. VerdictAI never auto-commits a disqualification from language interpretation.Three routes: • Auto-commit (≥ 0.85): deterministic criterion, no flags → logged automatically • HITL review (0.50–0.84): partial confidence or any LLM verdict → officer confirms/overrides • Mandatory review (< 0.50 or explicit flag): entity mismatch, stamp obscuration, debarment, GFR-mandatory FAIL → explicit officer decision requiredNever-silent guarantee: any mandatory-criterion FAIL routes to mandatory review before commitment — enforced by state machine. Output: (verdict, confidence, evaluation_method, route, routing_reason).L5 — Immutable Audit Ledger Append-only log — DB trigger blocks all UPDATE/DELETE. Events: document received, OCR completed, corrigendum linked, schema approved (officer ID + timestamp), debarment checked, evidence extracted, verdict computed, case routed, officer decision, report generated.Root of trust: (1) SHA-256 hash embedded in PDF as string + QR code. PDF signed via PKCS#7 / HSM (Azure Key Vault cloud; on-premise HSM for air-gapped). Modification invalidates signature. (2) Daily Merkle root anchored to blockchain (NIC National Blockchain Framework / Polygon fallback) — third-party timestamp independent of infrastructure.Reproducibility: any evaluation regenerated from stored inputs (original docs, ETS, pinned model versions, extracted values, officer decisions). Re-run output byte-identical.﻿CORE INNOVATION: CRITERION PRECEDENT MEMORY (CPM)Every officer decision generates a CPM entry: criterion_text (tokenised), resolved_interpretation, department, tender_category, verdict, officer_action, officer_id (anonymised), tender_id.Four compounding functions:Disambiguation: ambiguous language queries CPM for same-department, same-category precedents. Officer sees prior interpretations with tender IDs → confirms or modifies → becomes next entry. Interpretation becomes institutional record, not individual judgment.Similarity scoring: labeled (project_description, is_similar) pairs fine-tune a domain-specific model. Far more accurate than general embeddings on "RCC box culvert" vs "canal crossing structure."Confidence calibration: isotonic regression on (computed_confidence, officer_confirmed_correct) → calibrated extraction confidence. Temperature scaling for LLM confidence (Guo et al. 2017). 0.87 means 87% correct in practice.Routing optimisation: (confidence, verdict, officer_confirmed) triples → optimal auto-commit thresholds per criterion type per department. 50 evaluations in, thresholds are tuned to that department's document quality and language. Not replicable without the data. Cold start: conservative defaults (auto-commit ceiling 0.90, review floor 0.60) until data accumulates. Visible in dashboard. GFR 2017 + CVC COMPLIANCE — ENFORCED IN CODEGFR_override_permitted flag per criterion. Override physically disabled in HITL for mandatory FAILs — GFR rule number renders in place of the button. CVC-violating override is impossible at the interaction level.CVC debarment pre-check: PAN + company name cross-referenced against CVC debarred entities list + GeM blacklist. Match halts pipeline before any criterion evaluation.Second-officer confirmation for borderline overrides — both logged with ID + timestamp.HITL REVIEW INTERFACEFive components, one screen, no navigation:Criterion: full ETS text, type, mandatory status, GFR rule numberEvidence: original page image with extracted region in bounding box (always image for scanned docs — visual OCR error catch is the primary routing trigger)System analysis: precise routing reason ("turnover partially obscured by stamp — extracted as ₹7.2 Cr, confidence 0.41")CPM context: up to 3 matching precedents from same department with tender IDsDecision: Confirm or Override (structured reason required; disabled for GFR-mandatory FAIL)DEPLOYMENTOn-premise / air-gapped (CRPF, MoD, DRDO): Llama 3.1 70B via Ollama, no data egress, on-premise HSM, permissioned blockchain node. NIC MeghRaj + CERT-In compatible. SaaS (state PWDs, PSUs): Azure OpenAI GPT-4o under DPA, India data residency, per-tenant CPM isolation. Phased rollout: CRPF (0–12 mo) → MHA-wide BSF/CISF/SSB (12–24 mo) → GeM integration (24–36 mo).PROTOTYPE — 28 APRIL TO 04 MAY 2026Demo: (1) Live criterion extraction + corrigendum showing amended values. (2) Scanned CA certificate with stamp over turnover — confidence 0.41, mandatory review, HITL shows page with stamp bbox highlighted. (3) Entity mismatch on parent-company balance sheet. (4) CPM precedent injection during schema review.Day 1 (28 Apr): 5-step pre-processing + dual OCR + confidence arbitration Day 2 (29 Apr): Criterion extractor + type classifier + GFR annotation + ETS builder Day 3 (30 Apr): Schema review UI + corrigendum diff + approval gate Day 4 (01 May): Evidence extractor + evaluation logic + entity mismatch detector Day 5 (02 May): HITL queue + bbox overlay + GFR enforcement + CPM bootstrap + integration Day 6 (03 May): Audit log + SHA-256 PDF + blockchain stub + end-to-end test Buffer (04 May): Edge case testing + submission packagingStack: FastAPI · Tesseract 5 + PaddleOCR v3 · OpenCV + scikit-image · Azure OpenAI GPT-4o / Llama 3.1 70B · PostgreSQL + pgvector · React · Polygon / NIC blockchainWHAT MAKES THIS DIFFERENTTender = version-controlled specification — corrigenda are first-class inputsCriteria are typed — 5 types, 5 evaluation strategies, GFR permissions per criterionConfidence is calibrated — isotonic regression on real officer data, not LLM self-reportNo auto-commit without complete evidence chain — value + document + page + bbox + confidence all required; any missing → HITLEvery officer decision improves the system — CPM converts judgment into compounding institutional memoryThe result: a platform a procurement officer trusts, a minister can sign off on, and a CVC inquiry finds sufficient.Edit link

N neha.s.singh2027_7057
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
TenderEval AI – Explainable Tender Evaluation System
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 21, 2026 01:28 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
TENDER EVAL AI – EXPLAINABLE TENDER EVALUATION SYSTEM﻿PROBLEM UNDERSTANDING Government tender evaluation is currently a manual and time-consuming process. Evaluators must review large volumes of bidder documents such as financial statements, certifications, and compliance records. These documents are often unstructured, including PDFs, scanned images, and different formats. This makes the evaluation process slow, inconsistent, and prone to human error. The goal is to build a system that can automatically extract eligibility criteria from tender documents, analyze bidder submissions, and evaluate whether they meet the required conditions. The system must also provide clear explanations for every decision and flag uncertain cases for manual review.﻿PROJECT DESCRIPTION The proposed system, TenderEval AI, reads tender documents and extracts eligibility criteria such as financial, technical, and compliance requirements. It then processes bidder submissions, including PDFs and scanned documents, to extract relevant information. The extracted bidder data is compared with the tender criteria using a rule-based evaluation engine. Based on this comparison, the system classifies bidders as Eligible, Not Eligible, or Needs Manual Review. Each decision is accompanied by a clear explanation including the required condition, extracted value, and document source. If the document is unclear, the system flags it for manual review.﻿SOLUTION APPROACHAccept tender and bidder documentsExtract text using OCR and PDF parsingIdentify eligibility criteriaExtract bidder dataCompare using rule-based logicClassify: Eligible / Not Eligible / ReviewProvide explanation for every decision﻿ARCHITECTURE OVERVIEW The system follows a modular pipeline. Documents are processed using OCR and parsing tools to extract text. The text is split into two flows: tender criteria extraction and bidder data extraction. Both are passed to an evaluation engine which determines eligibility. An explainability layer links decisions to document evidence, and results are shown with manual review flags.﻿TECH STACK Python, Django, Tesseract OCR, pdfplumber, PostgreSQL﻿CHALLENGES AND SOLUTIONSUnstructured documents → OCR + parsingFormat variation → flexible extractionMissing/unclear data → manual reviewExplainability → detailed reasoning﻿FUTURE WORKBuild working prototypeImprove extraction accuracyDevelop UI dashboardTest with sample documents﻿KEY STRENGTH The solution focuses on practical implementation and explainability using OCR, structured extraction, and rule-based evaluation instead of black-box models.﻿ARCHITECTURE DIAGRAM  https://drive.google.com/file/d/17w529BoplUyMsbK1oN_zMycK-cbnSYR7/view?usp=sharing

Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 21, 2026 01:38 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Note: You can find out the detailed solution here:  https://docs.google.com/document/d/1aiFEMwYZq6lnJH7u2xoYzCiVTz3PpYai767bKOPX2X4/edit?usp=sharing  ﻿ PROBLEM STATEMENT Build an AI-powered system that reads government tender documents and bidder submissions, extracts requirements, evaluates eligibility automatically, and generates transparent, explainable reports for procurement officers.﻿ End-to-End Pipeline at a Glance: Tender -> Extract Criteria -> Parse Submissions -> Match -> Evaluate -> Explain -> Report﻿﻿﻿ This system delivers a complete, AI-driven workflow for tender evaluation, transforming raw documents into clear, decision-ready insights. It begins with uploading the tender, where AI extracts eligibility, technical, and financial criteria, which can be quickly reviewed and refined by the user through simple controls.Once the tender is finalized, multiple bidder submissions are uploaded and processed in parallel. These reports include extracted values, supporting document evidence, and clear reasoning for every score and decision.At its core, the system is built for auditability. Every decision is fully traceable and explainable, supported by a 24×7 audit chatbot that can answer any query about the evaluation process—ensuring transparency, accountability, and trust in procurement decisions.

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE

LEADERBOARD


M Makers_of_entanglement
TEAM SUBMISSION [2]
Pramaan — AI-Assisted, Audit-Ready Tender Eligibility Evaluation for CRPF
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 21, 2026 03:14 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
CRPF and other government buyers spend roughly 200 officer-hours evaluating each tender by hand, and at the end they still have no machine-readable audit trail — two officers reading the same documents can reach different verdicts. Pramaan fixes the boring, repeatable part of that work without taking the decision away from the officer. The platform reads a tender, extracts its eligibility criteria as typed, schema-constrained records, and asks the officer to review them before any bidder is touched. It then ingests every bidder's submission — typed PDFs, scanned documents, Word files, mobile-phone photographs of certificates — and for each criterion it locates the specific span in the specific document that satisfies or fails it. Each bidder × criterion pair gets a verdict: Eligible, Not Eligible, or Needs Review. Needs Review is the safety valve. Whenever OCR confidence is low, the criterion language is ambiguous, evidence is missing, or the model can't quote a literal source span, the case is escalated to the officer with the reason — a government system must never silently disqualify anyone. Numeric checks like "turnover ≥ ₹5 crore in each of the last 3 FYs" run in deterministic Python, not in the LLM, so there is no room for arithmetic hallucination. Deployment is hybrid and CRPF-grade. An on-prem open-source LLM (Llama 3.1 70B on NIC / MeghRaj) handles ~80% of traffic; the hardest 20% goes to frontier LLMs through a redaction gate that strips bidder name, address, PAN, GSTIN, and signatory before any external call. No bidder-identifying data ever leaves government infrastructure. Every verdict, every officer override, and every re-run is written to a hash-chained append-only audit log with prompt and model versions attached, so a decision made today can be reproduced byte-for-byte three years from now for an RTI query or a court hearing. A working FastAPI + React prototype already takes a mock CRPF construction tender and three bidders end-to-end on a laptop with no API key, exercising the Eligible, Not Eligible, and Needs Review paths and producing a verifiable audit chain.

AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 21, 2026 03:43 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
AI-Based Tender Evaluation & Eligibility Analysis PlatformOverviewGovernment tender evaluation is a high-stakes, document-intensive process that requires verifying whether each bidder satisfies a complex set of eligibility criteria spanning financial, technical, and compliance requirements. These criteria are typically embedded in lengthy, unstructured tender documents, while bidder submissions arrive in heterogeneous formats such as PDFs, scanned copies, Word files, tables, and even photographs.Our solution is an AI-powered decision-support platform that automates tender understanding, bidder analysis, and eligibility evaluation—while ensuring full explainability, auditability, and human oversight, making it suitable for real-world government procurement workflows.﻿Problem UnderstandingCurrent tender evaluation processes are:Manual and time-consuming (reviewing hundreds of pages per bidder)Inconsistent across evaluatorsProne to oversight and ambiguityDifficult to audit and justifyThe key challenge is not just extracting data, but making reliable, explainable decisions from unstructured and inconsistent documents.﻿Our SolutionWe propose an end-to-end AI platform that performs three core functions:﻿1. Tender UnderstandingAutomatically extracts eligibility criteria from tender documentsClassifies criteria into:Financial (e.g., turnover thresholds)Technical (e.g., project experience)Compliance (e.g., GST, certifications)Distinguishes mandatory vs optional conditionsConverts criteria into a structured, machine-readable format﻿2. Bidder UnderstandingParses bidder submissions across all formats:Typed PDFsScanned documentsTables and structured filesImages and photographsUses OCR and AI extraction to identify:Financial data (turnover, revenue)Experience (projects completed)Certifications (ISO, licenses)Registrations (GST, compliance records)Handles variation in wording, format, and document structureAttaches source evidence (document, page, snippet) to every extracted value﻿3. Evaluation & ExplainabilityMatches bidder data against each criterion using a hybrid rule-based + AI systemProduces criterion-level decisions:Eligible (PASS)Not Eligible (FAIL)Needs Manual Review (REVIEW)Assigns confidence scores for each decisionExplainability (Core Differentiator)Every verdict includes:The exact criterion being evaluatedThe value extracted from bidder documentsThe source document and locationA clear reasoning explanation﻿4. Human-in-the-Loop WorkflowAmbiguous or low-confidence cases are never auto-rejectedFlagged cases are routed to a review interface where officials can:ApproveEditRejectReviewer actions are stored to improve future decisions﻿5. Auditability & ComplianceEvery step is fully traceable and logged:Input documentsExtracted valuesEvaluation decisionsReviewer actionsProduces a complete audit trail suitable for government procurement standards﻿6. Output & ReportingGenerates a consolidated evaluation report:Criterion-by-criterion analysisEligible / Not Eligible / Review statusSupporting evidence for each decisionExportable and ready for official sign-off﻿Key FeaturesMulti-format document processing (PDF, scanned, images, tables)AI-driven structured extraction + deterministic rule engineExplainable, criterion-level decision makingConfidence-based ambiguity handlingHuman verification workflowEnd-to-end audit trailDashboard for evaluation and decision support﻿Technology ApproachOCR for scanned and image-based documentsLarge Language Models for document understanding and extractionRule-based evaluation engine for consistent eligibility decisionsVector search (RAG) for contextual document retrievalWeb-based dashboard for interaction and review﻿Handling Real-World ChallengesScanned/low-quality documents: OCR confidence + review fallbackAmbiguous language: conservative evaluation → REVIEW stateIncomplete data: never assumed → flagged explicitlyInconsistent formats: normalized via AI extraction﻿Expected ImpactReduces evaluation time from days to minutesEnsures consistent and unbiased decision-makingImproves transparency and accountabilityEnables trustworthy AI-assisted procurement decisions﻿ConclusionThis platform transforms tender evaluation from a manual, error-prone process into a structured, explainable, and auditable AI-assisted workflow. By combining AI extraction, rule-based reasoning, and human oversight, it aligns with real-world government constraints and delivers a scalable, trustworthy solution for procurement decision-making.

Tatva AI solves the liability problem, not just the speed problem.What Tatva AI DoesExtracts eligibility criteria from tender documents into typed, structured rule objects via a two-pass LLM pipelineClassifies every bidder document (digital PDF, scanned, handwritten, photograph) through a 4-class quality gate before extractionEvaluates each bidder criterion-by-criterion with composite confidence scoring (OCR quality + LLM certainty + source pin quality)Produces a fully traceable Officer Brief — every verdict pinned to exact document, page, and bounding boxDetects cross-bidder collusion signals (shared address, bid price proximity) and surfaces them to the evaluation committeeManages tender versioning with SHA-256 hashing — no bidder is ever evaluated against a version they didn't submit toKey InnovationsZero silent rejections — every disqualification requires a named criterion, named document, and officer confirmation10 unbreakable system guarantees covering the full evaluation lifecycleConsistency Checker that flags tender ambiguities before bids are even receivedTier 1/2/3 criterion classification — discretionary criteria never get an AI score, only an evidence dossierImmutable append-only audit log chain from raw document ingestion to signed PDF briefTech Stack Python · FastAPI · pymupdf · pytesseract · Anthropic API (tool-use) · Streamlit · ReportLab · pandasTeam Tatva AI is built by a 1st-year team from CMR University, Bengaluru. TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
Judgment2Action: AI System for Court Order Compliance & Decisions
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 21, 2026 09:16 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Judgment2Action is an AI-powered decision support system that converts court judgment PDFs into structured, actionable, and verifiable insights. The system can process both digital and scanned PDFs, extracting key information like case details, involved parties, dates, and court instructions. It then organizes this information into a structured format.In addition to extraction, the system creates an action plan to help officials understand what steps to take next. This includes identifying if the judgment requires compliance, considerations for appeal, key timelines (whether explicit or inferred), and which department is likely responsible based on the case context.To ensure trust and reliability, the system includes a human verification layer. All extracted information and suggested actions come with highlighted references from the original document and confidence scores. Officials can review, edit, or approve the data, making sure that only verified and accurate information is used for decision-making.The final output appears on a simple and user-friendly dashboard that displays only approved action plans. This dashboard allows for department-wise filtering. It highlights urgent cases and upcoming deadlines while providing a clear view of needed actions, helping decision-makers prioritize effectively.The solution enhances human decision-making instead of replacing it, ensuring accountability while significantly reducing manual effort. By improving speed, accuracy, and clarity, Judgment2Action helps government departments respond to court directives promptly and thoughtfully.﻿Proposed Solution Features﻿1. PDF Ingestion and Processing The system lets users upload court judgment PDFs directly or receive them through APIs. It can handle both digital PDFs, which already have readable text, and scanned documents, where text is extracted using OCR (Optical Character Recognition). This guarantees that all types of court documents can be processed efficiently.﻿2. Information Extraction Engine Once the document is processed, the system extracts important details and organizes them into a clear format. This includes information such as the case title and number, date of judgment, parties involved, key court directives, and any timelines mentioned in the document. The extraction uses a mix of OCR, text parsing techniques, pre-trained AI models (like NLP and LLMs), and rule-based methods to improve accuracy and reliability.﻿3. AI-Assisted Action Plan Generation After extracting the information, the system creates a structured action plan to guide officials on the next steps. This includes identifying the required actions, such as compliance, review, or consideration for appeal, along with key steps to take. It also highlights deadlines, whether explicitly stated or inferred using predefined rules, and suggests the responsible department based on the case's context. These recommendations aim to assist decision-making without replacing human judgment.﻿4. Human Verification Layer To ensure accuracy and trust, the system adds a human verification step. All extracted information and suggested actions appear alongside highlighted references from the original PDF and a confidence score indicating reliability. Reviewers can approve, edit, or reject these outputs. Only the verified and approved information moves forward for further use.

solutions.team_name: Aether: Intelligent Self-Healing Interoperability Layer
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


T Team Cipher
TEAM SUBMISSION [10]
Aether: Intelligent Self-Healing Interoperability Layer
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 21, 2026 11:18 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Problem Overview: Today the systems in Karnataka, such as SWS and the different department portals do not work together properly. A business may make a change in one system. This change is not updated in the other systems. This causes problems over time like having information in different places, confusion when people come to check and delays that should not happen. The main problem is not that Karnataka does not have systems. The main problem is that the systems in Karnataka like SWS and the department portals do not share information, with each other. 2. Our Approach: We propose a system called Aether. It helps keep our systems working smoothly. It sits between SWS and the department systems. It makes sure they all have the information. The good thing is that it does not need us to change our existing systems. It uses the connections that're already there like APIs or data access methods. This allows updates to flow in both directions smoothly, Everything stays up to date. Consistent without having to change our whole system.In terms: SWS ↔ Aether ↔ Department Systems3. How Data Moves Through the System: When something changes, like an address update, the system captures it. It uses tools like APIs, webhooks or polling to do this. The update then goes to a waiting area called an event queue also known as Kafka. The event queue is like a line where things wait to be processed. Once its in the queue the update is handled in the background. It is then sent to all the systems that need to know about the change. This ensures all systems remain in sync. So if a departments system is down for a while the update won't be lost. It will just be tried again later. The system makes sure everything stays up to date. 4. Handling Different Data Formats: Each department stores data differently. So direct syncing does not work. To solve this config-based mapping layer is used. This layer converts data from one format to another. For example: address_line (SWS) → addr (Department) This method makes it easier to onboard new departments. If you add a department you don't have to redo the whole system. You just need to add a connection, to Aether. 5. Finding Changes Fast (Advanced): Not all systems provide real time updates. When this happens the system uses polling. It checks for changes by comparing codes called hashes. To keep our records up to date we use a trick. We create a code called a SHA-256 hash for each record and save it. Then the time we check we create the code again. If the new code is different from the one we know something has changed in the record. This way we don't have to compare the record every time. It saves time and effort. This approach really speeds up finding changes. this is especially useful when working with large datasets. We use event IDs that are based on UBID and payload. This helps us make sure we do not process the update more, than once. 6. Handling Conflicts: Sometimes two systems may update the field around the same time. The system does not blindly overwrite data. It evaluates:• When the update happened • Which system it came from • How reliable the data is It makes a decision based on the information it has. If something is not clear it sends a signal for a person to check it manually. Every decision it makes is written down. So everything is out in the open.7. IdempotencyTo make the system dependable:• Kafka ensures no data is lost • updates are retried with delay • Redis helps track processed events. So the same update isn’t applied twice.This way under failures the system stays consistent. 8. Self-Healing Mechanism: The system is designed to maintain long-term consistency. It does not just process updates. It also checks if data across systems is starting to become inconsistent. This way we can make sure everything works together and smoothly over time with Aether. It continuously monitors data consistency.When the system notices that two different sets of data have information for the same business Aether can automatically start a process to sync them up again. It can point out the problem. This ability to fix itself helps keep the data accurate and consistent over time.9. Monitoring and Visibility: Since this system runs in the background we provide a dashboard. Officials can:• See sync status • Track failures and retries • View conflicts • Check system healthThis makes it transparent and easier to trust.10. Audit and Traceability: Every update is recorded with complete details. Each log includes source, destination, changes made, timestamp, retry attempts and conflict resolution details. This ensures full traceability and is important for government systems.11. Plan for Round 2: If we get chosen our next step will be to start building a working prototype of the system. We'll do it one step at a time.1: Set up core middleware with SWS and a couple of department systems 2: Add schema translation and event processing 3: Implement conflict handling, retry logic and audit logging 4: Build dashboard and demonstrate full flow. This includes failure and recovery scenarios.The goal is to show a working system. Not just a concept.

solutions.team_name: TenderMind — AI-Powered Tender Evaluation & Eligibility Analysis Platform for Government Procurement (CRPF)
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


S sinwilderr_c6c7
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
TenderMind — AI-Powered Tender Evaluation & Eligibility Analysis Platform for Government Procurement (CRPF)
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 22, 2026 12:27 AM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
TenderMind is an AI-powered procurement evaluation system designed to address one of the most document-intensive and inefficient processes in public administration. In large-scale government tenders, such as those issued by CRPF, dozens of bidders submit hundreds of pages of documents including typed PDFs, scanned certificates, Word files, Excel sheets, images, and even handwritten annexures. Evaluation committees are required to manually review and cross-check these submissions against eligibility criteria, often taking several days to complete the process. This leads to inconsistencies, as different evaluators may interpret the same document differently, and there is typically no standardized audit trail or scalable mechanism to ensure fairness and transparency. TenderMind solves this problem by acting as a reliable AI co-evaluator that assists procurement officers by automating document understanding, extraction, and comparison, while keeping humans in control of all final decisions.The system begins with tender criterion extraction, where the uploaded tender document is processed using a large language model to identify and structure all eligibility criteria. Each criterion is classified by type (such as financial, technical, compliance, or certification), marked as mandatory or optional, and associated with specific thresholds and temporal conditions. This structured output, called the Criterion Schema, is then presented to the procurement officer for review and approval before any bidder evaluation begins, ensuring that the AI’s understanding aligns perfectly with human intent.Once the criteria are finalized, TenderMind processes bidder submissions through a robust document ingestion and OCR pipeline capable of handling multiple file formats. Typed PDFs are parsed directly, while scanned documents and images go through an OCR pipeline that includes preprocessing steps such as deskewing, denoising, and contrast enhancement using OpenCV, followed by text extraction using Tesseract for standard inputs and AWS Textract for complex layouts. Each extracted text block is assigned a confidence score, and any content below a predefined threshold is automatically flagged for manual review rather than being used for automated decision-making, ensuring reliability and preventing incorrect disqualifications.The next stage involves bidder information extraction using a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approach. For each criterion, the system runs targeted queries across the bidder’s documents to locate relevant information, extract the required values, and record the source document, page number, and confidence level. The system is designed to handle variations in terminology using semantic understanding and a domain-specific synonym dictionary, allowing it to correctly interpret terms like “annual revenue,” “net sales,” and “total receipts” as equivalent in the context of turnover.Following extraction, the verdict engine evaluates each criterion and assigns one of three outcomes: Eligible, Not Eligible, or Manual Review. A criterion is marked Eligible when there is clear, high-confidence evidence of compliance, and Not Eligible when there is strong evidence of failure. However, in cases where the data is ambiguous, confidence is low, values are close to thresholds, or documents are missing or conflicting, the system assigns a Manual Review status. A key design principle is that the system never issues a negative verdict based on uncertain or low-confidence data, thereby maintaining fairness and reducing risk.TenderMind then generates a comprehensive report and audit trail that consolidates all bidder evaluations into a clear, structured format. Each verdict is accompanied by a detailed explanation that includes the extracted value, source document, confidence level, and reasoning behind the decision. All actions performed by the system are logged with timestamps, model versions, document hashes, and decision outputs, creating an immutable audit trail that can be exported in formats such as PDF or JSON. This ensures complete transparency and accountability in the evaluation process.The system is built using a modern and scalable technology stack that includes advanced language models such as Claude or GPT-4o for document understanding, Tesseract and AWS Textract for OCR, OpenCV for image preprocessing, PyMuPDF for document parsing, and LlamaIndex for retrieval-augmented generation. Chroma or Pinecone is used for vector storage, FastAPI powers the backend, React with TailwindCSS provides a clean user interface, and PostgreSQL manages structured data. Deployment is designed to comply with government data residency requirements using secure environments such as AWS GovCloud or NIC Cloud.To ensure robustness, TenderMind incorporates multiple risk mitigation strategies. Low-quality documents are handled through confidence scoring and mandatory manual review, ambiguous tender language is flagged for clarification before evaluation begins, and near-threshold values are automatically escalated. The system also prevents hallucination by grounding all extractions in retrieved document content and enforces strict data confidentiality through secure deployment practices.A key strength of TenderMind is its human-in-the-loop design, which ensures that procurement officers remain in control at all times. There are four critical checkpoints: initial review and approval of extracted criteria, a structured manual review queue for ambiguous cases, final report sign-off with override capability, and a post-evaluation audit review by senior officials. This design balances automation with human judgment, ensuring both efficiency and accountability.The implementation plan is structured over four weeks, starting with document ingestion and OCR setup, followed by criterion extraction, bidder analysis using RAG, development of the verdict engine, and finally report generation and user interface development. The system is tested end-to-end using sample tenders and mock bidder submissions before deployment.In a real-world scenario, TenderMind enables a procurement officer to upload a large tender document along with multiple bidder submissions and receive a fully evaluated report within minutes instead of days. The system clearly identifies eligible and non-eligible bidders, flags ambiguous cases for review, and provides a complete audit trail for every decision. This significantly reduces manual workload, improves consistency, enhances transparency, and allows officers to focus on decision-making rather than document processing.

C Code Storm
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
TenderLens: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 21, 2026 10:57 AM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Problem UnderstandingGovernment procurement evaluation is slow, inconsistent, and difficult to audit. A single tender can attract dozens of bidders each submitting hundreds of pages - typed PDFs, scanned certificates, Word files, and photographs. Two evaluators reading the same documents often reach different conclusions. TenderLens automates this process end-to-end while keeping a human officer in control of every uncertain decision.﻿Four hard constraints shape a usable solution:• Document chaos: submissions arrive in every format, with OCR errors, stamps over text, and mixed-language content.• Language ambiguity: tender criteria use legally hedged language with compound conditions, date arithmetic, and domain-specific terms.• No silent disqualification: a wrongly disqualified bidder is a legal liability. Uncertain cases must go to a human, not be auto-rejected.• Auditability: every verdict must trace to a source document, page, and extracted value suitable for formal sign-off.﻿2. Eligibility Criteria ExtractionThe tender document is parsed through a five-stage pipeline: section classification, individual criterion extraction (using a local LLM with JSON schema output), criterion typing (Financial / Technical / Compliance / Document), mandatory vs. optional detection (linguistic cues: 'shall', 'must', 'desirable'), and condition normalisation into structured logic objects.﻿Compound criteria are decomposed into atomic sub-criteria with explicit AND / OR connectors. For example:﻿"3 similar works >= Rs. 2 Cr each in last 5 years, OR 2 similar works >= Rs. 3 Cr each" → stored as: OR( count(similar_works, val>=2Cr, years=5) >= 3, count(similar_works, val>=3Cr, years=5) >= 2 )﻿Output is a Criteria Registry - a structured JSON of all criteria with condition logic and required evidence types. A procurement officer approves this registry before evaluation begins, catching mis-extractions before they affect any bidder.﻿3. Bidder Submission ParsingEach uploaded file is routed to the appropriate processing chain:﻿Document typeProcessingNative text PDFDirect text extraction via PyMuPDF; table layout analysisScanned PDF / photographDeskew, binarise, then OCR (Tesseract 5 + PaddleOCR). Confidence score retained per block.Word (.docx)Direct XML extraction preserving table structureMixed pagesPer-page routing: text layer if available, OCR fallback﻿A second LLM pass extracts criterion-relevant values per bidder: turnover figures, project completion dates, certificate numbers. Every extracted value carries a confidence score (0.0–1.0) from three factors: OCR confidence, LLM extraction confidence, and cross-document consistency. Low-confidence values are flagged for review, never silently used.﻿4. Criterion Matching and EvaluationA deterministic rule engine (not an LLM) evaluates each criterion's condition logic against extracted values - numeric comparisons with currency normalisation, count conditions with date-range filtering, document presence and expiry checks. Each criterion for each bidder receives one of three verdicts:﻿VerdictConditionReview?ELIGIBLEAll mandatory criteria met, confidence >= 0.85NoNOT ELIGIBLEMandatory criterion definitively not met, confidence >= 0.85No - officer can overrideNEEDS MANUAL REVIEWConfidence < 0.85, conflicting signals, or unresolvable ambiguityAlways﻿The system is biased towards review over rejection. A partially unreadable ISO certificate returns NEEDS MANUAL REVIEW, not NOT ELIGIBLE. Conflicting turnover figures from two documents surface both values for the officer rather than picking one.﻿5. Explainability and Human ReviewEvery verdict carries four elements: which criterion was evaluated, which document and page were used, what value was extracted, and why the verdict was reached in plain language (e.g., 'Turnover Rs. 6.2 Cr >= threshold Rs. 5 Cr: PASS').﻿NEEDS MANUAL REVIEW cases enter a structured officer queue showing the criterion text, the extracted evidence with a page image highlight, and the reason for uncertainty. The officer selects ELIGIBLE or NOT ELIGIBLE and must enter a free-text justification. All decisions are logged with timestamp and user ID and are irrevocable in the audit log (overrides create a chain, not an erasure). Corrections feed back as labelled training examples to reduce future escalation rates.﻿6. Architecture and TechnologyComponentTechnology & RationaleOCRTesseract 5 + PaddleOCR (on-premises, open source, Devanagari support). Vision model fallback (LLaVA) for low-quality images.LLM extractionLocally-hosted Mistral 7B / Llama-3 8B (quantised). No PII sent to external APIs. Fine-tuned on government document vocabulary.Condition evaluatorPython rule engine on structured JSON criteria - deterministic, testable, auditable.Backend / storageFastAPI + PostgreSQL + MinIO. All on-premises deployable.Audit logKafka append-only event stream mirrored to immutable object storage. Signed PDF export with SHA-256 manifest.﻿7. Risks and MitigationsRiskMitigationPoor OCR on low-quality scansPre-processing pipeline + per-block confidence; bidder can re-submit cleaner copy via review UICriteria mis-extractionOfficer approves Criteria Registry before any bidder is evaluatedLegal challenge to verdictEvery NOT ELIGIBLE verdict traces to source document, page, and extracted value. Audit log exportable.Domain language shiftModular per-tender-type prompts; feedback loop captures errors for retrainingData privacyAll processing on-premises; role-based access control on bidder submissions﻿8. Round 2 Implementation PlanWeekMilestones1Ingestion pipeline, OCR integration, per-page routing. Validate on sandbox scanned documents.2Criteria Engine with local LLM. Extraction prompts for all criterion types. Officer approval UI for Criteria Registry.3Extraction Engine, Evaluation Engine, condition logic evaluator, review queue and officer UI.4Report Service, audit log export, end-to-end testing, demo preparation.﻿9. ConclusionTenderLens delivers automation that procurement officers can trust. Robust document parsing handles every format bidders submit. Deterministic rule-based evaluation ensures consistent, auditable verdicts. A human-in-the-loop workflow guarantees no bidder is silently disqualified. An immutable audit trail makes every decision defensible in a formal government procurement context.Built for the real world - consistent, explainable, and auditable at every step.

TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Company Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 21, 2026 11:26 AM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Problem Statement : Government departments release tenders for different works. Many companies apply for these tenders by submitting their documents such as cost details, experience, certifications, and technical proposals. Officers must manually read all submitted documents and compare them with tender conditions to select the best company. This process is difficult and time-consuming because there are many applications and each document is long and in different formats. It is also easy to miss important eligibility criteria while checking manually. Therefore, there is a need for an AI system that can automatically analyze tender conditions and compare them with company submissions.﻿SOLUTION:We build an AI-based tender evaluation system. The system reads the government tender document and extracts important conditions. Then it reads the documents submitted by companies and compares them with the tender requirements. The system checks eligibility, gives score to each company, and ranks them. Finally, it shows the best suitable companies for the government to review. Techniques Used: PDF Text Extraction – to read tender and company documents NLP (Natural Language Processing) – to understand tender conditions Keyword Matching – to compare requirements with company details Rule-Based Scoring – to assign score based on eligibility Ranking Algorithm – to select best company Explainable Output – shows why a company is selected

solutions.solution_text:

M madukalmanoj_e400
TEAM SUBMISSION [4]
TenderMind AI — Intelligent Procurement Evaluation Platform
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 21, 2026 12:28 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Picture a CRPF procurement officer on a Monday morning. She has a 60-page tender document, ten bidder submissions totalling 800 pages, three colleagues helping her evaluate, and a deadline on Friday. By Wednesday, two of her colleagues have marked the same bidder as eligible and ineligible respectively — neither is wrong, they just read different pages. By Thursday, nobody can remember which document proved which criterion. On Friday, the report goes out. Two months later, a rejected bidder files a legal challenge, and nobody can reconstruct the paper trail.This is not a rare failure. This is how government procurement evaluation works across India today.We built TenderMind AI because we believe a procurement officer's job should be making judgment calls — not hunting through 800 pages to find a turnover certificate that may or may not have been photographed sideways on someone's phone.﻿What TenderMind AI actually does:You upload the tender document. Within 90 seconds, the system has read every clause and built a structured checklist — each eligibility criterion with its exact threshold, the time window it applies to, what kind of document satisfies it, and the page and clause it came from. Before anything else happens, the officer reviews this checklist and approves it. If the system misread something, she fixes it here — not after 10 bidders have been wrongly evaluated.Then the bidder packages go in. Typed PDFs, scanned certificates, Word documents, phone photographs of ISO certificates — the system handles all of it. It knows the difference between a clean typed P&L and a rubber-stamped scanned certificate that needs deskewing and noise removal before any OCR will work on it. It normalises "Rs. 62 lakhs" and "INR 6.2 Cr." and "₹ 6,20,00,000" into the same number before comparing against any threshold, because that inconsistency alone breaks most naive implementations.Before a single bidder document is evaluated, the system quietly does three things most procurement tools completely ignore: it checks every bidder's GST and PAN against the CPPP debarred vendor registry; it checks whether any bidder director's name or registration number matches anyone on the evaluation committee; and it flags if a previously disqualified vendor appears to have re-registered under a new name. None of this is glamorous. All of it matters.For the actual evaluation, the system splits criteria into two types. Numeric criteria — turnover, project count, certificate validity — are handled by a rule engine. The value is extracted, normalised, compared. Either it passes or it doesn't, and the evidence is right there: page 14, the number that was read, the threshold it was compared against. Qualitative criteria — "three similar projects", "relevant construction experience" — are handled by an LLM that reads the bidder's project descriptions and reasons about similarity. These never auto-commit. A human always reviews them.The confidence system is the part we're most proud of. If the system is 85% or more confident in an extraction, it commits the verdict and logs the full evidence chain. If it's between 65–84%, it suggests a verdict but asks the officer to confirm. Below 65% — typically phone photographs or heavily stamped documents — it emits no verdict at all. It shows the officer the raw image, what it tried to read, and asks her to make the call. A system that auto-rejects when uncertain is a liability. We built the opposite.When the tender itself changes — and real tenders get amended two or three times before the submission deadline — the system detects the corrigendum, diffs it against the approved criteria list, and brings the changed criteria back to the officer for re-approval before any evaluation proceeds. Evaluating ten bidders against outdated criteria is a procurement nightmare we've seen happen repeatedly.For consortium and joint venture bids — which make up a large share of infrastructure tenders — the system extracts the JV structure, identifies each partner's role, and aggregates eligibility across them according to the tender's own JV rules. It does not treat a consortium bid like a single-entity submission.The review dashboard is where the officer actually spends her time. Every bidder, every criterion, every verdict — laid out clearly. For each flagged case, she sees the suggested verdict, the extracted value, and the actual source document rendered on screen with the relevant region highlighted in yellow. She confirms, overrides, or escalates. She cannot bulk-approve. Each flagged case is individual, because rubber-stamping defeats the whole point. Every action she takes is logged with her ID and timestamp.We also added something that no current procurement tool offers: a criteria anomaly detector. It compares the extracted criteria against similar past tenders in the database and flags statistically unusual thresholds — the kind that seem written to let exactly one vendor through. It does not make accusations. It flags for a senior officer's attention. Procurement integrity requires that someone is paying attention to the criteria themselves, not just the evaluation.After eligibility is established, the platform runs a bid price reasonableness check on submitted financial bids — comparing L1 against cost benchmarks and historical bid distributions to catch suspiciously low bids before the contract is awarded. And over time, the historical bid intelligence layer surfaces patterns: which vendors consistently win, which criteria are most commonly failed, which document types cause the most extraction problems. Institutional memory that currently lives nowhere.The final report is a cryptographically signed PDF. Every verdict traceable to a source document and page. Every reviewer action logged. If a rejected bidder challenges the decision six months later, the procurement officer has a complete, unalterable record of exactly what was found, where, and who reviewed it.The whole process — for a 60-page tender and ten bidders — takes about 38 minutes instead of three days. But the time saving is almost secondary. The real value is that for the first time, the evaluation is consistent, documented, and defensible.﻿Tenders are issued in Hindi, Kannada, Telugu, and other regional languages across India. Our OCR and extraction pipeline handles multilingual documents, because a procurement platform that only works in English is not a procurement platform for India.For officers reviewing in the field on tablets and phones with unreliable connectivity, the interface is a Progressive Web App — it works offline and syncs when the connection returns. Government technology consistently fails at field deployment. We designed for it from the start.Every reviewer correction feeds back into model retraining. The system gets measurably more accurate with each tender cycle — not just more familiar, but provably better on the document types that caused the most errors.﻿Stack: FastAPI · PyMuPDF · OpenCV · Tesseract 5 · Azure Doc Intelligence · LayoutLMv3 · spaCy · Claude Sonnet (de-identified inputs only) · LangChain + pgvector · React · PostgreSQL · Docker → NIC CloudRound 2 sprint plan: Hours 0–20: Tender ingestion, criteria extraction, corrigendum diff, officer approval gate Hours 20–40: Bidder extraction, debarment and COI checks, verdict engine with confidence gating Hours 40–60: Review dashboard with live PDF viewer and bounding-box highlights Hours 60–72: Report generator and full end-to-end demo — 4 criteria, 10 bidders, 1 scanned certificate, 1 JV bid, 1 corrigendumWe are not building a tool that replaces procurement officers. We are building one that finally gives them what they deserve: a process they can stand behind.

solutions.team_name: VerdictAI — Criterion-Aware Tender Evaluation with Explainable Verdicts and Institutional Memory
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


P pavilion
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
VerdictAI — Criterion-Aware Tender Evaluation with Explainable Verdicts and Institutional Memory
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 21, 2026 12:39 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Clear doc link:  Click here (https://docs.google.com/document/d/1rv_D1L1mRCIiGX_YgDw-YVoOlp00xZvNHiQLambEW2I/edit?usp=sharing)  (https://docs.google.com/document/d/1rv_D1L1mRCIiGX_YgDw-YVoOlp00xZvNHiQLambEW2I/edit?usp=sharing) THE PROBLEMThree structural failures no checklist fixes:No institutional memory — "similar works" is interpreted differently by every evaluator. No record of how that phrase was resolved in prior tenders from the same department.No reproducible trace — a CVC complaint cannot be answered with a criterion-level record linking each verdict to a specific document, page, and value. Committee minutes are not an audit trail.No protection against silent errors — misread turnover figures, expired certificates, wrong measurement periods propagate to the final evaluation undetected. ARCHITECTURE: FIVE LAYERSL1 — Document Intelligence 5-step pre-processing before OCR: (1) Deskew via OpenCV Hough — corrects 2–15° scanner/phone rotation. (2) Sauvola adaptive binarisation — handles phone-camera gradients that global Otsu cannot. (3) Rubber stamp isolation + text recovery — red-channel separation, connected-component masking, colour-channel decomposition recovers text under stamps. A stamp over a CA certificate turnover figure is a daily occurrence in Indian procurement. (4) Non-local means denoising. (5) ESRGAN super-resolution to 300 DPI.Dual-engine OCR: Tesseract 5 (LSTM — English, Devanagari) + PaddleOCR v3 (tables, mixed-script, Kannada/Tamil) in parallel. Word-level confidence arbitration. Page-level confidence = length-weighted mean → directly drives routing. Degraded documents route to HITL, never auto-committed. Output: (page, bbox, text, ocr_confidence, source_format) per word.L2 — Effective Tender Specification (ETS) Builder The tender is a version-controlled object: base NIT + corrigenda applied chronologically. Each criterion carries originating document, clause reference, amendment history. No evaluation begins until the officer confirms the assembled ETS.Five criterion types — each evaluated differently: • numeric_threshold (Turnover ≥ ₹5 Cr): Deterministic. GFR override: NOT permitted. • categorical_presence (Valid GST): Document + validity date. GFR override: NOT permitted. • temporal_recency (3 similar works, last 5 yrs): Deterministic + CPM similarity. GFR override: NOT permitted. • composite (Turnover + experience + cert.): Decomposed sub-criteria. GFR override: per sub-criterion. • qualitative_assessment (Adequate technical capacity): LLM + CPM precedents. GFR override: Permitted.Schema review gate enforced by state machine — errors caught once, not propagated to all bidders. Output: Approved ETS — type, threshold, measurement period, evidence types, GFR_override_permitted, provenance chain.L3 — Evidence Extractor Per (bidder × criterion), type-specific search — not generic retrieval: • numeric_threshold: Table-aware parsing, extracts value + fiscal year + unit. Flags consolidated vs. standalone account ambiguity. • categorical_presence: Certificate search with regulatory synonyms. Routes to HITL if validity unreadable — does not assume invalidity. • temporal_recency: Project value, completion date, description → CPM similarity model. • qualitative_assessment: Relevant passages + CPM precedents, same department + category. • Entity mismatch detection: Fuzzy-matches company name from every financial document against registered bidder name. Parent-company financial substitution is a documented fraud vector — mismatch → mandatory HITL regardless of confidence. Output: (value, source_doc, page, bbox, ocr_conf, extraction_conf, entity_match_flag).L4 — Evaluation Engine + Confidence Router Type-specific logic: • numeric_threshold: extraction_conf < 0.75 → REVIEW regardless of comparison result. • categorical_presence: Found + current → PASS. Found + expired → FAIL. Unreadable date → REVIEW. Not found → REVIEW (officer confirms absence before disqualification — never auto-FAIL). • qualitative_assessment: Any LLM FAIL → HITL regardless of confidence. VerdictAI never auto-commits a disqualification from language interpretation.Three routes: • Auto-commit (≥ 0.85): deterministic criterion, no flags → logged automatically • HITL review (0.50–0.84): partial confidence or any LLM verdict → officer confirms/overrides • Mandatory review (< 0.50 or explicit flag): entity mismatch, stamp obscuration, debarment, GFR-mandatory FAIL → explicit officer decision requiredNever-silent guarantee: any mandatory-criterion FAIL routes to mandatory review before commitment — enforced by state machine. Output: (verdict, confidence, evaluation_method, route, routing_reason).L5 — Immutable Audit Ledger Append-only log — DB trigger blocks all UPDATE/DELETE. Events: document received, OCR completed, corrigendum linked, schema approved (officer ID + timestamp), debarment checked, evidence extracted, verdict computed, case routed, officer decision, report generated.Root of trust: (1) SHA-256 hash embedded in PDF as string + QR code. PDF signed via PKCS#7 / HSM (Azure Key Vault cloud; on-premise HSM for air-gapped). Modification invalidates signature. (2) Daily Merkle root anchored to blockchain (NIC National Blockchain Framework / Polygon fallback) — third-party timestamp independent of infrastructure.Reproducibility: any evaluation regenerated from stored inputs (original docs, ETS, pinned model versions, extracted values, officer decisions). Re-run output byte-identical.﻿CORE INNOVATION: CRITERION PRECEDENT MEMORY (CPM)Every officer decision generates a CPM entry: criterion_text (tokenised), resolved_interpretation, department, tender_category, verdict, officer_action, officer_id (anonymised), tender_id.Four compounding functions:Disambiguation: ambiguous language queries CPM for same-department, same-category precedents. Officer sees prior interpretations with tender IDs → confirms or modifies → becomes next entry. Interpretation becomes institutional record, not individual judgment.Similarity scoring: labeled (project_description, is_similar) pairs fine-tune a domain-specific model. Far more accurate than general embeddings on "RCC box culvert" vs "canal crossing structure."Confidence calibration: isotonic regression on (computed_confidence, officer_confirmed_correct) → calibrated extraction confidence. Temperature scaling for LLM confidence (Guo et al. 2017). 0.87 means 87% correct in practice.Routing optimisation: (confidence, verdict, officer_confirmed) triples → optimal auto-commit thresholds per criterion type per department. 50 evaluations in, thresholds are tuned to that department's document quality and language. Not replicable without the data. Cold start: conservative defaults (auto-commit ceiling 0.90, review floor 0.60) until data accumulates. Visible in dashboard. GFR 2017 + CVC COMPLIANCE — ENFORCED IN CODEGFR_override_permitted flag per criterion. Override physically disabled in HITL for mandatory FAILs — GFR rule number renders in place of the button. CVC-violating override is impossible at the interaction level.CVC debarment pre-check: PAN + company name cross-referenced against CVC debarred entities list + GeM blacklist. Match halts pipeline before any criterion evaluation.Second-officer confirmation for borderline overrides — both logged with ID + timestamp.HITL REVIEW INTERFACEFive components, one screen, no navigation:Criterion: full ETS text, type, mandatory status, GFR rule numberEvidence: original page image with extracted region in bounding box (always image for scanned docs — visual OCR error catch is the primary routing trigger)System analysis: precise routing reason ("turnover partially obscured by stamp — extracted as ₹7.2 Cr, confidence 0.41")CPM context: up to 3 matching precedents from same department with tender IDsDecision: Confirm or Override (structured reason required; disabled for GFR-mandatory FAIL)DEPLOYMENTOn-premise / air-gapped (CRPF, MoD, DRDO): Llama 3.1 70B via Ollama, no data egress, on-premise HSM, permissioned blockchain node. NIC MeghRaj + CERT-In compatible. SaaS (state PWDs, PSUs): Azure OpenAI GPT-4o under DPA, India data residency, per-tenant CPM isolation. Phased rollout: CRPF (0–12 mo) → MHA-wide BSF/CISF/SSB (12–24 mo) → GeM integration (24–36 mo).PROTOTYPE — 28 APRIL TO 04 MAY 2026Demo: (1) Live criterion extraction + corrigendum showing amended values. (2) Scanned CA certificate with stamp over turnover — confidence 0.41, mandatory review, HITL shows page with stamp bbox highlighted. (3) Entity mismatch on parent-company balance sheet. (4) CPM precedent injection during schema review.Day 1 (28 Apr): 5-step pre-processing + dual OCR + confidence arbitration Day 2 (29 Apr): Criterion extractor + type classifier + GFR annotation + ETS builder Day 3 (30 Apr): Schema review UI + corrigendum diff + approval gate Day 4 (01 May): Evidence extractor + evaluation logic + entity mismatch detector Day 5 (02 May): HITL queue + bbox overlay + GFR enforcement + CPM bootstrap + integration Day 6 (03 May): Audit log + SHA-256 PDF + blockchain stub + end-to-end test Buffer (04 May): Edge case testing + submission packagingStack: FastAPI · Tesseract 5 + PaddleOCR v3 · OpenCV + scikit-image · Azure OpenAI GPT-4o / Llama 3.1 70B · PostgreSQL + pgvector · React · Polygon / NIC blockchainWHAT MAKES THIS DIFFERENTTender = version-controlled specification — corrigenda are first-class inputsCriteria are typed — 5 types, 5 evaluation strategies, GFR permissions per criterionConfidence is calibrated — isotonic regression on real officer data, not LLM self-reportNo auto-commit without complete evidence chain — value + document + page + bbox + confidence all required; any missing → HITLEvery officer decision improves the system — CPM converts judgment into compounding institutional memoryThe result: a platform a procurement officer trusts, a minister can sign off on, and a CVC inquiry finds sufficient.Edit link

N neha.s.singh2027_7057
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
TenderEval AI – Explainable Tender Evaluation System
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 21, 2026 01:28 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
TENDER EVAL AI – EXPLAINABLE TENDER EVALUATION SYSTEM﻿PROBLEM UNDERSTANDING Government tender evaluation is currently a manual and time-consuming process. Evaluators must review large volumes of bidder documents such as financial statements, certifications, and compliance records. These documents are often unstructured, including PDFs, scanned images, and different formats. This makes the evaluation process slow, inconsistent, and prone to human error. The goal is to build a system that can automatically extract eligibility criteria from tender documents, analyze bidder submissions, and evaluate whether they meet the required conditions. The system must also provide clear explanations for every decision and flag uncertain cases for manual review.﻿PROJECT DESCRIPTION The proposed system, TenderEval AI, reads tender documents and extracts eligibility criteria such as financial, technical, and compliance requirements. It then processes bidder submissions, including PDFs and scanned documents, to extract relevant information. The extracted bidder data is compared with the tender criteria using a rule-based evaluation engine. Based on this comparison, the system classifies bidders as Eligible, Not Eligible, or Needs Manual Review. Each decision is accompanied by a clear explanation including the required condition, extracted value, and document source. If the document is unclear, the system flags it for manual review.﻿SOLUTION APPROACHAccept tender and bidder documentsExtract text using OCR and PDF parsingIdentify eligibility criteriaExtract bidder dataCompare using rule-based logicClassify: Eligible / Not Eligible / ReviewProvide explanation for every decision﻿ARCHITECTURE OVERVIEW The system follows a modular pipeline. Documents are processed using OCR and parsing tools to extract text. The text is split into two flows: tender criteria extraction and bidder data extraction. Both are passed to an evaluation engine which determines eligibility. An explainability layer links decisions to document evidence, and results are shown with manual review flags.﻿TECH STACK Python, Django, Tesseract OCR, pdfplumber, PostgreSQL﻿CHALLENGES AND SOLUTIONSUnstructured documents → OCR + parsingFormat variation → flexible extractionMissing/unclear data → manual reviewExplainability → detailed reasoning﻿FUTURE WORKBuild working prototypeImprove extraction accuracyDevelop UI dashboardTest with sample documents﻿KEY STRENGTH The solution focuses on practical implementation and explainability using OCR, structured extraction, and rule-based evaluation instead of black-box models.﻿ARCHITECTURE DIAGRAM  https://drive.google.com/file/d/17w529BoplUyMsbK1oN_zMycK-cbnSYR7/view?usp=sharing

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


M Makers_of_entanglement
TEAM SUBMISSION [2]
Pramaan — AI-Assisted, Audit-Ready Tender Eligibility Evaluation for CRPF
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 21, 2026 03:14 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
CRPF and other government buyers spend roughly 200 officer-hours evaluating each tender by hand, and at the end they still have no machine-readable audit trail — two officers reading the same documents can reach different verdicts. Pramaan fixes the boring, repeatable part of that work without taking the decision away from the officer. The platform reads a tender, extracts its eligibility criteria as typed, schema-constrained records, and asks the officer to review them before any bidder is touched. It then ingests every bidder's submission — typed PDFs, scanned documents, Word files, mobile-phone photographs of certificates — and for each criterion it locates the specific span in the specific document that satisfies or fails it. Each bidder × criterion pair gets a verdict: Eligible, Not Eligible, or Needs Review. Needs Review is the safety valve. Whenever OCR confidence is low, the criterion language is ambiguous, evidence is missing, or the model can't quote a literal source span, the case is escalated to the officer with the reason — a government system must never silently disqualify anyone. Numeric checks like "turnover ≥ ₹5 crore in each of the last 3 FYs" run in deterministic Python, not in the LLM, so there is no room for arithmetic hallucination. Deployment is hybrid and CRPF-grade. An on-prem open-source LLM (Llama 3.1 70B on NIC / MeghRaj) handles ~80% of traffic; the hardest 20% goes to frontier LLMs through a redaction gate that strips bidder name, address, PAN, GSTIN, and signatory before any external call. No bidder-identifying data ever leaves government infrastructure. Every verdict, every officer override, and every re-run is written to a hash-chained append-only audit log with prompt and model versions attached, so a decision made today can be reproduced byte-for-byte three years from now for an RTI query or a court hearing. A working FastAPI + React prototype already takes a mock CRPF construction tender and three bidders end-to-end on a laptop with no API key, exercising the Eligible, Not Eligible, and Needs Review paths and producing a verifiable audit chain.

TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 21, 2026 03:43 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
AI-Based Tender Evaluation & Eligibility Analysis PlatformOverviewGovernment tender evaluation is a high-stakes, document-intensive process that requires verifying whether each bidder satisfies a complex set of eligibility criteria spanning financial, technical, and compliance requirements. These criteria are typically embedded in lengthy, unstructured tender documents, while bidder submissions arrive in heterogeneous formats such as PDFs, scanned copies, Word files, tables, and even photographs.Our solution is an AI-powered decision-support platform that automates tender understanding, bidder analysis, and eligibility evaluation—while ensuring full explainability, auditability, and human oversight, making it suitable for real-world government procurement workflows.﻿Problem UnderstandingCurrent tender evaluation processes are:Manual and time-consuming (reviewing hundreds of pages per bidder)Inconsistent across evaluatorsProne to oversight and ambiguityDifficult to audit and justifyThe key challenge is not just extracting data, but making reliable, explainable decisions from unstructured and inconsistent documents.﻿Our SolutionWe propose an end-to-end AI platform that performs three core functions:﻿1. Tender UnderstandingAutomatically extracts eligibility criteria from tender documentsClassifies criteria into:Financial (e.g., turnover thresholds)Technical (e.g., project experience)Compliance (e.g., GST, certifications)Distinguishes mandatory vs optional conditionsConverts criteria into a structured, machine-readable format﻿2. Bidder UnderstandingParses bidder submissions across all formats:Typed PDFsScanned documentsTables and structured filesImages and photographsUses OCR and AI extraction to identify:Financial data (turnover, revenue)Experience (projects completed)Certifications (ISO, licenses)Registrations (GST, compliance records)Handles variation in wording, format, and document structureAttaches source evidence (document, page, snippet) to every extracted value﻿3. Evaluation & ExplainabilityMatches bidder data against each criterion using a hybrid rule-based + AI systemProduces criterion-level decisions:Eligible (PASS)Not Eligible (FAIL)Needs Manual Review (REVIEW)Assigns confidence scores for each decisionExplainability (Core Differentiator)Every verdict includes:The exact criterion being evaluatedThe value extracted from bidder documentsThe source document and locationA clear reasoning explanation﻿4. Human-in-the-Loop WorkflowAmbiguous or low-confidence cases are never auto-rejectedFlagged cases are routed to a review interface where officials can:ApproveEditRejectReviewer actions are stored to improve future decisions﻿5. Auditability & ComplianceEvery step is fully traceable and logged:Input documentsExtracted valuesEvaluation decisionsReviewer actionsProduces a complete audit trail suitable for government procurement standards﻿6. Output & ReportingGenerates a consolidated evaluation report:Criterion-by-criterion analysisEligible / Not Eligible / Review statusSupporting evidence for each decisionExportable and ready for official sign-off﻿Key FeaturesMulti-format document processing (PDF, scanned, images, tables)AI-driven structured extraction + deterministic rule engineExplainable, criterion-level decision makingConfidence-based ambiguity handlingHuman verification workflowEnd-to-end audit trailDashboard for evaluation and decision support﻿Technology ApproachOCR for scanned and image-based documentsLarge Language Models for document understanding and extractionRule-based evaluation engine for consistent eligibility decisionsVector search (RAG) for contextual document retrievalWeb-based dashboard for interaction and review﻿Handling Real-World ChallengesScanned/low-quality documents: OCR confidence + review fallbackAmbiguous language: conservative evaluation → REVIEW stateIncomplete data: never assumed → flagged explicitlyInconsistent formats: normalized via AI extraction﻿Expected ImpactReduces evaluation time from days to minutesEnsures consistent and unbiased decision-makingImproves transparency and accountabilityEnables trustworthy AI-assisted procurement decisions﻿ConclusionThis platform transforms tender evaluation from a manual, error-prone process into a structured, explainable, and auditable AI-assisted workflow. By combining AI extraction, rule-based reasoning, and human oversight, it aligns with real-world government constraints and delivers a scalable, trustworthy solution for procurement decision-making.

Project Description
We are building a system that turns long court judgment PDFs into short, clear, and verified action steps that government officers can use directly.The main idea is simple: instead of reading 20–30 pages, the officer should see a clean summary of what needs to be done, who should do it, and by when — with proof from the original document.﻿What we are buildingOur project is a web-based system where a user uploads a court judgment PDF and gets:Key details extracted in structured formA clear action plan based on the judgmentA review screen to verify and correct resultsA dashboard to track actions and deadlines﻿How our solution works1. Document Processing When a PDF is uploaded, we first clean and break the document into smaller readable parts. We support both scanned and digital PDFs.﻿2. Information Extraction We use a hybrid approach:Rules for structured data like dates and case numbersAI for understanding court directions and decisionsWe also store the exact lines from where each piece of data is taken.﻿3. Action Plan Creation This is the core of our system.We convert extracted information into simple actions such as:Compliance requiredConsider appealFollow specific court directionWe also:Identify possible deadlinesMap actions to departments using keyword logicThe goal is to make outputs practical, not just informative.﻿4. Verification Layer Before showing final results, we provide a review screen:Highlighted source text from PDFExtracted valuesConfidence scoreUsers can edit or approve the data. Only verified data moves forward.﻿5. DashboardWe provide a simple dashboard that shows:Department-wise actionsPending and completed tasksUpcoming deadlinesUsers can also open the original document with highlights for reference.﻿Our ApproachWe are not building a fully automatic system. Instead, we focus on:Accuracy over automationClear outputs instead of raw dataHuman control over final decisions﻿Why this idea is usefulSaves time spent on reading long documentsMakes important actions visible instantlyReduces chances of missing deadlinesHelps teams coordinate better﻿Tech Approach (High Level)Frontend: Next.js (for fast and clean UI)Backend: Node.js APIsAI Layer: LLM + structured promptsProcessing: OCR + text parsingDatabase: MongoDB
