A abhinav.24bce10992_5984
TEAM SUBMISSION [3]
SaakshyaNet — AI-Powered Tender Evaluation and Bid Eligibility Intelligence Platform
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 23, 2026 01:40 AM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
SaakshyaNet is an all-in-one AI tool used to automate the process of evidence extraction and eligibility assessment for government tenders, while ensuring that the procurement officer remains fully in charge of the decision-making process.The system processes its work in five steps. The first step involves the ingestion of tender documents and bids in whatever format they are — typed PDFs, scanned versions, Word documents, and even pictures of certificates — via OCR with confidence scoring to process poorly-scanned or handwritten material. The second step is transforming the tender document into a structure that is machine-readable — criterion registry — with financial criteria, technical requirements, compliance requirements, and certification requirements, classified into must-haves and nice-to-haves, and any truly ambiguities in the criterion is identified before the system touches anything pertaining to bidders. The third step consists of going through each bid and extracting its value with respect to each criterion, intelligently harmonizing the huge differences in reporting of the same data points across companies. While deterministic rules are used to make decisions in the clear-cut cases, any other ambiguous cases will be adjudicated using the LLM as a judge, but the system will never disqualify a bidder behind closed doors: the exact reason for any disqualification is given to the officer with full transparency. Fourth, when clearly defined cases are assessed, a deterministic rule engine will evaluate and resolve the case. When there are ambiguous cases, the LLM judge will make a determination based on the weight of the various cases presented to it. However, there will never be a case in which a bidder will be disqualified — if a case has a low confidence level(s), if there are conflicting pieces of evidence, if a document is ambiguous or if the dollar value of the bid is at or near the threshold value(s), SaakshyaNet will provide an explanation to the procurement officer as to why this bidder's bid is not being accepted. Fifth, there will be a consolidated evaluation report issued that will provide a criterion-by-criterion evaluation of all bids and will link each evaluation back to the specific documents, pages, and paragraphs that supported that evaluation.In addition, there will be a complete immutably auditable record of the evaluation for formal governmental procurement accountability purposes.The name reflects the mission: Saakshya means evidence — and SaakshyaNet is the network that captures, verifies and presents evidence so that no decision is made without it.

Impact:Reduces the time from "Judgment Received" to "Department Tasked" from weeks to minutes, while closing the loop on physical implementation through AI-driven visual auditing.

solutions.team_name: CRPF GTEMS - Government Tender Eligibility & Management System using AI Evaluation Engine
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


A axushgpRVCE
TEAM SUBMISSION [2]
CRPF GTEMS - Government Tender Eligibility & Management System using AI Evaluation Engine
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 23, 2026 11:34 AM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
WE ARE BUILDING: CRPF GTEMS is a full lifecycle management system for government tender evaluation - from the moment a tender is uploaded to the moment the officer signs off on a legally defensible evaluation report. It replaces the entire manual process, not just one step of it. A procurement officer uploads the tender document. The system reads it, extracts every eligibility criterion automatically, classifies each one by type and mandatory status, and builds a structured knowledge base. Bidder documents - in any format, any language - are then ingested, OCR-processed where needed, and matched against every criterion. The output is a criterion-by-criterion evaluation report for each bidder, with verdicts, source citations, and a comparative matrix across all bidders. Ambiguous cases surface to a human review queue. Nothing is silently rejected. Every decision is logged. It is an evaluation engine using neural networks and deep analytical AI Management System. It reads every document, applies every criterion, produces a verdict with a source citation for every single decision, and refuses to silently reject anyone. The system works in four steps:1. Tender Understanding - The system ingests the tender document and extracts every eligibility criterion into a structured knowledge base. Each criterion is classified by type (financial, experience, certification, registration, legal), tagged as mandatory or optional using the exact statutory language from the tender (shall/must = mandatory, should/desirable = optional), and annotated with its threshold value, time window, and applicable exemptions (MSME, JV, startup). It structurally parses with legal classification.2. Document Ingestion - The system accepts every document type a bidder submits: text PDFs, scanned PDFs, photographs of certificates, DOCX files. Scanned documents and images go through an OCR pipeline with Hindi and English support, image preprocessing for contrast and alignment, and a per-page confidence score. Low-confidence pages are flagged for human review - never trusted blindly. Each document is classified into one of 16 or more types (GST certificate, balance sheet, ISO cert, work order, completion certificate, etc.) and its key entities are extracted.3. Criterion Matching - For each bidder, the system compares extracted values against each criterion. Financial thresholds handle multi-year averaging, lakh/crore normalization, and currency conversion at RBI reference rate. Experience criteria use semantic similarity to evaluate "similar work" claims with a configurable threshold. Certification checks validate scope match against tender requirements and expiry date against submission deadline. Conditional logic applies MSME and JV exemptions correctly per the PPP Order 2012.4. Evaluation Report - For each bidder, the system produces a criterion-by-criterion verdict: Pass, Fail, or Manual Review. Every verdict cites the specific criterion, the specific document, the specific page, the extracted value, and the reason for the decision. Ambiguous or low-confidence cases surface to a human review queue - they are never silently rejected. The final output is a comparative matrix across all bidders and a signed-off evaluation report with a complete audit trail.The four non-negotiables: Never silently disqualify: If a scanned document is unreadable, the bidder is not marked as failing that criterion. The document is flagged to the human review queue with the exact reason. Nothing is dropped. Every verdict has a source: Not "ELIGIBLE." The officer sees: criterion text, document name, page number, extracted value, required value, and why it passed or failed. That is what a signed report means legally. Mandatory vs. optional is a legal distinction: Disqualifying a bidder on an optional criterion is grounds for a legal challenge. The system classifies these from the tender's own language, not a guess. The audit trail must survive an RTI: Every automated decision logs model version, input document hash, extracted value, verdict, and confidence. Human overrides record the original automated verdict plus the officer's stated reason. Domain depth that makes the difference. The gap between a demo and a deployable government system is entirely in the edge cases. GTEMS handles: average vs. single-year turnover under Ind AS, CA certificate membership number requirement per ICAI norms, ISO scope vs. tender scope mismatch detection, IAF-accredited certification body validation, JV experience credit at configurable percentage, MSME exemption application per PPP 2012, Udyam portal active-status verification, semantic interpretation of "similar work," related-party experience flagging, and scanned Hindi-language document processing. These are the exact failure modes documented in CAG audit reports on procurement irregularities. The system is designed around GFR 2017 and CVC guidelines, not a generic understanding of what procurement looks like. WHAT CHANGES WHEN OUR PROJECT WORKS:A full tender evaluation - 10 bidders, 12 criteria, 600 pages - completed in under 4 hours instead of 3 to 5 days. Every verdict cited to a specific document, page, and extracted value - no judgment calls buried in a committee meeting. Zero bidders silently disqualified due to evaluator oversight or OCR failure. A signed-off report and audit trail that withstands RTI scrutiny. Deployable across every central government ministry on CPPP and eventually across state governments on GeM.

solutions.team_name: Adaptive Consistency Interoperability Layer for Two-Way Government System Synchronization
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


L LimitlessDuo
TEAM SUBMISSION [2]
Adaptive Consistency Interoperability Layer for Two-Way Government System Synchronization
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 23, 2026 12:23 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Interoperability Layer for Two-Way Synchronization between SWS and Department Systems1. Problem UnderstandingKarnataka’s Single Window System (SWS) is intended to become the canonical front door for business service requests, but 40+ legacy department systems remain live and authoritative for existing businesses. Each system has its own schema, identifiers, APIs, and operational rules. Since neither SWS nor any department system can be modified, the real challenge is not migration but coordination: when the same business updates a record on either side, both sides must converge without silent overwrites, lost updates, or duplicate writes.Our solution is a non-intrusive interoperability layer that sits beside existing systems and synchronizes updates in both directions using SWS ID as the common join key. The system is designed for correctness first, but because the source systems are independent and failure-prone, it must also balance availability, latency, and auditability.2. Core Design PrincipleThe main idea is an adaptive consistency policy engine. This is not an ML model. It is a deterministic rule layer that selects how a request should be handled based on operation type, criticality, source system, and conflict risk.For example:· critical changes such as registered address or legal identifiers use stricter handling,· low-risk changes can use asynchronous propagation,· ambiguous or conflicting updates are routed to human review.This is practical for government systems because correctness is the priority, but global strong consistency across 40+ independent systems is not realistic under partial failures, timeouts, and system downtime.3. Two-Way PropagationSWS to department systems1. A request arrives in SWS.2. The middleware resolves the business through the SWS ID Registry, which maps SWS ID to the corresponding department record IDs.3. The request is translated into each department’s schema through a department-specific adapter.4. Updates are sent asynchronously to target systems.5. Every write is acknowledged, retried if needed, and stored in the audit trail.Department systems to SWS1. A change is detected in a department system.2. The change is mapped back to SWS ID through the registry.3. The request is translated into the SWS representation.4. The update is written to SWS and logged end-to-end.4. Schema Translation and Registry ManagementEvery department system gets a pluggable adapter. The adapter contains field mappings, normalization rules, and transformation logic for that system.To keep the solution maintainable, the mappings live in a Schema Registry:· version-controlled· configurable in JSON or YAML· updatable without changing core code· able to support new departments as they are onboardedLLM usage, narrowly scopedAn LLM is optional and only used to help bootstrap draft mappings from scrambled or synthetic sample payloads during onboarding. A human reviews and approves the generated mapping before it enters the registry. The LLM is not part of the live propagation path and does not make final decisions.5. Change Discovery for Systems Without EventsSome legacy systems will not emit events. For those systems, we use a hybrid change-capture approach:· API polling· snapshot comparison· baseline hashing for recordsEach polled record is compared against the last stored hash for that department record. Only differences emit events into the middleware. This keeps the system compatible with legacy systems while avoiding unnecessary reprocessing.6. Conflict Detection and ResolutionConflicts occur when multiple updates for the same SWS ID arrive within a short time window, or when different systems report incompatible values for the same field.Our conflict handling has three steps: 1. Detect the conflict. 2. Apply a policy based on operation criticality and source priority. 3. Escalate unresolved cases to a review queue instead of silently overwriting data.A losing update is never discarded invisibly. It is stored in a Conflict Queue, along with the competing values, the policy applied, and the reason the update was not auto-committed.Explainability and reversibilityEvery conflict outcome is traceable. The audit log stores:· source systems involved· timestamps· fields that disagreed· the policy applied· the final decision· the fallback path, if anyIf a decision must be reversed, the event log allows replay and correction.7. Reliability GuaranteesIdempotencyEvery update uses a deterministic idempotency key derived from the logical change, such as: SHA256(SWS ID + field + source_system + new_value)This ensures that duplicate deliveries do not create duplicate writes.At-least-once deliveryUpdates are retried with exponential backoff. If retries still fail, they are placed in a dead-letter queue or reconciliation queue.Audit trailThe system uses an append-only event store. Each event records:· origin· transformation steps· destination· retries· acknowledgements· conflicts· resolution decisionsThis gives a full end-to-end history of every propagation.8. Architecture OverviewThe proposed architecture has the following components:· API Gateway for ingestion from SWS or department connectors· SWS ID Registry for business lookup and mapping· Schema Registry for versioned department mappings· Event Bus for asynchronous propagation· Transformation Layer for schema translation· Policy Engine for adaptive consistency decisions· Sync Engine for retries and delivery· Conflict Resolver for collision handling· Audit Store for immutable traceability· Change Capture Layer for polling and snapshot-based discoveryWhy this architectureIt isolates the unstable parts of the ecosystem behind small adapters, keeps the core logic deterministic, and makes the system easier to onboard department by department.9. Risks and Trade-offsRiskTrade-off / MitigationTemporary inconsistencyUse eventual propagation where safe and reconciliation for driftStrong consistency across all systems is impracticalUse adaptive consistency only where neededSchema drift in legacy systemsVersioned registry and adapter updatesDuplicate deliveriesDeterministic idempotency keysAPI failures and timeoutsRetry, backoff, and reconciliation queuesConflict ambiguityHuman review for unresolved casesComplexity of many systemsModular connectors and per-department adapters10. Round 2 Implementation PlanPhase 1: Minimal working pipelineBuild a mock SWS and two mock department systems. Implement SWS ID lookup, schema translation, and one-way propagation.Phase 2: ReliabilityAdd idempotency keys, retry logic, and an append-only audit log.Phase 3: Two-way syncAdd change discovery from department systems using polling and snapshot comparison.Phase 4: Conflict handlingAdd conflict detection, review queue, and configurable resolution policies.Phase 5: Adaptive consistency engineImplement operation-based routing:· strict mode for critical updates· eventual mode for lower-risk updates· review mode for conflictsPhase 6: Simulation and proofAdd failure injection for delay, duplicate delivery, and API downtime. Demonstrate end-to-end traceability and recovery.11. Practical OutcomeA successful prototype should be able to:· take a request from SWS and propagate it to the matching department systems,· accept a change from a department system and reflect it in SWS,· detect and surface conflicts instead of overwriting data,· prove exactly what changed, where it came from, and how it was resolved,· adapt behavior based on the criticality of the request.12. ConclusionThis solution does not attempt to replace or modify existing government systems. Instead, it introduces a governed interoperability layer that coordinates them safely. The key contribution is a deterministic adaptive consistency policy engine combined with schema translation, idempotent delivery, conflict review, and full auditability. That makes the system practical, explainable, and realistic for deployment in a fragmented public-sector environment.

Project Description
PROJECT OVERVIEW: NYAYASETUAI NyayaSetuAI is an AI-powered decision-support system that bridges the gap between complex court judgments and actionable government decisions by transforming unstructured legal PDFs into structured, actionable, and human-verified insights. Challenges: Court judgments are lengthy, unstructured PDFs with complex legal languageCritical directives (actions, deadlines) are not explicitly highlighted Manual interpretation leads to delays and the risk of missing important instructions Lack of a system for tracking and verifying compliance actions Solution (NyayaSetuAI)Uses AI (NLP + RAG) to extract structured information from complex legal PDFs Converts legal text into clear, actionable plans (compliance/appeal decisions) Automatically identifies deadlines, priorities, and responsible departments Implements human-in-the-loop verification (Approve/Edit/Reject) Provides source highlighting and confidence scores for transparency Offers a centralized dashboard for tracking actions and deadlines Enables department-wise filtering and monitoring Ensures explainable and reliable outputs for real-world use Scalable and can be integrated with systems like CCMS HOW THE SYSTEM WORKS Input Processing:The system begins by accepting court judgment documents in PDF format, which may be either digitally generated or scanned copies. Since many legal documents are unstructured and vary in format, NyayaSetuAI is designed to handle both types efficiently. It first identifies whether the PDF contains selectable text or is image-based, ensuring compatibility with a wide range of court documents received through systems like CCMS.For scanned or image-based PDFs, the system applies Optical Character Recognition (OCR) using tools like Tesseract OCR to convert images into machine-readable text. For digitally generated PDFs, text is extracted directly using libraries such as PyMuPDF. The extracted text is then cleaned and structured into smaller chunks, making it ready for further processing by AI models for information extraction and analysis. Intelligent Extraction (AI Layer):The system uses Natural Language Processing (NLP) combined with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to understand and process court judgment text. By leveraging Llama 3, it can analyze complex legal documents with better contextual accuracy, retrieving only the most relevant information.It then extracts key details such as case information (title, court, date), parties involved, important directions/orders, and any deadlines or timelines. This converts unstructured legal text into structured data for further decision-making. Action Plan Generation (Core Innovation):This module transforms extracted legal information into clear and actionable administrative tasks that government officials can easily understand and act upon. Using intelligent reasoning powered by Llama 3, the system determines whether the judgment requires compliance with court directions or consideration for filing an appeal, along with identifying the responsible department, assigning priority levels, and extracting or inferring relevant deadlines.To ensure reliability, NyayaSetuAI adopts a hybrid approach combining AI and rule-based logic. While the AI model interprets complex legal language, predefined rules handle critical patterns such as deadline detection and action classification. This combination improves accuracy, reduces errors, and makes the generated action plans more practical and trustworthy for real-world government use. Human-in-the-Loop Verification (Trust Layer):To ensure reliability and accountability, NyayaSetuAI incorporates a human-in-the-loop verification mechanism before any AI-generated output is finalized. The system presents extracted information and action plans along with highlighted source text from the original PDF and corresponding confidence scores, allowing users to clearly understand how each result was derived.Officials can then approve, edit, or reject the AI-generated outputs through an intuitive interface. Only the verified and validated records are forwarded to the dashboard for decision-making. This approach ensures that the system supports human judgment rather than replacing it, maintaining accuracy, transparency, and trust in real-world government workflows.Dashboard (Decision Support)The system provides a centralized and user-friendly dashboard that displays only human-verified action plans, ensuring that decision-makers work with accurate and trusted information. This eliminates confusion and allows officials to focus directly on validated tasks derived from court judgments.The dashboard offers key features such as department-wise task organization, enabling each department to view its responsibilities clearly, along with deadline tracking to monitor upcoming or overdue actions. It also includes status monitoring (pending, completed, in-progress), providing a real-time overview of compliance and helping improve efficiency, accountability, and timely execution of decisions. Key Features:Handles complex and unstructured legal PDFs (scanned and digital) Provides explainable AI outputs with source traceability from the original document Generates confidence-based results for better decision support Ensures human-verified outputs through an approval workflow (not fully automated) Designed to be scalable and integrable with systems like CCMS Technology StackBackend: FastAPI Frontend: Next.js AI Model: Llama 3 Vector Database: FAISS OCR: Tesseract OCR PDF Processing: PyMuPDF ImpactReduces manual effort and processing time for government officials Ensures no critical directive or deadline is missed Improves speed, accuracy, and consistency of decision-making Promotes transparent, accountable, and efficient governance Final Summary“NyayaSetuAI bridges the gap between legal documents and administrative execution by converting unstructured court judgments into verified, actionable intelligence using AI with human oversight.”

solutions.solution_text:

COMPETE

2 2300031640cseh_576d
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
NYAY – Turning Court Judgments into Clear Action Plans for Government Officers
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 23, 2026 01:39 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
I'm a developer from Guntur, Andhra Pradesh. A few months ago I was watching the news and there was a story about how a government department missed a court deadline — not because anyone was careless, but simply because the judgment was buried in a pile of PDFs that nobody had time to fully read. That story stayed with me. I started digging into how this actually works. When a High Court closes a case, the judgment PDF lands in a government system called CCMS. It could be 10 pages or 70 pages of complex legal language. Somewhere in that document is a deadline, a directive, an instruction — and it's the officer's job to find it, understand it, and act on it. Manually. Every single time. That's the problem I'm trying to solve with NYAY.---What NYAY does, simply put: When a judgment PDF arrives, NYAY reads it automatically — even if it's a scanned photocopy. It pulls out what matters: what the court ordered, who is responsible, what the deadline is, and whether the government should comply or consider an appeal. Then it generates a plain, simple action plan in language any officer can understand without needing a law degree. But here's the part I feel strongly about — the AI never makes the final call. Every single suggestion goes through a verification screen where the officer sees the original PDF on the left, the AI's extraction on the right, and the exact sentence that was used highlighted in yellow. The officer reads it, checks it, and either approves, edits, or rejects it. Only after that does anything appear on the dashboard. I built the system this way because in government, a wrong action is worse than a slow one.---What makes NYAY different: First, NYAY is connected directly to CCMS — it processes every judgment automatically the moment it arrives, not when someone remembers to upload it. Second, it doesn't just summarize — it reasons. Legal documents often say things like "within the period prescribed under the Limitation Act" instead of giving an actual date. NYAY knows what that means and converts it to a real calendar deadline with automatic alerts as that date gets closer. Third — and this is the part I'm most excited about — NYAY tracks patterns across all judgments over time. If the Revenue Department keeps losing in court on the same issue month after month, NYAY surfaces that as an alert on the dashboard. An IAS officer can see at a glance which departments are legally vulnerable and why. That's something no tool gives them today.--- The technical side: I'm building this with a Python FastAPI backend and a Next.js frontend. For PDF reading I'm using PyMuPDF for digital documents and Tesseract OCR for scanned ones. The AI extraction uses Claude's API, which I've found handles long legal documents much better than other options. Everything runs within government infrastructure — no judgment data ever leaves to an external server. There's a full audit log of every decision, every edit, every approval.---What I want this to become: Right now I'm focused on making the core work really well — read the PDF, extract accurately, verify with the officer, show it cleanly on a dashboard. If this gets selected for a pilot, I want to expand the pattern detection into a proper department health scoring system, and eventually integrate with the national eCourts platform so this can work for any state, not just Karnataka. The Indian government is the largest litigant in the country. Every state has this exact problem. I think NYAY can be the standard for how governments manage their legal obligations — starting with Karnataka.

Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 23, 2026 02:37 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
India has over 5 crore pending court cases. Every single day, government departments receive High Court judgments in PDF format, and officials have to manually read through 50 to 100 pages of dense legal text just to figure out what they are supposed to do next. Deadlines get missed, compliance gets ignored, and the entire cycle slows down further. NirnayMarg is our solution to this problem.The name means"Path to Decision" and that is exactly what we are building. A simple web platform where a government official uploads a court judgment PDF and within seconds gets a clean, structured action plan.Here is how it works:The system reads the uploaded judgment, whether it is a typed PDF or a scanned copy, and extracts key details like case number, date of order, parties involved, key directives and timelinesIt then generates a structured action plan breaking down what compliance is required, whether an appeal needs to be considered, which department is responsible and what the deadline isA human reviewer sees the extracted information with confidence scores and source highlights from the original PDF, and can approve, edit or reject each fieldOnly after human verification does the action plan move to the final dashboardWe are not trying to replace legal judgment or automate court decisions. We are solving the last mile problem: turning a judgment into a checklist that a government officer can actually act on.The dashboard gives department officials:Department-wise view of all verified action plansKey actions required with deadlinesFull traceability back to the source documentClean, reliable information for decision makersTech Stack:Frontend: React with a clean reviewer interface and action plan dashboardBackend: Python with FastAPI for all processing and API handlingPDF Parsing: PyMuPDF for digital PDFs, Tesseract OCR for scanned documentsAI Layer: LLM with structured prompting to extract case details, directives, responsible parties and timelines in JSON formatDatabase: PostgreSQL for storing all verified recordsThis is a real problem that the Centre for e-Governance has already identified. We want to build the solution.

Problem UnderstandingGovernment departments often receive court judgment PDFs that contain important directions, deadlines, and administrative implications, but these documents are long, legally dense, and difficult to process quickly. Officials must manually read through them to determine what action is required, whether an appeal should be considered, which department is responsible, and what deadlines apply. This creates delays, increases the risk of missing important directives, and makes the workflow hard to scale.Our solution addresses this by building an AI-assisted judgment understanding system that extracts key details, generates structured action plans, highlights supporting evidence from the source PDF, and requires human verification before any output is shown in a trusted dashboard.Our SolutionWe propose JudgmentAction CCMS, a legal document understanding and decision-support platform that converts court judgment PDFs into verified action plans. The system combines OCR and layout-aware extraction, directive and timeline identification, action-plan generation, source evidence highlighting, and mandatory reviewer approval.The core design principle is that the system does not merely summarize a judgment. solutions.team_name: SyncBridge Karnataka
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


C coders
TEAM SUBMISSION [10]
SyncBridge Karnataka
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 23, 2026 12:55 AM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Problem UnderstandingKarnataka’s Single Window System (SWS) is intended to become the State’s forward-looking business-facing entry point, while multiple department systems continue to function as the authoritative systems for existing business records. Since these systems were built independently, they use different schemas, identifiers, APIs, and update mechanisms. As a result, the same service request raised on one side often does not propagate to the other, causing state drift, duplicate effort, and inconsistent records across systems.Our solution addresses this split-brain problem by building an interoperability layer that works with existing systems as they are, without requiring any modifications to SWS or department platforms.Our SolutionWe propose SyncBridge Karnataka, a UBID-first interoperability layer that enables service requests and record updates to propagate reliably in both directions between the Single Window System and legacy department systems.The platform uses UBID as the canonical join key and converts every incoming update into a common event format. Once normalized, the event is translated into the target system’s schema using department-specific adapters, written using idempotent operations, and tracked through a complete audit trail. The system also handles retry logic, detects conflicts when multiple updates arrive close together, and applies explainable conflict-resolution policies or routes high-risk cases for manual review.How It WorksCapture updates originating from SWS or a department systemConvert each update into a canonical event formatUse UBID to identify the same business across systemsTranslate the canonical event into each target system’s schemaPerform idempotent writes to avoid duplicatesRetry safely through a durable queue when failures occurDetect conflicting updates arriving within a short windowResolve conflicts using configurable policies or escalate for manual reviewRecord every propagation step in an immutable audit logCanonical Event ModelEvery incoming update is transformed into a standard event structure containing:UBIDsource systementity typechanged fieldstimestampversion / hashThis common representation ensures that updates from heterogeneous systems can be processed uniformly before being translated and written to target systems.Two-Way Interoperability DesignFrom SWS to Department SystemsWhen a business raises or updates a service request in SWS, the platform identifies all corresponding department records linked through the same UBID. It then translates the SWS data into each department’s schema and writes the update to all applicable target systems.From Department Systems to SWSWhen a change is initiated in a department system, the platform detects it using the best available integration surface:webhookAPI pollingsnapshot comparison / diffingThe detected update is then converted into the canonical event format, joined using UBID, translated into the SWS schema, and written back into SWS.Conflict Detection and ResolutionA major challenge in two-way interoperability is handling near-simultaneous updates to the same UBID from different systems. SyncBridge Karnataka addresses this through a configurable conflict-resolution engine.Conflict policies include:field-level source prioritylatest verified write wins within a defined policy windowmanual review for high-risk conflicting fieldsThis ensures that the final system state is not silently overwritten and that every resolution remains explainable and reversible.Idempotency, Retries, and ReliabilityTo guarantee robust propagation under failures:outbound writes use idempotency keys to prevent duplicate updatesretries are managed through a durable queuereconciliation logic ensures that failed writes are surfaced and reprocessed safelyall propagation attempts are tracked end-to-endThis design supports at-least-once delivery while preventing unintended duplication and lost updates.Audit TrailEvery propagation creates an immutable audit entry that records:where the change originatedwhat fields changedhow the event was translatedwhere it was writtenwhether a conflict occurredwhat resolution policy was appliedwhether retry or review was requiredFor example, an audit trace may read:Factories → detected signatory change → mapped to canonical schema → conflict with newer SWS value → sent to reviewer / resolved by policyThis makes the system fully traceable and suitable for administrative and operational oversight.Why This Approach Fits the ProblemOur approach is strong for Round 1 because it focuses on the real engineering challenge: heterogeneous live systems that cannot be migrated or modified. Instead of proposing a big-bang replacement, we build a practical interoperability layer that works around differences in APIs, schemas, and event capabilities.It directly respects the non-negotiables:no changes to SWS or department systemsUBID used as the common join keyidempotent and fully auditable propagationcompatible with sandbox, synthetic, or scrambled dataexplainable conflict handling with human review where requiredArchitectureThe proposed architecture includes:Connector layer for SWS and department integrationsCanonical event bus for standardized change propagationSchema adapter registry for department-specific translationsConflict resolution engine for competing updatesDurable queue and retry manager for reliable processingImmutable event store and audit logOperations dashboard for monitoring, review, and traceabilityTechnology StackFastAPI or Spring Boot for backend orchestration servicesKafka or RabbitMQ for event-driven propagation and retriesPostgreSQL as the event store and audit databaseAdapter registry for per-department schema translationReact dashboard for operational monitoring, audit review, and conflict handlingRound 2 Prototype PlanIf shortlisted, we will build a working sandbox prototype with:mock SWS and 2 department APIsone polling-based connectorone webhook-based connectorcanonical event conversion and UBID-based routingidempotent propagation with retry supportconflict detection and resolution demoend-to-end audit viewer for all propagated changesExpected OutcomeThe final system will allow Karnataka’s Single Window System and legacy department systems to stay in sync without requiring system replacement or schema modification. It will ensure that updates propagate reliably in both directions, conflicts are handled transparently, and every action remains fully auditable, explainable, and operationally trustworthy.

solutions.team_name: TenderSetu: A Trust-Anchored AI Platform for Explainable Tender Evaluation in Indian Government Procurement
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


A Arjuna
TEAM SUBMISSION [9]
TenderSetu: A Trust-Anchored AI Platform for Explainable Tender Evaluation in Indian Government Procurement
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 23, 2026 01:04 AM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Documentation ﻿Government procurement in India is the invisible machinery behind every road laid, every uniform stitched, and every piece of equipment that reaches a jawan posted at a forward base. For forces like the CRPF, procurement is not an administrative formality — it is an operational lifeline. Yet the process of evaluating tender bids remains stuck in an era of manila folders, highlighter pens, and committee rooms where officers spend fourteen-hour days cross-checking hundreds of pages against dozens of eligibility clauses. Two officers looking at the same stack of documents frequently arrive at different conclusions. A scanned turnover certificate with a faded stamp can delay an entire procurement cycle by weeks. A bidder who genuinely qualifies can be rejected because their evidence was buried on page 87 of a 200-page submission. The cost of this inefficiency is not just time — it is trust, transparency, and ultimately the readiness of the forces that depend on timely procurement.TenderSetu is our answer to this problem. The name combines "Tender" with the Hindi word "Setu" (bridge) — because what government procurement actually needs is not another black-box AI that spits out verdicts, but a bridge between the precision demanded by legal compliance and the messiness of real-world bidder documentation. Our platform ingests a tender document and any number of bidder submissions, and produces a criterion-by-criterion evaluation report where every single verdict is traceable to the exact clause it tested, the exact document it examined, the exact value it extracted, and the exact reasoning it applied. No silent rejections. No unexplained approvals. No AI hallucinations hiding behind confident language.What makes TenderSetu fundamentally different from a conventional document-processing pipeline is our Evidence Provenance Graph — a structured, queryable knowledge layer that links every extracted fact to its source coordinates (document ID, page number, bounding box, OCR confidence, extraction method) and to the specific eligibility criterion it addresses. This is not metadata bolted on afterwards; it is the backbone of the system. When a procurement officer asks "why was Bidder 7 marked as Needs Manual Review for the turnover criterion?", the system does not generate a post-hoc explanation — it replays the exact chain of evidence that led to that verdict, including the OCR confidence score on the scanned balance sheet, the ambiguity in the fiscal year mentioned, and the specific legal threshold from clause 4.2.1 of the tender. This provenance graph is what makes the system genuinely auditable under CVC guidelines and GFR 2017 compliance norms, not merely "AI-assisted."Our technical architecture combines three complementary intelligence layers. The Criterion Extraction Layer uses a fine-tuned legal-domain language model to parse tender documents and extract eligibility conditions, classifying each into technical, financial, or compliance categories and tagging mandatory versus optional status through dependency parsing of statutory verbs ("shall," "must," "may," "should"). The Multi-Modal Bid Ingestion Layer handles the genuine chaos of Indian bid submissions — typed PDFs, scanned photocopies, phone photographs of physical certificates, tables with merged cells, regional-language stamps, and handwritten annotations. We use a hybrid pipeline combining PaddleOCR for Indic script resilience, LayoutLMv3 for document structure understanding, and a custom table-transformer for financial statement parsing. The Reasoning and Verdict Layer performs criterion-to-evidence matching using a retrieval-augmented approach with explicit confidence calibration, routing low-confidence cases to human reviewers with a structured doubt-articulation template rather than forcing a binary decision.Three features set TenderSetu apart from anything currently in this space. First, our Adversarial Forgery Detection Module uses a vision transformer trained on synthetic forged certificates to flag documents where stamps, signatures, or figures show statistical inconsistencies with genuine government-issued templates — a critical capability given the documented prevalence of forged experience certificates and turnover statements in Indian tenders. Second, our Criterion Drift Detector compares the tender's eligibility language against a corpus of past CRPF tenders and flags clauses that deviate significantly from standard procurement language, helping officers catch both typos and potentially tailored specifications that might indicate favoritism. Third, our Doubt Ledger is a structured, immutable log of every case the AI routed for manual review, creating an institutional memory that both trains future model versions and serves as evidence during post-award audits or CAG reviews.TenderSetu is designed from day one for the realities of government deployment. It runs on-premise within CRPF's existing infrastructure with no data leaving the sandbox, supports air-gapped operation for sensitive procurements, integrates with the existing GeM and CPPP workflows through standardized export formats, and produces evaluation reports in the exact format that procurement committees already sign off on. Every model we use is either open-source or trainable on government hardware — no vendor lock-in, no API dependencies on foreign servers, no mystery costs. The system is not a replacement for procurement officers; it is a force multiplier that turns their fourteen-hour evaluation days into two-hour review sessions, while making every decision more defensible than before. In a country that spent over ₹14 lakh crore on public procurement last year, a platform that makes this process faster, fairer, and fully auditable is not a convenience — it is infrastructure for a more trustworthy state.

A abhinav.24bce10992_5984
TEAM SUBMISSION [3]
SaakshyaNet — AI-Powered Tender Evaluation and Bid Eligibility Intelligence Platform
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 23, 2026 01:40 AM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
SaakshyaNet is an all-in-one AI tool used to automate the process of evidence extraction and eligibility assessment for government tenders, while ensuring that the procurement officer remains fully in charge of the decision-making process.The system processes its work in five steps. The first step involves the ingestion of tender documents and bids in whatever format they are — typed PDFs, scanned versions, Word documents, and even pictures of certificates — via OCR with confidence scoring to process poorly-scanned or handwritten material. The second step is transforming the tender document into a structure that is machine-readable — criterion registry — with financial criteria, technical requirements, compliance requirements, and certification requirements, classified into must-haves and nice-to-haves, and any truly ambiguities in the criterion is identified before the system touches anything pertaining to bidders. The third step consists of going through each bid and extracting its value with respect to each criterion, intelligently harmonizing the huge differences in reporting of the same data points across companies. While deterministic rules are used to make decisions in the clear-cut cases, any other ambiguous cases will be adjudicated using the LLM as a judge, but the system will never disqualify a bidder behind closed doors: the exact reason for any disqualification is given to the officer with full transparency. Fourth, when clearly defined cases are assessed, a deterministic rule engine will evaluate and resolve the case. When there are ambiguous cases, the LLM judge will make a determination based on the weight of the various cases presented to it. However, there will never be a case in which a bidder will be disqualified — if a case has a low confidence level(s), if there are conflicting pieces of evidence, if a document is ambiguous or if the dollar value of the bid is at or near the threshold value(s), SaakshyaNet will provide an explanation to the procurement officer as to why this bidder's bid is not being accepted. Fifth, there will be a consolidated evaluation report issued that will provide a criterion-by-criterion evaluation of all bids and will link each evaluation back to the specific documents, pages, and paragraphs that supported that evaluation.In addition, there will be a complete immutably auditable record of the evaluation for formal governmental procurement accountability purposes.The name reflects the mission: Saakshya means evidence — and SaakshyaNet is the network that captures, verifies and presents evidence so that no decision is made without it.

solutions.team_name: CRPF GTEMS - Government Tender Eligibility & Management System using AI Evaluation Engine
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


A axushgpRVCE
TEAM SUBMISSION [2]
CRPF GTEMS - Government Tender Eligibility & Management System using AI Evaluation Engine
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 23, 2026 11:34 AM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
WE ARE BUILDING: CRPF GTEMS is a full lifecycle management system for government tender evaluation - from the moment a tender is uploaded to the moment the officer signs off on a legally defensible evaluation report. It replaces the entire manual process, not just one step of it. A procurement officer uploads the tender document. The system reads it, extracts every eligibility criterion automatically, classifies each one by type and mandatory status, and builds a structured knowledge base. Bidder documents - in any format, any language - are then ingested, OCR-processed where needed, and matched against every criterion. The output is a criterion-by-criterion evaluation report for each bidder, with verdicts, source citations, and a comparative matrix across all bidders. Ambiguous cases surface to a human review queue. Nothing is silently rejected. Every decision is logged. It is an evaluation engine using neural networks and deep analytical AI Management System. It reads every document, applies every criterion, produces a verdict with a source citation for every single decision, and refuses to silently reject anyone. The system works in four steps:1. Tender Understanding - The system ingests the tender document and extracts every eligibility criterion into a structured knowledge base. Each criterion is classified by type (financial, experience, certification, registration, legal), tagged as mandatory or optional using the exact statutory language from the tender (shall/must = mandatory, should/desirable = optional), and annotated with its threshold value, time window, and applicable exemptions (MSME, JV, startup). It structurally parses with legal classification.2. Document Ingestion - The system accepts every document type a bidder submits: text PDFs, scanned PDFs, photographs of certificates, DOCX files. Scanned documents and images go through an OCR pipeline with Hindi and English support, image preprocessing for contrast and alignment, and a per-page confidence score. Low-confidence pages are flagged for human review - never trusted blindly. Each document is classified into one of 16 or more types (GST certificate, balance sheet, ISO cert, work order, completion certificate, etc.) and its key entities are extracted.3. Criterion Matching - For each bidder, the system compares extracted values against each criterion. Financial thresholds handle multi-year averaging, lakh/crore normalization, and currency conversion at RBI reference rate. Experience criteria use semantic similarity to evaluate "similar work" claims with a configurable threshold. Certification checks validate scope match against tender requirements and expiry date against submission deadline. Conditional logic applies MSME and JV exemptions correctly per the PPP Order 2012.4. Evaluation Report - For each bidder, the system produces a criterion-by-criterion verdict: Pass, Fail, or Manual Review. Every verdict cites the specific criterion, the specific document, the specific page, the extracted value, and the reason for the decision. Ambiguous or low-confidence cases surface to a human review queue - they are never silently rejected. The final output is a comparative matrix across all bidders and a signed-off evaluation report with a complete audit trail.The four non-negotiables: Never silently disqualify: If a scanned document is unreadable, the bidder is not marked as failing that criterion. The document is flagged to the human review queue with the exact reason. Nothing is dropped. Every verdict has a source: Not "ELIGIBLE." The officer sees: criterion text, document name, page number, extracted value, required value, and why it passed or failed. That is what a signed report means legally. Mandatory vs. optional is a legal distinction: Disqualifying a bidder on an optional criterion is grounds for a legal challenge. The system classifies these from the tender's own language, not a guess. The audit trail must survive an RTI: Every automated decision logs model version, input document hash, extracted value, verdict, and confidence. Human overrides record the original automated verdict plus the officer's stated reason. Domain depth that makes the difference. The gap between a demo and a deployable government system is entirely in the edge cases. GTEMS handles: average vs. single-year turnover under Ind AS, CA certificate membership number requirement per ICAI norms, ISO scope vs. tender scope mismatch detection, IAF-accredited certification body validation, JV experience credit at configurable percentage, MSME exemption application per PPP 2012, Udyam portal active-status verification, semantic interpretation of "similar work," related-party experience flagging, and scanned Hindi-language document processing. These are the exact failure modes documented in CAG audit reports on procurement irregularities. The system is designed around GFR 2017 and CVC guidelines, not a generic understanding of what procurement looks like. WHAT CHANGES WHEN OUR PROJECT WORKS:A full tender evaluation - 10 bidders, 12 criteria, 600 pages - completed in under 4 hours instead of 3 to 5 days. Every verdict cited to a specific document, page, and extracted value - no judgment calls buried in a committee meeting. Zero bidders silently disqualified due to evaluator oversight or OCR failure. A signed-off report and audit trail that withstands RTI scrutiny. Deployable across every central government ministry on CPPP and eventually across state governments on GeM.

solutions.team_name: Adaptive Consistency Interoperability Layer for Two-Way Government System Synchronization
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


L LimitlessDuo
TEAM SUBMISSION [2]
Adaptive Consistency Interoperability Layer for Two-Way Government System Synchronization
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 23, 2026 12:23 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Interoperability Layer for Two-Way Synchronization between SWS and Department Systems1. Problem UnderstandingKarnataka’s Single Window System (SWS) is intended to become the canonical front door for business service requests, but 40+ legacy department systems remain live and authoritative for existing businesses. Each system has its own schema, identifiers, APIs, and operational rules. Since neither SWS nor any department system can be modified, the real challenge is not migration but coordination: when the same business updates a record on either side, both sides must converge without silent overwrites, lost updates, or duplicate writes.Our solution is a non-intrusive interoperability layer that sits beside existing systems and synchronizes updates in both directions using SWS ID as the common join key. The system is designed for correctness first, but because the source systems are independent and failure-prone, it must also balance availability, latency, and auditability.2. Core Design PrincipleThe main idea is an adaptive consistency policy engine. This is not an ML model. It is a deterministic rule layer that selects how a request should be handled based on operation type, criticality, source system, and conflict risk.For example:· critical changes such as registered address or legal identifiers use stricter handling,· low-risk changes can use asynchronous propagation,· ambiguous or conflicting updates are routed to human review.This is practical for government systems because correctness is the priority, but global strong consistency across 40+ independent systems is not realistic under partial failures, timeouts, and system downtime.3. Two-Way PropagationSWS to department systems1. A request arrives in SWS.2. The middleware resolves the business through the SWS ID Registry, which maps SWS ID to the corresponding department record IDs.3. The request is translated into each department’s schema through a department-specific adapter.4. Updates are sent asynchronously to target systems.5. Every write is acknowledged, retried if needed, and stored in the audit trail.Department systems to SWS1. A change is detected in a department system.2. The change is mapped back to SWS ID through the registry.3. The request is translated into the SWS representation.4. The update is written to SWS and logged end-to-end.4. Schema Translation and Registry ManagementEvery department system gets a pluggable adapter. The adapter contains field mappings, normalization rules, and transformation logic for that system.To keep the solution maintainable, the mappings live in a Schema Registry:· version-controlled· configurable in JSON or YAML· updatable without changing core code· able to support new departments as they are onboardedLLM usage, narrowly scopedAn LLM is optional and only used to help bootstrap draft mappings from scrambled or synthetic sample payloads during onboarding. A human reviews and approves the generated mapping before it enters the registry. The LLM is not part of the live propagation path and does not make final decisions.5. Change Discovery for Systems Without EventsSome legacy systems will not emit events. For those systems, we use a hybrid change-capture approach:· API polling· snapshot comparison· baseline hashing for recordsEach polled record is compared against the last stored hash for that department record. Only differences emit events into the middleware. This keeps the system compatible with legacy systems while avoiding unnecessary reprocessing.6. Conflict Detection and ResolutionConflicts occur when multiple updates for the same SWS ID arrive within a short time window, or when different systems report incompatible values for the same field.Our conflict handling has three steps: 1. Detect the conflict. 2. Apply a policy based on operation criticality and source priority. 3. Escalate unresolved cases to a review queue instead of silently overwriting data.A losing update is never discarded invisibly. It is stored in a Conflict Queue, along with the competing values, the policy applied, and the reason the update was not auto-committed.Explainability and reversibilityEvery conflict outcome is traceable. The audit log stores:· source systems involved· timestamps· fields that disagreed· the policy applied· the final decision· the fallback path, if anyIf a decision must be reversed, the event log allows replay and correction.7. Reliability GuaranteesIdempotencyEvery update uses a deterministic idempotency key derived from the logical change, such as: SHA256(SWS ID + field + source_system + new_value)This ensures that duplicate deliveries do not create duplicate writes.At-least-once deliveryUpdates are retried with exponential backoff. If retries still fail, they are placed in a dead-letter queue or reconciliation queue.Audit trailThe system uses an append-only event store. Each event records:· origin· transformation steps· destination· retries· acknowledgements· conflicts· resolution decisionsThis gives a full end-to-end history of every propagation.8. Architecture OverviewThe proposed architecture has the following components:· API Gateway for ingestion from SWS or department connectors· SWS ID Registry for business lookup and mapping· Schema Registry for versioned department mappings· Event Bus for asynchronous propagation· Transformation Layer for schema translation· Policy Engine for adaptive consistency decisions· Sync Engine for retries and delivery· Conflict Resolver for collision handling· Audit Store for immutable traceability· Change Capture Layer for polling and snapshot-based discoveryWhy this architectureIt isolates the unstable parts of the ecosystem behind small adapters, keeps the core logic deterministic, and makes the system easier to onboard department by department.9. Risks and Trade-offsRiskTrade-off / MitigationTemporary inconsistencyUse eventual propagation where safe and reconciliation for driftStrong consistency across all systems is impracticalUse adaptive consistency only where neededSchema drift in legacy systemsVersioned registry and adapter updatesDuplicate deliveriesDeterministic idempotency keysAPI failures and timeoutsRetry, backoff, and reconciliation queuesConflict ambiguityHuman review for unresolved casesComplexity of many systemsModular connectors and per-department adapters10. Round 2 Implementation PlanPhase 1: Minimal working pipelineBuild a mock SWS and two mock department systems. Implement SWS ID lookup, schema translation, and one-way propagation.Phase 2: ReliabilityAdd idempotency keys, retry logic, and an append-only audit log.Phase 3: Two-way syncAdd change discovery from department systems using polling and snapshot comparison.Phase 4: Conflict handlingAdd conflict detection, review queue, and configurable resolution policies.Phase 5: Adaptive consistency engineImplement operation-based routing:· strict mode for critical updates· eventual mode for lower-risk updates· review mode for conflictsPhase 6: Simulation and proofAdd failure injection for delay, duplicate delivery, and API downtime. Demonstrate end-to-end traceability and recovery.11. Practical OutcomeA successful prototype should be able to:· take a request from SWS and propagate it to the matching department systems,· accept a change from a department system and reflect it in SWS,· detect and surface conflicts instead of overwriting data,· prove exactly what changed, where it came from, and how it was resolved,· adapt behavior based on the criticality of the request.12. ConclusionThis solution does not attempt to replace or modify existing government systems. Instead, it introduces a governed interoperability layer that coordinates them safely. The key contribution is a deterministic adaptive consistency policy engine combined with schema translation, idempotent delivery, conflict review, and full auditability. That makes the system practical, explainable, and realistic for deployment in a fragmented public-sector environment.

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


A adarsh8578kumar_6952
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
NyayaSetu AI
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 23, 2026 01:08 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
PROJECT OVERVIEW: NYAYASETUAI NyayaSetuAI is an AI-powered decision-support system that bridges the gap between complex court judgments and actionable government decisions by transforming unstructured legal PDFs into structured, actionable, and human-verified insights. Challenges: Court judgments are lengthy, unstructured PDFs with complex legal languageCritical directives (actions, deadlines) are not explicitly highlighted Manual interpretation leads to delays and the risk of missing important instructions Lack of a system for tracking and verifying compliance actions Solution (NyayaSetuAI)Uses AI (NLP + RAG) to extract structured information from complex legal PDFs Converts legal text into clear, actionable plans (compliance/appeal decisions) Automatically identifies deadlines, priorities, and responsible departments Implements human-in-the-loop verification (Approve/Edit/Reject) Provides source highlighting and confidence scores for transparency Offers a centralized dashboard for tracking actions and deadlines Enables department-wise filtering and monitoring Ensures explainable and reliable outputs for real-world use Scalable and can be integrated with systems like CCMS HOW THE SYSTEM WORKS Input Processing:The system begins by accepting court judgment documents in PDF format, which may be either digitally generated or scanned copies. Since many legal documents are unstructured and vary in format, NyayaSetuAI is designed to handle both types efficiently. It first identifies whether the PDF contains selectable text or is image-based, ensuring compatibility with a wide range of court documents received through systems like CCMS.For scanned or image-based PDFs, the system applies Optical Character Recognition (OCR) using tools like Tesseract OCR to convert images into machine-readable text. For digitally generated PDFs, text is extracted directly using libraries such as PyMuPDF. The extracted text is then cleaned and structured into smaller chunks, making it ready for further processing by AI models for information extraction and analysis. Intelligent Extraction (AI Layer):The system uses Natural Language Processing (NLP) combined with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to understand and process court judgment text. By leveraging Llama 3, it can analyze complex legal documents with better contextual accuracy, retrieving only the most relevant information.It then extracts key details such as case information (title, court, date), parties involved, important directions/orders, and any deadlines or timelines. This converts unstructured legal text into structured data for further decision-making. Action Plan Generation (Core Innovation):This module transforms extracted legal information into clear and actionable administrative tasks that government officials can easily understand and act upon. Using intelligent reasoning powered by Llama 3, the system determines whether the judgment requires compliance with court directions or consideration for filing an appeal, along with identifying the responsible department, assigning priority levels, and extracting or inferring relevant deadlines.To ensure reliability, NyayaSetuAI adopts a hybrid approach combining AI and rule-based logic. While the AI model interprets complex legal language, predefined rules handle critical patterns such as deadline detection and action classification. This combination improves accuracy, reduces errors, and makes the generated action plans more practical and trustworthy for real-world government use. Human-in-the-Loop Verification (Trust Layer):To ensure reliability and accountability, NyayaSetuAI incorporates a human-in-the-loop verification mechanism before any AI-generated output is finalized. The system presents extracted information and action plans along with highlighted source text from the original PDF and corresponding confidence scores, allowing users to clearly understand how each result was derived.Officials can then approve, edit, or reject the AI-generated outputs through an intuitive interface. Only the verified and validated records are forwarded to the dashboard for decision-making. This approach ensures that the system supports human judgment rather than replacing it, maintaining accuracy, transparency, and trust in real-world government workflows.Dashboard (Decision Support)The system provides a centralized and user-friendly dashboard that displays only human-verified action plans, ensuring that decision-makers work with accurate and trusted information. This eliminates confusion and allows officials to focus directly on validated tasks derived from court judgments.The dashboard offers key features such as department-wise task organization, enabling each department to view its responsibilities clearly, along with deadline tracking to monitor upcoming or overdue actions. It also includes status monitoring (pending, completed, in-progress), providing a real-time overview of compliance and helping improve efficiency, accountability, and timely execution of decisions. Key Features:Handles complex and unstructured legal PDFs (scanned and digital) Provides explainable AI outputs with source traceability from the original document Generates confidence-based results for better decision support Ensures human-verified outputs through an approval workflow (not fully automated) Designed to be scalable and integrable with systems like CCMS Technology StackBackend: FastAPI Frontend: Next.js AI Model: Llama 3 Vector Database: FAISS OCR: Tesseract OCR PDF Processing: PyMuPDF ImpactReduces manual effort and processing time for government officials Ensures no critical directive or deadline is missed Improves speed, accuracy, and consistency of decision-making Promotes transparent, accountable, and efficient governance Final Summary“NyayaSetuAI bridges the gap between legal documents and administrative execution by converting unstructured court judgments into verified, actionable intelligence using AI with human oversight.”

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


2 2300031640cseh_576d
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
NYAY – Turning Court Judgments into Clear Action Plans for Government Officers
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 23, 2026 01:39 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
I'm a developer from Guntur, Andhra Pradesh. A few months ago I was watching the news and there was a story about how a government department missed a court deadline — not because anyone was careless, but simply because the judgment was buried in a pile of PDFs that nobody had time to fully read. That story stayed with me. I started digging into how this actually works. When a High Court closes a case, the judgment PDF lands in a government system called CCMS. It could be 10 pages or 70 pages of complex legal language. Somewhere in that document is a deadline, a directive, an instruction — and it's the officer's job to find it, understand it, and act on it. Manually. Every single time. That's the problem I'm trying to solve with NYAY.---What NYAY does, simply put: When a judgment PDF arrives, NYAY reads it automatically — even if it's a scanned photocopy. It pulls out what matters: what the court ordered, who is responsible, what the deadline is, and whether the government should comply or consider an appeal. Then it generates a plain, simple action plan in language any officer can understand without needing a law degree. But here's the part I feel strongly about — the AI never makes the final call. Every single suggestion goes through a verification screen where the officer sees the original PDF on the left, the AI's extraction on the right, and the exact sentence that was used highlighted in yellow. The officer reads it, checks it, and either approves, edits, or rejects it. Only after that does anything appear on the dashboard. I built the system this way because in government, a wrong action is worse than a slow one.---What makes NYAY different: First, NYAY is connected directly to CCMS — it processes every judgment automatically the moment it arrives, not when someone remembers to upload it. Second, it doesn't just summarize — it reasons. Legal documents often say things like "within the period prescribed under the Limitation Act" instead of giving an actual date. NYAY knows what that means and converts it to a real calendar deadline with automatic alerts as that date gets closer. Third — and this is the part I'm most excited about — NYAY tracks patterns across all judgments over time. If the Revenue Department keeps losing in court on the same issue month after month, NYAY surfaces that as an alert on the dashboard. An IAS officer can see at a glance which departments are legally vulnerable and why. That's something no tool gives them today.--- The technical side: I'm building this with a Python FastAPI backend and a Next.js frontend. For PDF reading I'm using PyMuPDF for digital documents and Tesseract OCR for scanned ones. The AI extraction uses Claude's API, which I've found handles long legal documents much better than other options. Everything runs within government infrastructure — no judgment data ever leaves to an external server. There's a full audit log of every decision, every edit, every approval.---What I want this to become: Right now I'm focused on making the core work really well — read the PDF, extract accurately, verify with the officer, show it cleanly on a dashboard. If this gets selected for a pilot, I want to expand the pattern detection into a proper department health scoring system, and eventually integrate with the national eCourts platform so this can work for any state, not just Karnataka. The Indian government is the largest litigant in the country. Every state has this exact problem. I think NYAY can be the standard for how governments manage their legal obligations — starting with Karnataka.

NirnayMarg: AI-Powered Court Judgment to Action Plan Converter
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 23, 2026 02:37 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
India has over 5 crore pending court cases. Every single day, government departments receive High Court judgments in PDF format, and officials have to manually read through 50 to 100 pages of dense legal text just to figure out what they are supposed to do next. Deadlines get missed, compliance gets ignored, and the entire cycle slows down further. NirnayMarg is our solution to this problem.The name means"Path to Decision" and that is exactly what we are building. A simple web platform where a government official uploads a court judgment PDF and within seconds gets a clean, structured action plan.Here is how it works:The system reads the uploaded judgment, whether it is a typed PDF or a scanned copy, and extracts key details like case number, date of order, parties involved, key directives and timelinesIt then generates a structured action plan breaking down what compliance is required, whether an appeal needs to be considered, which department is responsible and what the deadline isA human reviewer sees the extracted information with confidence scores and source highlights from the original PDF, and can approve, edit or reject each fieldOnly after human verification does the action plan move to the final dashboardWe are not trying to replace legal judgment or automate court decisions. We are solving the last mile problem: turning a judgment into a checklist that a government officer can actually act on.The dashboard gives department officials:Department-wise view of all verified action plansKey actions required with deadlinesFull traceability back to the source documentClean, reliable information for decision makersTech Stack:Frontend: React with a clean reviewer interface and action plan dashboardBackend: Python with FastAPI for all processing and API handlingPDF Parsing: PyMuPDF for digital PDFs, Tesseract OCR for scanned documentsAI Layer: LLM with structured prompting to extract case details, directives, responsible parties and timelines in JSON formatDatabase: PostgreSQL for storing all verified recordsThis is a real problem that the Centre for e-Governance has already identified. We want to build the solution.

J jainangel020_0856
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
NividaAI — AI-Powered Explainable Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis Platform for Government Procurement
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 23, 2026 03:07 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Title NividaAI, AI-Powered Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis Platform for Government Procurement﻿DescriptionGovernment tender evaluation in India is entirely manual today. Officers spend days reading hundreds of pages of vendor documents, two evaluators reading the same file often reach different conclusions, and there is no structured audit trail behind any decision. NividaAI fixes this.NividaAI is a dual-interface web platform built for both government officers and vendors. Officers log in as Administrator to upload tenders and run evaluations. Vendors log in to submit their documents directly. The AI engine running behind the scenes is completely hidden from vendors.Here is what the system does. The officer uploads a tender document. The AI reads it, extracts every eligibility criterion, and generates a structured summary separating mandatory from optional requirements. The officer reviews and confirms this list. Vendors then upload their bid documents in any format, whether typed PDFs, scanned files, Word documents, or phone photographs. The AI reads every document, highlights the key figures and values relevant to each criterion, and evaluates each vendor automatically.Every verdict is one of three outcomes. Pass means the requirement is clearly met. Fail comes with a mandatory warning prompting the officer to manually verify before the decision is treated as final. Needs Review means the system was not confident enough and escalates the case to the officer with a full explanation. No vendor is ever silently disqualified.Every decision cites exactly which criterion was checked, which document was used, what value was found, and why the verdict was given. Fraud patterns such as duplicate submissions and suspiciously identical figures across vendors are flagged automatically. At the end, a signed, exportable PDF report is generated that is ready for filing, archiving, or producing in court.The platform supports Hindi, English, and Kannada, is built on React, FastAPI, Claude API, and PostgreSQL, and is deployable on free-tier cloud infrastructure making it immediately accessible without heavy setup costs.NividaAI turns days of manual document checking into minutes of consistent, explainable, and fully auditable evaluation.﻿﻿ https://1drv.ms/p/c/6675de0ad7bb9725/IQBtp8gJR69TRIgybvhDMd3JATr6WaKOQQPRATGoR8to8ZY?e=RgrIvL ﻿Edit link

solutions.team_name: BidOptic: Layout-Aware Parsing and Rule-Bound Evaluation for Government Procurement
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


T Team Astra
TEAM SUBMISSION [10]
BidOptic: Layout-Aware Parsing and Rule-Bound Evaluation for Government Procurement
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 23, 2026 03:52 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Government tenders operate under tight legal constraints, and a single evaluation error can derail the entire process. Bidders submit a complete mess of file formats—from clean digital PDFs to blurry, scanned financial tables. To handle this safely, BidOptic completely separates the AI data extraction phase from the actual decision-making logic.Instead of letting an AI model "guess" the outcome and risk hallucinated results, we use LayoutLMv3 to understand the physical structure of the documents and Flan-T5 to pull out the exact text. Once those values are extracted, a hard-coded Python rule engine runs the actual Pass/Fail checks. This guarantees the math is perfectly accurate and ensures no bidder is ever silently disqualified.For text-based requirements, we use DeBERTa to check if the bidder's language matches the tender's rules. If a scan is too low-quality or the wording is ambiguous, the system stops and immediately flags it to a human-in-the-loop React dashboard. To make the entire process auditable, BidOptic links every extracted data point to its exact pixel coordinates on the original file. This gives CRPF officers a side-by-side visual reference so they can verify decisions quickly, speeding up the evaluation without risking legal compliance.

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
