solutions.team_name: SAMANVAYA: Zero-Disruption Interoperability OS
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


D dhaneshbhati2007_a6fb
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
SAMANVAYA: Zero-Disruption Interoperability OS
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 23, 2026 11:59 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Project SAMANVAYA: Zero-Disruption Interoperability OS PROBLEM UNDERSTANDING: A business in Karnataka updates its registered address on the Single Window System and walks away thinking it is done. It is not. The Factories system still has the old address. Attempting a "big-bang" migration to force all departments into SWS causes cascading failures, as seen in the GST rollout. SAMANVAYA is a stateless, event-driven middleware layer that synchronizes SWS and 40+ legacy systems bidirectionally—without modifying a single line of code on either side. UBID is the absolute join key. DIRECTION 1 — SWS TO DEPARTMENTS: When a service request is raised in SWS, SAMANVAYA intercepts the payload, queries the UBID Registry to find matching departments, and routes the event to department-specific Adapters. These adapters translate the SWS schema into the target's required format and execute the write via robust PostgreSQL-backed retry queues. Every write is idempotent: duplicate detection via a SHA-256 composite key (UBID + event + target + payload) prevents double-writes even on retries. DIRECTION 2 — DEPARTMENTS TO SWS: Legacy systems do not all emit events. SAMANVAYA uses a three-tier detection strategy: Webhooks for modern systems, Adaptive API Polling for standard systems (throttling checks based on how active the UBID is), and Merkle-Tree Snapshot Diffing for legacy databases (comparing lightweight cryptographic hashes to find changes without downloading the whole database). Detected changes are translated to the SWS schema and written with UBID as the join key. CONFLICT RESOLUTION: When updates to the same UBID arrive from two sources within a configurable window, SAMANVAYA detects the collision and applies deterministic Policy Routing. SWS is authoritative for global profile data (Legal Name), while legacy systems own compliance data (Factory License). For operational fields, the latest timestamp wins. For high-stakes legal fields, conflicting updates are quarantined for human review. No silent overwrites. Ever. AUDIT TRAIL & STATE-SYNC UI: Every propagation generates an immutable, cryptographically chained record in PostgreSQL: source, target, UBID, old value, new value, retry count, and conflict decision. Officers access the "State-Sync Time-Travel UI" to drag a timeline slider, reconstructing the exact business state across all departments at any historical second. Any incorrect merge can be instantly reverted with a single click. ADVANCED INNOVATIONS: * Dynamic Adapter Studio (AI Copilot): Admins upload a legacy database schema (column headers only). A local Llama-3-8B model auto-generates the YAML mapping to SWS. The AI is turned off after approval, ensuring 100% deterministic, hallucination-free runtime sync. Shadow Mode: New adapters run in simulation mode for 7 days, logging what would have changed without writing to production, ensuring zero onboarding risk. Auto-Heal DLQ: Failed writes enter middleware that automatically fixes common formatting errors (e.g., padding PIN codes) before alerting humans. NON-NEGOTIABLES MET: Zero modifications to SWS or legacy systems. UBID is the only join key. Idempotency and auditability guaranteed. Local LLM used only for design-time schema mapping on non-PII column headers. TECH STACK: React, Tailwind CSS, Python (FastAPI), PostgreSQL (Message Queue, Idempotency Cache, and Audit Ledger), and local Llama-3. Optimized for rapid, AI-assisted development ("vibe coding") without sacrificing enterprise durability.﻿ROUND 2 PLAN: PostgreSQL broker and FastAPI routing skeleton. SWS inbound listener, YAML schema translator, and bidirectional propagation engine. Adaptive polling engine, Conflict Resolution logic, and Auto-Heal formatting scripts. State-Sync UI dashboard and end-to-end integration testing on scrambled synthetic data.

solutions.team_name: CourtTrack AI: NyayaLens — From Court Judgments to Verified Government Action Plans
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


H Hackman
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
CourtTrack AI: NyayaLens — From Court Judgments to Verified Government Action Plans
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 12:23 AM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
The Problem:Missed court deadlines can lead to contempt proceedings and serious administrative consequences, yet government departments still rely on manually reading long judgment PDFs to identify required actions. Through systems like CCMS, judgments are already accessible, but these documents are often 20–40 pages long and written in dense legal language where key directives—what action is required, by when, and by which department—are buried deep inside the text. Officers have to interpret legal phrasing and sometimes even calculate deadlines like “within 8 weeks from the date of pronouncement,” which makes the process slow and at times error-prone or inconsistent. As a result, there are missed deadlines, a lack of clear accountability, poor coordination across departments, and increased legal risk. The core issue is not access to judgments but the absence of a structured system that converts them into clear, trackable, and accountable actions. Our Solution — CourtTrack AI: NyayaLens is designed as a layer on top of existing systems that converts court judgment PDFs into structured, verified, and actionable outputs, effectively moving from “judgment received” to “action clearly defined and tracked.” Most solutions today only try to summarize documents, but that still leaves interpretation work for the officer. In contrast, this system creates a complete action workflow. It follows a simple pipeline—Judgment PDF to AI-based extraction to confidence scoring to human verification to an action dashboard—where it identifies required actions, relevant deadlines (explicit or inferred), and the responsible department. Each output comes with a confidence level and is verified by a human officer before use, so that the system remains both fast and reliable in real-world conditions. Key features include a Contempt Risk Indicator that visually highlights urgency based on deadlines, helping officers prioritize actions before they become critical; Evidence-Linked Extraction where every output is connected to the exact sentence in the PDF for quick verification and trust; Decision Summary Cards that convert lengthy judgments into simple, usable action items; Bilingual Support in English and Kannada for practical deployment at the district level; Voice-Based Explanation that allows officers to quickly understand the judgment without going through the entire document; and a Timeline View that clearly shows the flow from judgment to action to deadline. What makes this approach different is that instead of just summarizing documents, it focuses on structured extraction, verification, and tracking, making it usable within real government workflows rather than just as a standalone AI tool. It ensures that every output is verifiable, every decision is traceable, and human review remains central to the system, which is important for actual government adoption. In terms of impact, the system can significantly reduce the time spent per judgment while improving accuracy and accountability, help prevent compliance failures, and improve coordination between departments. It can integrate with systems like CCMS without major changes and scale from state-level deployment to a national-level system through eCourts integration. The long-term vision is to ensure that court directives are not just stored but clearly understood, assigned, tracked, and completed, so that no important court order is missed simply because it was buried inside a PDF.

Title AdhikarAI: Intelligent System for Converting Court Judgments into Verified Government Action Plans
Problem Understanding Court judgments are lengthy and written in dense legal language, making it hard for officials to quickly identify required actions, deadlines, and responsibilities. Manual interpretation leads to delays, missed instructions, and compliance risks.
Proposed Solution AdhikarAI converts court judgments into structured, actionable workflows. It identifies what needs to be done, who is responsible, deadlines, and whether to comply or consider appeal—presented as a clear, ready-to-use action plan.
Key Features • Action Card System – Tasks with action, owner, deadline, and priority • Deadline Radar – Auto-detects deadlines and appeal windows • Decision Support Engine (Unique) – Suggests compliance, delay, or appeal based on context • Workflow Mapping – Coordinates tasks across departments • Legal Traceability – Links every output to exact source text • Priority Dashboard – Highlights urgent cases • Human-in-the-Loop – Review and correction by officials • Adaptive Learning – Improves with feedback

Officials understand and act instantly without reading the full document.

Conclusion AdhikarAI turns complex court judgments into clear, explainable, and actionable workflows for efficient governance.

TenderSahayak: Transparent, Explainable AI for Government Tender Eligibility Assessment
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 12:57 AM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Imagine a procurement officer sitting down with 300 pages of documents — tender clauses, financial statements, scanned certificates, compliance annexures — and being asked to decide, fairly and accurately, which of ten companies qualifies. Now imagine doing that same job in minutes, with every decision fully explained and every step permanently recorded.That is what TenderSahayak was built to do.The Real Problem Government procurement teams work under enormous pressure. They read through dense tender documents and stacks of bidder submissions, cross‑checking eligibility conditions one by one. The process is slow. It is inconsistent — two evaluators reading the same file can reach different conclusions. And when a decision is challenged, documenting exactly why a bidder was accepted or rejected is harder than it should be.This is not a technology failure. It is a volume and complexity problem that technology can now genuinely solve.What TenderSahayak Does TenderSahayak reads a tender document and automatically identifies every eligibility condition — financial thresholds, technical qualifications, compliance requirements, certification checklists. It separates what is mandatory from what is optional, based on the actual language of each clause.It then processes every bidder's submission, regardless of format. Typed PDFs, scanned documents, Word files, even photographs of physical certificates — all are handled. For poor‑quality scans, image correction is applied before text is extracted, so a blurry mobile photograph of a CA certificate does not automatically disqualify a legitimate bidder.For each bidder and each criterion, the system finds the relevant value, identifies exactly which document and which passage it came from, and checks it against the tender requirement. A confidence score is assigned to every extraction. When confidence is high and the answer is clear, a verdict is given. When anything is uncertain — an unclear scan, an ambiguous figure, a missing document — the case is sent to a human officer with a full explanation of what was found and what needs a second look.No bidder is ever silently rejected.What Makes It Different Most document tools extract text. TenderSahayak makes decisions — and explains them.Every verdict references the exact rule that was applied, the document that was used, and the value that was found. If a bidder is marked ineligible, the system shows precisely which criterion they failed and where in their own documents that evidence appears. If a case is uncertain, the officer sees exactly what the system saw and why it could not decide.Beyond basic evaluation, the system also flags data inconsistencies across a bidder's own documents — for instance, a turnover figure in a CA certificate that does not match the balance sheet. These signals are surfaced for human review rather than acted on automatically, keeping officers in control of judgement calls.Every action is recorded in a tamper‑proof audit log. No entry is ever modified or deleted. Human overrides are recorded alongside the original automated decision, not instead of it. The result is an evaluation trail that holds up to scrutiny in official and legal contexts.Who Stays in Control TenderSahayak is designed to support procurement officers, not replace them. Officers approve the extracted criterion list before evaluation begins. They review all flagged cases. They sign off on the final report.The system handles the volume, the consistency and the documentation. The officer handles the judgement and the accountability.Built to Scale The architecture is designed to grow — from a single department running one tender at a time, to multiple departments processing tenders in parallel, to a shared AI evaluation layer across state and national procurement systems. The core logic — extract, match, explain, audit — is the same at every scale.TechnologyClaude 3.5 Sonnet for language understanding and reasoning across formal legal textGoogle Document AI for OCR on scanned and photographed documentsFastAPI for a high‑performance backendPostgreSQL for audit‑grade data storageReact for a clean, officer‑friendly interfaceDeployable on‑premise, so tender and bidder data never leaves a controlled environment.Why It Matters Procurement decisions affect public money, public services and public trust. When those decisions are made inconsistently or without a clear record, the system fails — not just the bidders, but the institutions that depend on fair outcomes.TenderSahayak does not promise to replace human judgement. It promises to give human judgement the information it needs, the consistency it deserves, and the audit trail it requires.Speed, fairness and accountability — built into the process from the start.

TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
NyayaSetu — AI-Powered Court Judgment to Action Plan System
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 01:09 AM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Project nameNyayaSetu — AI-Powered Court Judgment to Action Plan SystemProblem understandingKarnataka's CCMS system receives hundreds of High Court judgment PDFs daily. Each judgment contains critical directives — comply, appeal, timelines, responsible departments — buried in 20–100 pages of complex legal text. Officers manually read every page, causing delays, missed deadlines, and inconsistent compliance. A judgment missed can result in contempt of court. There is no tool today that reads these PDFs and tells an officer exactly what action to take and by when.Proposed solutionNyayaSetu is an AI system that: (1) accepts court judgment PDFs (scanned or digital), (2) extracts case number, order date, directives, parties, and timelines using LLM-based document understanding, (3) generates a structured action plan classifying each directive as Comply / Consider Appeal / Monitor, with inferred deadlines, (4) presents extracted data with source highlights from the original PDF for human verification — officers can approve, edit, or reject each item, (5) only verified records flow into a department-wise action dashboard showing pending actions and upcoming deadlines.Tech stackPython, Streamlit (UI), Google Gemini 1.5 Flash API (free tier — PDF understanding + action plan generation), PyMuPDF (PDF parsing), SQLite (verified records store). Deployed on Streamlit Cloud (free). No cloud costs, no external dependencies.Real-world deployabilityDesigned as an add-on layer to the existing CCMS system — no modification to current systems. Uses only the PDF output already generated by CCMS. Runs on synthetic/redacted documents in sandbox. Every AI decision is explainable, reversible, and auditable. Scalable to all 30 Karnataka districts.ImpactKarnataka High Court disposes 50,000+ cases per year. Each judgment requires action from 1–5 government departments. NyayaSetu reduces review time from hours to minutes, eliminates missed deadlines, and creates a full audit trail for every directive — directly supporting Karnataka's e-governance mission.

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE

T Team Cipher
TEAM SUBMISSION [10]
PRAMAN-AVA (Action and Verification Assistant) : AI System for Court Judgment Understanding and Verified Action Planning
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 01:30 AM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Problem Understanding Government departments receive court judgments through systems such as CCMS in the form of multi-page PDF documents. These documents contain critical legal directives that require timely administrative action. However, they present several challenges: Judgments are unstructured and written in complex legal language Documents may be scanned, inconsistent, or poorly formatted Key directives are embedded within long paragraphs Important timelines and responsibilities are not explicitly structured Manual interpretation is time-consuming, error-prone, and non-scalable As a result, there is a significant gap between document availability and actionable understanding, leading to delays, inefficiencies, and potential non-compliance. The core problem is not only extracting information but converting legal text into structured, explainable, and verifiable action plans. Additionally, in real-world government workflows:Officers must quickly identify whether to comply, appeal, or escalateDeadlines and limitation periods are often missed due to lack of structured visibilityThere is no unified system to track actions after judgment interpretation PRAMAN-AVA addresses not just extraction, but the entire lifecycle from judgment to verified action execution.Proposed Solution Overview We propose PRAMAN-AVA (Action and Verification Assistant), an AI-powered system designed to: Extract structured information from court judgment PDFs Interpret legal directives and generate actionable plans Provide explainable outputs with source traceability Enable human verification with full control Present only verified data in a decision-support dashboard The system is designed as a decision-support layer, not a replacement for human judgment, ensuring reliability and compliance with government workflows. Additionally, the system ensures:No silent decision-makingNo loss of contextual legal meaningFull traceability from input document to final actionSystem Objectives The proposed solution aims to: Achieve high accuracy in extracting structured legal information Generate meaningful and legally relevant action plans Ensure complete explainability and auditability of outputs Integrate human verification as a core component Provide a clean and actionable interface for decision-makers Further objectives include:Minimizing missed directives and deadlinesStandardizing interpretation across departmentsReducing cognitive load on officersSystem Architecture 4.1 High-Level Pipeline Input PDF ? Preprocessing ? OCR ? Layout Analysis ? Information Extraction ? Legal Reasoning ? Action Plan Generation ? Confidence Scoring ? Human Verification ? Dashboard Output4.2 Component Description 4.2.1 Document Processing Layer Handles input PDFs of varying formats: Scanned documents Digitally generated PDFs Image-based documents Key processes: OCR for text extraction Layout analysis for identifying sections, headings, and structure Noise reduction and image preprocessing for better OCR accuracy4.2.2 Information Extraction Engine Extracts key structured elements: Case details (case number, court, parties) Date of judgment Key directions and orders Legal obligations Explicit timelines Additional extraction capabilities include:Identification of limitation periods for appealExtraction of conditional directivesDetection of multiple directives within a single paragraph Techniques used: Named Entity Recognition (NER) Context-aware text parsing Hybrid rule-based and ML-based extraction4.2.3 Legal Reasoning Engine Interprets extracted data to understand: Nature of directives Legal intent Required administrative response This component transforms extracted text into semantic meaning, enabling action generation. Additionally, it:Distinguishes between mandatory orders and observationsIdentifies whether a directive requires immediate action or evaluationHandles implicit instructions not explicitly written4.2.4 Action Plan Generator Generates structured outputs including: Type of action (compliance, appeal consideration, review) Responsible department or authority Timeline (explicit or inferred) Priority level Supporting evidence Extended outputs include:Suggested next steps for officersRisk classification of each directiveDependency mapping between actions This is the core differentiator, converting legal text into decision-ready intelligence.4.2.5 Confidence and Explainability Layer Each extracted and generated output includes: Confidence score Source reference Evidence highlighting Additionally:Low-confidence outputs are automatically flaggedConfidence thresholds determine routing to review This ensures transparency and enables informed human validation.4.2.6 Human Verification Module Provides a mandatory review interface where users can: Approve extracted and generated data Edit incorrect or incomplete outputs Reject unreliable results Additional features:Side-by-side view of source document and extracted dataAbility to override system decisions with justificationReviewer feedback stored for continuous learning All reviewer actions are recorded, ensuring: Full auditability Continuous system improvement4.2.7 Dashboard Layer Displays only verified information: Department-wise action items Important deadlines Case summaries Status tracking Extended capabilities:Deadline alerts and remindersAction lifecycle tracking (pending, verified, assigned, completed)Filtering by priority and risk level The dashboard is designed for clarity, usability, and quick decision-making.Detailed Workflow Step 1: Input Court judgment PDF is uploaded or fetched via API. Step 2: Extraction System processes the document and extracts structured data including case details, orders, and timelines. Step 3: Action Generation AI generates a structured action plan based on extracted information. Step 4: Verification Human reviewer validates outputs using highlighted evidence and confidence scores. Step 5: Dashboard Output Only approved and verified data is presented for decision-making.Explainability and Auditability The system ensures: Every output is linked to source text Evidence is highlighted within the document Confidence scores are provided for all decisions Reviewer actions are logged and traceable Additional guarantees:Immutable logs for every actionFull trace from input PDF to final decisionNo black-box reasoning This guarantees compliance with government standards for transparency and accountability.Handling Real-World Challenges Challenge Approach Scanned and noisy documents OCR with preprocessing and layout analysis Complex legal language Domain-adapted NLP models Missing or implicit timelines Contextual inference mechanisms Ambiguity in directives Human verification loop Inconsistent formats Hybrid rule-based and ML extraction Additional handling:Multi-language documentsOverlapping or nested directivesLarge multi-page judgmentsTechnology Stack AI/ML Transformer-based NLP models (BERT / Legal-BERT) Layout-aware models (LayoutLM) OCR Tesseract / PaddleOCR Backend Python (FastAPI / Django) Frontend React / Next.js Database PostgreSQL / MongoDB Additional components:Vector database for semantic retrieval (RAG)Caching layer for performance optimizationEvaluation Strategy The system will be evaluated based on: Accuracy of information extraction Correctness of generated action plans Effectiveness of human verification workflow Clarity and usability of dashboard Explainability and traceability of outputs Additional metrics:Reduction in manual processing timeReduction in missed deadlinesReviewer intervention rateRisks and Mitigation Risk Mitigation OCR inaccuracies Multi-stage preprocessing and validation Legal ambiguity Human-in-the-loop validation Over-reliance on automation Decision-support design only Data variability Adaptive and hybrid models Additional risks:Misinterpretation of legal context ? mitigated through verificationPerformance on large documents ? optimized processing pipelineScalability and Future Enhancements Integration with CCMS APIs Multi-language support Cross-case analytics Predictive legal insights Continuous learning from reviewer feedback Extended future scope:Integration with departmental workflow systemsAutomated notification and escalation systemsAI-assisted appeal recommendation supportImplementation Plan Phase 1 Basic document processing and extraction pipeline Phase 2 Action plan generation and reasoning engine Phase 3 Human verification interface Phase 4 Dashboard and analytics layer Phase 5 (Extended) Risk detection, timeline intelligence, and cross-case learningKey Differentiators Evidence-linked extraction and decision-making Confidence-based validation framework Legal-aware action planning (not generic summarization) Strong human-in-the-loop design Full auditability and traceability Additional differentiators:Directive-level risk classificationTimeline inference and delay detectionDepartment mapping intelligenceAction lifecycle trackingLegal ambiguity detectionCross-judgment learning systemConclusion PRAMAN-AVA provides a robust and scalable solution to transform unstructured court judgments into structured, explainable, and verified action plans. By combining AI-driven extraction with human validation, the system ensures reliability, transparency, and practical usability in government workflows. This approach enables faster decision-making, reduces manual effort, and minimizes the risk of missing critical legal directives while maintaining full control and accountability. PRAMAN-AVA acts as an AI co-pilot for government officers, supporting them in making accurate, timely, and well-informed decisions.

solutions.team_name: Judge AI  |   From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


S Shaik Rafi
TEAM SUBMISSION [2]
Judge AI | From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 11:08 AM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
JudgeAI — From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Theme 11 | Centre for e-Governance, Karnataka─────────────────────────────────────────────────────PROBLEM UNDERSTANDING ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────Every day, IAS officers across Karnataka receive court judgment PDFs through CCMS (Court Case Monitoring System), integrated with the High Court CIS. Hidden inside each PDF are critical directives: comply with an order, file an appeal within 30 days, take administrative action by a specific date. Miss a deadline and the government loses a case by default. Miss a directive and a department becomes non-compliant.Today this is entirely manual. Officers read 40-100 page judgments, highlight sections by hand, and construct action plans from memory. The process is slow, inconsistent, and carries real legal risk. Two evaluators may reach different conclusions from the same document. No audit trail. No escalation. No guarantee every directive is seen.Core constraint: CCMS and CIS cannot be modified. JudgeAI integrates as a read-only overlay, consuming PDFs via the existing API fetch, writing nothing back to source systems.───────────────────────────────────────────────────── 2. SOLUTION — FOUR-STAGE PIPELINE ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────Ingest → Extract → Verify → SurfaceNo automated decision reaches the government dashboard without explicit human approval. Every field traces to its exact source in the original PDF. Legally defensible by design.STAGE 1 — DOCUMENT INGESTIONWhen a judgment PDF arrives from CCMS, the ingestion layer detects document type:Digital PDFs: text extracted via pdf-parse. If character count exceeds threshold, proceeds directly to AI extraction.Scanned PDFs: pages rasterized to 300 DPI, processed through Tesseract.js OCR before extraction.Hybrid documents: each page processed individually; outputs tagged with source page and extraction method.Pre-processing: page segmentation, language detection (English vs Kannada), and structural parsing of headers and quoted lower-court orders.STAGE 2 — AI EXTRACTION ENGINEJudgeAI uses Claude (claude-sonnet) via Anthropic API with a deliberate four-pass extraction strategy. A single prompt to "extract everything" produces inconsistent results on legal text. Each pass is focused on one information type:Pass 1 — Case Identity: case number, court, bench, parties, date of order, disposal type. Pass 2 — Directives: all court directions classified as compliance / appeal / administrative / informational. Targets imperative language ("The respondent shall...", "It is hereby directed that..."), conditional directives, and statutory references. Pass 3 — Timelines: explicit dates, relative deadlines ("within 30 days"), and inferred statutory periods. Example: "Section 173 CrPC" automatically resolved to 60 days. Pass 4 — Responsibility: each directive mapped to the responsible Karnataka government department using a known taxonomy (Revenue, PWD, Health, Urban Development, Forest, Agriculture, Home, Finance, Law, Water Resources, Labour).Confidence Scoring: Every extracted field receives a score 0-1 based on three signals: (a) Extraction certainty: explicit = high; inferred = medium; extrapolated from statute = low. (b) Model agreement: same passage extracted twice with varied prompts; agreement raises score. (c) Structural validation: case numbers, dates, department names validated against known patterns. Fields above 0.85 shown green, 0.60-0.85 in amber, below 0.60 in red requiring mandatory human input.STAGE 3 — HUMAN VERIFICATION INTERFACEThe reviewer must be able to verify every AI claim against its exact source without switching windows or searching.Side-by-side layout:Left panel: original PDF rendered via pdf.js with the relevant passage highlighted in yellow for whichever field the reviewer is examining.Right panel: extracted data field by field, with confidence indicators and verbatim source text.Synchronized: clicking any field scrolls the PDF to its source and highlights it automatically.Three-state review per field:Approve: locked with reviewer ID and timestamp.Edit: reviewer modifies value inline. Both stored in audit trail.Reject: mandatory comment required. Field does not proceed to dashboard.Mandatory completion gate: the action plan cannot be promoted to the dashboard until every single field is explicitly approved, edited, or rejected. There is no way to skip a field. No AI output reaches decision-makers without human review.STAGE 4 — GOVERNMENT DASHBOARDShows only verified, human-approved action plans. AI-generated content still in review never appears here.Views:Department-wise: all action items sorted by deadline urgency.Deadline calendar: visual calendar of all compliance deadlines and appeal windows.Case pipeline: kanban view — Received, Extracting, Verifying, Verified, Closed.Escalation alerts: cases with deadlines within 7 days surfaced automatically with red flag.───────────────────────────────────────────────────── 3. ARCHITECTURE & TECHNOLOGY ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────Frontend: React + Tailwind CSS — fast build, component reuse, professional UI PDF Viewer: pdf.js (Mozilla) — browser-native, supports annotation overlays OCR: Tesseract.js client-side; Google Cloud Vision for production AI: Claude claude-sonnet (Anthropic API) — superior legal language understanding, reliable structured JSON output Database: Supabase (PostgreSQL) — managed DB, row-level security, REST API, no custom backend needed Hosting: Vercel — zero-config deploy, automatic SSL, edge CDNData schema: cases, extractions (one row per field with confidence + source_text), verifications (human decisions with before/after values), action_plans (approved items only), audit_log (append-only, never modifiable).───────────────────────────────────────────────────── 4. AUDITABILITY & EXPLAINABILITY ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────Every action plan item carries a full evidence chain:Directive text → exact source paragraph and page number in original PDFExtracted by JudgeAI (pass number, confidence score, timestamp)Verified by [Officer name, ID, timestamp, action: approve/edit/reject]Deadline: explicit or inferred (statute reference labeled clearly when inferred)The audit log is append-only. No action can delete an audit entry. Corrections create a superseding record — both preserved.───────────────────────────────────────────────────── 5. EDGE CASES & FAILURE MODES ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────Poor quality scanned PDFs: page-level quality scoring; reviewer notified if OCR confidence is low; fallback to manual transcription before AI extraction proceeds.Implicit deadlines: statute reference resolver maps common legal references to day counts. Unknown references explicitly flagged for manual reviewer entry, never silently omitted.Multi-department judgments: each directive independently assigned. Ambiguous assignments list all candidate departments for reviewer selection.Conflicting directives: temporal ordering preserved; later directives linked to earlier ones; reviewer explicitly confirms which supersedes.Claude API failure: results checkpointed after each pass. On failure, only the failed pass is retried. No work is lost.Reviewer bottleneck: time-in-queue visible to supervisors who can reassign. Imminent deadlines auto-escalated to top of queue.───────────────────────────────────────────────────── 6. RISKS & TRADE-OFFS ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────AI extraction accuracy: multi-pass strategy and confidence scoring surface uncertainty rather than hiding it. Accuracy improves as reviewer corrections inform prompt refinement.OCR on degraded scans: Tesseract.js degrades on low-DPI documents. Mitigated by quality gates and clear reviewer notification. Production uses Google Cloud Vision.Reviewer adoption: interface designed for speed — 3-5 minutes per straightforward judgment. Bulk-approve option for high-confidence field groups.CCMS API integration: production requires NIC/CeG credentials. Round 2 uses mock PDF endpoints simulating CCMS behaviour exactly.───────────────────────────────────────────────────── 7. ROUND 2 IMPLEMENTATION PLAN ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────Days 1-2: React scaffold, Supabase schema, PDF upload, Claude API returning structured JSON from all four passes. Days 3-4: Confidence scoring, OCR pipeline for scanned PDFs, all extraction rows saved to Supabase with source_text. Days 5-6: Side-by-side verification UI with PDF highlighting, approve/edit/reject with audit logging, mandatory completion gate. Day 7: Dashboard — department filter, deadline calendar, case pipeline, escalation alerts. Days 8-9: End-to-end demo with sample Karnataka HC judgments, edge case handling, demo video production.───────────────────────────────────────────────────── 8. SCALABILITY & IMPACT ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────Immediate: time-to-insight drops from hours to minutes. Missed directives eliminated. Legally defensible audit trail for every compliance decision.Scalable by design: new departments added via configuration table — no code changes. Additional document types (PIL, interim orders, contempt notices) supported by targeted prompt engineering. Deployable to any Indian state High Court with appropriate statute database.Broader vision: JudgeAI becomes a Karnataka-wide Judicial Compliance Intelligence platform — answering questions impossible today: which departments comply most reliably, which case types miss the most deadlines, where are bottlenecks between court orders and administrative action.

A anvita.r06_4dba

TenderAI: Intelligent Tender Evaluation & Eligibility Analysis System

Project Description
TenderAI: Intelligent Tender Evaluation & Eligibility Analysis SystemThe problem it solves: Evaluating government or enterprise tenders is a time-consuming, error-prone process. Procurement teams must manually sift through dense RFP documents, cross-check eligibility criteria, score requirements, and assess risk — often under tight deadlines and with high stakes for getting it wrong.What TenderAI does: It automates and augments this entire pipeline using AI, turning what used to take days of expert review into a structured, minutes-long analysis.Core capabilitiesEligibility Analysis — Automatically reads tender eligibility clauses and checks them against an organization's profile (turnover, certifications, past experience, registrations). Surfaces a clear pass/fail with clause-level detail.Requirement Scoring — Builds a weighted scoring matrix across technical, financial, and timeline dimensions. Flags deviation from standard requirements and quantifies exposure.Risk Detection — Identifies penalty clauses, unfavorable payment terms, compliance gaps, and ambiguous language that could create liability post-award.Competitive & Bid Intelligence — Benchmarks the opportunity against historical data and market signals to generate a win-probability estimate and bid positioning guidance.Go / No-Go Recommendation — The culmination of all modules: a confident, explainable bid recommendation with a confidence score and supporting rationale — ready to present to decision-makers.Who benefitsBid managers who need faster first-pass triage across high volumes of tendersFinance & legal teams who want consistent risk flagging without reading every clauseLeadership who need data-backed Go/No-Go decisions instead of gut-feel

solutions.team_name: Kartavya: A Compliance Operations Layer for the Karnataka CCMS
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


S saiesh2401_6f34
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
Kartavya: A Compliance Operations Layer for the Karnataka CCMS
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 12:09 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Kartavya: A Compliance-Operations Layer for the Karnataka CCMSThe ProblemSmt. Lakshmi, Deputy Secretary in Revenue, receives 14 disposed judgment PDFs on Monday. Three contain personal liability deadlines. If she misses one, contempt notices arrive at her Chief Secretary's desk with her name on them. One triggers a ₹1.4 crore direction on day 43. The CCMS pipeline ends at her inbox. Nothing tells her what to do next. The Karnataka High Court issues contempt notices regularly for three reasons: missed appeal windows, missed compliance deadlines, and department confusion over who acts.Why Existing Systems Don't Close This GapCCMS signals arrival but not meaning. Legal AI platforms (Indian Kanoon PRISM, Manupatra, SCC Online) were built for lawyers researching the law. Our user is a government officer whose obligation is different: act correctly and on time, under contempt liability. Kartavya is not a research tool. It converts disposed judgment PDFs into verified, deadline-bound action plans that survive court scrutiny.What It DoesThe system ingests judgments with scanned and text routing, preserving paragraph-level references. It classifies paragraphs as facts, arguments, precedents, reasoning, operative directions, or decree. It extracts verdict class (allowed, dismissed, partly allowed, disposed with directions, remanded), operative directions with exact source spans, and case metadata including parties and judgment date.The action plan is generated by a rules engine that computes limitation periods by statute. The system maps respondents to responsible departments by role designation rather than individual name. This is critical: when officers transfer, pending work follows the role, not the person. Results appear in a split-screen review interface where officers verify each extraction against the highlighted source text. Keyboard shortcuts enable rapid batch review. Only verified plans reach the deadline dashboard, which displays contempt-risk indicators and department-wise breakdowns. The interface is bilingual (English + ಕನ್ನಡ).Three Design CommitmentsConstitutional Foundation. Government action respects Article 14 (equal treatment, auditable reasoning across cases), Article 21 (due process), and the rule against delegation of statutory power. When the law grants power to an officer, she cannot hand it to an algorithm. Kartavya operates as staff assistance—equivalent to junior officer's notes—not the decision itself. High-stakes fields (financial exposure, service matters) require reviewer comments as documented evidence of application of mind.Rules, Not Guessing. A pure Python rules engine calculates limitation periods by citing exact statutes: Karnataka High Court Act 1961, Limitation Act 1963, CPC Order 47, Article 136, Contempt of Courts Act 1971. Every rule is versioned in YAML. Every action plan carries the rule-engine version that produced it. Every extraction logs prompt SHA, model ID, temperature. This makes action plans from April 2026 reproducible against the exact rule-set and model active at generation. We never ask a language model to guess limitation periods.Data Sovereignty. Ollama runs locally on on-premise or State Data Centre infrastructure. Case data never flows to OpenAI, AWS Bedrock, Google, or Azure. Hosted models are accessible only through feature flags, and only for data that has been deterministically scrambled or is synthetic.How It Works: A Real CaseSri V. Venkateshulu vs The Secretary (WP 13296/2022, KHC, April 17, 2026). Petitioner challenged Section 8A mining lease denial. Respondents: Union Ministry of Mines, State Principal Secretary, Department Director. Writ dismissed.A compliance-extraction system would auto-generate action items. Kartavya reads the verdict class: "dismissed as devoid of merit." The rules engine matches this to Article 136 (Supreme Court SLP window). It calculates: 90 days from April 17, 2026 = July 16, 2026. It assigns monitoring responsibility to the Principal Secretary, Commerce & Industries by designation, not by name. It closes the plan with a defensive readiness posture: watch for petitioner appeal, maintain evidence file. This "no action needed" case is non-trivial—systems that generate phantom compliance actions waste officer time and clog the dashboard.Four Core FeaturesPetitioner history sidebar shows prior filings and outcomes at a glance. No competing system flags patterns across petitioner litigation history, letting officers adjust response strategy instantly. Consequence chips on every action plan display what happens if deadlines are missed—reframing the dashboard from a to-do list into a risk register. No other tool quantifies the cost of inaction. Cross-department aggregation combines directives from a single judgment across multiple agencies into a Chief Secretary-level command view. No single-department system surfaces inter-agency dependencies. Near-miss tracker surfaces patterns like "Revenue Dept has 4 near-misses this quarter" before they escalate into contempt notices. Leadership intervention happens at warning stage, not at the contempt notice stage.Technology and FeasibilityFastAPI backend, Ollama (Qwen 2.5 or Llama 3.1 with constrained JSON), pdfplumber plus Surya for OCR on both scanned and hybrid PDFs, PostgreSQL with append-only audit logs, HTMX and Jinja for server-rendered UI screens, React for the split-screen review interface, Redis and RQ for async job processing. All components are open source and on-premises deployable. CCMS integration happens through API only.Market and ScaleEvery Indian state operates a CCMS system with identical compliance problems and officer liability. Karnataka is first to articulate it publicly. Adjacent markets include State PSU litigation management, municipal body compliance, and State Advocate General offices. Harvey AI was valued at $5 billion building solutions for private law firms. No equivalent exists for government respondents in India. If successful in Karnataka, the model scales to all 28 other states.Next StepsOn advancing to the next round, the prototype will demonstrate the system working against three canonical cases. These will test verdict disambiguation (determining whether the government won, lost, or received directions), handling of stay orders, and multi-department scenarios where one judgment directs multiple agencies to act.In Round 2, key questions will be resolved with the Centre for e-Governance. These include understanding the exact API shape for how CCMS communicates judgment events, identifying the pilot department, planning SSO integration through the State Data Centre, and aligning audit logging practices with Karnataka's data retention policies.The vision remains simple: from judgment to action plan, with the officer making the final decision, not past her.

solutions.team_name: NyaySetu: Bridging Court Judgments to Ground-Level Action
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


Y yashwanthprabhu2007_3a4a
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
NyaySetu: Bridging Court Judgments to Ground-Level Action
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 12:12 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
NyayTrack: AI-Powered Court Order Compliance EnginePROBLEMIndia's judiciary delivers over 73 lakh orders and judgments every year across district courts, High Courts, and the Supreme Court. Every order carries full constitutional authority — directing government departments, municipal corporations, police, PSUs, hospitals, and private entities to take specific, time-bound actions. Yet the moment a judgment is uploaded as a PDF, the system goes blind.There is no automated mechanism to read what was ordered, identify who is responsible, set a deadline, send a reminder, verify execution, or escalate non-compliance. A court clerk manually reads the document, dispatches a copy, and the trail goes cold. The result: potholes ordered repaired remain unfilled for years, compensation awarded to victims is never disbursed, FIRs courts directed to be registered are quietly ignored, and environmental violations continue unchecked. Citizens who spent years fighting legal battles win on paper but lose in reality.This is not a failure of the law or the judiciary. It is a failure of data infrastructure and process automation — precisely what AI is built to fix.WHAT IT ISNyayTrack is a full-stack, end-to-end AI platform that transforms court judgment PDFs into structured, assigned, deadline-bound, trackable, and verifiable action plans. It is the missing operating system between what courts order and what governments do.DOCUMENT INGESTION AND INTELLIGENT PARSINGThe pipeline begins the moment a judgment PDF is uploaded — either manually by a court registrar or automatically via the eCourts API or NIC's Case Information System. Native PDFs are parsed while preserving paragraph structure and metadata. Scanned documents are routed through a multi-engine OCR pipeline: Tesseract with OpenCV preprocessing (deskewing, noise removal, binarization) as the primary engine, escalating to AWS Textract for complex layouts, tabular data, and handwritten annotations when confidence scores fall below threshold.SPATIAL DOCUMENT UNDERSTANDING WITH LAYOUTLMCourt judgments are structurally complex — not linear narratives. LayoutLM, a transformer model pre-trained on document images, processes each page as a combination of text tokens and bounding box coordinates. This lets the system distinguish case numbers in headers from precedents in the body, identify operative clauses versus background reasoning, and correctly associate directives with the parties they address even across paragraphs. The model is fine-tuned on Indian court judgments spanning multiple High Courts and the Supreme Court, covering formatting variations specific to Indian judicial documents including cause titles, coram sections, prayer clauses, and order portions.LLM-POWERED DIRECTIVE EXTRACTION AND ENTITY RECOGNITIONOn top of the LayoutLM layer, a fine-tuned LLM pipeline (Claude / GPT-4o) performs deep semantic extraction of all actionable content. For each directive identified, the system extracts: case number and year, court name and level, presiding judge or bench, judgment date, all parties on both sides, the precise directive text, the specific authority directed to act, the deadline, the directive category (one of twelve, including infrastructure repair, financial compensation, criminal action, environmental compliance, and contempt proceedings), the consequence for non-compliance, the proof type required, and any interim relief or stay orders.Every extracted field carries a confidence score between 0 and 1, and every field hyperlinks back to the exact highlighted sentence in the source PDF. This source-linked traceability is a non-negotiable design requirement — no authority can claim the AI misread the order, because any reviewer can click on any field and be taken to the precise location in the original document.ACTION CARD GENERATION AND AUTHORITY ASSIGNMENTEvery identified directive is converted into a structured Action Card — a self-contained unit of accountability. Each card contains: a plain-language summary (readable without a law degree), the responsible authority's full name and designation, an automatically calculated hard deadline, acceptable compliance proof types, a link to the highlighted judgment section, a unique Action Card ID, and current compliance status. Authority mapping uses a maintained directory of government departments and jurisdictional mappings — so if a judgment orders BBMP to repair a road in Rajajinagar, the system automatically identifies the correct division and officer. Action Cards are stored in PostgreSQL with full indexing on authority, court, district, directive type, deadline, and compliance status.REAL-TIME COMPLIANCE DASHBOARDAll active Action Cards populate a real-time web dashboard (React.js + Tailwind CSS) with role-specific views. The court registrar view shows all orders, their compliance status, upcoming deadlines, and overdue cases. The department head view shows all assigned Action Cards, filterable by deadline proximity, directive type, and district. The citizen view lets anyone search by case number and see the live compliance status of orders in their matter — transforming an opaque black box into a transparent public service.Every Action Card displays a RAG status indicator (red for overdue, amber for due within seven days, green for compliant). Aggregate analytics include compliance rate by department, average time from order to compliance, a timeline heatmap of order volume, and a district-level geographic map of active and overdue orders.AUTOMATED REMINDERS AND ESCALATION ENGINEA scheduled job system (Redis + Celery) drives notifications. Reminders go out at seven days before deadline (via Twilio SMS and SendGrid email), again at three days, and on the day of the deadline simultaneously to the responsible authority and the court registrar. If the deadline passes without a compliance submission, the Action Card is marked overdue and an escalation workflow triggers — moving up the authority hierarchy until the appropriate level is reached. Every reminder, escalation, and status change is logged with a precise timestamp in an immutable audit trail presentable to the court as evidence.AI-POWERED COMPLIANCE VERIFICATIONWhen a responsible authority submits compliance proof — photographs of completed work, payment receipts, FIR confirmations, affidavits, official correspondence — a vision AI model (GPT-4 Vision / Gemini Vision) analyzes the submission against the directive before the Action Card status is updated. For photographic evidence, it checks whether the image reflects the required action, whether the location matches, and whether the image metadata postdates the judgment. For documents, it extracts and cross-validates key fields against the Action Card values.If verification passes above the confidence threshold, the Action Card is automatically marked compliant and the court registrar is notified. If confidence is below threshold or evidence appears inconsistent, the submission is flagged for human review — not automatically accepted or rejected. This hybrid AI-plus-human model ensures both speed and accountability.INTEGRATION WITH ECOURTS INFRASTRUCTURENyayTrack is designed to deploy on top of India's existing eCourts infrastructure without requiring changes to current court workflows. It connects to the eCourts API to pull newly uploaded judgments automatically, reads from the Case Information System to pre-populate case metadata, and is hosted on NIC Cloud or AWS GovCloud in compliance with judicial data residency requirements. Courts onboard without disrupting any existing process — the system operates invisibly in the background.INNOVATIONNyayTrack combines three capabilities never brought together in Indian legal technology:Legal-domain document intelligence — LayoutLM's spatial understanding correctly parses the structural complexity of Indian court judgments across all formatting variations, not just extracting text.Source-linked extraction with confidence scoring — every extracted field is traceable to its exact location in the source document, making the AI's output auditable and legally defensible.Closed-loop compliance lifecycle — the system owns the entire chain from document ingestion through authority notification, deadline tracking, escalation management, and evidence-based compliance verification. No existing government system or legal tech platform in India provides this complete chain.TECH STACKOCR: Tesseract + OpenCV + AWS Textract Document AI: LayoutLM (fine-tuned on Indian court documents) NLP: spaCy (legal entity recognition) LLMs: Claude API / GPT-4o (directive extraction), GPT-4 Vision / Gemini Vision (compliance verification) Backend: Python FastAPI Frontend: React.js + Tailwind CSS Database: PostgreSQL Job queue: Redis + Celery Notifications: Twilio (SMS), SendGrid (email) Deployment: Docker + AWS EC2 / NIC CloudIMPACTNyayTrack reduces the time to convert a court judgment into a structured, assigned, deadline-bound action plan from an average of 15 days (current manual process) to under 2 minutes. It provides 100% real-time compliance visibility across all active court orders — for the first time in Indian judicial history. It frees registrars and clerks from manually reading complex judgments. It prevents contempt-of-court situations by proactively reminding authorities before deadlines are missed. And it gives citizens transparent, real-time access to the status of orders in their favour.At national scale, NyayTrack has the potential to dramatically improve the enforcement rate of court orders across India — strengthening the rule of law, reducing contempt petitions that burden an already overloaded judiciary, and restoring citizen faith in the ability of the legal system to deliver not just justice on paper, but justice in reality.

solutions.team_name: LegalDirect AI: Automated Compliance Mapping & Verification for CCMS
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


D DIGI BITS
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
LegalDirect AI: Automated Compliance Mapping & Verification for CCMS
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 12:38 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
PROJECT TITLE: LEGALDIRECT AI – VERIFIED COMPLIANCE & ACTION FRAMEWORK1. PROBLEM UNDERSTANDINGThe Karnataka Court Case Monitoring System (CCMS) successfully aggregates High Court judgments, but these remain "dead data" trapped in complex, unstructured PDFs. Currently, legal officers must manually parse hundreds of pages to identify actionable mandates. This leads to:Compliance Delays: Missing critical deadlines for court-ordered actions.Missed Appeals: Failing to challenge adverse orders within the limitation period.Administrative Bottlenecks: Difficulty in routing tasks to the correct departments.LegalDirect AI transforms these PDFs into a structured, human-verified pipeline of executive actions.﻿2. CORE SOLUTION: EXTRACTION & INTELLIGENT ACTION PLANNINGOur solution utilizes a multi-stage NLP pipeline (OCR + LLM) to process both digital and scanned judgments:Data Extraction: Automatically captures Case Number, Order Date, Involved Parties, and specific "Operative Paragraphs."The Action Plan Engine (Key Differentiator): Beyond mere extraction, the AI reasons through the text to:Categorize the mandate (e.g., Compliance Required vs. Grounds for Appeal).Determine the Limitation Period (calculating the deadline for filing an appeal based on the order date).Route to the Responsible Authority by identifying department names mentioned in the judgment.﻿3. THE MANDATORY "HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP" VERIFICATIONWe recognize that AI cannot replace legal judgment. Our system acts as Decision Support:Verification Interface: Every piece of extracted data is presented side-by-side with the original PDF.Source Highlighting: When a user clicks an extracted "Direction," the system automatically scrolls and highlights the specific paragraph in the PDF that served as the source.Approval Workflow: A Legal Officer must review and Approve, Edit, or Reject the AI-generated plan. No data reaches the dashboard without a "Verified" timestamp from a human user.﻿4. EXECUTIVE DASHBOARD & GOVERNANCEOnly "Verified" action plans are promoted to the final dashboard, ensuring 100% data trust.Department-Wise Views: Segregates actions by department for targeted monitoring.Urgency Sorting: Prioritizes cases based on nearing limitation periods or compliance deadlines.Audit Trail: Every action plan shows which officer verified the AI's output, creating a clear chain of accountability.﻿5. TECHNICAL FEASIBILITY & CONSTRAINTSPrivacy First: No PII is sent to hosted LLMs. The system is designed to run on-premise or via secured private endpoints.OCR for Scanned Documents: Utilizes advanced OCR to handle low-quality scanned judgments common in legacy High Court records.Explainability: The link between the PDF source and the Action Plan is maintained at the database level, ensuring every decision is auditable.﻿6. WHY THIS WINSLegalDirect AI is not just a summarizer; it is a compliance engine. It reduces the manual reading load of legal officers by 70% while increasing the speed of administrative response, ensuring the Government of Karnataka remains proactive in its legal obligations.

solutions.team_name: Lex-Gov AI|Judicial Interpretation and Verified Action Pipeline
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


B beledevere
TEAM SUBMISSION [4]
Lex-Gov AI|Judicial Interpretation and Verified Action Pipeline
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 12:53 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Executive SummaryKarnataka government departments receive court judgment PDFs through CCMS. Each contains directives requiring follow-up: compliance, appeal consideration, or departmental assignment. Officials identify these manually, leading to lapsed appeal windows, missed deadlines, and misdirected directives.Lex-Gov AI extracts a structured action plan using a two-pass pipeline on Sarvam AI models, routes directives to responsible departments, requires nodal officer verification before anything reaches a dashboard, and tracks statutory deadlines with alerts. All processing runs on-premise at the State Data Centre.﻿1. Problem Statement1.1 Where the Current Workflow BreaksA High Court judgment is typically 40 to 100 pages. The operative order occupies the last 5 to 10 pages; the rest is case history and precedent. Officials read the full document to locate directions, decide comply-or-appeal, assign responsibility, and calculate deadlines without structured tooling.Contempt of Court Risk. Missed compliance deadlines put department heads at risk of contempt proceedings.Missed Appeal Windows. Adverse orders can be appealed within 30, 60, or 90 days. Slow processing closes this window without a filing decision.Unassigned Responsibility. A judgment may direct multiple departments. Without extraction, directives go unacted because the wrong department received the PDF.1.2 Why a Generic LLM Falls ShortFirst, many judgments include scanned pages, Kannada text, and court stamps. Standard text extraction fails; Indic-aware OCR is required.Second, directives are often conditional: "If the petitioner files documents within 15 days, the respondent shall process within 30 days." This requires context across the document, not just the operative section.Third, 100-page context windows introduce noise from procedural content, increasing hallucination risk.﻿2. Technical Architecture2.1 Pipeline Overview+-----------------------------+| CCMS API: Judgment PDF |+-------------+---------------+ | v+-----------------------------+ +---------------------+| Pass 1: Sarvam Vision (3B) |<------| Re-scan Feedback || Structural Mapping & OCR | | (Model or Officer |+-------------+---------------+ | Correction Note) | | +----------+----------+ v |+-----------------------------+ || Page Index: e.g. [1-5, | || 45-50] Operative Located | |+-------------+---------------+ | | | v |+-----------------------------+ || PDF Slicer: Relevant Pages | |+-------------+---------------+ | | | v |+-----------------------------+ || Pass 2: Sarvam-105B | || Extraction and Reasoning | |+-------------+---------------+ | | | v |+-----------------------------+ || is_complete_info_present? |---- No ----------++-------------+---------------+ | Yes v+-----------------------------+| Structured JSON Action || Plan (Directives & Dates) |+-------------+---------------+ | v+-----------------------------+| Click-to-Cite Verification || UI (Nodal Officer Review) |+-----+----------+------------+ | | Approve Redo (Officer writes correction note) | | v +----> Pass 1 reruns with note appended+-----------------------------+| Department Dashboard || (Verified Records Only) |+-----------------------------+2.2 Pass 1: Structural Mapping (Sarvam Vision 3B)Pass 1 locates structure, not meaning. Sarvam Vision (3B) identifies: the Preamble (case reference, parties, date), the Operative Order (final directions), and Breadcrumbs (back-references in the operative order). Output is a Page Index of ranges needed for Pass 2, typically pages 1-5 and 47-53. Handles Indic OCR across all 22 scheduled languages, scanned pages, and court stamps.2.3 PDF SlicingA temporary PDF is built from the Page Index. A 90-page judgment typically produces a 10-15 page slice, removing procedural noise from the Pass 2 context window.2.4 Pass 2: Extraction and Reasoning (Sarvam-105B)FieldDescriptioncase_idCCMS case referencedate_of_orderDate of the HC orderpartiesPetitioner and respondentdirectives[]Directions issued by the courtdirective.action_typeCOMPLY or CONSIDER_APPEALdirective.responsible_deptRevenue, Home, Law, etc.directive.deadline_explicitDeadline from judgment textdirective.deadline_inferredCalculated via limitation rulesdirective.source_pageSource page for click-to-citedirective.source_paragraphSource paragraph for click-to-citeis_complete_info_presentBoolean; false triggers re-scanconfidence_scorePer-directive confidence (0 to 1)Sarvam-105B is trained on Indian legal language across all 22 official languages, handles conditional directives, and has a 128,000-token context window.2.5 Self-Correction LoopIf is_complete_info_present is false, the document returns to Pass 1 with failure context appended, triggering a wider page scan. Runs until resolved or a max iteration count, then flagged for manual review. Incomplete outputs are never published.﻿3. Human Verification3.1 Click-to-Cite InterfaceEvery action plan requires nodal officer sign-off before reaching the dashboard. Split-screen UI: left shows directives, departments, deadlines, and confidence scores; right shows the original PDF. Clicking any directive jumps the PDF to the source page and highlights the source paragraph.3.2 Officer ActionsActionOutcomeApproveDirective verified; published to department dashboardEditOfficer amends directive or deadline; logged with ID and timestampRedoOfficer writes correction note; pipeline reruns with note in Pass 1 promptOn Redo, the note (e.g. "paragraph 34 direction missed" or "should be Revenue") is added as a human-seeded constraint before Pass 1 reruns. Output returns for re-verification.3.3 Audit LogEach action recorded immutably: officer identity, timestamp, original AI output, edits. Full chain from source PDF to published directive.﻿4. Department DashboardVerified plans only, by department. Sorted by deadline. Urgency: Green (30+ days), Amber (10-30), Red (under 10). Directives tagged COMPLIANCE REQUIRED or CONSIDER APPEAL. Status: Unprocessed, Pending Verification, Verified, Actioned.﻿5. Limitation Tracker30-day (specific categories), 60-day (most HC orders), 90-day (constitutional matters). Stored in directive.deadline_inferred.TriggerRecipientAlertNo verified plan within 7 daysNodal OfficerReminder15 days to appeal deadline, unverifiedLaw DepartmentEscalation10 days to compliance deadline, unactionedDepartment HeadUrgentCompliance deadline passed, unactionedLaw Dept + Dept HeadContempt flag﻿6. Data Sovereignty and SecurityDeployment: On-premise at Karnataka State Data Centre. Judgment data never leaves State infrastructure.Models: Sarvam Vision and Sarvam-105B are Indian foundational models. No foreign API used.Access: Role-based by department; authenticated credentials; all sessions logged.﻿7. Implementation PlanPhase 1: Prototype (28 April to 4 May 2025)DeliverableDescriptionPDF IngestionFile upload for prototype; CCMS API in Phase 2Two-Pass PipelineSarvam Vision + Sarvam-105B end-to-endSelf-Correction Loopis_complete_info_present with re-scan logicClick-to-Cite UISplit-screen with PDF jump-to-paragraphJSON OutputFull schema with source references per directiveDemo DashboardDepartment-filtered verified viewLimitation Calculator30/60/90-day computation from order date﻿﻿8. ScalabilityAny Indian High Court with a CIS-integrated system can run this pipeline without changes to core logic. NJDG integration enables centralised compliance tracking nationally. The model — domain-specific AI, on-premise deployment, mandatory sign-off, complete audit log — extends to any workflow converting unstructured documents into structured action.﻿9. Architecture ComparisonDimensionGeneric LLMLex-Gov AIPDF HandlingFails on scanned/Indic documentsSarvam Vision; handles scanned, stamped, mixed-scriptExtractionFull-doc summarisationTwo-pass + slicing; relevant pages onlyLegal LanguageGeneral English modelsSarvam-105B; 22 languages + Indian legal textFailure ModeIncomplete outputs published silentlySelf-correction; escalates to manual reviewHuman OversightOptionalMandatory per-directive verificationDataForeign-hosted APIsOn-premise SDC; no external APIAccountabilityNo recordImmutable log: officer, timestamp, output, edits

TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
JudgeAI — From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 01:11 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
LINK -  PROJECT  (https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/176FodMvuMggfCtYd2k7spzbFTS-suvE8yHtCXt5ReEY/edit?usp=sharing) (Click the project)
We created JudgeAI to solve a critical and costly problem for Karnataka’s government departments: the manual, inconsistent, and error-prone process of reading and acting on lengthy court judgment PDFs. This manual process leads to inconsistent interpretations, missed deadlines for appeals, and zero accountability or audit trail. 1  (https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/176FodMvuMggfCtYd2k7spzbFTS-suvE8yHtCXt5ReEY/edit)﻿﻿Our solution transforms unstructured court judgment PDFs into structured, verified, and actionable compliance plans automatically. The platform’s core functions are: 1  (https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/176FodMvuMggfCtYd2k7spzbFTS-suvE8yHtCXt5ReEY/edit)﻿AI Extraction Engine: Uses Legal NLP, fine-tuned on the Indian legal corpus, to identify case details, key directives, and compute explicit and inferred deadlines. 1  (https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/176FodMvuMggfCtYd2k7spzbFTS-suvE8yHtCXt5ReEY/edit)﻿AI Action Plan Generator: Generates a suggested path (Comply vs. Appeal) and assigns accountability to the correct department. 1  (https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/176FodMvuMggfCtYd2k7spzbFTS-suvE8yHtCXt5ReEY/edit)﻿Mandatory Human Verification Loop: This is our non-negotiable safety gate. Every extracted piece of data, including deadlines and responsible departments, is shown to an officer alongside the highlighted source section in the PDF and a confidence score. No AI output ever proceeds without mandatory officer sign-off. 1  (https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/176FodMvuMggfCtYd2k7spzbFTS-suvE8yHtCXt5ReEY/edit)﻿Trusted Audit Trail: All decisions and verifications are recorded in an immutable audit log, satisfying e-governance requirements for full accountability. 1  (https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/176FodMvuMggfCtYd2k7spzbFTS-suvE8yHtCXt5ReEY/edit)﻿The entire technology stack is designed to prioritize security and trust, focusing on on-premise deployment and using open-source tools where possible to ensure that no raw Personal Identifiable Information (PII) ever leaves the secured environment. We are confident JudgeAI can make judicial compliance faster, fairer, and fully accountable.

NyayaFlow — AI-Powered Court Judgment to Verified Action Plan System
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 01:27 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
The Problem Government departments receive disposed High Court judgment PDFs through CCMS daily. These documents are: Lengthy — often 20–100+ pages of dense legal text Unstructured — no standard format across judgments Time-sensitive — compliance deadlines buried inside Manually reviewed — officials spend hours per document Missing a directive = Contempt of Court Missing an appeal window = Permanent loss of legal recourse
? Our Solution NyayaFlow is a 4-stage AI pipeline that converts raw judgment PDFs into verified, actionable decisions. Stage 1 — Intelligent Extraction Reads both digital and scanned PDFs using OCR + LLM to extract: Case number, court, bench, date of order Parties involved Key directions and operative clauses Timelines (explicit and inferred) Relevant Acts and Sections cited Stage 2 — AI Action Plan Generation Automatically generates a structured plan: Compliance required? Yes / No / Partial Appeal recommended? Yes / No with reasoning Limitation period calculated from order date Responsible department identified Priority level: High / Medium / Low Step-by-step action items with deadlines Stage 3 — Human Verification (Mandatory Gate) Original PDF shown alongside extracted fields Yellow highlights show exact source passage per field Confidence score shown per field Reviewer can Approve, Edit, or Reject each field Full audit trail with reviewer ID and timestamp Nothing reaches dashboard without human approval Stage 4 — Trusted Dashboard Department-wise filtered view Color-coded urgency indicators Calendar view of all upcoming deadlines Search by case number, department, date Export to PDF and Excel Role-based access for reviewers and decision-makers

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


L lakshmisree1573_abc3
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
AI-Powered Tender Intelligence System for Explainable Eligibility Evaluation
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 01:38 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Problem Understanding Government tender evaluation is not just a document processing task—it is a decision-making problem under uncertainty. Eligibility criteria are often expressed in long, legally structured sentences, spread across multiple sections, and sometimes partially defined or implicit (e.g., “similar work”). At the same time, bidder submissions are highly inconsistent in format (scanned PDFs, tables, certificates) and rarely align structurally with the tender requirements.
Proposed Solution
We propose an AI-assisted Tender Intelligence System that converts unstructured tender and bidder documents into structured, explainable decision outputs.
The system operates in four stages:
Tender Understanding

Extract eligibility criteria from tender documents using a hybrid approach (OCR + rule-based filtering + LLM reasoning)

Convert natural language conditions into structured, machine-interpretable rules

Distinguish between financial, experience, and compliance criteria

Identify incomplete or ambiguous criteria

Bidder Document Understanding

Parse heterogeneous bidder submissions (PDFs, scans, images)

Extract relevant facts such as turnover, certifications, and experience

Normalize variations in representation (e.g., “similar works” vs “completed projects”)

Evaluation Engine

Match extracted bidder data against structured criteria using deterministic logic

Support derived conditions (e.g., percentage-based thresholds)

Perform criterion-level evaluation rather than binary decisions

Explainability & Human-in-the-Loop

Every decision is backed by:

the criterion being checked

extracted value

source document reference

Confidence scores are assigned to each extraction and decision

Ambiguous or low-confidence cases are flagged for manual review

Key Innovations
Ambiguity-Aware Decision Making Instead of forcing binary outputs, the system explicitly detects uncertainty (e.g., unclear OCR, missing values, vague criteria) and routes such cases for human validation.

Dynamic Rule Construction Converts complex natural language conditions (e.g., “30% of estimated cost”) into executable logic, enabling accurate and consistent evaluation.

Semantic Alignment Layer Bridges the mismatch between tender language and bidder documents by mapping conceptually similar terms (e.g., “similar works” ↔ “project experience”).

Evidence-Linked Outputs Every verdict is traceable to specific document sections, ensuring auditability and trust.

Feasibility
The system is built using
OCR for scanned documents and PDF parsing for structured text (pdfplumber) and LLMs for semantic extraction and classification

Rule-based evaluation engine for deterministic decisions

A working prototype can be implemented using publicly available tender documents and simulated bidder submissions.

Expected Impact
Prevents incorrect or non-transparent disqualification

Provides audit-ready, explainable decision reports

This solution augments human evaluators with structured intelligence, ensuring that decisions remain transparent, consistent, and trustworthy in government procurement workflows.
