LEADERBOARD


A Ankit Tiwari
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
Nirikshan AI
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 03:12 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Government tender evaluation is a high-stakes, audit-sensitive process where accuracy and transparency are critical. Officers manually verify bidder submissions against complex eligibility criteria spread across lengthy tender documents. This process is slow, inconsistent, and difficult to audit, especially because bidder documents come in heterogeneous formats such as PDFs, scanned images, tables, and photographs. The evaluation of public tenders is a high-profile process requiring significant accuracy and transparency, and high levels of audit compliance. A Manual Evaluation Process (AEP) exists where agency representatives validate every bidder's submission against an extensive set of eligibility criteria described in lengthy public invitation for bid (PIFB) documents. Bidder submission reviews are time-consuming, inconsistent, difficult to audit, and complex because bidders submit their documents in many different formats (e.g., pdf files, scanned images, tables, photographs).There are also significant implications when errors are made during the evaluation process. The approval of an ineligible bidder may result in an agency audit of the PIFB creation process, and the rejection of an eligible bidder could lead to significant legal disputes. The new Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis System would serve as a decision support assistant to the procurement committee via explanation, audit trail, and human oversight of decision-making processes; it will not completely automate decision making for the procurement committees.Proposed Solution:We are proposing a multi-agent architecture that mimics the behavior of a "real" procurement committee, ensuring that the tender evaluation process will be performed in a manner that is structured, reliable and transparent.Extracting Evaluation Criteria (Agent A) -Agent A will extract, parse and convert the information included in the PIFB documents into structured evaluation rules. -While extracting the evaluation criteria from the PIFB documents, Agent A will categorize each criterion as belonging to one of the following types: -Technical CriteriaFinancial CriteriaCompliance CriteriaMandatory Conditions vs Optional Conditions -Agent A will output a structured schema (e.g. JSON) so that the procurement committee can evaluate each tender in a consistent manner2.Formatting Bidder Submissions (agent B) Rather than using simple OCR processes, we utilize layout models designed with knowledge of the entire document structure to extract valuable information from real-world documents, such as:Typing PDFs (or any typed format) Scanned certificates Tables, charts and images Understanding how information is laid out or organized in a document, including their layout and visual elements (stamps, seals, etc.) Providing data to Agents C and D along with confidence scores. Less than 1 minute.The Parser focuses on fact extraction only; it will not make any decisions.3.Evaluating Engine (agent C) The evaluating engine uses the extracted bidder data to check against structured criteria.It will deal with many issues:Format variations (e.g., ₹5 Cr and 50,000,000) Missing or partial data Ambiguous language It outputs the extracted data based on three criteria (eligible, not eligible, and manual review) to all agents. The evaluator does not determine qualifications for all bidders as all uncertain or ambiguous situations will be flagged.4.Reviewing/Validating by Auditor/Agent Inloop (agent D) The auditor reviews and validates Agent C’s decision regarding eligibility.A flag will be placed in front of the auditor by the evaluater when there is low confidence or the reasoning for disqualifying a bidder is deficient in any manner.The auditor/viewing officer will have access to the Reviewing Dashboard to:See the extracted values and associated criteria for each case flagged for review See the highlighted sections of the documents as evidence used to disqualify biddersExplanability/Auditability: All decisions are traceable to the following:The criteria you have evaluatedThe source document you have extracted fromThe value you have extractedWhy you evaluated the outcome as you didThis creates a complete chain of custody (audit trail), which complies with the government's requirements and allows for easy verification during an audit.Architecture & Technology: Multi-Agent System: A) Criteria Extraction—Agent A B) Document Parsing (Layout Aware AI)—Agent B C) Evaluation—Agent C D) Auditing/HITL—Agent DTechnology Stack: Layout LMV3/Vision Language Models for Document Understanding LLMs for Reasoning/Rule Extraction FastAPI/Flask Back-End (Core Processing) React-Based DashboardWhy This Approach? Decreased Hallucinations and Errors Modularity and Scalability Real-World Workflow AlignedRisk/Mitigating Factors: Challenges: Poor Quality Scans Mitigation: Confidence Scoring + HITL Challenges: Ambiguous Language Mitigation: Conservative Evaluation + Manual Review Challenges: Format Variability Mitigation: Layout Aware ModelsImplementation Strategy for Round 2: Build Agent A to Extract Criteria Implement Layout Aware Models for Document Parsing with Agent B Build Agent C for Framework Based Evaluation Add Auditing/HITL Routing to Agent D Audit/HITL Reports—Exportable and with Explanations Creating Simple Dashboard for Uploading/Reviewing/Generating and Reporting.
Conclusion: This system transforms tender evaluation into a structured, transparent, and AI-assisted workflow. By combining advanced document understanding, modular architecture, and human oversight, it ensures accurate, explainable, and auditable decision-making, making it highly suitable for real-world government procurement.I have mapped out the detail implementation in the document:  https://docs.google.com/document/d/14zUE3savy0ceTNoRi5EVgTKZTGk5VBh9/edit?usp=sharing&ouid=113065783741275967164&rtpof=true&sd=true

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


O On Probation
TEAM SUBMISSION [3]
Judgment-to-Action Intelligence System A Verified and Explainable Decision Architecture for Government Compliance
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 03:15 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
JUDGMENT-TO-ACTION INTELLIGENCE SYSTEM (JAIS)
From Court Judgments to Verified Administrative Action Plans

PROBLEM STATEMENT

Government platforms such as the Court Case Monitoring System provide access to court judgments in PDF format. However, these judgments are unstructured, lengthy, and written in complex legal language.
Officials must manually interpret these documents to determine required actions, leading to:
High manual effort in extracting key directives

Difficulty identifying deadlines and compliance requirements

Uncertainty in deciding whether to appeal or comply

Risk of missing critical instructions

Delays in decision-making and execution

Core Problem: The issue is not access to judgments, but the absence of a system that converts them into structured, actionable decisions.

PROPOSED SOLUTION

A system that transforms unstructured court judgments into structured, verified, and explainable action plans.
Key Capabilities:
Understands semantic structure of legal documents

Extracts critical information using specialized components

Interprets judgment intent

Generates structured administrative action plans

Ensures human verification before usage

Displays only trusted data in a decision-ready dashboard

Focus: Decision support, explainability, and accountability (not just automation)

SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE

Flow:
Court Judgment PDF → Ingestion and Normalization Layer → Semantic Understanding Layer → Action Plan Generation Layer → Human Verification Layer → Trusted Output Dashboard

ARCHITECTURE DETAILS

4.1 Ingestion and Normalization Layer
Purpose: Convert raw PDFs into structured, machine-readable content
Functionality:
Handles scanned and digital PDFs

Performs text extraction and recognition

Identifies layout (headings, sections, paragraphs)

Preserves document structure

Design Rationale: Maintaining structure improves accuracy in legal interpretation
4.2 Semantic Understanding Layer
Purpose: Interpret document meaning beyond plain text
Components:
a) Intelligent Segmentation
Splits document into logical sections (background, arguments, orders)

b) Multi-Component Extraction Parallel extraction of:
Case details

Parties involved

Key directives

Timelines and deadlines

c) Legal Intent Classification Classifies judgment into:
Mandatory compliance

Appeal consideration

Advisory observation

Financial/administrative impact

4.3 Action Plan Generation Layer
Purpose: Convert interpreted data into structured decisions
Generated Output Includes:
Action type (compliance / appeal / review)

Urgency level

Deadline (explicit or inferred)

Responsible authority

Risk level

Step-by-step actions

Working Mechanism:
Deterministic Logic: Extracts explicit instructions (orders, deadlines)

Reasoning Component: Interprets implicit requirements

Mapping Component: Assigns responsibility based on administrative structure

Decision Justification:
Each action includes:
Source reference (exact text)

Explanation of reasoning

Confidence score

4.4 Human Verification Layer
Purpose: Ensure all outputs are validated before use
Features:
Displays extracted data with original document

Highlights source text

Shows confidence scores

Allows approve/edit/reject

Additional Capabilities:
Flags low-confidence outputs

Highlights critical directives

Maintains audit trail

Feedback Loop: Human corrections improve system over time
4.5 Trusted Output Dashboard
Purpose: Provide clean, verified, actionable insights
Key Principle: Only human-verified data is shown
Features:
Department-wise pending actions

Prioritized case list (urgency + deadlines)

Clear action + responsibility mapping

Lifecycle tracking of cases

DIFFERENTIATION FROM TYPICAL SYSTEMS

Typical Approach:
Single-stage extraction

Unstructured outputs

Limited explainability

Optional human review

Static processing

Proposed Approach:
Multi-layer semantic architecture

Specialized extraction components

Structured action plans (not summaries)

Built-in justification

Mandatory human verification

Continuous learning via feedback

CONCLUSION

This system bridges the gap between legal data availability and actionable governance.
By combining:
Structured understanding

Decision generation

Human verification

It ensures court judgments are converted into reliable, timely administrative actions.
The architecture is modular, explainable, and aligned with real-world workflows, making it suitable for practical deployment.

solutions.team_name: NyayaSetu
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


T Team Byte_Me
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
NyayaSetu
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 03:18 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
NyayaSetu A Court Judgment Compliance System

CCMS delivers the judgment PDF. It does not tell the officer what to do or when. NyayaSetu does.

NyayaSetu is the operational layer CCMS was always missing the step between "judgment received" and "officer knows what to do, by when, and at what personal risk." 1. Problem Context CCMS fetches judgment PDFs from the High Court’s CIS via API. Once a case is disposed, the PDF lands in CCMS but nothing tells the law officer what to do next, by when, or who is responsible. India has over 55.8 million pending cases across all court levels as of March 2026, and the government itself is the single largest litigant responsible for roughly 50% of the entire legal burden. Each disposed case generates a judgment PDF that departments must act on. The compliance problem begins the moment that PDF lands in CCMS with no downstream workflow. 1.1 Pain Points from the perspective of a Government Law Officer
Statutory limitation periods are strict. Delays in internal approvals, officer transfers and inadequate case monitoring can jeopardize timely filing and may lead to dismissal of appeals unless delay is condoned by the court. - Critical
Where judicial orders are addressed to the State or multiple public authorities, internal responsibility mapping may be required to identify the implementing department. - High
No standard process exists for summarising lengthy orders, logging decisions, or routing for legal review across large litigation portfolios. - High
Wilful disobedience of judicial directions may expose the concerned authority or responsible officials to contempt proceedings under applicable law. - Critical

Example: Supreme court judgement against Chhattisgarh State Minor Forest Product Federation

The Supreme Court has recently tightened this further

Government officers arrayed in contempt proceedings cannot treat Court orders as optional. Accountability for non-compliance extends beyond named parties to all those in the chain of implementation who have knowledge of the order. Supreme Court of India on Israr Ahmad Khan v. Amarnath Prasad & Ors., May 2025

1.2 Positioning NyayaSetu is a personal liability shield for law officers, not a PDF summarizer. Every officer who receives a CCMS judgment notification has personal exposure if they fail to act correctly and on time. 1.3 Deployment Model The audit trail and timestamped approvals eliminate ambiguity that the current system provides as cover. NyayaSetu requires a top-down mandate from a Principal Secretary or Law Department head it is a procurement decision, not a self-service tool. 2. System Architecture and Modes 2.1 Modes
Reactive Mode: New judgment arrives -> Extract -> Verify ->officer approves → dashboard updated → officer records action taken → case closed.
Proactive Mode: Nightly job surfaces cases approaching deadline with no recorded action, departments with overdue compliances, and repeat petitioners with escalation history. No new AI required, pure aggregation over the verified database.

2. System Architecture ┌─────────────────────────────────┐ │ INGESTION LAYER │ │ PDF upload → PyMuPDF (digital PDFs, char offset) │ │ → Sarvam Vision API (scanned + Kannada/mixed-script PDFs) │ │ Pre-processing: Unicode normalization, ligature fix │ │ Output: text + character offsets + Sarvam output │ │ CCMS metadata consumed as extraction context │ └───────────────────────┬─────────┘ ┌───────────────────────▼────────┐ │ INTELLIGENCE LAYER │ │ Claude API — Chains 1 & 2 (extraction + comply/appeal reasoning) │
│Sarvam LLM: Chain 3 (department attribution, Kannada NER)│ │ Prompt caching on static document content │ │ It reduces cost by up to 90% after chain 1 , chains 2 and 3, pay 10% of input token price│ │ Chain 1: Extraction with CCMS priors │ │ Chain 2: comply/appeal chain-of-thought reasoning │ │ Chain 3: department attribution (NER + classify) │ │ Two-pass validation: structurally different prompts │ │ OCR confidence scores cap extraction confidence │ │ Section-aware chunking for long documents │ └───────────────────────┬────────┘ ┌───────────────────────▼────────┐ │ APPLICATION LAYER │ │ FastAPI backend │ │ React + PDF.js│ │ PostgreSQL - case lifecycle, audit trail, corrections │ │ Celery + Redis - notification jobs, nightly proactive │ │ CCMS SSO / NIC identity integration │ └────────────────────────────────┘ 3. Feature Set3.1 Source-Grounded PDF Extraction with Inline Highlights Every extracted field is anchored to its exact location in the source PDF. Tiered precision: character-level for digital PDFs, region-level for OCR with a "position approximate" indicator using PyMuPDF and Sarvam Vision.

Eg: deadline: { type: "infer", base_date: "2024-03-14", expression: "within six weeks", calc: "2024-04-25", conf: "medium" }, src_span: { pg: 12, start: 4521, end: 4687 }

3.2 Deadline Intelligence Engine: Two separate clocks: compliance deadline and appeal window. Converts relative expressions to absolute calendar dates. Appeal window auto-calculated from court type. 3.3 Comply vs Appeal Structured Reasoning: LLMs prompted through a fixed chain-of-thought: identify the holding,extract ratio decidendi vs obiter dicta, establish petitioner/respondent posture, surface appeal decision factors. Output is a structured table per direction 3.4 Responsible Department Attribution: Named entity extraction from parties and directions sections, followed by subject matter classification if no department is named. 3.5 Confidence Scoring: High (two structurally different passes agree), Medium (inferred or relative), Low (conflicting signals). Low-confidence fields surface the specific reason. 3.6 Human Verification Flow: States: EXTRACTED → VERIFIED → DISPUTED. No record reaches the dashboard until VERIFIED. Field-level approve/edit/reject. Every action timestamped and attributed. 3.7 Compliance Action Tracker: State machine: JUDGMENT RECEIVED → UNDER REVIEW → VERIFIED → ACTION TAKEN → CLOSED. Officer records action type, date, and reference number. Contempt Risk Register only flags VERIFIED cases with passed deadlines not ACTION TAKEN cases. Without this closure loop, the register is permanently red and officers stop trusting it. 3.8 Contempt Risk Register + Dashboard: Weekly action view by department and urgency. Contempt Risk Register as a dedicated separate view, never buried in routine traffic. 3.9 Escalation and Notification Engine: Notifications escalate upward: new judgment → reviewer. 48 hours unreviewed → department head. <30 days → officer + head. <14 days → Law Department. Overdue → Law Secretary. NIC mail + SMS integration. 3.10 Department Risk Profile: Win/loss ratio, most common directions, repeat petitioners, average time from judgment to action. 4. CCMS Integration NyayaSetu sits downstream of CCMS. No CCMS modification required. CCMS metadata: case type, department as petitioner or respondent, disposal date consumed as extraction priors: case type informs limitation period selection, government role pre-seeds the comply/appeal reasoning branch, disposal date is the base for all deadline calculations. Authentication via CCMS SSO through NIC identity infrastructure. NyayaSetu appears as a tab within CCMS. Data residency: Production deployment uses an on-premise or MeitY-approved cloud model.


5. Scale and Business Model Each High Court jurisdiction is a state government procurement. The model is SaaS licensed per jurisdiction an annual fee per High Court / state Law Department, priced on disposed cases processed per year. The path to national scale runs through MeitY/NIC. If NyayaSetu is adopted as a shared service under the Digital India programme, it becomes a central deployment serving all state governments rather than 28 separate procurements. eCourts Phase III, approved at ₹7,210 crore, explicitly mandates AI, OCR, and NLP in court workflows. NyayaSetu is the compliance intelligence layer that infrastructure enables but does not yet deliver.

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


P pallavikairamkonda24_014f
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
NyayScan — AI Procurement Eligibility Auditor
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 03:25 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
A government procurement officer sits down on a Monday morning with a 60-page tender document on one side and a stack of 10 bidder submissions on the other, each 30 pages thick. Somewhere buried in those pages are financial statements, experience certificates, GST registrations, ISO certifications — some typed neatly, some photocopied badly, some photographed with a phone camera. Her job is to check every bidder against 15 to 20 eligibility criteria, consistently, without missing anything, and finish before the committee meeting on Thursday. She has done this before. So has her colleague. Last month they evaluated the same tender independently and came to different conclusions on two bidders. Nobody is wrong — the documents are just genuinely hard to read, and the criteria are written in legal language that leaves room for interpretation. One missed certification and the entire procurement gets challenged in court. One ineligible vendor who slips through and public money is wasted. The pressure is real, the process is manual, and there is no record of why any decision was made. That is the problem we are solving with NyayScan. We built the system in four straightforward steps. When a tender PDF is uploaded, NyayScan reads it and pulls out every eligibility condition — minimum turnover, years of experience, required certifications, compliance documents — and organises them into a clear checklist, separating the must-haves from the nice-to-haves. When bidder documents come in, the system handles whatever format they arrive in. Clean PDFs get read directly. Scanned pages and photos go through OCR first so the text becomes readable. Every piece of information extracted is pinned back to the exact document and page it came from, with a confidence score attached. Then comes the matching. For straightforward numeric checks — like whether turnover exceeds 5 crore — we use simple rule-based logic because that is faster, cleaner, and easier to explain in a dispute. For criteria written in varying legal language, we use an LLM to understand what the document is actually saying. Every bidder gets a clear verdict on each criterion: Eligible, Not Eligible, or flagged for Manual Review. Nobody is silently rejected. If the system is not sure, it says so and hands it to a human officer. That officer sees exactly what the system found, where it found it, and why it made the call it did. They can approve it, correct it, or override it — and every one of those actions is recorded permanently. When the evaluation is done, the entire decision trail can be exported and used as the official audit record. The risks we thought hardest about were the small things that break real-world systems — a bidder who writes their turnover in lakhs when the tender asks for crores, a scanned certificate that is too blurry to read reliably, criteria worded differently in different tenders, two officers disagreeing on a borderline case. NyayScan handles each of these with a specific mechanism, not a workaround. For Round 2 we will bring a fully working prototype to the sandbox — upload a tender, upload bidder documents, get a complete evaluation report with audit trail, ready for an officer to sign off on.GitHub Repo:  https://github.com/Pallavikairamkonda/nyayscan  (https://github.com/Pallavikairamkonda/nyayscan)

solutions.team_name: BidInsight AI is an intelligent system that automates tender evaluation for government procurement.
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


K kaviyarasu kasi
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
BidInsight AI is an intelligent system that automates tender evaluation for government procurement.
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 03:54 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
BidInsight AI
GITHUB


1. Problem Understanding
Government procurement processes, particularly in organizations such as CRPF, involve evaluating multiple bidder submissions against detailed tender documents. These documents define eligibility through financial thresholds, technical qualifications, compliance requirements, and mandatory certifications.In practice, this evaluation is performed manually by committees who must cross-check each bidder’s documents against the stated criteria. This introduces several challenges:
Significant time consumption due to large volumes of document

Inconsistent interpretation of criteria across evaluators

Difficulty in verifying compliance across heterogeneous document formats

Limited traceability and auditability of decisions

Bidder submissions further increase complexity as they include a mix of typed PDFs, scanned documents, images, and tabular data, often presenting the same information in different formats.A critical requirement is that every decision must be explainable, auditable, and suitable for formal approval processes.

2. Proposed Idea

BidInsight AI is an evidence-driven tender evaluation system designed to automate the interpretation of tender requirements and the evaluation of bidder submissions while maintaining transparency and auditability.Unlike traditional rule-based systems that directly compare values, this approach focuses on identifying and validating the evidence used to make each decision.For every criterion, the system determines:
What the requirement is

What evidence was found in bidder documents

Whether the evidence satisfies the requirement

The confidence level of the evaluation

The reason behind the final decision

This ensures that every output is traceable and defensible.
3. Detailed Approach

3.1 Tender Understanding

The system begins by processing the tender document to extract eligibility criteria.The document is first segmented into logical sections such as financial requirements, technical specifications, and compliance conditions. Using a combination of natural language processing and rule-based parsing, each eligibility condition is identified and structured.Each criterion is represented in a standardized format containing:
Category (financial, technical, compliance)

Condition type (e.g., minimum, mandatory)

Expected value or requirement

Whether the condition is mandatory or optional

This structured representation enables consistent comparison across bidders.

3.2 Bidder Document Processing

Bidder submissions are processed through a unified pipeline capable of handling multiple formats, including scanned documents and images.The system performs:
Optical Character Recognition (OCR) for scanned content

Layout-aware parsing to identify sections and tables

Entity extraction to identify relevant fields such as turnover, certifications, and project experience

Each extracted piece of information is stored along with its source, including the document name, page number, and surrounding context. This ensures traceability.

3.3 Evidence Mapping

Once both criteria and bidder data are structured, the system maps each criterion to relevant evidence extracted from bidder documents.This mapping is performed using a hybrid approach:
Semantic matching to handle variations in terminology

Rule-based validation for numerical and logical conditions

For example, a criterion requiring minimum turnover will be matched with financial statements or certificates containing revenue information, even if terminology differs.

3.4 Evaluation and Decision Logic

For each criterion, the system evaluates whether the bidder meets the requirement.Instead of a binary decision, the system produces three possible outcomes:
Eligible

Not Eligible

Needs Manual Review

The third category is critical for handling ambiguity, such as low-quality scanned documents or incomplete data.Each evaluation is supported by a confidence score derived from extraction accuracy, document quality, and matching reliability.

3.5 Explainability and Audit

A key feature of the system is its ability to generate explainable results.For every criterion, the output includes:

The criterion description

The document used for evaluation

The extracted value

The reason for the decision

In addition, the system maintains a complete audit trail, recording all intermediate steps, including extraction results, matching logic, and final decisions. This ensures the system can be used in formal procurement workflows.
4. System Architecture

The system is composed of the following layers:

Document Processing Layer: Handles text extraction and OCR

AI Extraction Layer: Identifies criteria and extracts bidder data

Evidence Mapping Engine: Links criteria with relevant document evidence

Evaluation Engine: Applies validation logic and determines outcomes

Explainability Layer: Generates human-readable explanations

User Interface: Enables review and manual intervention

This modular design ensures scalability and maintainability.
5. Handling Real-World Challenges

The system is designed to address practical challenges in procurement workflows:
Unstructured Documents: Managed through OCR and layout-aware parsing

Variability in Terminology: Handled using semantic matching techniques

Incomplete or Ambiguous Data: Flagged for manual review instead of rejection

Requirement for Transparency: Ensured through detailed explanations and audit logs


6. Expected Impact

The proposed system delivers measurable improvements:
Significant reduction in evaluation time

Consistent and unbiased decision-making

Improved transparency and accountability

Enhanced scalability for large-scale procurement


7. Feasibility

The solution is feasible using existing technologies, including OCR tools, large language models for text understanding, and rule-based systems for validation. The architecture is modular, allowing incremental development and deployment.
8. Future Scope

Future enhancements may include:
Multilingual document processing

Integration with government procurement platforms

Advanced anomaly and fraud detection

Vendor performance analytics


9. Conclusion

BidInsight AI presents a structured and practical approach to modernizing tender evaluation. By combining AI-driven document understanding with evidence-based reasoning and explainable decision-making, the system ensures efficiency, reliability, and transparency in government procurement processes.

C ClearBid
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
AI-Based Tender Evaluation & Eligibility Analysis
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 04:21 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Our proposed Intelligent Tender Evaluation System addresses the high-stakes, manual bottleneck of government procurement by shifting the Tender Evaluation Committee from weeks of tedious document parsing to hours of high-judgment review. To process unstructured tender documents and chaotic, heterogeneous bidder submissions, the platform utilizes layout-aware parsing, Tesseract 5 OCR with an 80% confidence threshold to catch degraded scans, and LLMs (like Gemini 1.5 Pro or GPT-4o) to extract, categorize, and normalize critical entities while distinguishing mandatory from optional criteria. Extracted evidence is evaluated against tender requirements using a three-layer hybrid matching architecture that progresses from lightning-fast deterministic rules for hard numbers, to semantic embeddings for descriptive text, and finally to LLM reasoning for complex ambiguities - strategically trading raw processing speed for layered accuracy to mitigate hallucinations. Crucially, the system operates on the principle that no bidder is ever silently disqualified; a confidence-based routing mechanism ensures that any low-confidence or ambiguous matches are escalated to a Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) dashboard where reviewers can verify the exact highlighted source snippets. To guarantee strict compliance with CVC, CAG, and RTI guidelines, every automated verdict and identity-linked human override is permanently recorded in an ACID-compliant PostgreSQL database, generating an immutable, fully traceable audit log across the planned three-phase sandbox implementation of data ingestion, core matching, and UI development.

P Phantom
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
CDP (Court Directive Processor): From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 04:40 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
CDP (Court Directive Processor) is an AI-assisted decision support system designed to help government departments efficiently interpret and act on court judgments.
Court judgments are typically delivered as long, unstructured PDF documents containing critical directives, deadlines, and compliance requirements. At present, officials are required to manually read and interpret these documents, which is:
Time-consuming

Prone to human error

Difficult to standardize

Likely to result in missed directives or delayed action

This creates a gap between judgment delivery and administrative action.

Our Solution

CDP bridges this gap by transforming court judgment PDFs into structured, actionable insights through a multi-stage processing pipeline.
The system performs:
Extraction of key information:

Case details

Parties involved

Date of order

Key directions/orders

Relevant timelines

Analysis of legal content to identify:

Obligations and directives

Time-sensitive actions

Contextual importance

Action Plan Generation, including:

Compliance requirements

Consideration for appeal

Responsible department(s)

Priority level

Deadlines (explicit or inferred)


Human-in-the-Loop Verification (Core Feature)

A key strength of CDP is its verification-first approach, ensuring reliability and trust.
Each extracted field and generated action is presented with:
Contextual references from the original document

Confidence indicators

solutions.team_name: NyayaSetu — From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


A adwikavishal.cs24_80fb
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
NyayaSetu — From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 04:50 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
The ProblemEvery missed court directive can trigger contempt proceedings against a government department. Yet today, officials manually read 50-page judgments just to identify what action is required. A government official must read the entire document manually just to answer four critical questions:Did we win or lose?What exactly must we do to comply?Which department is responsible?What is the deadline — and have we already missed it?A single missed directive doesn't just cause delays. It can trigger contempt of court proceedings against the entire department. The current system gives officials access to documents. It gives them no path to action.﻿﻿Our SolutionNyayaSetu is an AI-powered compliance support system that converts unstructured court judgment PDFs into structured, verified, trackable action plans — in under 10 minutes per judgment, without replacing the human officer. It is designed for deployment within government infrastructure, supporting on-premise or private LLM endpoints to ensure data security. All outputs are fully auditable, with human verification mandatory before any action is recorded."We convert complex court judgments into verified, structured action plans — so no government official ever misses a deadline again."﻿﻿How It Works: 4-Step PipelineStep 1 — Extract The judgment PDF is ingested and auto-detected as digital or scanned. Digital PDFs are parsed using pdfplumber. Scanned documents are rasterized page-by-page and passed through Tesseract OCR. Output: a clean text string ready for AI processing.Step 2 — Analyze The text is passed once to an LLM (Claude / GPT-4o-mini) using a strict schema-enforced prompt designed specifically for Indian court language. The model returns a validated JSON object containing: case number, judgment date, court name, parties, all directives with deadlines and responsible departments, a plain-English summary, and a confidence score per field. Relative deadlines like "within 30 days" are automatically resolved to exact calendar dates. The appeal window (90 days from order date) is auto-calculated and flagged.Step 3 — Verify (Human-in-the-Loop — Mandatory) No directive enters the system without officer sign-off. The verification screen shows a split view: the original PDF on the left with the relevant passage highlighted, the AI-extracted action plan on the right with confidence scores. The officer can Approve, Edit, or Reject each directive individually. This step is non-negotiable — it ensures AI is a tool, not a decision-maker, and creates a full audit trail.Step 4 — Track All approved actions appear on a live dashboard with department-wise filtering, deadline countdown timers, and color-coded urgency indicators — Red (< 7 days), Yellow (< 14 days), Green (> 14 days). Overdue alerts are triggered automatically. The appeal window is shown as a live countdown so the Legal Cell never misses an SLP filing deadline.﻿﻿Concrete ExampleInput (raw PDF excerpt):"The respondent (State of Karnataka) is directed to submit a compliance report within 30 days from the date of this order. The petitioner shall be paid compensation of ₹5,00,000 within 60 days."Output (dashboard after verification):﻿﻿ActionDepartmentDeadlineDays LeftStatusSubmit compliance reportGeneral Administration01 May 20249 daysPENDINGPay compensation ₹5LFinance Department31 May 202439 daysPENDINGAppeal window closesLegal Cell30 Jun 202469 daysAUTO-TRACKED﻿﻿What Makes This DifferentMost systems stop at extraction. NyayaSetu ensures execution.Knowledge graph over flat search. Instead of basic vector similarity, we build a knowledge graph from each judgment that captures structured relationships — between directives, departments, deadlines, and case parties. This enables cross-judgment queries like "show all Revenue Department compliance actions due this month across all active cases" — something no flat RAG pipeline can do.Context-aware chat interface. An MCP-powered Claude integration lets officers ask plain-language questions directly about any judgment: "Does this affect the PWD department?" or "What happens if we miss the 30-day deadline?" The chat is grounded in the knowledge graph, not free-form generation, so answers are always traceable to source text.Hybrid architecture — LLM called exactly once. PDF extraction is local (zero API cost). Deadline arithmetic is pure Python (deterministic). Department mapping is rule-based (auditable). The LLM is invoked only once per document for the extraction step where natural language understanding is genuinely needed. This keeps costs low and makes every output explainable.Appeal window calculator. Automatically computes the limitation period from the date of order and flags SLP / writ deadlines. No existing CCMS tool does this. One missed SLP window can mean a final adverse judgment the government cannot challenge.Built for Bharat. Action plan summaries are available in both English and Kannada, making directives accessible to field-level officers across Karnataka. The system is designed to integrate natively with CCMS via the existing CIS API — judgments flow in automatically, no manual upload required.﻿﻿Tech StackComponentTechnologyPDF Extractionpdfplumber + PyMuPDFOCR FallbackTesseract + PillowAI ExtractionClaude / GPT-4o-mini (schema-enforced JSON)Knowledge GraphNeo4jVector SearchFAISSChat InterfaceClaude via MCPBackendFastAPI + PythonDatabaseSQLite + SQLAlchemyFrontendReact + Tailwind CSSChartsRechartsImpactReduces per-judgment processing time from 2–3 hours to under 10 minutesEliminates manual deadline calculation errorsPrevents contempt of court filings through appeal window trackingCreates a full audit trail for every compliance decisionScalable across all 28 departments of the Government of Karnataka and extensible to other state High Courts

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


A Automios
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
Scan to Score — AI-Powered Tender Evaluation Platform for Government Procurement
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 05:05 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
The ProblemGovernment procurement bodies like CRPF evaluate tender bids manually — a process that is slow, inconsistent across evaluators, prone to oversight, and difficult to audit. A single tender can involve hundreds of pages of bidder documents in mixed formats (typed PDFs, scanned certificates, photographs, Word files), evaluated against complex eligibility criteria written in formal legal language.Our Solution: Scan to ScoreScan to Score is an agentic AI platform that takes every bidder submission — regardless of format or quality — from raw scan to a fully scored, explainable eligibility verdict, while keeping the procurement officer firmly in control of every final decision.The Name Says It All Every step of the process lives in the name:Scan — Ingest any document: typed PDF, scanned certificate, photograph, Word fileto — Through extraction, parsing, matching, and evaluationScore — A criterion-level verdict with evidence, ready for officer sign-off﻿How It WorksStep 1 — Tender Parsing The officer uploads the tender document. A parsing agent automatically extracts all eligibility criteria — Pre-Qualification (PQ), Technical, and Commercial — classifies each as mandatory or optional, and converts them into structured, machine-readable rules. No manual configuration needed per tender.Step 2 — Document Ingestion (The "Scan") All bidder submissions are ingested regardless of format. Scanned documents and photographs pass through OCR. All content is preprocessed, cleaned, and embedded into a vector database — enabling fast, accurate retrieval of evidence mapped to each criterion.Step 3 — Agentic Evaluation Loop For each bidder, three specialised agents evaluate in sequence:PQ Agent — mandatory gates: GST registration, turnover thresholds, certificationsTechnical Agent — project experience, compliance requirements, technical specificationsCommercial Agent — financial figures, pricing criteria, financial thresholdsStep 4 — Explainable Verdicts (The "Score") Every agent produces a criterion-level verdict — Eligible, Not Eligible, or Needs Review — with full reasoning: which criterion was checked, which document was referenced, what value was extracted, and why the decision was made.Step 5 — Human-in-the-Loop Ambiguous cases (e.g., low-confidence OCR on a scanned financial certificate) are never silently rejected. They are flagged with the specific reason and routed to the officer for manual review.Step 6 — Audit-Ready Report An aggregation agent compiles all verdicts into an exportable report (JSON / CSV). Every automated decision is traceable to a source document and extracted value — suitable for formal government sign-off.﻿Why Scan to Score Works for Government ProcurementRequirementHow Scan to Score Addresses ItMixed document formatsOCR + preprocessing handles scans, photos, PDFs, Word filesMandatory vs optional criteriaAutomatically classified during tender parsingNo silent disqualificationAll ambiguous cases flagged for human reviewExplainabilityEvery verdict references criterion, document, and extracted valueAuditabilityEnd-to-end evidence chain on every decisionAdaptable to any tenderStructured rule extraction — no code changes per tender﻿What a Procurement Officer SeesUpload tender + bidder documents → Review extracted criteria → Get criterion-by-criterion scores per bidder → Ambiguous cases flagged → Export signed-off report. The entire evaluation that once took days is reduced to a review-and-approve workflow.

(Verified Actionable Legal Order Resolution) is a hybrid AI-powered system designed to transform complex court judgment documents into structured, actionable, and human-verified insights for faster and more reliable administrative decision-making.Court judgments are typically delivered as long, unstructured PDF documents where critical directives — such as what actions must be taken, deadlines, and responsible authorities — are buried within dense legal text. This forces officials to manually read entire documents, leading to inefficiencies, delays, and risk of misinterpretation.V.A.L.O.R. addresses this challenge by converting raw legal documents into clear, execution-ready insights, while ensuring that humans remain in control of final decisions through a verification layer.﻿Problem StatementGovernment officials and administrative users face several challenges when processing court orders:Legal PDFs are lengthy, complex, and difficult to navigate Critical instructions are not clearly structured Manual reading is time-consuming and error-prone Lack of clarity on deadlines and responsibilities No standardized system for extracting actionable insights High risk of missed compliance deadlines ﻿SolutionV.A.L.O.R. converts unstructured court judgments into:Clearly defined action items Identified responsible parties Extracted deadlines and timelines Highlighted compliance risks Structured outputs for faster decision-making The system uses a hybrid approach combining rule-based logic and lightweight AI, ensuring speed, reliability, and reduced hallucination risk.﻿How the System Works1. Document IngestionAccepts both digital PDFs and scanned documents Supports single and batch processing for multiple files ﻿2. Text Extraction & CleaningUses PDF parsing tools for digital files Uses OCR for scanned documents Cleans extracted text to remove: Noise Formatting issues Irrelevant symbols ﻿3. Section IdentificationDetects the most critical part of the judgment: Final Order / Directions section Uses keyword-based filtering to isolate actionable content ﻿4. Information Extraction (Rule-Based + AI Hybrid)Applies pattern-based logic to extract: Actions to be taken Responsible entities Deadlines Uses a lightweight local LLM (Google Colab) to: Improve clarity Structure outputs Enhance readability ﻿5. Action StructuringTransforms extracted content into:Actionable steps Responsibility mapping Timeline definition Risk indicators ﻿6. Human Verification LayerA critical feature ensuring real-world reliability:Users can: Review extracted data Edit or correct outputs Approve or reject results Only verified outputs are used in the final system ﻿7. Dashboard OutputThe system displays structured insights in a clean interface:What actions need to be taken Who is responsible Important deadlines Risks if not followed ﻿Example WorkflowInput (From Judgment)"The authority is directed to complete the required process within 6 weeks from the date of this order."System OutputAction: Complete the required process Responsible Authority: Identified department/authority Deadline: 6 weeks from order date Risk: Delay may lead to non-compliance ﻿Dashboard & Workflow SystemProcessing FlowPDF Uploaded → Text Extracted → Key Section Identified → Data Extracted → User Verification → Structured Output Displayed﻿Dashboard FeaturesVerified action items only Clear deadline tracking Responsibility mapping Risk highlighting Simple and minimal UI for quick understanding ﻿Core FeaturesHybrid AI + rule-based extraction system OCR support for scanned legal documents Final-order-focused analysis Human-in-the-loop verification system Batch PDF processing Lightweight and fast architecture Structured dashboard output ﻿Security & ReliabilityDoes not fully depend on AI (reduces hallucination risk) Local LLM processing ensures better control Human verification ensures accuracy before usage Designed as a decision-support tool, not full automation ﻿Key InnovationFocuses on actionable insights instead of summarization Uses hybrid architecture (rules + AI) for stability Prioritizes final judgment sections for relevance Ensures human validation before final output Designed for real administrative workflows ﻿ImpactReduces manual document reading effort significantly Speeds up identification of key legal directives Improves accuracy in compliance understanding Enables faster administrative decision-making Makes legal documents more accessible and usable ﻿Technology StackFrontend: Vite-based UI Backend: Flask Processing: OCR + PDF parsing AI Layer: Lightweight Local LLM (Google Colab) Logic Layer: Rule-based extraction engine ﻿Project LinksGitHub Repository:  https://github.com/chinmayaranjanswain/Verified-Actionable-Legal-Order-Resolution.git ﻿Live Demo:  https://valorai-drab.vercel.app/ ﻿﻿Project VideoDemo Video: Y yaminishreebaskar_2910
TEAM SUBMISSION [2]
TenderIQ: Multi-Agent AI Copilot for Government Procurement Evaluation
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 05:26 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Problem Understanding: Government tender evaluation is currently a manual, time-consuming, and inconsistent process, where evaluators must cross-check large volumes of unstructured documents against complex eligibility criteria.
Proposed Solution: We propose TenderIQ, an AI-powered, multi-agent system that transforms tender evaluation into a structured, explainable, and auditable pipeline. The system:
Extracts eligibility criteria from tender documents

Parses bidder submissions across formats

Matches bidder data to criteria

Produces criterion-level verdicts with evidence

Flags ambiguous cases for human review

Generates a complete audit-ready evaluation report

System Architecture:
Criteria Extraction Module Input: Tender document (PDF) Output: Structured criteria JSONApproach: - NLP + LLM-based parsing - Classification into: Technical, Financial, Compliance. - Mandatory vs optional detection using rule heuristics + semantic cues

Document Intelligence Module Handles: Typed PDFs, Scanned documents, Images/photos Pipeline: - OCR (for scanned docs) - Layout-aware parsing - Entity extraction: Financial values, Dates, Certifications, Project experience.

Multi-Agent Evaluation Engine We use a multi-agent architecture for modular, explainable reasoning: Agents: 1. Criteria Agent:Converts tender text → structured rules 2. Extraction Agent:Extracts bidder data from documents 3. Matching Agent: Maps extracted values to criteria 4. Decision Agent: It outputs into three categories : - Eligible - Not Eligible - Needs Review 5. Explanation Agent: It generates: - Criterion checked - Source document - Extracted value - Reasoning 6. Audit Agent : It detects: - Missing evidence - Low-confidence OCR - Conflicting data Forces “Needs Review” instead of wrong decisions

Human-in-the-Loop Design We explicitly design for safe decision-making:

Ambiguous cases are never auto-rejected

Review interface shows:

Highlighted source text

Extracted values

Confidence scores

Reviewer actions: Approve, Edit, Reject




Reviewer decisions are logged and used for continuous improvement

Output & Reporting The system generates:

Criterion-Level Evaluation Table with Evidence and Verdict

Consolidated Report - Final eligibility status - Supporting evidence - Full audit trail

Exportable Decision File

Handling Key Challenges
Heterogeneous Documents - OCR + layout-aware models - Confidence scoring for extracted data

Ambiguous Language - Semantic matching using embeddings - “Needs Review” fallback

Missing / Partial Data - Explicit detection - Never silent failure

Explainability - Every decision linked to: Criterion, Document, Extracted value.

Technology Stack
Backend: Python (FastAPI)

OCR: Tesseract / TrOCR

LLM: For reasoning + extraction (on non-sensitive data)

Vector DB: For semantic retrieval

Frontend: React dashboard

Storage: Structured JSON + document store.

Risks & Trade-offs
Risk
Mitigation
OCR inaccuracies
Confidence thresholds + human review
Legal language variability
LLM + semantic matching
Over-automation risk
Human-in-the-loop mandatory
Data inconsistency
Audit agent + validation layer

Impact & Scalability
Reduces evaluation time from days → minutes

Ensures consistent, bias-free decisions

Provides full auditability

Scalable across: Ministries, State governments, Public sector units

solutions.team_name: “TrustLayer: An Explainable AI Platform for Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis”
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


O On Probation
TEAM SUBMISSION [3]
“TrustLayer: An Explainable AI Platform for Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis”
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 03:12 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis Platform
Problem Understanding Government tender evaluation is a high-stakes, document-intensive process involving interpretation of complex eligibility criteria and validation against diverse bidder submissions. Key challenges include: Unstructured and heterogeneous data formats (scanned documents, PDFs, images), Ambiguity in legal and technical language, Manual, inconsistent evaluation processes, Lack of transparent and auditable decision trails. The core problem is not just extracting information, but ensuring that every eligibility decision is accurate, consistent, explainable, and defensible.
Solution Overview We propose a Trust-Centric AI Evaluation Platform that transforms tender evaluation into a structured, explainable, and auditable workflow. The system is designed around three core capabilities: a. Understanding tender requirements as structured, machine-interpretable criteria, b. Extracting and validating evidence from bidder submissions, c. Producing explainable, criterion-level eligibility decisions with confidence awareness. The platform prioritizes reliability over automation, ensuring that uncertain cases are always surfaced for human review.
Functional Architecture The system operates as a multi-layer verification pipeline, where each layer validates and strengthens the output of the previous one. Document Ingestion ↓ Document Understanding ↓ Information Extraction ↓ Data Normalization ↓ Cross-Verification ↓ Rule-Based Evaluation ↓ Confidence Assessment ↓ Human Review Interface ↓ Audit & Reporting
Tender Understanding Capability The platform automatically interprets tender documents and identifies: a. Financial requirements, b. Technical qualifications, c. Compliance and certification criteria. Each requirement is converted into a structured evaluation rule, capturing: The condition to be satisfied, Whether it is mandatory or optional, The type of supporting evidence required. This ensures that all criteria are standardized and consistently applied across bidders.
Bidder Document Understanding The system is designed to handle real-world document variability, including: Digitally generated documents, Scanned files and photographs. It extracts relevant information and converts it into structured evidence units, each linked to: A specific criterion, The source document, A confidence level. This allows the system to treat all submissions in a uniform and comparable manner.
Multi-Layer Verification Approach (Core Strength) To ensure reliability, the platform applies a structured multi-layer verification process before any evaluation is performed. Each layer validates the integrity and consistency of extracted evidence.
Extraction Consistency Validates that extracted values are consistent within the same document. a. Detects conflicting values (e.g., multiple turnover figures) b.Flags ambiguous or low-confidence extractions

Cross-Document Validation Compares related data across multiple submitted documents. a. Identifies mismatches (e.g., turnover differs between balance sheet and certificate) b. Detects missing or contradictory evidence

Semantic Normalization Ensures that variations in terminology, units, and formats are standardized before evaluation.

External Verification Validates key data against authoritative external sources. a. Government APIs (e.g., GST, MCA) b. Registration and compliance checks

Reliability & Confidence Assessment Evaluates the trustworthiness of each evidence unit based on: extraction confidence a. cross-source consistency b. external verification results

This layered approach ensures that decisions are based on verified and trustworthy data.
Evaluation and Decision Framework Eligibility is determined using a rule-based evaluation on the verified evidence from the previous stage.
For each criterion:
a. Apply the defined condition to the validated data b. Assign status
Final decision:
All mandatory satisfied → Eligible
Any mandatory failed → Not Eligible
Any uncertainty → Needs Review
The system ensures that no bidder is rejected without clear justification.
Explainability and Transparency Every decision is accompanied by a clear explanation, including: The criterion being evaluated, The evidence used, The reasoning behind the outcome. This allows procurement officers to: Understand how conclusions were reached, Verify decisions quickly, Build confidence in the system.
Confidence and Risk Awareness Each evaluation is associated with a confidence level, based on: Data reliability, Consistency across sources, Clarity of the criterion. Based on this, cases are categorized into: High Confidence – Suitable for automated decisions, Moderate Confidence – Requires attention, Low Confidence – Mandatory manual review. This ensures that the system behaves cautiously in uncertain scenarios.
Human-in-the-Loop Design The platform is designed to augment, not replace, human decision-making. It provides: Criterion-wise evaluation summaries, Direct access to supporting evidence, Clear identification of ambiguous cases. Procurement officers can review, validate, and override decisions, ensuring full control over final outcomes.
Auditability and Compliance The system maintains a complete and traceable audit trail of: Extracted information, Evaluation steps, Decision outcomes, Human interventions. This enables: Transparent decision-making, Easy auditing and verification, Compliance with government procurement standards.
Risks and Mitigation Strategy The system is designed with awareness of real-world challenges: Variability in document quality, Ambiguity in language, Incomplete or inconsistent submissions. These are addressed through: Multi-layer verification, Confidence-based decision handling, Mandatory human review for uncertain cases.
Implementation Roadmap The solution can be developed incrementally: Phase 1: Document ingestion and structured evaluation framework, Phase 2: Automated criterion understanding and evidence extraction, Phase 3: Multi-layer verification and explainability, Phase 4: Human review interface and audit reporting.
Key Differentiators Verification-first architecture, not extraction-first, Evidence-linked decision-making, Confidence-aware evaluation framework, Strict human-in-the-loop enforcement, Audit-ready design for government use.
Conclusion This platform redefines tender evaluation as a transparent, structured, and trustworthy process.
By combining intelligent document understanding with multi-layer verification and explainable decision-making, the system ensures: Consistent and fair evaluation, Reduced processing time, High confidence in outcomes, Full auditability for governance.

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE

A Ankit Tiwari
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
Nirikshan AI
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 03:12 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Government tender evaluation is a high-stakes, audit-sensitive process where accuracy and transparency are critical. Officers manually verify bidder submissions against complex eligibility criteria spread across lengthy tender documents. This process is slow, inconsistent, and difficult to audit, especially because bidder documents come in heterogeneous formats such as PDFs, scanned images, tables, and photographs. The evaluation of public tenders is a high-profile process requiring significant accuracy and transparency, and high levels of audit compliance. A Manual Evaluation Process (AEP) exists where agency representatives validate every bidder's submission against an extensive set of eligibility criteria described in lengthy public invitation for bid (PIFB) documents. Bidder submission reviews are time-consuming, inconsistent, difficult to audit, and complex because bidders submit their documents in many different formats (e.g., pdf files, scanned images, tables, photographs).There are also significant implications when errors are made during the evaluation process. The approval of an ineligible bidder may result in an agency audit of the PIFB creation process, and the rejection of an eligible bidder could lead to significant legal disputes. The new Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis System would serve as a decision support assistant to the procurement committee via explanation, audit trail, and human oversight of decision-making processes; it will not completely automate decision making for the procurement committees.Proposed Solution:We are proposing a multi-agent architecture that mimics the behavior of a "real" procurement committee, ensuring that the tender evaluation process will be performed in a manner that is structured, reliable and transparent.Extracting Evaluation Criteria (Agent A) -Agent A will extract, parse and convert the information included in the PIFB documents into structured evaluation rules. -While extracting the evaluation criteria from the PIFB documents, Agent A will categorize each criterion as belonging to one of the following types: -Technical CriteriaFinancial CriteriaCompliance CriteriaMandatory Conditions vs Optional Conditions -Agent A will output a structured schema (e.g. JSON) so that the procurement committee can evaluate each tender in a consistent manner2.Formatting Bidder Submissions (agent B) Rather than using simple OCR processes, we utilize layout models designed with knowledge of the entire document structure to extract valuable information from real-world documents, such as:Typing PDFs (or any typed format) Scanned certificates Tables, charts and images Understanding how information is laid out or organized in a document, including their layout and visual elements (stamps, seals, etc.) Providing data to Agents C and D along with confidence scores. Less than 1 minute.The Parser focuses on fact extraction only; it will not make any decisions.3.Evaluating Engine (agent C) The evaluating engine uses the extracted bidder data to check against structured criteria.It will deal with many issues:Format variations (e.g., ₹5 Cr and 50,000,000) Missing or partial data Ambiguous language It outputs the extracted data based on three criteria (eligible, not eligible, and manual review) to all agents. The evaluator does not determine qualifications for all bidders as all uncertain or ambiguous situations will be flagged.4.Reviewing/Validating by Auditor/Agent Inloop (agent D) The auditor reviews and validates Agent C’s decision regarding eligibility.A flag will be placed in front of the auditor by the evaluater when there is low confidence or the reasoning for disqualifying a bidder is deficient in any manner.The auditor/viewing officer will have access to the Reviewing Dashboard to:See the extracted values and associated criteria for each case flagged for review See the highlighted sections of the documents as evidence used to disqualify biddersExplanability/Auditability: All decisions are traceable to the following:The criteria you have evaluatedThe source document you have extracted fromThe value you have extractedWhy you evaluated the outcome as you didThis creates a complete chain of custody (audit trail), which complies with the government's requirements and allows for easy verification during an audit.Architecture & Technology: Multi-Agent System: A) Criteria Extraction—Agent A B) Document Parsing (Layout Aware AI)—Agent B C) Evaluation—Agent C D) Auditing/HITL—Agent DTechnology Stack: Layout LMV3/Vision Language Models for Document Understanding LLMs for Reasoning/Rule Extraction FastAPI/Flask Back-End (Core Processing) React-Based DashboardWhy This Approach? Decreased Hallucinations and Errors Modularity and Scalability Real-World Workflow AlignedRisk/Mitigating Factors: Challenges: Poor Quality Scans Mitigation: Confidence Scoring + HITL Challenges: Ambiguous Language Mitigation: Conservative Evaluation + Manual Review Challenges: Format Variability Mitigation: Layout Aware ModelsImplementation Strategy for Round 2: Build Agent A to Extract Criteria Implement Layout Aware Models for Document Parsing with Agent B Build Agent C for Framework Based Evaluation Add Auditing/HITL Routing to Agent D Audit/HITL Reports—Exportable and with Explanations Creating Simple Dashboard for Uploading/Reviewing/Generating and Reporting.
Conclusion: This system transforms tender evaluation into a structured, transparent, and AI-assisted workflow. By combining advanced document understanding, modular architecture, and human oversight, it ensures accurate, explainable, and auditable decision-making, making it highly suitable for real-world government procurement.I have mapped out the detail implementation in the document:  https://docs.google.com/document/d/14zUE3savy0ceTNoRi5EVgTKZTGk5VBh9/edit?usp=sharing&ouid=113065783741275967164&rtpof=true&sd=true

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE

O On Probation
TEAM SUBMISSION [3]
Judgment-to-Action Intelligence System A Verified and Explainable Decision Architecture for Government Compliance
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 03:15 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
JUDGMENT-TO-ACTION INTELLIGENCE SYSTEM (JAIS)
From Court Judgments to Verified Administrative Action Plans

PROBLEM STATEMENT

Government platforms such as the Court Case Monitoring System provide access to court judgments in PDF format. However, these judgments are unstructured, lengthy, and written in complex legal language.
Officials must manually interpret these documents to determine required actions, leading to:
High manual effort in extracting key directives

Difficulty identifying deadlines and compliance requirements

Uncertainty in deciding whether to appeal or comply

Risk of missing critical instructions

Delays in decision-making and execution

Core Problem: The issue is not access to judgments, but the absence of a system that converts them into structured, actionable decisions.

PROPOSED SOLUTION

A system that transforms unstructured court judgments into structured, verified, and explainable action plans.
Key Capabilities:
Understands semantic structure of legal documents

Extracts critical information using specialized components

Interprets judgment intent

Generates structured administrative action plans

Ensures human verification before usage

Displays only trusted data in a decision-ready dashboard

Focus: Decision support, explainability, and accountability (not just automation)

SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE

Flow:
Court Judgment PDF → Ingestion and Normalization Layer → Semantic Understanding Layer → Action Plan Generation Layer → Human Verification Layer → Trusted Output Dashboard

ARCHITECTURE DETAILS

4.1 Ingestion and Normalization Layer
Purpose: Convert raw PDFs into structured, machine-readable content
Functionality:
Handles scanned and digital PDFs

Performs text extraction and recognition

Identifies layout (headings, sections, paragraphs)

Preserves document structure

Design Rationale: Maintaining structure improves accuracy in legal interpretation
4.2 Semantic Understanding Layer
Purpose: Interpret document meaning beyond plain text
Components:
a) Intelligent Segmentation
Splits document into logical sections (background, arguments, orders)

b) Multi-Component Extraction Parallel extraction of:
Case details

Parties involved

Key directives

Timelines and deadlines

c) Legal Intent Classification Classifies judgment into:
Mandatory compliance

Appeal consideration

Advisory observation

Financial/administrative impact

4.3 Action Plan Generation Layer
Purpose: Convert interpreted data into structured decisions
Generated Output Includes:
Action type (compliance / appeal / review)

Urgency level

Deadline (explicit or inferred)

Responsible authority

Risk level

Step-by-step actions

Working Mechanism:
Deterministic Logic: Extracts explicit instructions (orders, deadlines)

Reasoning Component: Interprets implicit requirements

Mapping Component: Assigns responsibility based on administrative structure

Decision Justification:
Each action includes:
Source reference (exact text)

Explanation of reasoning

Confidence score

4.4 Human Verification Layer
Purpose: Ensure all outputs are validated before use
Features:
Displays extracted data with original document

Highlights source text

Shows confidence scores

Allows approve/edit/reject

Additional Capabilities:
Flags low-confidence outputs

Highlights critical directives

Maintains audit trail

Feedback Loop: Human corrections improve system over time
4.5 Trusted Output Dashboard
Purpose: Provide clean, verified, actionable insights
Key Principle: Only human-verified data is shown
Features:
Department-wise pending actions

Prioritized case list (urgency + deadlines)

Clear action + responsibility mapping

Lifecycle tracking of cases

DIFFERENTIATION FROM TYPICAL SYSTEMS

Typical Approach:
Single-stage extraction

Unstructured outputs

Limited explainability

Optional human review

Static processing

Proposed Approach:
Multi-layer semantic architecture

Specialized extraction components

Structured action plans (not summaries)

Built-in justification

Mandatory human verification

Continuous learning via feedback

CONCLUSION

This system bridges the gap between legal data availability and actionable governance.
By combining:
Structured understanding

Decision generation

Human verification

It ensures court judgments are converted into reliable, timely administrative actions.
The architecture is modular, explainable, and aligned with real-world workflows, making it suitable for practical deployment.

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE

T Team Byte_Me
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
NyayaSetu
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 03:18 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
NyayaSetu A Court Judgment Compliance System

CCMS delivers the judgment PDF. It does not tell the officer what to do or when. NyayaSetu does.

NyayaSetu is the operational layer CCMS was always missing the step between "judgment received" and "officer knows what to do, by when, and at what personal risk." 1. Problem Context CCMS fetches judgment PDFs from the High Court’s CIS via API. Once a case is disposed, the PDF lands in CCMS but nothing tells the law officer what to do next, by when, or who is responsible. India has over 55.8 million pending cases across all court levels as of March 2026, and the government itself is the single largest litigant responsible for roughly 50% of the entire legal burden. Each disposed case generates a judgment PDF that departments must act on. The compliance problem begins the moment that PDF lands in CCMS with no downstream workflow. 1.1 Pain Points from the perspective of a Government Law Officer
Statutory limitation periods are strict. Delays in internal approvals, officer transfers and inadequate case monitoring can jeopardize timely filing and may lead to dismissal of appeals unless delay is condoned by the court. - Critical
Where judicial orders are addressed to the State or multiple public authorities, internal responsibility mapping may be required to identify the implementing department. - High
No standard process exists for summarising lengthy orders, logging decisions, or routing for legal review across large litigation portfolios. - High
Wilful disobedience of judicial directions may expose the concerned authority or responsible officials to contempt proceedings under applicable law. - Critical

Example: Supreme court judgement against Chhattisgarh State Minor Forest Product Federation

The Supreme Court has recently tightened this further

Government officers arrayed in contempt proceedings cannot treat Court orders as optional. Accountability for non-compliance extends beyond named parties to all those in the chain of implementation who have knowledge of the order. Supreme Court of India on Israr Ahmad Khan v. Amarnath Prasad & Ors., May 2025

1.2 Positioning NyayaSetu is a personal liability shield for law officers, not a PDF summarizer. Every officer who receives a CCMS judgment notification has personal exposure if they fail to act correctly and on time. 1.3 Deployment Model The audit trail and timestamped approvals eliminate ambiguity that the current system provides as cover. NyayaSetu requires a top-down mandate from a Principal Secretary or Law Department head it is a procurement decision, not a self-service tool. 2. System Architecture and Modes 2.1 Modes
Reactive Mode: New judgment arrives -> Extract -> Verify ->officer approves → dashboard updated → officer records action taken → case closed.
Proactive Mode: Nightly job surfaces cases approaching deadline with no recorded action, departments with overdue compliances, and repeat petitioners with escalation history. No new AI required, pure aggregation over the verified database.

2. System Architecture ┌─────────────────────────────────┐ │ INGESTION LAYER │ │ PDF upload → PyMuPDF (digital PDFs, char offset) │ │ → Sarvam Vision API (scanned + Kannada/mixed-script PDFs) │ │ Pre-processing: Unicode normalization, ligature fix │ │ Output: text + character offsets + Sarvam output │ │ CCMS metadata consumed as extraction context │ └───────────────────────┬─────────┘ ┌───────────────────────▼────────┐ │ INTELLIGENCE LAYER │ │ Claude API — Chains 1 & 2 (extraction + comply/appeal reasoning) │
│Sarvam LLM: Chain 3 (department attribution, Kannada NER)│ │ Prompt caching on static document content │ │ It reduces cost by up to 90% after chain 1 , chains 2 and 3, pay 10% of input token price│ │ Chain 1: Extraction with CCMS priors │ │ Chain 2: comply/appeal chain-of-thought reasoning │ │ Chain 3: department attribution (NER + classify) │ │ Two-pass validation: structurally different prompts │ │ OCR confidence scores cap extraction confidence │ │ Section-aware chunking for long documents │ └───────────────────────┬────────┘ ┌───────────────────────▼────────┐ │ APPLICATION LAYER │ │ FastAPI backend │ │ React + PDF.js│ │ PostgreSQL - case lifecycle, audit trail, corrections │ │ Celery + Redis - notification jobs, nightly proactive │ │ CCMS SSO / NIC identity integration │ └────────────────────────────────┘ 3. Feature Set3.1 Source-Grounded PDF Extraction with Inline Highlights Every extracted field is anchored to its exact location in the source PDF. Tiered precision: character-level for digital PDFs, region-level for OCR with a "position approximate" indicator using PyMuPDF and Sarvam Vision.

Eg: deadline: { type: "infer", base_date: "2024-03-14", expression: "within six weeks", calc: "2024-04-25", conf: "medium" }, src_span: { pg: 12, start: 4521, end: 4687 }

3.2 Deadline Intelligence Engine: Two separate clocks: compliance deadline and appeal window. Converts relative expressions to absolute calendar dates. Appeal window auto-calculated from court type. 3.3 Comply vs Appeal Structured Reasoning: LLMs prompted through a fixed chain-of-thought: identify the holding,extract ratio decidendi vs obiter dicta, establish petitioner/respondent posture, surface appeal decision factors. Output is a structured table per direction 3.4 Responsible Department Attribution: Named entity extraction from parties and directions sections, followed by subject matter classification if no department is named. 3.5 Confidence Scoring: High (two structurally different passes agree), Medium (inferred or relative), Low (conflicting signals). Low-confidence fields surface the specific reason. 3.6 Human Verification Flow: States: EXTRACTED → VERIFIED → DISPUTED. No record reaches the dashboard until VERIFIED. Field-level approve/edit/reject. Every action timestamped and attributed. 3.7 Compliance Action Tracker: State machine: JUDGMENT RECEIVED → UNDER REVIEW → VERIFIED → ACTION TAKEN → CLOSED. Officer records action type, date, and reference number. Contempt Risk Register only flags VERIFIED cases with passed deadlines not ACTION TAKEN cases. Without this closure loop, the register is permanently red and officers stop trusting it. 3.8 Contempt Risk Register + Dashboard: Weekly action view by department and urgency. Contempt Risk Register as a dedicated separate view, never buried in routine traffic. 3.9 Escalation and Notification Engine: Notifications escalate upward: new judgment → reviewer. 48 hours unreviewed → department head. <30 days → officer + head. <14 days → Law Department. Overdue → Law Secretary. NIC mail + SMS integration. 3.10 Department Risk Profile: Win/loss ratio, most common directions, repeat petitioners, average time from judgment to action. 4. CCMS Integration NyayaSetu sits downstream of CCMS. No CCMS modification required. CCMS metadata: case type, department as petitioner or respondent, disposal date consumed as extraction priors: case type informs limitation period selection, government role pre-seeds the comply/appeal reasoning branch, disposal date is the base for all deadline calculations. Authentication via CCMS SSO through NIC identity infrastructure. NyayaSetu appears as a tab within CCMS. Data residency: Production deployment uses an on-premise or MeitY-approved cloud model.


5. Scale and Business Model Each High Court jurisdiction is a state government procurement. The model is SaaS licensed per jurisdiction an annual fee per High Court / state Law Department, priced on disposed cases processed per year. The path to national scale runs through MeitY/NIC. If NyayaSetu is adopted as a shared service under the Digital India programme, it becomes a central deployment serving all state governments rather than 28 separate procurements. eCourts Phase III, approved at ₹7,210 crore, explicitly mandates AI, OCR, and NLP in court workflows. NyayaSetu is the compliance intelligence layer that infrastructure enables but does not yet deliver.
