solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


P pallavikairamkonda24_014f
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
NyayScan — AI Procurement Eligibility Auditor
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 03:25 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
A government procurement officer sits down on a Monday morning with a 60-page tender document on one side and a stack of 10 bidder submissions on the other, each 30 pages thick. Somewhere buried in those pages are financial statements, experience certificates, GST registrations, ISO certifications — some typed neatly, some photocopied badly, some photographed with a phone camera. Her job is to check every bidder against 15 to 20 eligibility criteria, consistently, without missing anything, and finish before the committee meeting on Thursday. She has done this before. So has her colleague. Last month they evaluated the same tender independently and came to different conclusions on two bidders. Nobody is wrong — the documents are just genuinely hard to read, and the criteria are written in legal language that leaves room for interpretation. One missed certification and the entire procurement gets challenged in court. One ineligible vendor who slips through and public money is wasted. The pressure is real, the process is manual, and there is no record of why any decision was made. That is the problem we are solving with NyayScan. We built the system in four straightforward steps. When a tender PDF is uploaded, NyayScan reads it and pulls out every eligibility condition — minimum turnover, years of experience, required certifications, compliance documents — and organises them into a clear checklist, separating the must-haves from the nice-to-haves. When bidder documents come in, the system handles whatever format they arrive in. Clean PDFs get read directly. Scanned pages and photos go through OCR first so the text becomes readable. Every piece of information extracted is pinned back to the exact document and page it came from, with a confidence score attached. Then comes the matching. For straightforward numeric checks — like whether turnover exceeds 5 crore — we use simple rule-based logic because that is faster, cleaner, and easier to explain in a dispute. For criteria written in varying legal language, we use an LLM to understand what the document is actually saying. Every bidder gets a clear verdict on each criterion: Eligible, Not Eligible, or flagged for Manual Review. Nobody is silently rejected. If the system is not sure, it says so and hands it to a human officer. That officer sees exactly what the system found, where it found it, and why it made the call it did. They can approve it, correct it, or override it — and every one of those actions is recorded permanently. When the evaluation is done, the entire decision trail can be exported and used as the official audit record. The risks we thought hardest about were the small things that break real-world systems — a bidder who writes their turnover in lakhs when the tender asks for crores, a scanned certificate that is too blurry to read reliably, criteria worded differently in different tenders, two officers disagreeing on a borderline case. NyayScan handles each of these with a specific mechanism, not a workaround. For Round 2 we will bring a fully working prototype to the sandbox — upload a tender, upload bidder documents, get a complete evaluation report with audit trail, ready for an officer to sign off on.GitHub Repo:  https://github.com/Pallavikairamkonda/nyayscan  (https://github.com/Pallavikairamkonda/nyayscan)

solutions.team_name: BidInsight AI is an intelligent system that automates tender evaluation for government procurement.
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


K kaviyarasu kasi
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
BidInsight AI is an intelligent system that automates tender evaluation for government procurement.
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 03:54 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
BidInsight AI
GITHUB


1. Problem Understanding
Government procurement processes, particularly in organizations such as CRPF, involve evaluating multiple bidder submissions against detailed tender documents. These documents define eligibility through financial thresholds, technical qualifications, compliance requirements, and mandatory certifications.In practice, this evaluation is performed manually by committees who must cross-check each bidder’s documents against the stated criteria. This introduces several challenges:
Significant time consumption due to large volumes of document

Inconsistent interpretation of criteria across evaluators

Difficulty in verifying compliance across heterogeneous document formats

Limited traceability and auditability of decisions

Bidder submissions further increase complexity as they include a mix of typed PDFs, scanned documents, images, and tabular data, often presenting the same information in different formats.A critical requirement is that every decision must be explainable, auditable, and suitable for formal approval processes.

2. Proposed Idea

BidInsight AI is an evidence-driven tender evaluation system designed to automate the interpretation of tender requirements and the evaluation of bidder submissions while maintaining transparency and auditability.Unlike traditional rule-based systems that directly compare values, this approach focuses on identifying and validating the evidence used to make each decision.For every criterion, the system determines:
What the requirement is

What evidence was found in bidder documents

Whether the evidence satisfies the requirement

The confidence level of the evaluation

The reason behind the final decision

This ensures that every output is traceable and defensible.
3. Detailed Approach

3.1 Tender Understanding

The system begins by processing the tender document to extract eligibility criteria.The document is first segmented into logical sections such as financial requirements, technical specifications, and compliance conditions. Using a combination of natural language processing and rule-based parsing, each eligibility condition is identified and structured.Each criterion is represented in a standardized format containing:
Category (financial, technical, compliance)

Condition type (e.g., minimum, mandatory)

Expected value or requirement

Whether the condition is mandatory or optional

This structured representation enables consistent comparison across bidders.

3.2 Bidder Document Processing

Bidder submissions are processed through a unified pipeline capable of handling multiple formats, including scanned documents and images.The system performs:
Optical Character Recognition (OCR) for scanned content

Layout-aware parsing to identify sections and tables

Entity extraction to identify relevant fields such as turnover, certifications, and project experience

Each extracted piece of information is stored along with its source, including the document name, page number, and surrounding context. This ensures traceability.

3.3 Evidence Mapping

Once both criteria and bidder data are structured, the system maps each criterion to relevant evidence extracted from bidder documents.This mapping is performed using a hybrid approach:
Semantic matching to handle variations in terminology

Rule-based validation for numerical and logical conditions

For example, a criterion requiring minimum turnover will be matched with financial statements or certificates containing revenue information, even if terminology differs.

3.4 Evaluation and Decision Logic

For each criterion, the system evaluates whether the bidder meets the requirement.Instead of a binary decision, the system produces three possible outcomes:
Eligible

Not Eligible

Needs Manual Review

The third category is critical for handling ambiguity, such as low-quality scanned documents or incomplete data.Each evaluation is supported by a confidence score derived from extraction accuracy, document quality, and matching reliability.

3.5 Explainability and Audit

A key feature of the system is its ability to generate explainable results.For every criterion, the output includes:

The criterion description

The document used for evaluation

The extracted value

The reason for the decision

In addition, the system maintains a complete audit trail, recording all intermediate steps, including extraction results, matching logic, and final decisions. This ensures the system can be used in formal procurement workflows.
4. System Architecture

The system is composed of the following layers:

Document Processing Layer: Handles text extraction and OCR

AI Extraction Layer: Identifies criteria and extracts bidder data

Evidence Mapping Engine: Links criteria with relevant document evidence

Evaluation Engine: Applies validation logic and determines outcomes

Explainability Layer: Generates human-readable explanations

User Interface: Enables review and manual intervention

This modular design ensures scalability and maintainability.
5. Handling Real-World Challenges

The system is designed to address practical challenges in procurement workflows:
Unstructured Documents: Managed through OCR and layout-aware parsing

Variability in Terminology: Handled using semantic matching techniques

Incomplete or Ambiguous Data: Flagged for manual review instead of rejection

Requirement for Transparency: Ensured through detailed explanations and audit logs


6. Expected Impact

The proposed system delivers measurable improvements:
Significant reduction in evaluation time

Consistent and unbiased decision-making

Improved transparency and accountability

Enhanced scalability for large-scale procurement


7. Feasibility

The solution is feasible using existing technologies, including OCR tools, large language models for text understanding, and rule-based systems for validation. The architecture is modular, allowing incremental development and deployment.
8. Future Scope

Future enhancements may include:
Multilingual document processing

Integration with government procurement platforms

Advanced anomaly and fraud detection

Vendor performance analytics


9. Conclusion

BidInsight AI presents a structured and practical approach to modernizing tender evaluation. By combining AI-driven document understanding with evidence-based reasoning and explainable decision-making, the system ensures efficiency, reliability, and transparency in government procurement processes.

C ClearBid
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
AI-Based Tender Evaluation & Eligibility Analysis
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 04:21 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Our proposed Intelligent Tender Evaluation System addresses the high-stakes, manual bottleneck of government procurement by shifting the Tender Evaluation Committee from weeks of tedious document parsing to hours of high-judgment review. To process unstructured tender documents and chaotic, heterogeneous bidder submissions, the platform utilizes layout-aware parsing, Tesseract 5 OCR with an 80% confidence threshold to catch degraded scans, and LLMs (like Gemini 1.5 Pro or GPT-4o) to extract, categorize, and normalize critical entities while distinguishing mandatory from optional criteria. Extracted evidence is evaluated against tender requirements using a three-layer hybrid matching architecture that progresses from lightning-fast deterministic rules for hard numbers, to semantic embeddings for descriptive text, and finally to LLM reasoning for complex ambiguities - strategically trading raw processing speed for layered accuracy to mitigate hallucinations. Crucially, the system operates on the principle that no bidder is ever silently disqualified; a confidence-based routing mechanism ensures that any low-confidence or ambiguous matches are escalated to a Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) dashboard where reviewers can verify the exact highlighted source snippets. To guarantee strict compliance with CVC, CAG, and RTI guidelines, every automated verdict and identity-linked human override is permanently recorded in an ACID-compliant PostgreSQL database, generating an immutable, fully traceable audit log across the planned three-phase sandbox implementation of data ingestion, core matching, and UI development.

TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
CDP (Court Directive Processor): From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 04:40 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
CDP (Court Directive Processor) is an AI-assisted decision support system designed to help government departments efficiently interpret and act on court judgments.
Court judgments are typically delivered as long, unstructured PDF documents containing critical directives, deadlines, and compliance requirements. At present, officials are required to manually read and interpret these documents, which is:
Time-consuming

Prone to human error

Difficult to standardize

Likely to result in missed directives or delayed action

This creates a gap between judgment delivery and administrative action.

Our Solution

CDP bridges this gap by transforming court judgment PDFs into structured, actionable insights through a multi-stage processing pipeline.
The system performs:
Extraction of key information:

Case details

Parties involved

Date of order

Key directions/orders

Relevant timelines

Analysis of legal content to identify:

Obligations and directives

Time-sensitive actions

Contextual importance

Action Plan Generation, including:

Compliance requirements

Consideration for appeal

Responsible department(s)

Priority level

Deadlines (explicit or inferred)


Human-in-the-Loop Verification (Core Feature)

A key strength of CDP is its verification-first approach, ensuring reliability and trust.
Each extracted field and generated action is presented with:
Contextual references from the original document

Confidence indicators

Editable fields for correction

Reviewers can:
Approve

Edit

Reject

➡ Only verified records move forward into the system.

Dashboard for Decision-Makers

The final output is displayed in a clean, structured dashboard that shows:
Department-wise view

Key actions required

Important dates and deadlines

Priority levels

Verified, reliable records only

This enables faster, clearer, and more accountable decision-making.

Real-World Applicability

CDP is designed to integrate seamlessly with systems like CCMS, enhancing existing workflows without disruption.
Works with real court judgment PDFs

Supports administrative decision-making

Maintains human control while improving efficiency

For demonstration, the system has been tested using real court judgment PDFs from the eCourts platform, ensuring practical relevance and realistic performance.

Impact

CDP delivers measurable value by:
Reducing manual effort in judgment interpretation

Minimizing missed directives and deadlines

Improving compliance response time

Enabling structured and auditable workflows

Supporting officials with clear, actionable insights


In essence, CDP acts as a bridge between court judgments and administrative action — converting complex legal text into verified, decision-ready outputs.

solutions.team_name: NyayaSetu — From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


A adwikavishal.cs24_80fb
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
NyayaSetu — From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 04:50 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
The ProblemEvery missed court directive can trigger contempt proceedings against a government department. Yet today, officials manually read 50-page judgments just to identify what action is required. A government official must read the entire document manually just to answer four critical questions:Did we win or lose?What exactly must we do to comply?Which department is responsible?What is the deadline — and have we already missed it?A single missed directive doesn't just cause delays. It can trigger contempt of court proceedings against the entire department. The current system gives officials access to documents. It gives them no path to action.﻿﻿Our SolutionNyayaSetu is an AI-powered compliance support system that converts unstructured court judgment PDFs into structured, verified, trackable action plans — in under 10 minutes per judgment, without replacing the human officer. It is designed for deployment within government infrastructure, supporting on-premise or private LLM endpoints to ensure data security. All outputs are fully auditable, with human verification mandatory before any action is recorded."We convert complex court judgments into verified, structured action plans — so no government official ever misses a deadline again."﻿﻿How It Works: 4-Step PipelineStep 1 — Extract The judgment PDF is ingested and auto-detected as digital or scanned. Digital PDFs are parsed using pdfplumber. Scanned documents are rasterized page-by-page and passed through Tesseract OCR. Output: a clean text string ready for AI processing.Step 2 — Analyze The text is passed once to an LLM (Claude / GPT-4o-mini) using a strict schema-enforced prompt designed specifically for Indian court language. The model returns a validated JSON object containing: case number, judgment date, court name, parties, all directives with deadlines and responsible departments, a plain-English summary, and a confidence score per field. Relative deadlines like "within 30 days" are automatically resolved to exact calendar dates. The appeal window (90 days from order date) is auto-calculated and flagged.Step 3 — Verify (Human-in-the-Loop — Mandatory) No directive enters the system without officer sign-off. The verification screen shows a split view: the original PDF on the left with the relevant passage highlighted, the AI-extracted action plan on the right with confidence scores. The officer can Approve, Edit, or Reject each directive individually. This step is non-negotiable — it ensures AI is a tool, not a decision-maker, and creates a full audit trail.Step 4 — Track All approved actions appear on a live dashboard with department-wise filtering, deadline countdown timers, and color-coded urgency indicators — Red (< 7 days), Yellow (< 14 days), Green (> 14 days). Overdue alerts are triggered automatically. The appeal window is shown as a live countdown so the Legal Cell never misses an SLP filing deadline.﻿﻿Concrete ExampleInput (raw PDF excerpt):"The respondent (State of Karnataka) is directed to submit a compliance report within 30 days from the date of this order. The petitioner shall be paid compensation of ₹5,00,000 within 60 days."Output (dashboard after verification):﻿﻿ActionDepartmentDeadlineDays LeftStatusSubmit compliance reportGeneral Administration01 May 20249 daysPENDINGPay compensation ₹5LFinance Department31 May 202439 daysPENDINGAppeal window closesLegal Cell30 Jun 202469 daysAUTO-TRACKED﻿﻿What Makes This DifferentMost systems stop at extraction. NyayaSetu ensures execution.Knowledge graph over flat search. Instead of basic vector similarity, we build a knowledge graph from each judgment that captures structured relationships — between directives, departments, deadlines, and case parties. This enables cross-judgment queries like "show all Revenue Department compliance actions due this month across all active cases" — something no flat RAG pipeline can do.Context-aware chat interface. An MCP-powered Claude integration lets officers ask plain-language questions directly about any judgment: "Does this affect the PWD department?" or "What happens if we miss the 30-day deadline?" The chat is grounded in the knowledge graph, not free-form generation, so answers are always traceable to source text.Hybrid architecture — LLM called exactly once. PDF extraction is local (zero API cost). Deadline arithmetic is pure Python (deterministic). Department mapping is rule-based (auditable). The LLM is invoked only once per document for the extraction step where natural language understanding is genuinely needed. This keeps costs low and makes every output explainable.Appeal window calculator. Automatically computes the limitation period from the date of order and flags SLP / writ deadlines. No existing CCMS tool does this. One missed SLP window can mean a final adverse judgment the government cannot challenge.Built for Bharat. Action plan summaries are available in both English and Kannada, making directives accessible to field-level officers across Karnataka. The system is designed to integrate natively with CCMS via the existing CIS API — judgments flow in automatically, no manual upload required.﻿﻿Tech StackComponentTechnologyPDF Extractionpdfplumber + PyMuPDFOCR FallbackTesseract + PillowAI ExtractionClaude / GPT-4o-mini (schema-enforced JSON)Knowledge GraphNeo4jVector SearchFAISSChat InterfaceClaude via MCPBackendFastAPI + PythonDatabaseSQLite + SQLAlchemyFrontendReact + Tailwind CSSChartsRechartsImpactReduces per-judgment processing time from 2–3 hours to under 10 minutesEliminates manual deadline calculation errorsPrevents contempt of court filings through appeal window trackingCreates a full audit trail for every compliance decisionScalable across all 28 departments of the Government of Karnataka and extensible to other state High Courts

Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 04:53 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
CourtPilot is an AI-powered decision intelligence platform that converts court judgments into verified, actionable, and trackable compliance workflows for government departments. Problem:
Court judgments are long, unstructured PDFs with dense legal language

Critical directives are buried and not explicitly highlighted

No clarity on: What action is required? | Who is responsible? | When it must be completed?

Leads to delays, missed directives, and lack of accountability

In high-stakes cases, missing even a single directive can lead to legal penalties, financial loss, or contempt of court

Solution Approach:
Extract: OCR + NLP to parse judgments and extract directives, timelines, and case details

Analyze: Identify actionable instructions, infer deadlines, and map responsibilities

Verify: Human-in-the-loop with PDF highlighting, confidence scores, and approve/edit/reject workflow

Track: Convert into structured action plans with dashboards, lifecycle tracking, and escalation alerts

"Unlike traditional solutions that stop at extraction, CourtPilot ensures end-to-end execution with ownership, tracking, and accountability."

Key Innovations:
Risk Score Engine → Prioritizes cases (Low / Medium / High / Critical)

Appeal Success Predictor → Guides appeal decisions using similar case analysis (RAG)

Auto-Compliance Draft Generator → Creates ready-to-use compliance reports

Core Features:
Explainable AI with source-level traceability

Lifecycle tracking with ownership and audit logs

Smart alerts with risk-based escalation

AI chat assistant for natural language queries

Impact:
Faster decision-making

Zero missed directives

Reduced manual effort

Improved accountability and governance

Transforms weeks of manual effort into minutes of automated execution

CourtPilot transforms court judgments into accountable, trackable actions — ensuring zero missed directives and bridging the gap between legal outcomes and real-world execution. We built a multi-layer legal intelligence pipeline that parses, filters, validates, and converts unstructured court judgments into structured, verifiable actions.Missing a court directive can lead to legal penalties — CourtPilot ensures zero misses through intelligent tracking and accountability.

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


A Automios
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
Scan to Score — AI-Powered Tender Evaluation Platform for Government Procurement
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 05:05 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
The ProblemGovernment procurement bodies like CRPF evaluate tender bids manually — a process that is slow, inconsistent across evaluators, prone to oversight, and difficult to audit. A single tender can involve hundreds of pages of bidder documents in mixed formats (typed PDFs, scanned certificates, photographs, Word files), evaluated against complex eligibility criteria written in formal legal language.Our Solution: Scan to ScoreScan to Score is an agentic AI platform that takes every bidder submission — regardless of format or quality — from raw scan to a fully scored, explainable eligibility verdict, while keeping the procurement officer firmly in control of every final decision.The Name Says It All Every step of the process lives in the name:Scan — Ingest any document: typed PDF, scanned certificate, photograph, Word fileto — Through extraction, parsing, matching, and evaluationScore — A criterion-level verdict with evidence, ready for officer sign-off﻿How It WorksStep 1 — Tender Parsing The officer uploads the tender document. A parsing agent automatically extracts all eligibility criteria — Pre-Qualification (PQ), Technical, and Commercial — classifies each as mandatory or optional, and converts them into structured, machine-readable rules. No manual configuration needed per tender.Step 2 — Document Ingestion (The "Scan") All bidder submissions are ingested regardless of format. Scanned documents and photographs pass through OCR. All content is preprocessed, cleaned, and embedded into a vector database — enabling fast, accurate retrieval of evidence mapped to each criterion.Step 3 — Agentic Evaluation Loop For each bidder, three specialised agents evaluate in sequence:PQ Agent — mandatory gates: GST registration, turnover thresholds, certificationsTechnical Agent — project experience, compliance requirements, technical specificationsCommercial Agent — financial figures, pricing criteria, financial thresholdsStep 4 — Explainable Verdicts (The "Score") Every agent produces a criterion-level verdict — Eligible, Not Eligible, or Needs Review — with full reasoning: which criterion was checked, which document was referenced, what value was extracted, and why the decision was made.Step 5 — Human-in-the-Loop Ambiguous cases (e.g., low-confidence OCR on a scanned financial certificate) are never silently rejected. They are flagged with the specific reason and routed to the officer for manual review.Step 6 — Audit-Ready Report An aggregation agent compiles all verdicts into an exportable report (JSON / CSV). Every automated decision is traceable to a source document and extracted value — suitable for formal government sign-off.﻿Why Scan to Score Works for Government ProcurementRequirementHow Scan to Score Addresses ItMixed document formatsOCR + preprocessing handles scans, photos, PDFs, Word filesMandatory vs optional criteriaAutomatically classified during tender parsingNo silent disqualificationAll ambiguous cases flagged for human reviewExplainabilityEvery verdict references criterion, document, and extracted valueAuditabilityEnd-to-end evidence chain on every decisionAdaptable to any tenderStructured rule extraction — no code changes per tender﻿What a Procurement Officer SeesUpload tender + bidder documents → Review extracted criteria → Get criterion-by-criterion scores per bidder → Ambiguous cases flagged → Export signed-off report. The entire evaluation that once took days is reduced to a review-and-approve workflow.

Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 05:25 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)

V.A.L.O.R. — Verified Actionable Legal Order ResolutionA Decision-Support System for Converting Court Judgments into Actionable Compliance WorkflowsProject Type: AI-Assisted Legal Document Intelligence System Role: Developer / System Designer Tech Stack: Python, Flask, Vite, OCR, PDF Parsing, Local LLM (Google Colab), Rule-Based Engine﻿Project OverviewV.A.L.O.R. (Verified Actionable Legal Order Resolution) is a hybrid AI-powered system designed to transform complex court judgment documents into structured, actionable, and human-verified insights for faster and more reliable administrative decision-making.Court judgments are typically delivered as long, unstructured PDF documents where critical directives — such as what actions must be taken, deadlines, and responsible authorities — are buried within dense legal text. This forces officials to manually read entire documents, leading to inefficiencies, delays, and risk of misinterpretation.V.A.L.O.R. addresses this challenge by converting raw legal documents into clear, execution-ready insights, while ensuring that humans remain in control of final decisions through a verification layer.﻿Problem StatementGovernment officials and administrative users face several challenges when processing court orders:Legal PDFs are lengthy, complex, and difficult to navigate Critical instructions are not clearly structured Manual reading is time-consuming and error-prone Lack of clarity on deadlines and responsibilities No standardized system for extracting actionable insights High risk of missed compliance deadlines ﻿SolutionV.A.L.O.R. converts unstructured court judgments into:Clearly defined action items Identified responsible parties Extracted deadlines and timelines Highlighted compliance risks Structured outputs for faster decision-making The system uses a hybrid approach combining rule-based logic and lightweight AI, ensuring speed, reliability, and reduced hallucination risk.﻿How the System Works1. Document IngestionAccepts both digital PDFs and scanned documents Supports single and batch processing for multiple files ﻿2. Text Extraction & CleaningUses PDF parsing tools for digital files Uses OCR for scanned documents Cleans extracted text to remove: Noise Formatting issues Irrelevant symbols ﻿3. Section IdentificationDetects the most critical part of the judgment: Final Order / Directions section Uses keyword-based filtering to isolate actionable content ﻿4. Information Extraction (Rule-Based + AI Hybrid)Applies pattern-based logic to extract: Actions to be taken Responsible entities Deadlines Uses a lightweight local LLM (Google Colab) to: Improve clarity Structure outputs Enhance readability ﻿5. Action StructuringTransforms extracted content into:Actionable steps Responsibility mapping Timeline definition Risk indicators ﻿6. Human Verification LayerA critical feature ensuring real-world reliability:Users can: Review extracted data Edit or correct outputs Approve or reject results Only verified outputs are used in the final system ﻿7. Dashboard OutputThe system displays structured insights in a clean interface:What actions need to be taken Who is responsible Important deadlines Risks if not followed ﻿Example WorkflowInput (From Judgment)"The authority is directed to complete the required process within 6 weeks from the date of this order."System OutputAction: Complete the required process Responsible Authority: Identified department/authority Deadline: 6 weeks from order date Risk: Delay may lead to non-compliance ﻿Dashboard & Workflow SystemProcessing FlowPDF Uploaded → Text Extracted → Key Section Identified → Data Extracted → User Verification → Structured Output Displayed﻿Dashboard FeaturesVerified action items only Clear deadline tracking Responsibility mapping Risk highlighting Simple and minimal UI for quick understanding ﻿Core FeaturesHybrid AI + rule-based extraction system OCR support for scanned legal documents Final-order-focused analysis Human-in-the-loop verification system Batch PDF processing Lightweight and fast architecture Structured dashboard output ﻿Security & ReliabilityDoes not fully depend on AI (reduces hallucination risk) Local LLM processing ensures better control Human verification ensures accuracy before usage Designed as a decision-support tool, not full automation ﻿Key InnovationFocuses on actionable insights instead of summarization Uses hybrid architecture (rules + AI) for stability Prioritizes final judgment sections for relevance Ensures human validation before final output Designed for real administrative workflows ﻿ImpactReduces manual document reading effort significantly Speeds up identification of key legal directives Improves accuracy in compliance understanding Enables faster administrative decision-making Makes legal documents more accessible and usable ﻿Technology StackFrontend: Vite-based UI Backend: Flask Processing: OCR + PDF parsing AI Layer: Lightweight Local LLM (Google Colab) Logic Layer: Rule-based extraction engine ﻿Project LinksGitHub Repository:  https://github.com/chinmayaranjanswain/Verified-Actionable-Legal-Order-Resolution.git ﻿Live Demo:  https://valorai-drab.vercel.app/ ﻿﻿Project VideoDemo Video: solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


Y yaminishreebaskar_2910
TEAM SUBMISSION [2]
TenderIQ: Multi-Agent AI Copilot for Government Procurement Evaluation
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 05:26 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Problem Understanding: Government tender evaluation is currently a manual, time-consuming, and inconsistent process, where evaluators must cross-check large volumes of unstructured documents against complex eligibility criteria.
Proposed Solution: We propose TenderIQ, an AI-powered, multi-agent system that transforms tender evaluation into a structured, explainable, and auditable pipeline. The system:
Extracts eligibility criteria from tender documents

Parses bidder submissions across formats

Matches bidder data to criteria

Produces criterion-level verdicts with evidence

Flags ambiguous cases for human review

Generates a complete audit-ready evaluation report

System Architecture:
Criteria Extraction Module Input: Tender document (PDF) Output: Structured criteria JSONApproach: - NLP + LLM-based parsing - Classification into: Technical, Financial, Compliance. - Mandatory vs optional detection using rule heuristics + semantic cues

Document Intelligence Module Handles: Typed PDFs, Scanned documents, Images/photos Pipeline: - OCR (for scanned docs) - Layout-aware parsing - Entity extraction: Financial values, Dates, Certifications, Project experience.

Multi-Agent Evaluation Engine We use a multi-agent architecture for modular, explainable reasoning: Agents: 1. Criteria Agent:Converts tender text → structured rules 2. Extraction Agent:Extracts bidder data from documents 3. Matching Agent: Maps extracted values to criteria 4. Decision Agent: It outputs into three categories : - Eligible - Not Eligible - Needs Review 5. Explanation Agent: It generates: - Criterion checked - Source document - Extracted value - Reasoning 6. Audit Agent : It detects: - Missing evidence - Low-confidence OCR - Conflicting data Forces “Needs Review” instead of wrong decisions

Human-in-the-Loop Design We explicitly design for safe decision-making:

Ambiguous cases are never auto-rejected

Review interface shows:

Highlighted source text

Extracted values

Confidence scores

Reviewer actions: Approve, Edit, Reject




Reviewer decisions are logged and used for continuous improvement

Output & Reporting The system generates:

Criterion-Level Evaluation Table with Evidence and Verdict

Consolidated Report - Final eligibility status - Supporting evidence - Full audit trail

Exportable Decision File

Handling Key Challenges
Heterogeneous Documents - OCR + layout-aware models - Confidence scoring for extracted data

Ambiguous Language - Semantic matching using embeddings - “Needs Review” fallback

Missing / Partial Data - Explicit detection - Never silent failure

Explainability - Every decision linked to: Criterion, Document, Extracted value.

Technology Stack
Backend: Python (FastAPI)

Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 05:32 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Government procurement teams spend days manually checking whether each bidder meets the eligibility criteria in a tender document. Evaluators read through hundreds of pages of typed PDFs, scanned certificates, financial statements, and experience letters — and two evaluators can reach different conclusions from the exact same set of documents. The process is slow, inconsistent, and hard to audit.TenderLens AI solves this by automating the first reading — extracting criteria from the tender and checking each bidder against them — while keeping a human procurement officer in control of every final decision.How it works:Procurement officer uploads the tender document (PDF)AI extracts all eligibility criteria — financial thresholds, technical requirements, certifications, compliance conditions — and separates mandatory from optionalOfficer uploads bidder submission documents (typed PDFs, scanned copies, Word files)OCR processes scanned documents; AI extracts relevant values from each bidder's filesSystem evaluates each bidder criterion-by-criterion and classifies them as Eligible, Not Eligible, or Needs Manual ReviewEvery verdict references the exact criterion, the exact document, and the exact value that drove the decisionAmbiguous or low-confidence cases are flagged for human review — never silently rejectedOfficer reviews, edits if needed, and approves the evaluationA consolidated evaluation report is generated with a full audit trailThe human verification step is mandatory — no evaluation reaches the final report without officer approval. The AI handles the reading; the officer makes the decision.Tech Stack: Python, Django, HTML/CSS/JavaScript, Tesseract OCR, Google Gemini Free API, Hugging Face Inference API, MongoDB Atlas (Free Tier) / PostgreSQLImpact: Reduces tender evaluation time from days to hours, ensures every bidder is checked against the same criteria consistently, eliminates silent disqualifications, and produces a fully auditable report suitable for formal government procurement decisions.

solutions.team_name: JudgeAI — Turning Court Judgments into Actionable Compliance Plans
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


V vatskshitij6_2dd0
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
JudgeAI — Turning Court Judgments into Actionable Compliance Plans
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 06:00 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
The Problem We're SolvingAnyone who has worked in a government department knows this situation: a court judgment arrives as a PDF, it's 40 pages long, and someone has to read the whole thing, figure out what the court actually wants done, decide whether to comply or appeal, calculate the deadline, and make sure the right person handles it — all while managing everything else on their plate.This happens every single day across Karnataka's departments. And right now, it's entirely manual. Deadlines get missed. Directives get misread. Two officers reading the same judgment sometimes reach different conclusions about what it requires.We built JudgeAI because this problem has a straightforward fix that nobody has built yet.What We BuiltThe core idea is simple: an officer uploads a judgment PDF, and the system reads it, pulls out everything that matters, and turns it into a clear action plan — before a human reviews and approves it.The workflow looks like this:Officer uploads the judgment (works on both typed and scanned PDFs)System extracts the case details, the specific directions the court gave, which departments are responsible, and any timelines mentionedFor each direction, it generates an action item: comply or consider appeal, with a computed deadline using Limitation Act rulesA reviewer then sees the extracted data next to the original PDF, with the exact line highlighted that the system used. They can approve, fix, or reject anything before it moves forwardOnly verified records appear on the dashboard — clean, filterable, reliableThe Part We're Most Proud OfWhen a judgment says "within six weeks," most tools just extract that phrase. Ours converts it to an actual calendar date from the judgment date. When an appeal is possible, it automatically flags the 90-day window to approach the Supreme Court. Officers currently track this mentally or miss it entirely. We made it automatic.We also built partial verification — reviewers don't have to approve or reject a whole document at once. They can approve some fields and flag others for a second look. This matters a lot when you're processing dozens of judgments a week.Why It Will Actually Get UsedWe were very deliberate about one thing: this doesn't touch CCMS, doesn't touch CIS, doesn't require any department to change how they work. Judgments already arrive as PDFs via API. JudgeAI sits on top of that existing flow. The only new thing for an officer is a dashboard that tells them what to do and by when.Tech StackPython, Flask, PyMuPDF, Tesseract for scanned documents, LLM for structured extraction, PDF.js for the side-by-side verification view, React dashboard, PostgreSQL. Fully deployed with a live demo.Bigger PictureKarnataka High Court closes around 1,500 to 2,000 cases a month. Multiply that by every state government department that receives those judgments, and the manual effort being spent just on reading and routing is enormous. Scale this to other High Courts and the impact compounds fast.

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


T The team
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
SmartTender AI — Intelligent Tender Evaluation & Eligibility Analysis Platform for Government Procurement
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 06:45 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
The ProblemGovernment procurement bodies like CRPF process hundreds of pages per tender cycle. Manual evaluation of bidder eligibility is slow, inconsistent, and difficult to audit. Different evaluators interpret the same criteria differently, critical documents get missed, and rejected bidders can challenge decisions within 72 hours — making every verdict legally accountable.Our SolutionSmartTender AI is an end-to-end intelligent platform that automates the technical bid evaluation process while keeping the procurement officer fully in control.The system works in five stages:Document Ingestion & OCR — Accepts all real-world document formats: typed PDFs, scanned documents, photographs of certificates, and handwritten submissions. Uses multimodal AI to extract clean, structured text regardless of quality.Tender Criteria Extraction — Automatically parses the tender document and builds a structured Criteria Registry — every eligibility rule, threshold, and mandatory/optional flag — before touching any bidder submission. Officer reviews and confirms the criteria list before evaluation begins.Bidder Evidence Extraction — For each criterion, the system performs targeted retrieval from each bidder's documents, extracts the relevant value, and records the exact source document, page number, and quoted text as evidence.Verdict Engine — A three-tier verdict model: Eligible (auto-approved, confidence ≥ 0.85), Ineligible (auto-rejected with clear reason), and Manual Review (escalated to officer with full context, candidate interpretations, and source document viewer). No bidder is disqualified without officer confirmation.Explainable Audit Trail — Every decision is traceable to its source. SHA-256 document hashing at upload, append-only event logs, and criterion-level verdict cards with quoted evidence make the evaluation legally defensible and instantly reconstructable if challenged.Key DifferentiatorsAI as support, not decision-maker — The procurement officer confirms all rejections. The system handles volume; the officer handles accountability.Handles real-world document chaos — Scanned certificates, low-quality photographs, Hindi-language documents, expired certificates, joint venture submissions, and multi-condition eligibility clauses are all handled with confidence scoring and smart escalation.Zero silent failures — If the system cannot read a value, it says so and escalates. It never guesses on critical eligibility data.Audit-ready by design — Every auto-verdict can be reconstructed months later: what document was uploaded, what the AI extracted, what the rule compared, what the officer confirmed.ImpactA tender evaluation that takes a committee 3–4 days can be reduced to a same-day process, with higher consistency, complete documentation, and a human officer who spends their time on genuine ambiguities — not paperwork.Theme AlignmentThis solution directly addresses Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF — building the exact pipeline described in the problem statement, with practical handling of the document quality, language ambiguity, and legal accountability challenges that make this problem hard in the real world.

solutions.team_name: SyncBridge — Bidirectional Event-Driven Interoperability Layer for Karnataka Government Systems
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


A ankit797_eaea
TEAM SUBMISSION [10]
SyncBridge — Bidirectional Event-Driven Interoperability Layer for Karnataka Government Systems
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 07:02 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
SyncBridge — Bidirectional Event-Driven Interoperability Layer for Karnataka Government SystemsKarnataka Commerce & Industry﻿Prototype OverviewFieldDetailsTheme2 — Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department SystemsDepartmentKarnataka Commerce & IndustryPrototype IdeaAn event-driven middleware layer that propagates service requests bidirectionally between Karnataka's Single Window System and 40+ legacy department systems using UBID as the join key, with per-department schema translation adapters, configurable conflict resolution policies, idempotent delivery guarantees, and a complete audit trail for every propagation — without modifying either side.﻿What Is This Prototype?SyncBridge is a middleware bus that sits between SWS and legacy department systems, acting as the single propagation authority for service request changes. When a business updates its registered address in SWS, SyncBridge translates the change into each target department's schema and delivers it with at-least-once guarantees and idempotency keys — no duplicates, no lost updates. When a change is made directly inside a department system (via API, webhook, or detected via snapshot diff), SyncBridge picks it up, translates it into the SWS schema, and writes it back to SWS. When two updates for the same UBID arrive within a conflict window, SyncBridge detects the conflict, applies the configured resolution policy, and records both the conflict and its resolution in an immutable audit log. Neither SWS nor any department system is modified at any point.﻿What It Is Used ForPropagating SWS service requests (address change, signatory update, licence renewal) to every department system that holds a record for the same UBIDDetecting changes made directly inside department systems — via REST API, webhook, or scheduled snapshot comparison — and propagating them back to SWSTranslating between SWS's canonical schema and each department's proprietary field names, data types, and validation rules using per-department adapter pluginsDetecting concurrent conflicting updates for the same UBID and applying a configurable resolution policy (latest-writer-wins, SWS-authoritative, or manual escalation)Maintaining an immutable audit trail for every propagation: what changed, from which system, to which systems, at what time, and how any conflict was resolvedProviding a reconciliation dashboard for operators to monitor in-flight propagations, failed deliveries, and pending conflict resolutions﻿Why It Is HelpfulKarnataka's "split-brain" problem — the same business holding inconsistent state across SWS and legacy systems — causes citizen confusion, officer errors, and audit failures; SyncBridge eliminates this without a big-bang migrationUBID (from Theme 1) is the only reliable join key that makes cross-system propagation possible at all; this solution treats UBID as a hard precondition, not an assumptionEvent-driven architecture means propagations happen within seconds of a change being detected, not in overnight batch runs that leave systems inconsistent for hoursIdempotency keys ensure that retry storms (common in distributed systems) never produce duplicate writes in target systemsConflict detection with a configurable resolution policy means that simultaneous updates — which will inevitably happen — are handled explicitly rather than producing silent data corruptionThe adapter plugin architecture means adding a new department system requires writing one adapter, not changing the core bus or any existing system﻿Implementation GuidePhase 1: Core Event Bus & UBID RoutingDeploy Apache Kafka as the central event backbone; one topic per event type (address-change, signatory-update, licence-renewal, etc.)Implement a UBID Router service: every inbound event carries a UBID; the router looks up which department systems hold a record for that UBID and routes the event to the appropriate per-department delivery queuesAssign each event a globally unique idempotency key (UUID + source-system + timestamp hash); target adapters use this key to detect and discard duplicate deliveriesImplement a dead-letter queue for events that fail delivery after 3 retries; surface failed events in the reconciliation dashboard with the failure reasonPhase 2: Department Adapter Plugin FrameworkDesign a standard Adapter Interface with four methods: detect_changes(), translate_inbound(event), translate_outbound(event), and write(translated_event)Implement one adapter per department system; each adapter handles that system's specific API, authentication, field mapping, and retry logicStore field-mapping definitions in a Schema Registry (a versioned YAML configuration per department); the adapter reads mappings at runtime so a field rename in a department system requires only a config update, not a code deploymentFor department systems without native event emission, implement a Snapshot Differ: takes a daily snapshot of the system's state, diffs it against the previous snapshot, and emits change events for any modified recordsFor systems with webhooks or REST APIs, implement webhook listeners and polling adapters as appropriatePhase 3: Conflict Detection & ResolutionDefine a conflict window (configurable, default 60 seconds): if two updates for the same UBID arrive within this window from different source systems, flag them as a conflictImplement three resolution policies, configurable per event type:latest_writer_wins: the update with the later timestamp winssws_authoritative: the SWS version always wins for the affected fieldmanual_escalation: both updates are held pending and routed to a human operator via the reconciliation dashboardRecord every conflict and its resolution in an immutable audit log stored in an append-only PostgreSQL table with row-level lockingPhase 4: Audit Trail & Reconciliation DashboardEvery propagation writes an audit record: event ID, idempotency key, source system, target systems, event payload hash (not raw PII), delivery status, conflict flag, resolution applied, timestampsBuild a reconciliation dashboard (React + FastAPI) showing: in-flight propagations, failed deliveries, active conflicts, and a searchable audit log filterable by UBID, system, date range, and event typeImplement a "trace" view: given a UBID and a date range, show the complete propagation history for that business across all systems — what changed, where it originated, where it was writtenAdd a health dashboard: per-department delivery success rate, average propagation latency, and conflict frequency — to surface systemic issues before they accumulate﻿Recommended Technology StackComponentTechnology / ToolEvent BusApache Kafka (durable, ordered, replayable event log)UBID RouterPython microservice consuming Kafka topicsSchema RegistryCustom versioned YAML registry + Git-backed config managementAdapter FrameworkPython plugin system (one adapter class per department)Snapshot DifferPython + SQLAlchemy; hashes row state to detect changesIdempotency StoreRedis (idempotency key cache with TTL)Conflict DetectionKafka Streams consumer group with tumbling conflict windowAudit LogPostgreSQL (append-only table, no UPDATE/DELETE allowed)Dead Letter QueueKafka DLQ topic + Slack/email alertingReconciliation DashboardReact + FastAPI; real-time via Server-Sent EventsLLM UsageNot required; schema translation is rule-based via adapter config﻿Key Constraints & Risk MitigationsNeither SWS nor any department system can be modified — the adapter interface is purely consumer/producer; all writes use the target system's existing APIs; no schema changes, no new tables, no new endpoints created in source systemsUBID is a hard precondition — SyncBridge will not propagate any event without a valid UBID; events with unresolved UBIDs are held in a pending queue and surfaced for manual resolutionAt-least-once delivery with idempotency — Kafka guarantees at-least-once; idempotency keys at the adapter layer prevent duplicate writes from becoming duplicate records in target systemsSchema drift — department APIs change; the schema registry is versioned and changes require a reviewed PR; a schema validation check runs on every adapter startup and alerts on mismatchSnapshot diff lag — for systems without native event emission, the snapshot differ runs daily, meaning changes can take up to 24 hours to propagate; this lag is explicitly surfaced in the dashboard and is acceptable as a stepping-stone toward webhook-based detectionConflict window tuning — the 60-second default may be too short for some workflows and too long for others; the window is configurable per event type and can be tuned based on observed conflict frequency after deploymentPII in audit log — audit records store payload hashes and field names, not raw field values, to avoid accumulating a PII store; full payloads are retained only in Kafka with a 90-day retention policy and access controlsRollback — every propagation is reversible: the audit log contains the pre-change state hash; a rollback event can be emitted to re-propagate the previous state to all target systems

solutions.team_name: TenderLens — Explainable AI Procurement Evaluation Platform for Government Tenders
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


A ankit797_eaea
TEAM SUBMISSION [10]
TenderLens — Explainable AI Procurement Evaluation Platform for Government Tenders
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 07:05 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Theme 3TenderLens — Explainable AI Procurement Evaluation Platform for Government TendersCRPF / Government Procurement﻿What Is This Prototype?TenderLens is a document intelligence platform designed for government procurement. A procurement officer uploads a tender document and a batch of bidder submissions in any format — typed PDF, scanned copy, Word file, or photograph of a certificate. The system first extracts and structures the eligibility criteria from the tender, distinguishing mandatory from optional and tagging each criterion's data type (financial threshold, date-bound experience, certification name, compliance flag). It then parses each bidder's document set, extracting the values relevant to each criterion with page-level source references. An evaluation engine matches extracted values against criteria, producing per-criterion verdicts of Eligible, Not Eligible, or Needs Manual Review — with the specific document, page, and extracted value cited for every verdict. Ambiguous or low-confidence extractions are always escalated to human review rather than silently failing the bidder. The final output is a consolidated evaluation report the procurement officer can review, annotate, export, and sign off on.﻿What It Is Used ForAutomatically extracting eligibility criteria from government tender documents, including technical specifications, financial thresholds, compliance requirements, and certification checklistsParsing bidder submissions regardless of format: typed PDFs, scanned documents with OCR, Word files, Excel tables, and photographs of physical certificatesEvaluating each bidder against each criterion with a source-referenced verdict: which document, which page, which extracted value, and why the bidder passed, failed, or needs reviewEnsuring no bidder is silently disqualified — every ambiguous or uncertain case is escalated to a human reviewer with the specific reason for ambiguity surfaced clearlyProducing a consolidated procurement report that procurement officers can use as the basis for a final decision, with a full audit trail of every automated decisionDetecting potential inconsistencies within a single bidder's submission (e.g., turnover figures that differ across financial statements and a summary certificate)﻿Why It Is HelpfulManual evaluation of a single tender can take days for a committee and produces inconsistent results across evaluators — TenderLens reduces this to hours and eliminates inter-evaluator inconsistencyThe "never silent disqualification" guarantee directly addresses the highest-risk failure mode in procurement AI: a system that quietly excludes a legitimate bidder without explanationSource-level references (document name, page number, extracted text snippet) make every verdict auditable — a challenged decision can be traced back to the exact evidence in secondsScanned document handling removes the advantage currently enjoyed by bidders who submit better-formatted documents; all submissions are evaluated on their content, not their formattingInconsistency detection within a single bidder's submission catches fraudulent or erroneous documents before the committee review, not afterThe system is designed as a decision-support tool, not a replacement for the procurement committee; the human sign-off step is mandatory and built into the workflow﻿Implementation GuidePhase 1: Tender Criterion ExtractionIngest the tender document (PDF or Word); run layout-aware PDF parsing (pdfplumber for typed PDFs; Sarvam Document Intelligence API for mixed/scanned content) to preserve table structure and section hierarchyUse Sarvam API (Sarvam-1) to extract eligibility criteria and classify each as:Type: financial threshold, experience requirement, certification, compliance condition, document checklistCardinality: mandatory or optionalMatching rule: numeric comparison (e.g., turnover ≥ ₹5 crore), date-bound count (e.g., 3 projects in 5 years), string match (e.g., valid GST registration), binary presence (e.g., ISO 9001 certificate)Output a structured criterion list; surface it to the procurement officer for review and correction before evaluation begins — the officer's confirmed criterion list is the ground truth for all subsequent evaluationPhase 2: Bidder Document ParsingFor each bidder, ingest all submitted documents; classify each document by type (financial statement, experience letter, tax registration, certification, company profile)Apply Sarvam Document Intelligence API to all non-digital documents (printed scans, photographs, complex layouts); flag low-confidence OCR outputs (confidence < 0.75) for manual review at the extraction stageFor each criterion, run a targeted extraction pass over the relevant document type: extract numeric values, dates, project counts, certificate names, GST registration numbersNormalise extracted values: convert financial figures to a common unit (₹ lakhs), parse dates to ISO format, standardise certification name spellingsTag every extracted value with: source document name, page number, bounding box coordinates (for highlight display), raw extracted text, normalised value, and extraction confidence scorePhase 3: Criterion-Level Evaluation & EscalationFor each (bidder, criterion) pair, apply the matching rule using the extracted value:Numeric threshold: compare normalised value against criterion threshold with a ±5% tolerance band that triggers "Needs Review" rather than hard failDate-bound experience: count qualifying projects within the specified date window; flag if project dates are ambiguous or overlap suspiciouslyCertification presence: match certificate name with fuzzy string matching; flag partial matches for reviewCompliance condition: check for presence of required registration numbers; cross-validate format against known patterns (GSTIN format regex, PAN format)Assign verdict: Eligible (all conditions met with extraction confidence ≥ 0.85), Not Eligible (clear failure with high-confidence evidence), or Needs Manual Review (low extraction confidence, ambiguous value, conflicting documents, or value in a tolerance band)For every Not Eligible verdict, surface the specific criterion, the evidence found (or explicitly note what was missing), and the document where the deficiency was detectedFor every Needs Manual Review verdict, surface the reason for ambiguity with the raw extracted text highlighted in the source document — reviewers see exactly what the system sawPhase 4: Consolidated Report & Audit TrailGenerate a consolidated evaluation report: per-bidder summary (overall verdict, pass/fail per criterion), a comparison table across all bidders, and a flagged-for-review sectionExport the report as PDF with source highlights embedded — clicking a criterion verdict shows the relevant page and extracted snippet from the bidder's original documentRecord the complete audit trail: extraction confidence per field, matching logic applied per criterion, verdict timestamp, reviewer actions (overrides with reasons), final sign-offProvide an API for programmatic integration with the government procurement portal; support bulk tender and bidder ingestion for high-volume procurement events﻿Recommended Technology StackComponentTechnology / ToolTyped PDF Parsingpdfplumber + PyMuPDF for layout-aware text and table extractionDocument Intelligence (OCR)Sarvam Document Intelligence API (all document types: scanned, photo, mixed layout)Criterion Extraction LLMSarvam API (Sarvam-1); Llama-3-8B via Ollama as on-premises fallbackValue ExtractionRegex + spaCy NER for structured fields; Sarvam API for complex languageFuzzy MatchingRapidFuzz for certification name matchingNumeric NormalisationPython (pint library for unit conversion)Evaluation EnginePython rule engine (transparent, auditable, no ML black box)Report GenerationWeasyPrint (PDF with embedded source highlights)Reviewer InterfaceReact + FastAPI; PDF viewer with highlight overlayAudit StorePostgreSQL (immutable audit log with row-level provenance)﻿Key Constraints & Risk MitigationsNever silent disqualification — the system's architecture makes it structurally impossible to silently fail a bidder; the Needs Manual Review path is the default when confidence is insufficient, not an exceptionScanned document quality — low-resolution scans (< 150 DPI) produce unreliable OCR; flag these at ingestion with a quality score and ask the officer to request a higher-resolution copy before evaluation proceedsLanguage variation in legal documents — tender language varies across government departments; the criterion extraction prompt is tested on diverse tender samples and includes few-shot examples covering financial, compliance, and technical criterion typesFraudulent documents — the system detects internal inconsistencies (e.g., turnover figures that differ across documents) and flags them as a conflict for human review; it does not make forgery allegations but surfaces the discrepancy clearlyEvaluation at scale — for tenders with 50+ bidders, batch processing runs in parallel; each bidder's document set is processed independently with no shared stateCriterion extraction errors — the procurement officer reviews and confirms the extracted criterion list before any bidder is evaluated; an officer correction at this stage propagates to all bidder evaluations, eliminating systematic errorsData sensitivity — tender documents and bidder financials are sensitive; Sarvam API processes documents via its secure API; for strictly air-gapped environments, Llama-3-8B via Ollama is the on-premises fallbackAuditability under challenge — every verdict can be traced to a specific extracted value, a specific document page, and the matching logic applied; the audit trail is exportable as a structured evidence pack for use in procurement disputes

A ankit797_eaea
TEAM SUBMISSION [10]
NyayaSetu — AI-Assisted Court Judgment Extraction, Action Planning & Compliance Tracking
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 07:31 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
NyayaSetu — AI-Assisted Court Judgment Extraction, Action Planning & Compliance TrackingCentre for e-Governance / Karnataka CCMS﻿Prototype OverviewFieldDetailsTheme11 — From Court Judgments to Verified Action PlansDepartmentCentre for e-Governance, Government of Karnataka (CCMS)Prototype IdeaAn AI system that ingests Karnataka High Court judgment PDFs from CCMS, extracts case details and key directives with source-highlighted evidence, generates structured action plans with automatically computed compliance deadlines and limitation periods, routes every extracted item through mandatory human verification before it enters a trusted dashboard, and provides department-wise compliance tracking with escalation alerts.﻿What Is This Prototype?NyayaSetu ("Justice Bridge") connects the automatic arrival of court judgment PDFs in CCMS to the verified action plans government departments need to respond in time. When a judgment PDF arrives via the CCMS-High Court CIS integration, NyayaSetu ingests it, runs layout-aware PDF parsing, and uses an LLM-based pipeline to identify case details, key directives, parties, and timeline references. Critically, it automatically computes compliance deadlines and appeal limitation periods — a High Court order typically carries a 90-day appeal window under Article 226 — so officials no longer manually calculate whether an appeal is still possible. Every extracted item is presented to a human reviewer with the exact source PDF passage highlighted; nothing enters the trusted dashboard without a verified tick.﻿What It Is Used ForAutomatically ingesting and parsing Karnataka High Court judgment PDFs arriving via CCMS-CIS API, including scanned and digital formatsExtracting structured case information: case number, judgment date, court, parties, key directives, compliance conditions, and relevant timelinesGenerating structured action plans: compliance requirement, responsible department, action type (comply/appeal/escalate), computed deadlines, and limitation period remainingAutomatically computing appeal limitation periods (90 days for High Court, 30 days for contempt compliance, or as specified) and surfacing them as countdown timersRouting all extracted data through mandatory human verification with source-highlighted evidence before any item appears in the trusted dashboardProviding a department-wise compliance dashboard with countdown timers, pending actions, and escalation alerts for approaching deadlines﻿Why It Is HelpfulOfficials currently read entire lengthy judgment PDFs manually to find the 2–3 directives requiring action — NyayaSetu reduces this from hours to minutes per judgmentMissed court directives and appeal deadlines have serious legal consequences; automatic deadline computation and countdown alerts directly prevent this failure modeMandatory human verification ensures no AI extraction error enters the official record — the system is a decision-support tool, not a replacement for legal reviewSource-highlighted evidence (clicking an extracted field shows the exact PDF passage) makes the reviewer's job fast: they confirm what the AI found rather than re-reading the whole documentDepartment-wise organisation makes the dashboard directly useful for the responsible officer — they see their department's pending actions, not a global legal firehose﻿Implementation GuidePhase 1: PDF Ingestion & ParsingConnect to the CCMS API endpoint delivering new judgment PDFs; poll every 15 minutesClassify each PDF: digital text (direct extraction) vs. scanned/mixed (OCR required)For digital PDFs: use pdfplumber for layout-aware extraction preserving paragraph structure and section headersFor scanned/mixed PDFs: apply Sarvam Document Intelligence API for OCR with layout analysis; preserve bounding box coordinates per text block for source highlightingChunk extracted text into sections (case header, background, findings, directions, order); store with original PDF, extracted text by section, bounding box coordinates, OCR confidence, and document quality scorePhase 2: LLM Extraction PipelineRun a three-pass structured extraction pipeline via Sarvam API (Sarvam-1):Pass 1 — Case Header: case number, court, bench, judgment date, petitioner and respondent names, case categoryPass 2 — Directive Extraction: each distinct directive as a structured object with directive text, direction type (comply/file report/pay compensation/appeal consideration/contempt notice), parties bound, and explicit or implicit timelinePass 3 — Action Plan Generation: for each directive — plain English action required, responsible department (from respondent name and department lookup), action type (Compliance / Appeal / Internal Escalation / No Action), and computed deadlineTag every extracted field with: LLM confidence score, source PDF passage, and bounding box reference for source highlightingPhase 3: Deadline Computation EngineImplement a legal deadline calculator:High Court judgment appeal to Supreme Court: 90 days from judgment date (Article 136)Contempt compliance orders: as specified, default 30 daysStatutory compliance directives: parse explicit dates; flag ambiguous references for manual entryLimitation period remaining: colour-coded as Green (> 60 days), Amber (30–60 days), Red (< 30 days), ExpiredClearly distinguish computed deadlines ("computed — 90-day default") from explicit deadlines ("stated in judgment")Phase 4: Human Verification Interface & Trusted DashboardReviewer Interface (React + FastAPI + PDF.js): side-by-side extracted fields and PDF viewer with source passages highlighted in yellow; per-field Approve / Edit / Reject with confidence score visible; escalation to senior reviewer for legal ambiguity; only judgments with all mandatory fields verified move to the trusted dashboardTrusted Dashboard: department-scoped view with action plan cards (directive summary, action type, deadline countdown, responsible officer, verification timestamp); Red/Amber/Green status; daily email digest with deadlines in the next 7 days; escalation push notification when < 7 days remain and action is not yet completed﻿Recommended Technology StackComponentTechnology / ToolPDF Ingestion & Parsingpdfplumber (digital PDFs) + Sarvam Document Intelligence API (scanned/mixed)LLM ExtractionSarvam API (Sarvam-1); Llama-3-8B via Ollama as on-premises fallbackDeadline ComputationPython date arithmetic engine (explicit legal period rules)Source HighlightingPDF.js with annotation overlayReviewer InterfaceReact + FastAPI + PDF.jsTrusted DashboardReact + FastAPI; department-scoped access controlSimilar Judgment LookupPostgreSQL full-text search + pgvectorNotificationsSMTP email digest + browser push notificationsDatabasePostgreSQL (case data, action plans, reviewer decisions, audit log)Document StorageMinIO (on-premises object storage for judgment PDFs)﻿Key Constraints & Risk MitigationsComplex and inconsistent legal PDFs — the extraction pipeline focuses on the "Directions" / "Order" section rather than full-document extraction; the reviewer sees the full PDF, not just extracted snippetsLow OCR confidence — blocks with OCR confidence < 0.7 are flagged in the reviewer interface; auto-populated values from low-confidence blocks are marked "Needs Verification"Hallucination on legal directives — Sarvam API extractions are always anchored to a source passage; the reviewer cannot approve a directive without a source referenceComputed vs. explicit deadlines — the system clearly distinguishes between computed deadlines (standard limitation periods) and deadlines explicitly stated in the judgment; reviewers treat computed deadlines as starting points, not legal factsHuman verification is mandatory and not bypassable — the API endpoint moving records from review queue to the dashboard requires a reviewer signature token; there is no bulk-approve without reviewDepartment responsibility mapping — assigning the correct department from a respondent name is error-prone for complex multi-party judgments; the reviewer always confirms the department assignment, which feeds back into the classification lookupData security — court judgments are public but action plans are government-sensitive; the system runs on-premises within Karnataka's network perimeter; no external API calls on judgment contentVolume surge handling — the ingestion pipeline queues judgments by judgment date (oldest deadline first) with a target SLA of < 2 hours from PDF arrival to reviewer queue entry

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


C Charan Tej U
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
NyayaSaar: AI-Powered Court Judgment to Action Intelligence System
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 07:43 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Government departments deal with a large number of court cases, and once a judgment is issued, officials must go through lengthy and complex legal documents to understand what actions need to be taken. These judgments are often unstructured, written in technical legal language, and require careful interpretation to identify key decisions such as whether to comply with the order, file an appeal, or act within specific deadlines. This manual process is time-consuming and can lead to delays, missed timelines, and inefficiencies in decision-making.
In Karnataka, this challenge becomes even more significant because while High Court judgments are usually in English, many district and trial court documents are in Kannada. This creates an additional barrier for centralized processing and analysis.
Our solution, NyayaSaar, is an AI-powered system designed to transform court judgment PDFs into clear, structured, and actionable insights. The system automatically extracts important information such as case details, key directions, involved parties, and timelines. It then generates an intelligent action plan, suggesting whether the department should comply with the judgment or consider filing an appeal, identifying responsible departments, and highlighting critical deadlines.
To address the multilingual challenge, the system includes a language detection and translation layer that enables it to process both English and Kannada documents. Kannada judgments are translated into English for accurate AI processing, while preserving the original text for reference, ensuring the system works effectively across all court levels.
To ensure reliability and trust, NyayaSaar incorporates a human-in-the-loop verification process, where legal officers can review, edit, and approve the AI-generated outputs with supporting highlights and confidence indicators. Only verified information is presented on a clean, role-based dashboard for decision-makers.
Additionally, the system includes features such as risk scoring to prioritize urgent cases, deadline alerts to prevent delays, and analytics to monitor performance across departments. By combining AI-driven intelligence with human oversight and multilingual support, NyayaSaar aims to significantly reduce manual effort, improve accuracy, and enhance the efficiency of government legal workflows.
