solutions.team_name: SWS-Sync — Bidirectional Interoperability Layer for SWS and Legacy Departments
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


A AGENTS.md
TEAM SUBMISSION [5]
SWS-Sync — Bidirectional Interoperability Layer for SWS and Legacy Departments
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 25, 2026 06:24 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
SWS-Sync is an interoperability layer that propagates service requests both ways between Karnataka's Single Window System and 40+ legacy department systems, without modifying either side. Each source system is fronted by a lightweight adapter that uses whatever change-detection surface is available — webhook, polling API, or snapshot-diff. All propagations flow as canonical events through a durable bus, keyed and ordered by UBID. A schema translator driven by declarative mapping files converts between canonical and target representations. Idempotency is enforced through deterministic event IDs. When two updates collide, a configurable conflict resolver applies a per-field policy — source priority, last-write-wins, or human escalation — and every decision is captured in an immutable audit log. Innovation:- Zero source-system modification with declarative-mapping onboarding — new departments are added by writing a mapping file, not by writing code; conflict policies are hot-reloadable per field, configurable by governance teams without redeployment.
Tech Stack:- Node.js / TypeScript, Kafka (or NATS JetStream), PostgreSQL, Temporal; self-hosted open-source LLM (Gemma / Llama-3) used only in offline admin tooling to propose schema mappings — no LLM in the runtime path

Impact & Usefulness:- Avoids the big-bang migration trap that has broken previous government rollouts; SWS becomes authoritative over time because it stays in sync, not because departments are forced to switch; training burden is one hour for IT operations, zero for officers and citizens.

More details doc:-https://drive.google.com/file/d/1BNaTq9VMFA00Mr-cXV01yk3nRr0-t2_Y/view?usp=drive_link

solutions.team_name: TenderLens — AI-Powered Tender Eligibility Analysis and Vendor Evaluation Engine for Government Procurement
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


H harshith.aluguri0807_4276
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
TenderLens — AI-Powered Tender Eligibility Analysis and Vendor Evaluation Engine for Government Procurement
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 25, 2026 07:16 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
The problem
Government procurement in India processes over ₹85,000 crore in tenders annually. Yet eligibility evaluation — the most critical step — remains entirely manual. Officers read through 80–150 page tender documents, manually extract eligibility criteria, and compare them against vendor submissions one by one. This process takes days per tender, is prone to inconsistency, and creates significant opacity that enables manipulation.The core technical challenge is not just reading documents — it is converting ambiguous legal language into executable logic. A sentence like the bidder must demonstrate a minimum average annual turnover of ₹5 crore over the last three financial years, with at least one completed project of similar nature exceeding ₹2 crore in value contains multiple nested conditions, implicit constraints, and domain-specific thresholds that no simple keyword extraction can reliably parse.Opens with the pain, quantifies it, then immediately signals technical understanding. This tells the technical jury: we know why this is hard.

""

Our solution — TenderLens
TenderLens is an end-to-end AI pipeline that ingests raw tender documents and vendor bids, extracts and structures eligibility criteria into machine-readable rules, and generates an explainable, auditable vendor scorecard — reducing evaluation time from days to minutes.

Technical architecture
Layer 1 — Intelligent document ingestion: Multi-format ingestion supporting scanned PDFs, digital PDFs, and Word files. Tesseract OCR with preprocessing (deskew, denoising) handles low-quality government scans. LayoutLM identifies document structure — sections, tables, clauses — preserving spatial context that pure text extraction loses.Layer 2 — Criteria extraction engine: A fine-tuned LLM (instruction-tuned on government tender corpora) extracts eligibility criteria with section-level provenance. Each extracted criterion carries a source reference — the exact page, section, and sentence it was drawn from — enabling full auditability.Layer 3 — Rule structuring engine (core innovation): Extracted natural language criteria are converted into structured, executable rule objects: {type, field, operator, value, unit, years, mandatory}. Compound conditions are decomposed. Ambiguous language triggers a confidence score; low-confidence rules are flagged for human review rather than silently auto-resolved. This transforms legal prose into logic that a machine can evaluate — and a human can verify.Layer 4 — Eligibility matching engine: Structured vendor attributes (financials, certifications, past project values, registration details) are matched against the rule set. Each criterion produces a pass, fail, or insufficient-evidence verdict with an explicit evidence reference from the vendors submission. A weighted scoring model aggregates results, with mandatory criteria treated as hard disqualifiers.Layer 5 — Officer dashboard: A procurement officer sees a per-vendor scorecard with colour-coded criterion verdicts, clickable source links into the original documents, and an override mechanism that requires a logged reason — preserving audit integrity. Reports are exportable for file noting and RTI compliance.The rule structuring engine section is the technical centrepiece — this is what no other team will have thought through at this depth.



'


Innovation over the sample idea
The provided sample idea covers criterion extraction. TenderLens goes three layers further: it structures criteria into executable rules, matches them against vendor data with evidence linking, and produces an auditable decision record. The explainability layer — where every verdict traces back to a specific line in the tender document — is the differentiator that makes this deployable in a government context, where decisions must withstand RTI scrutiny.

Real-world deployability
TenderLens is designed for plug-in deployment alongside GeM (Government e-Marketplace) and existing CRPF procurement workflows. It does not replace the procurement officer — it augments them. All AI verdicts are reviewable and overridable, satisfying due process requirements. The system works on existing tender document formats without requiring process changes from vendors or departments.

Scalability
The rule schema is domain-agnostic — the same pipeline applies to defence procurement, infrastructure tenders, and state-level GeM categories with minimal reconfiguration. As more tenders are processed, the system builds an institutional knowledge base of criteria patterns, improving extraction accuracy over time through human-in-the-loop feedback.

Tech stack
Tesseract OCR · LayoutLM · LLM (instruction-tuned, open-source) · spaCy NLP · FastAPI · React · PostgreSQL · Docker

solutions.solution_text:

C commitNpray
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
LexFlow — AI-Powered Court Judgment Intelligence System for Verified Government Action
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 25, 2026 07:58 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
The Problem
Every day, High Courts across India dispose of cases involving government departments. Each judgment may contain critical directives — compliance orders, appeal deadlines, departmental responsibilities — requiring immediate administrative response. Today these arrive as lengthy, complex PDFs in CCMS. Officials must manually read every document to identify what action is needed and by when. The result is delayed compliance, missed appeal windows, and court directives going unacted upon — not because officials are negligent, but because the system provides no intelligent support.

Our Solution — LexFlow
LexFlow is an end-to-end AI-powered court judgment intelligence system with five integrated stages:
Stage 1 — Automated PDF Ingestion Judgment PDFs are automatically fetched from CCMS via API the moment a case is disposed. The system handles both digital and scanned PDFs using OCR. Every sentence is stored with its exact page and line number for full source traceability.
Stage 2 — RAG + LLM Extraction A Retrieval-Augmented Generation pipeline chunks and embeds the judgment, retrieves the most legally relevant sections, and feeds them to an LLM. Extracted data includes: case number, parties involved, date of order, key directions, responsible department, compliance deadline, and appeal limitation period — output as structured JSON.
Stage 3 — AI Action Plan Generation Based on extracted facts, LexFlow generates a recommended action plan: comply or consider appeal, which authority must act, key timelines, and urgency level. Explicit deadlines are extracted directly; implicit ones are inferred from standard limitation periods.
Stage 4 — Blind Human Verification (Our Core Differentiator) Before any data reaches government officials, it is independently verified through a blind cross-check mechanism. Verified law students are shown the original PDF alongside five structured factual questions — without seeing the AI's extracted answers. They answer independently. The system then compares their responses against the AI's extraction.
If answers match → high confidence, case auto-approved ✅

If answers mismatch → AI may have hallucinated, case flagged for admin review ⚑

This is not a simple approve/reject button. Because the student never sees the AI's answer before submitting, their agreement constitutes genuine independent verification — not a rubber stamp. Every extracted fact is also linked to its source sentence in the original PDF, so reviewers can see exactly where each piece of information came from.
Stage 5 — Verified Government Dashboard Only verified, approved action plans appear here. Features include: deadline urgency indicators (Red < 7 days, Amber < 30 days, Green = safe), department-wise case view, one-click action assignment to officers, natural language case search, and auto-generated compliance reports for official meetings.

Why LexFlow is Different
Every other solution places a human reviewer in front of the AI's output and asks them to approve or reject it. The reviewer sees the AI's answer — so they are influenced by it, not independently checking it. LexFlow's verification is genuinely blind. The result is a system where confidence scores are earned, not assumed — and where AI hallucinations are caught before they reach government decision-makers.

Tech Stack
PDF Processing: pdfplumber + pytesseract (OCR)

AI Pipeline: RAG + LLM (Claude API)

Automation: n8n (CCMS API trigger, deadline alerts)

Backend: FastAPI + PostgreSQL

Frontend: React with PDF viewer and source highlighting

Project Description
AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis Platform



Overview
We propose an AI-driven platform that automates the evaluation of government tenders by intelligently analyzing both tender documents and bidder submissions. The system transforms a slow, manual, and inconsistent process into a fast, transparent, and explainable workflow.





Problem Statement



Government tender evaluation involves reviewing large volumes of documents with complex eligibility criteria. This process is:


Time-consuming and resource-intensive




Prone to human error and inconsistency




Difficult to audit and standardize



There is a need for a system that can understand unstructured documents, apply rules consistently, and generate explainable decisions.





Our Solution



Our platform performs three key functions:

1. Intelligent Tender Understanding




Automatically extracts eligibility criteria (technical, financial, compliance)




Identifies mandatory vs optional conditions




Structures criteria into machine-readable format








2. Bidder Document Analysis




Supports multiple formats: PDFs, scanned documents, images, Word files




Uses OCR and AI to extract relevant information such as:




Financial turnover




Certifications (e.g., GST, ISO)




Project experience








3. Automated Evaluation & Explainability




Matches extracted bidder data with tender criteria




Generates outcomes:




Eligible




Not Eligible




Needs Manual Review




Provides clear, criterion-level explanations with document references



Example: "Eligible: Turnover of ₹6 crore found in financial statement (Page 3)."


Key Features


End-to-end automation of tender evaluation

Explainable AI for every decision

Human-in-the-loop for ambiguous cases

Full audit trail for compliance and transparency

Handles unstructured and scanned documents




System Architecture


Document Upload Interface

OCR & Document Parsing Layer

NLP/LLM-based Criteria Extraction

Data Extraction & Normalization

Matching & Decision Engine

Explanation Generator

Reporting Dashboard




Technology Stack


Frontend: React

Backend: Python (FastAPI) / Java (Spring Boot)

AI/NLP: Large Language Models (LLMs)

OCR: Tesseract / EasyOCR

Database: MongoDB / PostgreSQL




Innovation & Impact


Reduces evaluation time from days to minutes

Ensures consistency across all bidders

Improves fairness and transparency in procurement

Enables audit-ready decision-making




Conclusion

This platform leverages AI, OCR, and explainable decision systems to modernize government procurement. It ensures faster, fairer, and more reliable tender evaluations while maintaining human oversight where needed.

AI-Powered Tender Eligibility Evaluator for CRPF Procurement
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 25, 2026 08:07 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF
The Procurement Process For Organizations Like The CRPF
The CRPF has to look through hundreds of documents from people who want to do business with them. They have to check these documents against the rules they have for the tender. This process is done by hand. It is
Very Time-Consuming: The people on the committee have to spend a lot of time checking the financial and technical parts of the documents.
Open To Mistakes: When people do this by hand they can make mistakes. Not see everything.
Not Clear: It is hard to know why someone was not chosen because it is all on paper.
The Solution To These Problems
Sentinel-Procure is a system that uses computers to help with the part of the procurement process. It looks at the rules in the tender document. Checks them against what the people who want to do business with the CRPF have submitted. It uses three computer programs to do this
It Looks At The Rules: It uses a type of computer program that can understand what people are saying to find out what is required and what is not.
It Looks At The Documents: It uses a program that can read documents even if they are not typed up.
It Explains What It Does: of just saying yes or no it tells you why it made that decision.
Important Things About Sentinel-Procure
It Does Not Disqualify People Without Telling Them: If a document is not clear it asks someone to look at it.
It Can Read Documents: It is good at reading documents that are not perfect.
It Makes Reports That Are Easy To Understand: It makes a report that the people in charge can look at and be sure everything is correct.
The Technical Part Of Sentinel-Procure
It Uses Python To Work
It Uses Special Programs For Artificial Intelligence: It uses PyTorch and other programs to understand what people are saying and to look at documents.
It Uses Special Programs To Read Documents: It uses Tesseract and other programs to read documents.
It Uses A Special Database: It uses a database that can search for things, in documents and another database that keeps track of everything.
The Good Things About Sentinel-Procure
The CRPF can use Sentinel-Procure to make the process of looking at documents faster. It can also make sure that everyone is treated fairly and that all the decisions can be looked at later.

Project Description
NYAYATRACK — AI-Powered Court Judgment Intelligence System with Verified Action Planning Proposal Link :- Link ? ━━━ PROBLEM ━━━ Karnataka government departments receive hundreds of court judgment PDFs via CCMS every year. Each is lengthy, scanned, and written in dense legal language. Hidden inside are critical directives — comply, appeal within 90 days, or face contempt. Today, officers read entire documents manually, track deadlines on personal spreadsheets, and leave no audit trail. Result: missed deadlines, lapsed appeal windows, and contempt notices against government departments. This is not a technology problem. It is a workflow problem technology has never solved. ━━━ WHY CURRENT SYSTEMS FAIL ━━━ CCMS fetches PDFs and stops. What follows is entirely manual:
Officers read 30–80 pages to find 3–4 actionable sentences

No system extracts, structures, or prioritises court directions

Appeal limitation periods (30–90 days) are frequently missed

No audit trail of who read what, decided what, or when

Department heads have zero visibility into compliance status

Case number, court, date of order

Parties involved and responsible department

Key directions verbatim (not paraphrased)

Explicit timelines and deadlines

Nature of action: Compliance or Appeal

Appeal limitation period Each field gets a confidence score: High / Medium / Low.

Left: Original PDF with the exact source sentence highlighted in yellow

Right: Extracted field with confidence score Officer can Approve, Edit, or Reject each field. Only approved fields move forward. Full audit trail: who verified, when, what was changed.

Action required: Comply / Seek legal opinion / File appeal

Responsible officer and department

Deadline with days-remaining counter

Comply vs Appeal recommendation with plain-English reasoning

Deadline proximity

Department compliance history

Judgment severity classification

Prior extensions or adjournments

Deadline urgency sorted by Contempt Risk Score

Case status: Pending → Verified → Assigned → Completed

Full audit trail per case

PDF export for official records

Officer: 2–3 hours → under 15 minutes per judgment

Department head: Real-time compliance visibility, no surprise deadlines

Legal cell: Human-verified outputs only, AI never acts autonomously

Karnataka: Measurable reduction in contempt proceedings with auditable trail

Replicable to all 28 state High Courts with CCMS or equivalent

Expandable to District Courts and Tribunals

Language-agnostic: Kannada, Hindi, English

Contempt Risk engine improves with more data

Scale: 25 state governments | 650+ district courts | 50,000+ departments

━━━ OUR SOLUTION — NyayaTrack ━━━NyayaTrack is not an AI summarizer. It is a complete government workflow system — from judgment receipt to verified action assignment and deadline tracking.Core Principle: No AI output reaches an officer's dashboard without explicit human verification.━━━ HOW IT WORKS ━━━Step 1 — Judgment IngestionPDFs arrive via CCMS API. Scanned judgments go through an OCR pipeline (Tesseract/Google Vision) before processing — handling real Karnataka HC documents, not just clean PDFs.Step 2 — Structured AI ExtractionA structured extraction engine (not summarization) identifies specific fields:Step 3 — Human-in-the-Loop Verification (Core Differentiator)Side-by-side interface:Step 4 — AI Action Plan GenerationVerified data generates:Step 5 — Contempt Risk Score [UNIQUE INNOVATION]For each judgment, NyayaTrack calculates a Contempt Risk Score (0–100) based on:Score above 70 = red alert + automated officer reminder.This answers the question every IAS officer needs answered: "Which of my 200 pending court orders will cause a contempt notice this month?"Step 6 — Verified DashboardDepartment-wise view with:━━━ GOVERNMENT IMPACT ━━━━━━ FEASIBILITY ━━━Phase 1 : Working prototype — OCR, extraction, verification UI, Contempt Risk Score, dashboard. Demonstrated on real Karnataka HC judgment samples.Phase 2 : CCMS API integration. Pilot with 3 departments.Phase 3 : Full Karnataka rollout. Automated alerts. Audit trail reportable to High Court.Tech Stack: Python (FastAPI) + React + PostgreSQL + Tesseract/Google Vision + Claude/GPT-4 APIInfrastructure: NIC Cloud compatible. Full data residency within government. No vendor lock-in.━━━ SCALABILITY ━━━ ━━━ WHY THIS WINS ━━━ Most teams: PDF → GPT → text summary → call it done. NyayaTrack: 5 distinct components — OCR pipeline + structured extraction + source-highlighted verification UI + Contempt Risk Score engine + verified dashboard. The Contempt Risk Score is not in the problem statement. We identified it as the missing feature government departments need most. IAS officers who have received a contempt notice will recognise its value immediately. Full proposal document with architecture, workflow diagrams, and deployment plan attached. NyayaTrack is not a prototype. It is ready to pilot.

PROJECT DRISHTI - JudgeAI Intelligence Layer: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 25, 2026 08:18 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
IDEATION DOCUMENT LINK - STATISTICS, VISUALISATIONS, TABLES, DESCRIPTION
Overview:

Project Drishti is an AI-driven system designed to bridge the critical gap between court judgments and their real-world implementation. Government departments currently receive court judgments in PDF format through systems like CCMS (Court Case Monitoring System), but these documents are lengthy, complex, and require manual interpretation. As a result, key directives are often missed, deadlines are overlooked, and compliance is delayed - leading to legal consequences such as contempt notices. Drishti addresses this systemic inefficiency by transforming unstructured legal documents into structured, actionable plans within a reliable and verifiable workflow. It ensures that no critical directive goes unnoticed and that every action is tracked, verified, and executed in a timely manner.
Core Problem Being Solved:

The existing workflow is entirely manual and inefficient. Officials must read through extensive legal PDFs, identify key instructions, determine responsibilities, and track deadlines manually. This leads to:
Delayed interpretation (8–10 days per judgment)

High error rates in deadline tracking (32%)

Lack of accountability across departments

Absence of real-time monitoring and escalation systems

The consequence is a nationwide backlog of execution petitions and increasing contempt cases due to non-compliance.
Real Case Studies:

Case Study 1 - BBMP Palace Grounds TDR Case (10 Years of Non-Compliance)

Supreme Court ordered issuance of TDR certificates in 2014, but the directive was not properly acted upon for nearly a decade.

Lack of clear responsibility and no structured tracking led to delays and suppression of key financial information (₹1,396 crore liability).

In 2024, the Supreme Court held Karnataka authorities guilty of wilful disobedience and imposed penalties.

Drishti would have extracted the directive early, assigned responsibility, inferred deadlines, and triggered escalation alerts—preventing long-term non-compliance.

Case Study 2 - Karnataka High Court Suo Motu (44 Departments, Systemic Failure)

The High Court observed widespread non-compliance across 44 government bodies due to failure in reading and acting on court orders.

There was no system to extract, assign, or track actions from judgment PDFs, leading to a “casual and insensitive” approach toward compliance.

The court mandated creation of compliance cells in each department for time-bound implementation.

Drishti directly fulfills this requirement by acting as an automated compliance system across all departments simultaneously.

Proposed Solution:

Drishti introduces a four-stage AI pipeline that automates the journey from judgment receipt to compliance tracking, while ensuring human oversight at critical points.
Sub-Stage
Technology

PDF Type Detection

1. Intelligent Extraction Layer

Once a judgment PDF is received, Drishti activates a multi-modal AI engine that processes both text-based and scanned documents. Using OCR and Natural Language Processing, the system extracts:
Case metadata (case number, court, date)

Parties involved

Legal directives and obligations

Deadlines (explicit and inferred)

Relevant statutes and legal context Each extracted element is linked back to its exact location in the source PDF, ensuring traceability and explainability.

2. Action Plan Generation Engine

The extracted information is transformed into a structured administrative action plan. This step converts complex legal language into clear, executable tasks. Each action plan includes:
Required action (compliance or appeal)

Responsible department and officers

Deadlines and escalation timelines

Decision type (mandatory or discretionary)

Appeal windows

Contempt Risk Score (CRS)

3. Human Verification Layer (Critical Control Point)

To ensure reliability and eliminate AI risks, Drishti incorporates a mandatory human-in-the-loop verification stage. Key features include:
Split-screen interface with PDF and extracted data

Confidence scoring for each extracted item

Options to Approve, Edit, or Reject outputs

Mandatory digital signature by authorized officers

Full audit trail of all actions

4. Real-Time Compliance Dashboard

Only verified action plans are displayed in a centralized dashboard designed for decision-makers. The dashboard provides:
Department-wise case tracking

Deadline-based alerts (T-7, T-3, T-1 days)

Risk categorization (Low, Medium, High, Critical)

Real-time compliance status

Calendar view of upcoming actions

Automated escalation mechanisms

Key Innovations:

Drishti is not just a document processing tool; it is a complete decision-support system with several unique innovations:
Contempt Risk Score (CRS) for proactive legal risk prevention

End-to-end automation with mandatory human validation

Source-linked explainability for full transparency

Cross-department intelligence through pattern detection

Multilingual support for regional legal systems

System Architecture:

Drishti is deployed entirely within a secure government infrastructure (State Data Centre), ensuring data privacy and compliance. The architecture includes:
PDF processing engines

OCR modules for multilingual extraction

NLP models for legal understanding

Large Language Models for reasoning

Backend APIs for real-time processing

Frontend dashboards for interaction

Secure databases for audit and storage

Impact and Benefits:

Drishti significantly improves efficiency, accuracy, and accountability in judicial compliance:
Reduces judgment processing time from days to hours

Minimizes human error in interpreting directives

Ensures timely compliance and reduces contempt cases

Provides real-time visibility for decision-makers

Saves administrative and legal costs

Conclusion:

Project Drishti transforms the way governments interact with judicial directives by converting passive document storage into active decision intelligence. It replaces a fragmented manual workflow with a structured, accountable, and technology-driven system. By combining AI automation with human validation, Drishti ensures both efficiency and reliability - enabling faster compliance, reduced legal risk, and improved governance.

I insharajranawat06_3e61
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
TenderLens : AI-Powered Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis Platform
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 25, 2026 08:32 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Description:
Government procurement in organizations like CRPF is not just slow, it is legally vulnerable. When a disqualified vendor challenges a decision in court or a CAG audit questions a shortlist, the evaluation committee often cannot produce a clear reason tied to a specific line in a document. TenderLens solves this by turning every procurement evaluation into a transparent, evidence-backed, and auditable process that an officer can confidently sign off on.
TenderLens works in three stages. First, the officer uploads the tender document. The platform reads it section by section and extracts every eligibility rule, including financial thresholds, technical requirements, certification conditions, and compliance mandates, converting them into a structured checklist with mandatory and optional flags clearly separated. Before evaluation begins, the officer reviews and confirms this checklist on an editable screen, correcting anything missed. This input-stage verification prevents the critical failure of evaluating against incorrect criteria.
Second, the officer uploads bidder submissions. The system supports all document formats, including PDFs, Word files, scanned certificates, and photographs. Scanned files go through preprocessing to correct tilt, enhance contrast, and remove noise before text extraction. Each extraction is assigned a confidence score, and low-confidence results are flagged for human review with the original image shown. The system does not proceed on uncertain data.
Third, the evaluation engine compares each bidder against each criterion and produces one of three outcomes: Eligible with exact supporting proof, Not Eligible with specific missing or failing evidence, or Needs Human Review with a clear explanation. Ambiguous cases such as borderline values, unreadable expiry dates, conflicting data, or unclear wording are sent to a review queue where the officer makes the final decision with a mandatory note. No decision is made silently.
All decisions, automated or manual, are recorded in an append-only audit log capturing the criterion, document source, extracted value, confidence score, and timestamp. Sensitive information such as company names, PAN, and GST details is anonymized locally before processing, ensuring that raw data does not leave the system environment. The final output is a formal evaluation report exported as a signed PDF with a complete audit trail, ready for audit or legal use.
TenderLens is built using FastAPI and React, with document parsing and text extraction pipelines, OCR supported by preprocessing techniques, and PostgreSQL for audit storage. The Round 2 plan spans four weeks: tender parsing and criteria extraction, document ingestion pipeline, evaluation engine with review queue, and audit report generation tested on synthetic CRPF bidder data.

The platform then provides a centralized dashboard where decision makers can monitor actions, track compliance status, and identify risks such as missed deadlines or pending appeals. In essence, NyayaSarthi bridges the critical gap between judgment and execution. N NeuralClause
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
JusticeTrace — AI Pipeline for Court Judgment Intelligence, Directive Extraction & Compliance Tracking
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 25, 2026 09:41 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
JusticeTrace. Bridging the gap between court orders and government actions.
THE PROBLEM India's courts deliver thousands of judgments each day. And with each judgment, there can be a dozen directives hiding within 100 to 200 pages of complex legal documentation. What happens next is flawed – officers manually identify the directives, incorrectly interpret them, and do not track them at all. There is currently no way to map a court order to verified government action. This results in an inability to monitor compliance – by courts, citizens and relevant government departments alike.
WHAT JUSTICE TRACE DOES JusticeTrace is an end-to-end AI-driven workflow, which accepts raw judgments from the court, identifies all the obligations within, maps it to a responsible government department, creates an automated action plan based on it, and tracks compliance – all of that, while not changing a single step in a judicial process.
HOW IT WORKS
Ingestion Processes both scanned and digital PDFs in HC format, powered by Tesseract OCR and LayoutLM. Processes non-clean documents, not just the perfect ones.
Directive Extraction Instructional LLM detects all directives and parses them into individual action elements: action, responsible party, deadline, condition, source paragraph, confidence level. Multiple directives are parsed separately. Extractions with low confidence levels are reviewed by human operators and validated before entering pipeline.
Department Mapping All the responsible parties are mapped to the corresponding departments of the Government of India.
Action Plan Generation Automatically generated for each department, formatted as typed tasks with deadlines and required compliance evidence. Machine-readable, not free-form.
Compliance Dashboard Officers can provide the required evidence for directives; overdue directives automatically generate alerts. Judges have a read-only verified compliance dashboard for their cases – for the first time ever.
DEPLOYABLE ANYWHERE Can easily integrate into any judicial process, anywhere.
SCALABILITY Works for any court in India regardless of its jurisdiction or language, with only one schema to cover all of India's 25 HC and Supreme Court.
TECH STACK Technology stack could be adjusted if some tools do not fit: Tesseract OCR, LayoutLM, spaCy, Mistral, FastAPI, PostgreSQL, React, Docker.
NJDG monitors case disposal. No tool monitors compliance after disposal. JusticeTrace does precisely that.

We've seen firsthand how compliance deadlines get missed - not because officers are careless, but because the system hands them a 90-page PDF and no guidance on what to do next. When a judgment lands in CCMS, CaseAlert reads it and turns it into a structured action card - telling the officer: this case needs a compliance report by May 2nd, Revenue department is responsible, appeal window closes in 11 days. What makes us different from every other team: Most will build a PDF summarizer and call it done. We didn't. It learns your workload: Every officer correction feeds back into model retraining. CaseAlert gets sharper specifically for your department's judgment types over time. The dashboard shows only verified records. Nothing AI-generated reaches a decision-maker without human sign-off. That's not a limitation - that's the design. Built to plug directly into existing CCMS infrastructure. No new login, no parallel system.

solutions.solution_text:

J jurix.ai
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
Jurix.ai - From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 25, 2026 09:46 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
" jurix.ai converts court judgment PDFs into clear, structured, and verified action plans for government officials."
The system: - Extracts key information from the judgment - Generates a practical action plan - Allows human verification before final use - Displays only trusted outputs in a simple dashboard
The focus is on helping decisions, not automating them blindly.
Key Features:1. Structured Extraction - Case details, parties, orders, and timelines from PDFs
2. Action Plan Generation - What needs to be done (comply or appeal) - Responsible department - Relevant deadlines
3. Human-in-the-Loop Verification - Review, edit, or reject AI outputs - Ensures accuracy and trust
4. Explainable Outputs - Link extracted data to the source text - Basic confidence indicators
5. Clean Dashboard - Only approved cases - Clear view of actions and deadlines

6. Multilingual Support
- Instantly translates verified action plans into regional languages (e.g., Hindi, Kannada) - Ensures local clerks can execute compliance directives accurately in their native language

Impact:
Reduces time spent reading lengthy judgments

Helps officials quickly understand required actions

Minimizes risk of missing deadlines or directives

Improves consistency and accountability in decision-making

Breaks language barriers across state and regional departments


Under the Hood: How Jurix Actually Works:
We all know what Indian court judgments look like: 50 pages of dense legalese and historical case references, just to deliver a two-paragraph order on page 49. Feeding that directly into an AI is a recipe for hallucinations.
To prevent that, Jurix uses a multi-stage, self-checking pipeline:
Cutting the Noise (Fact Isolation): Before anything else, our first AI agent strips away the historical fluff. It extracts only the hard facts and the final directives, and—crucially—maps them to the exact visual coordinates on the PDF so human reviewers can instantly trace the source.

The Action Mapper (Sandboxed Synthesis): Our second agent acts like an experienced section officer. It takes only those isolated facts and figures out the logistics: Who needs to act on this? Is it the Revenue Department? PWD? And what is the legal deadline?

The Double-Check (Adversarial Auditor): You cannot blindly trust generative AI in government work. So, our third agent acts as a strict supervisor. Its only job is to cross-check the Action Mapper’s plan against the raw PDF to catch logic gaps or hallucinated deadlines before the data ever reaches the human dashboard.

The Last Mile (Regional Translation): High Courts operate in English, but ground-level execution happens in Marathi or Hindi. Once a human officer approves the action plan, our translation microservice instantly localizes it so local clerks can execute the orders without a language barrier.

solutions.team_name: An AI-Driven Procurement Evaluation System
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


H harshitajuneja23444_c864
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
An AI-Driven Procurement Evaluation System
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 25, 2026 09:47 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
An AI-Driven Procurement Evaluation System
Solution Proposal for AI Bharath Hackathon Theme 3
CRPF Government Procurement Automation
1. Understanding the Problem

Government procurement in India, particularly in security organisations like CRPF, involves an enormous amount of paperwork and legal precision. A single tender for construction, equipment, or services can generate hundreds of pages of eligibility conditions, while each of the responding vendors adds their own pile of financial statements, registration certificates, experience letters, and scanned copies of decade-old documents. The committee assigned to evaluate these submissions is expected to cross-check every criterion against every bidder, systematically and without bias, while also being fast enough to not delay procurement timelines. In practice, this rarely happens cleanly.
The real problem is not just about automation. It is about trust. Any system that replaces or assists a government officer must be explainable, auditable, and conservative. It cannot silently reject a bidder because an OCR engine failed to read a number correctly on a scanned balance sheet. It cannot produce a verdict that an officer cannot trace back to a specific document and a specific line. And it must never give the appearance of being a black box. Our solution, TenderMind, is designed with this understanding at its core.
2. The Core Idea: A Three-Layer Reasoning Engine

TenderMind is built around three distinct reasoning layers that operate in sequence. Each layer has a clearly defined job, and the output of each layer is logged independently so the audit trail is complete.
Layer 1 Criterion Extraction from the Tender Document

When a tender document is uploaded, TenderMind runs it through a large language model with a structured extraction prompt. The model is instructed to read the tender and produce a machine-readable list of criteria. Each criterion is categorised as financial, technical, compliance, or certification. For each criterion, the model is asked to identify whether the language is mandatory (indicated by words like "shall", "must", "required") or optional (indicated by "preferred", "desirable", "where applicable"). The model also extracts the type of evidence the bidder must produce for instance, an audited balance sheet for a turnover threshold, or a copy of an ISO certificate for a quality requirement.
What makes this step unique in our approach is that the extracted criteria list is immediately shown to the procurement officer for review and confirmation before any bidder evaluation begins. This is not just a courtesy it makes the officer a co-author of the evaluation logic, which is important for legal defensibility. If the officer believes a criterion has been missed or mischaracterised, they can correct it at this stage. The confirmed criteria list becomes the locked evaluation rubric for that tender.
Layer 2 Document Parsing with Confidence Scoring

Each bidder's submission is processed through a multi-modal document pipeline. Typed PDFs and Word files are parsed directly for text. Scanned documents and photographs are first processed through an OCR engine we propose using Azure Document Intelligence or AWS Textract, both of which handle government-style documents reasonably well. Tables within documents are extracted using structure-aware parsing.
Crucially, every piece of extracted text comes with a confidence score. An OCR engine reading a clean typed document will produce near-perfect confidence. A handwritten figure on a scanned certificate might produce 60 or 70 percent confidence. This score is not hidden internally it travels with the extracted value all the way to the final report. The system is designed so that if the confidence on any value relevant to a mandatory criterion falls below a defined threshold, that criterion is automatically marked for human review rather than being evaluated algorithmically. This is the single most important design decision in TenderMind: the system never pretends to know something it does not know with sufficient confidence.
Layer 3 Criterion Matching and Verdict Generation

Once the bidder's documents have been parsed and values extracted, TenderMind matches each extracted value against the corresponding criterion. Financial thresholds are checked numerically. Certification validity is checked against dates. Project counts are verified against experience letters. The matching is done criterion by criterion, and every match produces a structured output containing four pieces of information: which criterion was being checked, which document was used, what value was found in that document, and what verdict was reached. There is no verdict without all four of these fields being present.
To handle the real-world variation in how different companies describe the same thing, TenderMind uses semantic similarity matching. "Annual Turnover", "Yearly Revenue", and "T/O as per books" all mean the same thing and are treated as such. The underlying embedding model computes closeness of meaning rather than requiring exact keyword matches. This significantly reduces false negatives caused by language variation across bidders.
3. What Makes TenderMind Different

Most AI systems built for document evaluation focus on accuracy of extraction. TenderMind adds three design principles that make it suitable specifically for formal government use.
Conservative bias: When in doubt, the system flags for review. It never auto-rejects based on low-confidence reads. A bidder who is genuinely ineligible can still be manually confirmed as such. A bidder who is genuinely eligible must never be silently disqualified by a parsing error.

Immutable audit log: Every step criteria extraction, document parsing, value extraction, criterion matching is logged with timestamps, model version numbers, and confidence scores. This log is write-once. No verdict can be changed without the change being recorded. This is essential for procurement decisions that may be legally challenged.

Officer-in-the-loop design: TenderMind is not intended to replace the procurement committee. It is intended to prepare the committee's work. The final report it produces is structured as a draft that an officer reviews, annotates, and signs off. For flagged cases, the officer sees exactly why the system was uncertain and makes the call themselves.

4. Architecture and Technology Choices

The backend is built in Python using FastAPI. Document storage and the audit log live in PostgreSQL, with raw files in object storage. The OCR pipeline uses Azure Document Intelligence for structured extraction from scanned documents. The LLM used for criterion extraction and criterion matching is a large instruction-following model such as Claude or GPT-4o, accessed via API with carefully designed prompts that enforce structured JSON output. The embedding model for semantic similarity is a sentence-level transformer. The frontend is a simple web dashboard where the procurement officer uploads documents, reviews extracted criteria, monitors evaluation progress, handles the manual review queue, and exports the final signed report.
For deployment in a government context, the system can be hosted within a private cloud or on-premise infrastructure, ensuring that sensitive bid documents never leave the organisation's network. The LLM calls are made to on-premise or enterprise-tier endpoints with data retention disabled.
5. Key Risks and How We Handle Them

The most significant risk in this system is the LLM producing a plausible but incorrect extraction what is commonly called a hallucination. We handle this by grounding every LLM output in extracted source text. The model is never asked to reason from memory; it is always given the actual document text and asked to find values within it. Every extracted value is quoted back to a specific page and paragraph. If the model cannot find a value in the text it was given, it must say so rather than invent one.
A second risk is OCR failure on poor-quality scanned documents. Our mitigation is the confidence scoring system described earlier. A third risk is criteria ambiguity where the tender document itself is not clearly written and a criterion could be interpreted in more than one way. TenderMind surfaces these ambiguities to the procurement officer during the criteria review stage, before any bidder evaluation begins, so that the officer can clarify the intended interpretation.
6. Round 2 Implementation Plan

In Round 2, given a sandbox with sample tender and bidder documents, the first step would be to run the tender document through the criteria extraction pipeline and manually validate the output against the actual criteria listed in the document. This gives a baseline accuracy figure. The second step would be to process each bidder submission through the document parsing pipeline and check whether the values relevant to each criterion are being extracted correctly. The third step would be to run the full evaluation and check that verdicts for clearly eligible and clearly ineligible bidders are correct, and that ambiguous cases are properly flagged.
The deliverable at the end of Round 2 would be a working prototype that accepts a tender document and a set of bidder submissions, produces a criterion-level evaluation report with source references and confidence scores, flags ambiguous cases for review, and exports a final PDF report ready for officer sign-off. We believe this is achievable within the round timeline given the modular architecture described above.

S Sketch
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
TenderEye: An Auditable, On-Premise AI Evaluation Engine for Government Tenders
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 25, 2026 10:01 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
The solution Our workflow is built on strict governance and auditability: Standardized Ingestion: Blurry scans and nested tables are converted to text blocks with exact coordinates and OCR confidence scores. Locked Rules: An authorized officer must review, edit and mathematically lock the tender base criteria before any bidder is evaluated. Targeted Matchers: we use deterministic code for rigid rule (e.g. GSTIN checksum validation) and isolated LLM reasoning for subjective ones (e.g. matching “similar projects”).
The Core Edge Our primary differentiator is our 3-state verdict (eligible, not eligible, needs review) paired with total visual traceability. In government procurement, silent AI features are catastrophic. If OCR confidence drops below 75% or legal language ambiguous the system refuses to guess and flags needs review. Every decision is backed by an immutable hand-chained audit log in the Ui clicking a verdict opens the original source document highlighting the exact evidence in a yellow bounding box. By radically shrinking the scope to execute four criteria perfectly, we prioritize auditable precision over generic AI hype.

Project DRISHTI — Document Review and Intelligent Screening for Tender Inspection — is an AI-powered procurement evaluation platform designed for government tendering processes in India.
At its core, DRISHTI addresses a critical gap: the current tender evaluation process is slow, inconsistent, and vulnerable to errors and fraud — yet it carries serious legal and regulatory consequences. DRISHTI automates the heavy lifting of document reading, criterion matching, and cross-document verification, while keeping a human officer firmly in the decision seat.

Automated tender parsing that generates a structured, machine-readable evaluation schema from any tender document

Smart criterion matching across financial, technical, compliance, and documentary requirements

An immutable, cryptographically chained audit trail for full accountability and legal defensibility

Regulatory intelligence built in — covering GFR 2017, MSME provisions, DPIIT orders, and more

The system is designed around one guiding principle: the AI reads, the officer decides — every recommendation is transparent, traceable, and overridable.

The Solution
Veritas-Legis is a secure, Full-Stack platform designed to bridge the gap between the courtroom and the community. By combining Natural Language Processing (NLP) with Zero-Trust Security principles, the platform ensures that every legal mandate is accurately translated into a task and verified with immutable proof. AI Mandate Extraction: Uses LLMs to ingest complex PDF judgments and automatically extract the Operative Portion, identifying the specific "Who," "What," and "When" of the court's order.

ABIL – Agent Based Interoperability Layer
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 25, 2026 10:15 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Overview Government systems in Karnataka operate in silos, where the Single Window System (SWS) and 40+ department systems independently manage business data. This leads to inconsistencies, duplicate records, and lack of a unified view.
ABIL (Agent-Based Interoperability Layer) is a middleware solution that enables bidirectional synchronization between SWS and legacy systems without modifying existing infrastructure, ensuring consistency, auditability, and reliability.
Problem Understanding This is a distributed system consistency problem caused by:
Multiple authoritative systems (SWS and department systems)

Lack of real-time communication

Schema and API heterogeneity

No reliable synchronization mechanism

Inability to modify existing systems

Resulting in:
Data inconsistencies

Conflicting updates

Lack of auditability

Proposed Solution: ABIL ABIL introduces a modular agent-based middleware layer that:
Detects changes across systems

Translates heterogeneous data formats

Synchronizes updates in both directions

Resolves conflicts using defined policies

Maintains a complete audit trail

System Architecture
Listener Agents: Detect changes via APIs or polling

Translation Agents: Map schemas across systems

Sync Agents: Handle SWS ↔ Department updates

Conflict Resolution Agent: Resolve simultaneous updates (timestamp/source-based)

Audit Agent: Log all operations for traceability

Key Features
Bidirectional synchronization

No modification of existing systems

Schema translation across APIs

Conflict detection and resolution

Idempotent and fault-tolerant processing

End-to-end auditability

Technical Approach
Backend: Node.js (microservices architecture)

Communication: Event-driven (Kafka or queue simulation)

Integration: REST APIs

Storage: MongoDB / PostgreSQL

Monitoring: Dashboard for audit and tracking

Impact
Eliminates data inconsistencies across systems

Improves transparency and trust

Enables reliable decision-making

Scales across departments and future integrations

Feasibility
Middleware-only approach (no system modification)

Works with synthetic/sandbox data

Fully compliant with constraints

Future Scope
Integration with UBID system

AI-based schema mapping and anomaly detection

Production-grade real-time event streaming

TenderGraph Zero-Hallucination, Evidence-Anchored Evaluation Engine for Government Procurement
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 25, 2026 10:41 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Evaluating government tenders is a legally critical, manual process. Standard AI solutions fail here because they hallucinate decisions and violate data privacy by sending sensitive PII to hosted cloud APIs. TenderGraph solves this by introducing a Two-Tier, privacy-first architecture. First, Local Vision-Language Models (VLMs) securely extract data from messy bidder documents (scanned certificates, photos) within an air-gapped sandbox. Second, a deterministic logic engine mathematically evaluates the extracted data against the tender's rules, guaranteeing zero hallucination. To ensure 100% auditability, TenderGraph features a "Sniper-Highlight UI" that draws visual bounding boxes directly on the original document, proving exactly where the data was found. Ambiguous documents are never silently rejected; they are safely routed to a Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) review dashboard, making the procurement process fast, explainable, and legally defensible.

TEAM SUBMISSION [10]
InteropSync Karnataka — Two-Way SWS ↔ Department Middleware
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 25, 2026 11:03 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Problem. Karnataka's Single Window System (SWS) is the front door for new business registrations, but 40+ legacy department systems remain authoritative for businesses already on their books. Today, an address change in SWS doesn't reach Labour, Tax, or KSPCB. A change inside a department never reaches SWS. The two sides drift apart silently. Solution. InteropSync is a non-invasive bidirectional middleware:
Push engine — propagates SWS changes to all affected departments in seconds

Pull engine — webhooks where supported, snapshot-diff polling everywhere else

Schema translation — JSON-config-driven; adding a new department is config, not code

Conflict detection — side-by-side diff with configurable resolution policy (last-writer-wins / source-wins / human review)

Idempotency via payload-hash deduplication, at-least-once delivery, exponential-backoff retries

Immutable audit log — every propagation is an event-sourced record

Builds on the UBID identifier shipped in our Theme 1 submission as the common join key. Neither SWS nor any department is ever modified. Demo. 27 sync events, 6 detected conflicts (status mismatches, address mismatches), live conflict-resolution UI with side-by-side diff, full audit trail. Video walkthrough (5 min):https://github.com/sridhar7601/interopsync-karnataka/blob/main/demo/video/demo.mp4 GitHub:https://github.com/sridhar7601/interopsync-karnatakaStack. FastAPI · SQLAlchemy · APScheduler · React · TypeScript · Tailwind · event-sourced architecture Deployment. Two services (sync-engine + poll-worker) on a single VM. No production data leaves the perimeter. Pilot scope: 3 departments + Karnataka SWS, 90 days. Year-one cost ~₹35L — far cheaper than retroactively rebuilding any source system.

T Team Duo
TEAM SUBMISSION [10]
TenderEval AI — AI-Powered Tender Evaluation for Government Procurement
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 25, 2026 11:04 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Problem. CRPF and other government bodies issue thousands of tenders a year. Each has detailed eligibility criteria — turnover thresholds, similar-work experience, GST registration, ISO certifications, EMD requirements. Bidder submissions arrive as typed PDFs, scanned copies, Word files, even photographs. A committee spends days cross-checking hundreds of pages, and two evaluators often reach different conclusions from the same documents. Solution. TenderEval AI is a three-stage explainable pipeline:
Extract — Docling parses the tender PDF; Claude API extracts structured eligibility criteria with verbatim quotes, page references, and mandatory/optional flags

Parse — every bidder submission, regardless of format. Typed → Docling. Scans → PaddleOCR. Photos → both

Evaluate — Claude API renders one of three verdicts per (bidder × criterion) cell: Eligible · Not Eligible · Needs Review

Every cell is criterion-level explainable with reasoning + page-level source citation. Ambiguous cases (low-confidence OCR, unclear documents) are explicitly Needs Review — never silently disqualified. Audit-ready by construction; CVC-compliant. Demo. Real CRPF "Construction of Border Outpost" tender → 8 criteria extracted automatically (Annual Turnover ≥ ₹5Cr, 3 similar projects, GST, PAN, ISO 9001, EMD, Solvency, No Blacklisting). 3 bidders × 8 criteria = 24-cell evaluation matrix with click-through reasoning per cell. Video walkthrough (5 min):https://github.com/sridhar7601/tendereval-ai/blob/main/demo/video/demo.mp4 GitHub:https://github.com/sridhar7601/tendereval-aiStack. FastAPI · SQLAlchemy · Docling · PaddleOCR · Claude API (JSON-mode) · React · TypeScript · Tailwind Deployment. Single VM + LLM endpoint. CRPF can host on internal infrastructure, route LLM calls through Azure OpenAI for data sovereignty. Pilot: 1 CRPF division, 90 days, real tender data inside their sandbox. Year-one cost ~₹40L — saves committee weeks per tender.

TenderSense AI: Explainable Multi-Modal AI Platform for Automated Tender Evaluation & Eligibility Analysis in Government Procurement
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 25, 2026 11:08 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
? Overview
Government procurement processes, such as those conducted by CRPF, rely on detailed tender documents and complex bidder submissions. These documents are often unstructured, spread across multiple formats (PDFs, scanned copies, images), and require extensive manual evaluation.TenderSense AI is an end-to-end, explainable AI platform that automates tender evaluation while ensuring transparency, auditability, and human oversight. It is designed specifically for government environments where every decision must be legally defensible and traceable.



⚠️ Problem Statement
Current tender evaluation systems face critical challenges:


Manual evaluation takes days and is highly resource-intensive

Bidder documents are inconsistent and unstructured across formats

Evaluations are prone to human error and subjective interpretation

Lack of auditability creates risks during RTI and legal scrutiny

No standardized mechanism to ensure consistent eligibility assessment
This results in inefficiency, delays, and reduced trust in procurement outcomes.


? Key Insight
This is not just a document processing problem — it is a trust and governance problem.A viable solution must:



Provide criterion-level explainability

Maintain full audit trails

Ensure no bidder is silently rejected

Support human-in-the-loop decision-making


?️ Proposed Solution: TenderSense AI
TenderSense AI is a modular, AI-powered platform that:


Understands Tender Documents

Extracts financial, technical, and compliance criteria

Identifies mandatory vs optional requirements

Parses Bidder Submissions

Processes PDFs, scanned documents, images, and tables

Extracts structured data such as turnover, certifications, and project experience

Evaluates Eligibility

Matches extracted data against criteria using a hybrid AI + rule engine

Outputs: Eligible / Not Eligible / Needs Review

Generates Explainable Decisions

Provides justification for every verdict

Links each decision to source document, page, and extracted value

Supports Human Oversight

Flags low-confidence or ambiguous cases

Allows officers to review, override, and annotate decisions

Ensures Auditability

Maintains tamper-proof logs of all actions and decisions

Generates audit-ready reports for compliance and legal review


⚙️ Technical Approach

OCR & Document Processing: Tesseract, Google Vision API

Layout Understanding: LayoutLM for structured extraction

NLP & Semantic Parsing: LLMs (LLaMA/GPT), BERT-based NER

Table Extraction: Camelot / Tabula

Decision System: Hybrid rule engine + AI validation

Explainability Layer: Criterion-level reasoning with traceability

Architecture: Modular microservices (deployable on secure government infrastructure)


? Real-World Impact

Reduces evaluation time by 70–80%

Ensures consistent, unbiased decision-making

Improves transparency and accountability

Minimizes legal and compliance risks

Enables scalable adoption across ministries and public sector units


? Scalability
TenderSense AI can be extended beyond CRPF to:


Defence procurement systems

Railways and infrastructure tenders

State government procurement bodies

Integration with platforms like GeM


? Why It Stands Out

Fully explainable AI (no black-box decisions)

Handles multi-format, real-world documents

Designed for government compliance and audit needs

Incorporates human-in-the-loop governance

Ready for practical deployment, not just prototype demonstration


? Conclusion
TenderSense AI transforms procurement from a manual, error-prone process into an intelligent, transparent, and auditable system — ensuring that every decision is backed by data, logic, and trust.

From manual scrutiny to intelligent, explainable governance.

A avik.chaterjee_b281
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
AI-Powered End-to-End Tender Eligibility Evaluation for Government Procurement Developed for the Central Reserve Police Force (CRPF)
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 25, 2026 11:20 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
PRISM (Procurement Review & Intelligent Scoring Module) is a full-stack AI platform designed to modernize government tender evaluation. It transforms a slow, inconsistent, and error-prone manual process into a fast, transparent, and legally auditable system—while ensuring that human procurement officers retain final decision authority.
The platform ingests tender documents and bidder submissions in diverse formats, including PDFs, scanned images, Word files, and photographs. Using large language models (LLMs), PRISM extracts structured eligibility criteria from complex legal text, even when criteria are scattered across multiple sections such as eligibility clauses, annexures, and scope of work.
To ensure completeness and accuracy, PRISM employs a four-pass extraction strategy: (1) identifying relevant sections, (2) extracting structured criteria into a standardized schema, (3) validating and deduplicating criteria while detecting conflicts, and (4) enabling officer review to establish a legally approved ground truth.
Document processing is handled through a robust multi-stage pipeline. Files are first classified, then enhanced using image preprocessing techniques such as deskewing, contrast adjustment, and noise removal. A five-tier OCR system ensures high accuracy across document types, combining tools like PyMuPDF, Azure OCR, Tesseract, and GPT-4 Vision, with fallback to human review when confidence is low.
PRISM’s core innovation lies in its hybrid evaluation engine. Objective criteria—such as financial thresholds, certificate validity, and document presence—are evaluated using deterministic rule-based logic, ensuring full reproducibility. Subjective criteria—such as “similar project experience”—are assessed using LLM-based semantic reasoning supported by Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), which retrieves the most relevant evidence from bidder documents.
The system also handles real-world complexities including terminology variation, multi-document aggregation, borderline values, and genuine ambiguity. Cases with uncertainty are automatically routed to human reviewers, ensuring zero silent disqualifications.
Every decision in PRISM is fully explainable. Each verdict is linked to specific criteria, source documents, extracted values, and a step-by-step reasoning chain. A confidence-based routing system determines whether decisions are automated or require manual review.
For legal defensibility, PRISM maintains a tamper-proof, hash-chained audit log that records all actions, including AI outputs and human overrides. This ensures complete traceability for audits by authorities such as CAG, CVC, or courts.
By combining automation with accountability, PRISM delivers faster evaluations, consistent decision-making, and full transparency—setting a new standard for government procurement systems.

solutions.team_name: OmniBridge - An Event-Driven, Self-Healing Integration Fabric for SWS
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


A anshikasaini989_e213
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
OmniBridge - An Event-Driven, Self-Healing Integration Fabric for SWS
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 25, 2026 11:27 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
PPT link : https://canva.link/ad6mv8iurjxui93 Video Link : https://drive.google.com/file/d/1xkB1fW6s678r4aZI6xAhtj3iW5V6Gbad/view?usp=drive_link Website Link : https://omni-bridge-nine.vercel.app/ Github Repo : https://github.com/TheAnushree25/OmniBridge
OmniBridge – Core Idea

OmniBridge is an intelligent middleware layer between SWS (Single Window System) and 40+ legacy government systems. Instead of forcing legacy systems to change, OmniBridge adapts to them and keeps data synchronized in both directions.


1. Problem & Constraints

SWS A unified portal, but backed by 40+ independent systems with different schemas, tech levels, and unsynchronized clocks. These systems cannot be modified. Why migration fails Departments are independent. Coordinating schema changes is slow and risky. New systems keep getting added. UBID necessity Universal Business ID is required to map records across departments.


2. Bidirectional Sync Without Changes

Constraint No agents, no triggers, no code changes. Only APIs are used. SWS → Departments Webhook → Ingestion Gateway → YAML mapping → Saga dispatch with idempotency. Departments → SWS Webhooks for modern systems. Virtual CDC for legacy systems using polling + change detection.


3. Schema Mapping & Onboarding

YAML Mapper Defines field mappings in readable config files. No coding needed. New department onboarding AI generates YAML → human review → shadow testing → activation. No redeployment required.


4. Change Detection in Legacy Systems

Problem No events or webhooks. Solution (Virtual CDC) Poll APIs → hash records → compare with stored values. If changed, emit event. Optimization Adaptive polling based on activity.


5. Conflict Detection & Resolution

Detection Vector clocks instead of timestamps. Resolution (CRDT rules)
Single values → Last Writer Wins

Collections → Union (no data loss)

Authority fields → SWS priority

Safety fields → Most restrictive value

Transparency All decisions are logged and reversible.


6. Reliability & Audit

Idempotency Unique keys prevent duplicate writes. Saga Orchestrator Tracks multi-step operations with retries. Partial success is allowed. Circuit Breaker Stops failed systems temporarily and resumes after recovery. Merkle Audit Ledger Hash-linked logs ensure tamper detection. Failure handling Retries, alerts, checkpoint recovery, and nightly reconciliation.


7. Architecture & Tech Stack

Core Components Gateway, Mapper, Saga Engine, CDC Poller, Conflict Engine, Audit Ledger. Stack
Django (backend + admin)

Celery + Redis (async + retries)

PostgreSQL (state + JSONB)

Redis (cache + state)

YAML (config-based mapping)

AI Usage Only for one-time YAML generation. Not used in runtime. Design Choice Lightweight alternatives to Kafka/Temporal. Fully modular and replaceable.


8. Risks & Trade-offs


Polling delay → handled by adaptive polling

Vector size → small and manageable

Wrong merge rules → mitigated via testing + audit logs

API rate limits → controlled via throttling

Temporary inconsistencies → resolved via reconciliation

Audit growth → handled via archival




Demo Scenarios


Happy path → SWS update syncs to departments with audit log

Conflict → Detected via vector clocks, resolved via rules

Failure → Circuit breaker + auto recovery

Legacy update → CDC detects and merges safely

Tampering → Merkle chain detects instantlydiately flags the inconsistency.

B bulchandanibhoomi639_ff78
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
Pramana.AI: Enterprise Tender Intelligence & Automated Auditing
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 25, 2026 11:36 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
The Problem: Government procurement is heavily bottlenecked by manual document verification. Evaluating hundreds of scanned, unstructured vendor bids against strict legal requirements (turnover, GSTINs, ISO certifications) takes days, introduces human error, and creates compliance vulnerabilities.
Our Solution: Pramana.AI is a Palantir-style document intelligence dashboard built for modern governance. It completely automates the tender evaluation pipeline. By ingesting scanned vendor documents, our deterministic AI extracts key data points and mathematically grades them against a configurable JSON rulebook, instantly outputting a "VERIFIED" or "FAILED" audit matrix.
Why it solves the problem (Explainable AI): Unlike black box LLMs that hallucinate, Pramana.AI uses strict Regex and NLP logic for its final decision making. If a government rule requires a 15-digit GSTIN, our engine uses mathematical pattern matching. This guarantees 100% auditable, transparent, and legally defensible results for IAS officers.
The Architecture & Tech Stack:
The Face (Frontend): A decoupled, high contrast React application designed for enterprise usability and speed.

The Brain (Backend): A lightning fast FastAPI server handling asynchronous processing.

The Eyes (Pipeline): A custom computer vision and Optical Character Recognition (OCR) pipeline utilizing EasyOCR and OpenCV to extract text from blurry, low-quality document scans.

The Engine (Logic): Pure Python, SpaCy, and Regex driven rule matchers to enforce financial, legal, and certification requirements. Source Code & Architecture

Explore our complete, decoupled monorepo (React Frontend + FastAPI Logic Engine + OCR Pipeline) here: https://github.com/bhoomibulchandani/Pramana.ai
