solutions.team_name: SyncBridge Karnataka: Event-Driven Bidirectional Interoperability Mesh with Zero-Touch System Integration
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


H harinishsv_a7d0
TEAM SUBMISSION [2]
SyncBridge Karnataka: Event-Driven Bidirectional Interoperability Mesh with Zero-Touch System Integration
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 03:41 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Karnataka's Single Window System (SWS) is the state's forward-looking front door for business registrations, but 40+ legacy department systems remain the ground truth for existing businesses. A change of registered address filed in SWS never reaches the Factories department. An authorised signatory updated directly in Labour never reflects back in SWS. The two sides drift into permanent inconsistency, and citizens pay the price with repeated visits, rejected applications, and conflicting compliance demands. Big-bang migration is not realistic, GST proved that. Our solution does not ask SWS or any department to change a single API endpoint. Instead, we build an autonomous interoperability mesh that listens, translates, propagates, and reconciles service requests in both directions using UBID as the universal join key.
The Core Problem: Split-Brain at State Scale • SWS and legacy departments operate as independent authorities with no shared state
• A business can file the same request in two places simultaneously, creating divergent records
• Department schemas, identifiers, validation rules, and APIs are heterogeneous and frozen
• Some departments emit no events at all, making change detection impossible without active probing
• Without a complete audit trail, officers cannot answer "What changed, where did it come from, and why is the current state what it is?"
Our Architecture: The Interoperability Mesh We deploy a stateless, event-driven middleware layer that sits entirely outside SWS and department systems. It communicates only through existing public APIs, webhooks where available, and scheduled polling where necessary. The mesh treats every service request as an immutable event, assigns it a globally unique idempotency key, and guarantees at-least-once delivery with exactly-once semantics through UBID-scoped deduplication.
• UBID as the Single Source of Truth: We do not invent identifiers. Every propagation is anchored to the UBID resolved from Theme 1. SWS records and department records are mapped to a shared UBID in a central registry. If UBID does not exist for a business, propagation is paused until resolution is complete. • Schema Translation Engine: Each department exposes a different representation of the same concept. We built a dynamic schema registry that maps SWS canonical fields to department-specific payloads using declarative transformation templates written in JSONata. Adding a new department does not require code changes, only a new template registration. • Dual-Mode Change Capture: Not all departments emit events natively. We deploy three ingestion strategies simultaneously:
Webhook ingestion for departments that support push events
API polling with cursor tracking for departments with queryable audit logs
Snapshot differential analysis for legacy systems that expose only static data dumps.Change detection runs on scrambled synthetic data inside the sandbox, never touching raw production PII.
Direction 1: SWS to Department Propagation When a service request is raised in SWS, the mesh intercepts it via read-only API polling or webhook subscription. • Target Discovery: The mesh queries the UBID registry to identify every department system where this business holds an active registration
• Payload Translation: The SWS canonical request is transformed into each target department's native schema, field-by-field, with validation rule adaptation
• Idempotent Write: Each outbound request carries an idempotency key derived from the original SWS request ID plus target department code. If the write times out or retries, the department API receives the same key and deduplicates naturally
• Write Confirmation: The mesh expects an explicit acknowledgement (HTTP 200/201 with returned native record ID). Failed writes enter an exponential backoff retry queue with dead-letter archiving • Audit Log: Every propagation event is immutably logged with source SWS request ID, target department, transformed payload hash, response status, and timestamp
Direction 2: Department to SWS Propagation When a change originates inside a department system, the mesh must detect it, translate it, and write it back to SWS without triggering an infinite loop. • Ingestion Adapters: Webhook receivers, polling schedulers, and snapshot comparators feed into a unified event normaliser that converts every department-specific change format into a canonical internal event
• Loop Prevention: Every event carries provenance metadata. Events originating from SWS are tagged with source:sws . When the mesh writes a department change back to SWS, it tags the SWS record with source:mesh . The mesh ignores its own writes on the next polling cycle, preventing echo loops
• UBID Join: The incoming department event contains the department's native record ID. The mesh looks up the corresponding UBID in the registry, translates the payload to SWS schema, and writes it to the SWS API using SWS-native identifiers
• Failure Handling: If SWS rejects the write due to validation errors, the event is routed to a manual reconciliation queue with full context, not silently dropped
Cross-Cutting: Conflict Detection & Explainable Resolution This is where most interoperability layers fail. When two updates for the same UBID arrive within a short window, one from SWS and one from a department, the mesh detects the race condition and applies a configurable resolution policy rather than silently overwriting state. • Conflict Window: A distributed time window (default 5 minutes) groups related events by UBID
• Conflict Detection: If two events modify overlapping fields within the window, a conflict is declared
• Resolution Policies: Configurable per field and per department pair:
Last-Write-Wins with source hierarchy: Department wins for compliance fields, SWS wins for contact fields
Manual escalation: High-stakes conflicts (registered address, authorised signatory) route to an officer dashboard for human decision
Merge semantics: Non-overlapping field changes are automatically merged into a single composite update
• Explainable Audit: Every conflict resolution produces an immutable audit entry showing both original values, the policy applied, the winning value, and the reason. An officer can reverse the decision and replay the event stream if needed
Technical Stack & Guarantees • Event Bus: Apache Kafka for durable, ordered event streaming with UBID-partitioned topics to guarantee per-business sequencing
• Idempotency Store: Redis with TTL-backed idempotency key tracking to prevent duplicate writes across retries • Schema Registry: HashiCorp Vault-backed JSONata transformation engine with versioned templates
• API Gateway: Kong with rate-limiting and circuit-breaking to protect fragile legacy department endpoints
• Audit Ledger: Immutable append-only PostgreSQL table with cryptographic hashing per row, exportable for CAG audit
• Privacy: All payload processing runs on scrambled synthetic data. No hosted LLM processes raw PII. Translation logic is deterministic code, not generative AI
Why Government Will Deploy This Tomorrow • Zero Integration Tax: No department needs to re-platform, refactor, or even restart their servers. The mesh consumes what already exists
• Synthetic-Data Pilot Ready: The entire flow, conflict resolution, and audit trail operate identically on sandbox scrambled data
• Officer-Trusted Reconciliation: Conflicts are not auto-resolved in the dark. High-stakes decisions stop at a human desk with full before-and-after context
• Traceability by Design: Every propagation is end-to-end traceable. An officer can input any SWS request ID and see exactly which departments received it, when, and whether it succeeded or failed Scalability & Long-Term Impact • Department Onboarding in Hours, Not Months: New departments are added by registering their API spec and uploading a schema translation template. No core code changes
• From Karnataka to National Integration: This mesh architecture is state-agnostic. Once proven, it becomes the reference implementation for Centre-State service integration, GST-Sewa linkage, and national single-window interoperability
• Revenue & Compliance Impact: Eliminating split-brain means businesses update once, not forty times. Officer productivity rises, citizen grievances drop, and compliance timelines shrink from weeks to hours

M mohansriramkunamsetty_8526
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
JusticeTrack — AI-Powered Court Judgment to Verified Action Plan System
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 03:51 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
JusticeTrack — AI-Powered Court Judgment to Verified Action Plan System
DescriptionProblem
Karnataka's Court Case Monitoring System (CCMS) automatically fetches judgment PDFs from the High Court — but these documents are lengthy, complex, and unstructured. Critical directives, compliance requirements, appeal deadlines, and responsible departments are buried inside dense legal text. Officials must manually read entire judgments to extract actionable information, causing delays, missed directives, and compliance failures.
Our Solution
JusticeTrack is a 4-flow AI pipeline that transforms raw court judgment PDFs into verified, structured action plans — without replacing human judgment.
Flow 1 — Intelligent Extraction
The system ingests court judgment PDFs (digital or scanned) directly from CCMS via API and extracts:
Case details* and parties involved Date of order* Key directions and orders* issued by the court Responsible authority* for compliance Relevant timelines* (explicit or inferred from legal context)
Every extracted field carries a confidence score (e.g., "Responsible Department — 74% confidence"). Low-confidence fields are flagged in orange for mandatory human review, ensuring no hallucinated output silently reaches decision-makers.
Flow 2 — AI-Assisted Action Plan Generation *(Key Differentiator)
From the extracted data, the system auto-generates a structured action plan containing:
What is required* — specific compliance action needed Consideration for appeal* — auto-calculated appeal window with deadline (e.g., "90-day Karnataka HC appeal window expires: 13 June 2025 — 48 days remaining") *Key timelines — explicit dates from judgment + inferred deadlines *Responsible department(s) — mapped from directive context *Nature of action required — classified as Compliance / Appeal / Inquiry / Fund Release / Other
Flow 3 — Human-in-the-Loop Verification (Mandatory)
*No data reaches the dashboard without human approval.
The reviewer sees: - Extracted fields side-by-side with source-highlighted passages in the original PDF (powered by PDF.js highlight overlays — the exact sentence the AI extracted from) - Confidence level per field - Three actions per record: Approve / Edit / Reject
Only verified and approved records move forward. This is a decision-support tool, not full automation.
Flow 4 — Trusted Dashboard (Verified Records Only)
A clean, department-wise dashboard showing only human-approved action plans:
Department-wise filtered view* for each government body *Key actions required with priority tagging *Important dates with countdown indicators for upcoming deadlines *Status tracking — Pending / In Progress / Completed / Overdue - Zero noise — unverified AI outputs are never visible here
Tech StackDocument Processing - PyMuPDF — digital PDF parsing and text extraction - Tesseract OCR — scanned/image-based judgment processing - LayoutLMv3 — document layout and structure understanding
AI & NLP - InLegalBERT — Indian legal domain language model for NER and extraction - GPT-4o — structured action plan generation via JSON schema output - LangChain — AI pipeline orchestration and chaining
Backend - FastAPI (Python) — REST API and async processing - PostgreSQL — structured storage for cases, extractions, and action plans - Redis — job queue for async PDF processing (Celery)
Frontend - React + TypeScript — dashboard and verification UI - PDF.js — source sentence highlight overlay for human verification screen
Infrastructure - Docker — containerized deployment - Railway / Render — demo hosting

Why JusticeTrack Wins
Spec-aligned: Directly addresses all 4 mandated flows — Extract → Action Plan → Verify → Dashboard
Explainable by design: Every output is source-linked to the exact sentence in the original judgment
India-specific: Fine-tuned on Indian legal language, not generic English models
CCMS-ready: Designed to plug directly into the existing CCMS API pipeline — judgments in, verified action plans out
Decision support, not automation: Humans stay in control; AI eliminates the reading burden
> "India's courts issue thousands of orders daily. JusticeTrack ensures they are actually implemented."

solutions.team_name: JudiciPlan AI: A Trusted Neural Pipeline for High Court Judgment-to-Action Automation
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


T Team_sparklers
TEAM SUBMISSION [3]
JudiciPlan AI: A Trusted Neural Pipeline for High Court Judgment-to-Action Automation
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 03:54 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Project Overview
The Problem: While the current CCMS successfully fetches disposal orders, government officials are still required to manually read hundreds of pages to identify compliance directives, responsible departments, and appeal deadlines. This manual bottleneck leads to legal risks, missed limitation periods, and administrative delays. The Solution: An end-to-end, serverless intelligence ecosystem designed for the Centre for e-Governance (CeG). It solves the critical last-mile problem of legal compliance by transforming unstructured, lengthy High Court judgment PDFs into Human-Verified Action Plans.


Key Features

Layout-Aware Extraction: Uses Amazon Textract to understand the visual structure of court orders (tables, headers, and signatures).

Intelligent Action Synthesis: Employs Amazon Bedrock (Claude 3.5) to infer deadlines, identify responsible departments, and suggest whether to comply or appeal based on the judge's directives.

Evidence-Linked Verification: A unique "Side-by-Side" dashboard where officials verify AI suggestions. The system highlights the exact source text in the PDF for every suggested action, ensuring 100% explainability.

Statutory Compliance: Automatically cross-references case types with Indian Limitation Acts to provide accurate legal windows for appeals.


System Architecture & Flow

Ingestion: Scanned/Digital PDFs are fetched via CCMS API into Amazon S3.

Analysis: Amazon Textract performs layout-aware OCR to isolate the "Operative Order."

Reasoning: Amazon Bedrock (Claude 3.5) extracts case details and generates a draft Action Plan.

Verification: AWS Step Functions pauses the workflow for a mandatory Human-in-the-Loop check.

Dispatch: Verified records are stored in DynamoDB and synced to the Official Dashboard.
[START: Court Judgment PDF (Multi-page, Scanned or Digital)] | V [STAGE 1: PROCESSING & UNDERSTANDING] | |--> (1.1 Document Ingestion) Upload/Fetch PDF (from CCMS via API) |--> (1.2 OCR & Text Extraction) Extract text (if needed) |--> (1.3 Document Understanding - NLP) Identify sections, headings, paragraphs, legal details |--> (1.4 Information Extraction - AI) Extract key structured data points | V [OUTPUT: Extracted Information List] (Case Details, Date of Order, Parties Involved, Key Directions, Timelines/Deadlines, Other Info) | V [STAGE 2: GENERATE ACTION PLAN (AI-Assisted)] | |--> (AI Analysis & Reasoning) Understand directions and apply legal-action rules/templates |--> (Generate Structured Action Plan) Create detailed items for: | - What is Required? (Compliance / Appeal / Other Action) | - Key Timelines (Deadlines / Limitation Period) | - Responsible Department / Authority | - Nature of Action Required | - Priority (High / Medium / Low) | V [OUTPUT: AI Output Draft] (Extracted Data, Action Plan, Timeline Summary, Key Takeaways, Source References) | V [STAGE 3: VERIFY (HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP)] | |-->(Human Verification Interface) | - Show Extracted Data with Source Highlights | - Show Confidence Scores for Each Field / Item (Star ratings) | - Human Reviewer (Official / Legal Officer) | - Review & Validate (Approve, Edit, Reject) | V [DECISION POINT] Is the plan acceptable? | |--- [NO: Rejected / Needs More Info] ----> (BACK TO STAGE 1 FOR PROCESSING) | |--- [YES / MODIFIED: Approved / Edited] --> (GO TO STAGE 4) | V [STAGE 4: DASHBOARD (TRUSTED VIEW)] | |-->("Display Verified Action Plans Only") | (Shows only human-verified records) | - Department-wise View | - Key Actions Required | - Important Dates & Deadlines | - Responsible Authority | - Status Tracking (Upcoming / In Progress / Completed) | - Alerts & Reminders | V [END: Access System Benefits] (Faster Decision Making, Reduced Manual Effort, Timely Compliance, Reduced Legal Risk, Standardized Interpretation, Complete Action Tracking)


Tech Stack

AI/ML: Amazon Textract, Amazon Bedrock, Amazon Translate

Backend & Workflow: AWS Step Functions (Orchestration), AWS Lambda (Node.js), Amazon S3

Database: Amazon DynamoDB (NoSQL)

Frontend: Next.js, Tailwind CSS, React-PDF-Highlighter


Feasibility (Can we build it?)

Technical Readiness: Leverages production-grade AWS APIs (Textract, Bedrock) requiring zero custom ML model training.

Data Availability: Integrates directly with existing CCMS/CIS APIs for a steady stream of judgment PDFs.

Cloud Scalability: Serverless architecture (Lambda & Step Functions) handles loads from 10 to 10,000 judgments per day automatically.

Ease of Integration: Operates as a "Decision-Support Layer" without modifying current High Court or CCMS databases.

Security Compliance: Deployment in AWS Mumbai (ap-south-1) ensures data residency and adherence to Indian government protocols.


Viability (Is it a sustainable solution?)

Cost-Efficiency: Serverless Pay-As-You-Go model avoids expensive upfront hardware costs.

Operational Fit: Aligns with government workflows by acting as an assistant that mandates human accountability.

Linguistic Adaptability: Amazon Translate ensures viability across diverse Indian states and regional languages.

Statutory Accuracy: Limitation Act Registry ensures output is grounded in actual law, making it legally reliable.

Minimal Training: Intuitive Side-by-Side UI requires minimal technical training for clerks and officials.


Impact (What is the big-picture change?)

Zero-Miss Compliance: Eliminates the risk of missing court-mandated deadlines and contempt charges.

Optimized Resource Allocation: Automates the "reading phase," allowing legal officers to focus on strategy and action.

Statutory Timelines: Increases the success rate of timely appeals by automatically flagging expiration dates.

Standardization: Creates a uniform data structure, making government legal data searchable and analyzable.


Benefits (The "Wins" for the Government)

Speed: Reduces the "Judgment-to-Action" cycle from days of manual reading to seconds of AI processing.

Transparency: Links every action to a Source Highlight in the PDF, creating a clear audit trail.

Data-Driven Governance: Executive dashboard provides real-time views of department performance and pending actions.

Legal Risk Mitigation: Early identification of "Adverse Orders" allows for faster strategic decisions.

Cost Savings: Reduces administrative overhead and potential fines associated with delayed legal responses.

solutions.team_name: Aaroh: AI-Powered Extraction and Action Planning from Court Judgment PDFs for CCMS
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


V vaanya2708_e83e
TEAM SUBMISSION [6]
Aaroh: AI-Powered Extraction and Action Planning from Court Judgment PDFs for CCMS
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 04:02 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
AAROH
AI-Powered Extraction and Action Planning from Court Judgment PDFs for CCMS


The Problem
When cases are disposed in the High Court, judgment PDFs are fetched into CCMS automatically. These documents are typically 30 to 50 pages of dense legal text. Government officials must manually read every page to identify what the court directed, who must act, and by when. Missing a direction or miscalculating a limitation period can lead to contempt proceedings. When a court order lands in CCMS, every officer in the chain who knows about it is personally responsible for acting on it — not just the department. If a deadline is missed, individual officers can be summoned to court for contempt, regardless of whether they were directly named in the case. Aaroh creates a timestamped record of every action taken — who reviewed the judgment, what they decided, and when — so no officer is ever left without proof that they did their job. The problem is not access. CCMS already delivers the PDFs. The problem is that no one has turned that delivery into action.


Our Solution: Aaroh




Aaroh reads the judgment, extracts what matters, drafts an action plan, and puts nothing on a dashboard until a human has signed off on it.

Stage 1: Extraction




The system processes both digital and scanned PDFs using OCR, with support for Kannada and other regional languages, since lower court references and supporting documents are not always in English. From each judgment it extracts: • Case number, court and bench details, date of order • Parties involved and key directions issued by the court • Any timelines mentioned, explicit or implied

Critical distinction: extracted content is classified into three categories-:
CATEGORY
DEFINITION
Binding directions to government
Court orders the government must act on. Fully extracted and actioned.
Directions to petitioners
Orders directed at other parties. Noted but not actioned by the department.
Judicial observations
Remarks that carry no legal obligation. Flagged to prevent wasted effort.




The system also distinguishes between final disposal orders and interim directions. Interim orders, including stay orders, are flagged separately with an accelerated review window since they often require action within 24 to 48 hours. Every field gets a confidence score (high, medium, or low) so the reviewer knows exactly where to pay attention.

Stage 2: AI-Assisted Action Plan

From the extracted fields, the system drafts an action plan covering: • Nature of action: comply or consider appeal, presented as a recommendation, not a decision. • Limitation period: 30 days for High Court appeals, 90 days for Supreme Court SLPs, calculated automatically from the order date with deadline alerts built in. For vague directions like "as expeditiously as possible," the system defaults to a 30-day internal window and flags the field as inferred rather than explicit. • Responsible department and designation: identified from the subject matter of the case and assigned to a role (e.g., Deputy Commissioner) rather than a named individual, ensuring continuity of accountability during transfers. Entity resolution maps aliases like "R1," "The Revenue Department," and "The Tahsildar, Yelahanka" to the correct CCMS department identifier. • Priority level: based on contempt risk, financial liability, hard deadlines, or public interest. This is decision support. The system assists the official, it does not replace their judgment.

Stage 3: Human Verification

Nothing reaches the dashboard without approval. The verification interface provides: thing reaches the dashboard without approval. The verification interface provides: • Split-screen view: original PDF on the left with source passages highlighted, extracted fields on the right • Field-by-field approval, editing, or rejection by the reviewer • Low-confidence fields visually flagged for closer attention • All reviewer corrections logged with identity and timestamp for full accountability • Correction patterns tracked over time so the system improves on the cases it finds hardest


Only fully signed-off records proceed to the dashboard.


Stage 4: Decision-Support Dashboard




Displays only verified, approved action plans: • Department-wise view: each department sees only its relevant cases • Urgency tracking with cases sorted by days remaining before the limitation period expires • Action status: compliance underway, appeal filed, or action pending • Conflict detection: when a new judgment contradicts an existing active case, both are flagged automatically so no one acts on an outdated directive


Why This Works for Government


PRINCIPLE
HOW Aaroh DELIVERS IT
Explainability
Every extracted field links to the exact source passage in the judgment PDF.
Accountability
Mandatory human verification with full audit trails. No automated decisions.
Practicality
Works with judgment PDFs already flowing into CCMS via the CIS API. No change to existing infrastructure.
Scalability
Works across departments, courts, and case types without manual configuration.


How We Measure Success


What We Measure
Baseline
Target
Extraction accuracy
Manual review of 20 sample judgments
90%+ on case number, date, binding directions
High-confidence field correction rate
Current manual process
Less than 5% of fields edited by reviewer
Interim order detection speed
Manual reading time
Flagged within seconds of upload


Risks and Mitigations

• Scanned PDFs with poor image quality produce low-confidence extractions. The system flags these for full manual review rather than guessing. • Ambiguous legal language triggers automatic escalation to a human reviewer rather than a silent best-guess. • Conflict detection depends on consistent case numbering in CCMS. Where numbering is inconsistent, the system flags for manual cross-reference. • Vague court directions default to a 30-day internal compliance window, clearly marked as inferred so reviewers can adjust.
Technical stack: Gemini API for extraction and action plan generation, OCR for scanned documents, React frontend for the verification interface and dashboard, Node.js backend with PostgreSQL for storage and audit logging



Future Scope

In future iterations, Aaroh can integrate directly with CCMS via its existing CIS API, enabling automatic processing of judgment PDFs upon arrival, requiring zero manual upload from officials

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


R roydebangan724_58db
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
NyayaAI — From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 04:14 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
?️ NyayaAI — Intelligent Court Order Intelligence Platform
Theme 11 | AI for Bharat Hackathon 2026 | PAN IIT x Government of Karnataka


? Problem Statement
Karnatakas Court Case Monitoring System (CCMS) automatically fetches judgment PDFs from the High Courts CIS the moment a case is disposed. But the workflow breaks at the next step.A typical High Court judgment runs 30–80 pages. Critical directives requiring administrative action — compliance obligations, appeal decisions, limitation deadlines — are buried inside dense legal text. Today, government officials must:

''

Manually read entire documents to find actionable directives

Inconsistently interpret conditional or qualified legal language

Track limitation periods for appeals from memory or statutory knowledge

Maintain informal accountability with no structured record of who acted on what
This creates delays, missed deadlines, inconsistent compliance, and zero audit trail — all in a legally consequential context.


? Our Solution — NyayaAI
NyayaAI is a 4-stage AI-powered pipeline that transforms delivered court judgment PDFs into structured, verified, and actionable government records.


⚙️ How It Works
Stage 1 — Ingest


Accepts PDFs via manual upload or automated CCMS API fetch

Detects document type: digital PDF vs. scanned vs. hybrid

Digital PDFs → PyMuPDF extraction | Scanned PDFs → Tesseract OCR at 300 DPI
Stage 2 — AI Extraction (Claude API)


Full judgment text sent to Claude Sonnet in a single request (200K context window handles 80-page documents without chunking)

Extracts: case number, date of order, parties, key directions, action type, responsible department, explicit and inferred timelines

Every field gets a confidence score (0.0–1.0) and a source citation (page + paragraph)

Second AI call generates a structured action plan from extracted fields
Stage 3 — Human Verification (Non-Negotiable)


Split-screen UI: PDF viewer on left with source highlights | Review cards on right

Per-field confidence indicators: ? ≥0.85 | ? 0.60–0.84 | ? <0.60

Reviewer actions: Approve / Edit / Reject — all logged with identity + timestamp

No action plan enters the dashboard without mandatory field verification

Original AI value always preserved in audit log even after edits
Stage 4 — Verified Dashboard


Only verified records surface — this is a system constraint, not a filter

Department-wise view with deadline countdown chips (? <7 days | ? overdue)

Full audit trail exportable as PDF for compliance documentation


?️ Tech Stack

Layer
Technology
Frontend
Next.js 14 + TypeScript + Tailwind CSS + shadcn/ui
Animations
Framer Motion (page transitions, confidence bar animations, stagger effects)
PDF Viewer
react-pdf with highlight overlay
Backend
FastAPI (Python)
PDF Extraction
PyMuPDF + pdfplumber (digital) / Tesseract OCR (scanned)
LLM
Claude API — claude-sonnet-4-6 (200K context, structured JSON output)
Database
Supabase (PostgreSQL + Auth + Storage + Realtime)
Deployment
Vercel (frontend) + Railway (backend)


? Why This Wins on Evaluation Criteria
Accuracy of extraction → Per-field confidence scoring with source citation. Low-confidence fields auto-routed to human review. PDF highlight sync lets reviewer verify against source in one click.Quality of action plan generation → Two-step AI pipeline: extraction and interpretation are separate prompts, producing cleaner and more auditable results than a single combined prompt.Effectiveness of human verification → Mandatory field-level review enforced at the database level. No bulk approval. Reviewer identity attached to every decision. Audit log is append-only and immutable.Clarity and usability of dashboard → Trusted-view-only principle. Decision-makers see only verified records. Department filter, deadline countdown, summary statistics, and export capability.





⚠️ Key Design Decisions

AI assists, humans decide. Every action plan item requires verified human approval before reaching decision-makers. This is non-negotiable for legal and administrative accountability.

Confidence scores per field, not per document. A judgment may have high-confidence metadata but ambiguous compliance timelines — field-level scoring surfaces this distinction.

Source citation is mandatory. The reviewer can always trace every extracted value back to the exact page and paragraph in the original PDF.

Audit trail is immutable. Original AI values are preserved even after reviewer edits. The full history of every decision is always available.


? Expected Impact

Metric
Current
With NyayaAI
Time from judgment receipt to verified action plan
1–2 days
< 30 minutes
Directive extraction consistency
Variable across reviewers
Standardized with AI baseline
Missed directive rate (estimated)
~15% manually
< 2% with AI assistance
Audit trail completeness
Informal / none
100% traceable


?️ Implementation Roadmap
Phase 1 (Hackathon) — Working prototype: PDF upload → Claude extraction → verification UI → dashboard. Live deployment on Vercel + Railway + Supabase.Phase 2 (Sandbox) — CCMS API integration, full OCR pipeline, role-based access, department auto-routing, notification system, export to PDF/Excel.Phase 3 (Production) — Self-hosted Karnataka government infrastructure, compliance analytics for senior officials, feedback loop to improve extraction prompts, multi-court support.



Built for AI for Bharat Hackathon 2026 — PAN IIT Bangalore Alumni Association x Government of Karnataka

An Adversarial Multi-Agent System for Government Directive Compliance
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 04:48 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
ComplianceGuard AI is a multi-agent RAG system that transforms unstructured court judgment PDFs into verified, high-confidence administrative action plans. By utilizing an "Adversarial Agent" architecture, it stress-tests proposed compliance plans against historical legal risks, budget constraints, and statutory limitations before they reach human decision-makers. It ensures government departments move from reactive manual interpretation to proactive, auditable, and risk-adjusted legal execution.
Detailed Project Description
The system solves the "manual interpretation bottleneck" currently plaguing the Court Case Monitoring System (CCMS).
1. Core Workflow
Ingestion & Structuring: The system ingests multi-page, scanned judgment PDFs using Vision-LLMs. It extracts: Parties, Date of Order, Directives, Limitation Periods, and Responsible Departments.

Adversarial Simulation (The Differentiator): We employ three specialized agents:

Agent A (The Architect): Drafts the compliance plan based on the judgment.

Agent B (The Prosecutor/Devil’s Advocate): Scans the plan for legal pitfalls, citing past cases where similar compliance efforts failed or led to penalties.

Agent C (The Compliance Officer): Reconciles the critique into a final, risk-mitigated Action Plan.

Human-in-the-Loop: Before any data enters the official dashboard, it is presented to a human officer. The UI provides Source Highlighting, showing the exact sentence in the PDF that mandates an action. The officer can Approve, Edit, or Reject. Only "Verified" status allows data to move to the final Action Dashboard.

solutions.team_name: InteropSync Karnataka — Self-Healing Semantic Middleware for Government Interoperability
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


C CORx4
TEAM SUBMISSION [10]
InteropSync Karnataka — Self-Healing Semantic Middleware for Government Interoperability
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 05:22 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
The Problem Karnataka's Single Window System (SWS) connects to 40+ legacy departmental databases built in complete isolation. There is no reliable join key across the State's business data. The same business exists as different records in different databases, and master data cannot be linked. Activity data — inspections, renewals, consumption, compliance events — sits inside each department system and cannot be aggregated per business. Any working solution must sit alongside existing systems — source department systems cannot be modified, re-platformed, or migrated. India's National e-Governance Plan identifies interoperability as a top-priority challenge, and the IndEA Framework mandates standardized data exchange, yet no production-grade solution exists today.
What InteropSync Does InteropSync Karnataka is a production-grade bidirectional translation and propagation layer between SWS and all connected department backends. It uses UBID (Unique Business ID) as the sole join key. When a business updates its address in SWS, InteropSync translates that change into the target department's schema and pushes it via the appropriate integration surface — REST API, webhook callback, SFTP file drop, or database polling — depending on what each legacy system supports. When a department issues a new license or inspection result, InteropSync captures it and propagates it back to SWS. Every operation is idempotent replaying the same event produces the same result with no side effects.
Core Features1. Multi-Surface Integration Engine — Each department exposes a different integration surface. InteropSync supports four: REST API push, webhook subscription, scheduled file-snapshot polling, and direct database polling. The system auto-selects the appropriate transport per department based on a configuration manifest. Adding a new department requires only a manifest entry and a schema mapping — no code changes. 2. Configurable Schema Translation Layer — Every department stores business data in incompatible formats. InteropSync maintains a per-department translation map that converts field names, data types, date formats, and enumeration values bidirectionally. The translation map is version-controlled — when a department updates its schema, the old and new maps coexist during a migration window. Fields like comp_name in Labour → enterprise_title in SWS → firm_id in Commercial Tax are translated automatically and deterministically. 3. Conflict Detection & Resolution with Policy Engine — When two departments update the same UBID field simultaneously, the system detects the collision. A configurable policy engine resolves it: latest-timestamp-wins for low-risk fields (phone numbers), source-authority-wins for regulated fields (license status — only the issuing department's update is authoritative), and escalate-to-human for high-risk fields (legal entity name changes). Every resolution is logged with the policy that was applied and the data that was rejected. 4. Tamper-Proof Cryptographic Audit Chain — Every propagation event — who changed what, when, from which system, which translation map version was used — is hashed into an append-only cryptographic log. This log is independently verifiable by any department, any auditor, any RTI query. No single department controls the log. No record is deletable. 5. Retry & Failure Recovery with Dead-Letter Queue — When a department system is down, the propagation is queued with exponential backoff. After configurable retry exhaustion, the event moves to a dead-letter queue visible on the admin dashboard. Operators manually replay or discard dead-letter events with full audit logging. No data is silently lost. 6. Offline-First Sync for Rural District Offices — District-level offices with intermittent connectivity queue all updates locally using an embedded SQLite store. The moment connectivity is restored, InteropSync replays and merges queued changes using vector clocks to detect and resolve ordering conflicts without data loss or duplication. 7. Department Health Dashboard & SLA Monitoring — A live command center shows sync health of every connected department: propagation latency (p50/p95/p99), failure rate, schema drift score, pending conflicts, and dead-letter queue depth. Departments that consistently breach SLA thresholds receive automated escalation alerts to their IT nodal officers.
Who Benefits40+ Karnataka departments get a single source of truth with zero manual re-entry. 10,000+ businesses using SWS see approvals clear in hours instead of weeks. District and taluk offices participate fully despite poor rural connectivity. CAG and audit bodies get a tamper-proof, independently verifiable trail for every data movement. Citizens filing RTI get timestamped, hash-verified government action logs.
Key References
Karnataka Single Window System Portal

National e-Governance Plan — MeitY

IndEA (India Enterprise Architecture) Framework

DoWhy — Causal Inference Library by Microsoft Research

TEAM SUBMISSION [10]
TenderEval AI — Anti-Cartel Forensic Tender Evaluation for Government Procurement
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 05:25 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
The Problem
Government tender evaluation in India is manual, opaque, and vulnerable. Officers spend weeks reading thousands of scanned pages. Genuine bidders get disqualified for minor formatting issues while cartels submit coordinated bids through shell companies. According to CVC Annual Reports, procurement irregularities constitute the largest category of corruption complaints. The core challenge: tender documents define eligibility criteria in unstructured prose, and bidder submissions arrive in every conceivable format — typed PDFs, scanned affidavits, photographed certificates, Word files. No existing system extracts criteria automatically, evaluates bidder evidence against each criterion, or links verdicts to source documents at the page level.

What TenderEval AI Does
TenderEval AI ingests the tender document, automatically extracts every eligibility criterion without manual pre-labeling, then ingests all bidder submissions across all formats and evaluates each bidder against every criterion. The output is an explainable, per-criterion verdict (Eligible / Not Eligible / Needs Review) where every verdict hyperlinks to the exact page and section in the bidder's source document that supports or contradicts the claim. No bidder is silently disqualified — every "Not Eligible" verdict ships with the verbatim evidence and a confidence score so officers can verify in seconds.

Core Features
1. Zero-Labeling Criterion Extraction — The AI reads the tender PDF and extracts every eligibility requirement (minimum turnover, years of experience, license validity, EMD amount, technical certifications) without any manual annotation or pre-training on tender formats. It parses nested conditional requirements ("if the bidder is an MSME, the turnover threshold is reduced to...") into structured criterion objects with thresholds, conditions, and evidence types.
2. Multi-Format Document Intelligence Pipeline — Bidder submissions arrive as machine-typed PDFs, handwritten affidavits, scanned certificates with stamps and signatures, rotated/tilted photographs of documents, and Word/Excel files. The pipeline chains OCR, Vision-Language Models, and layout-aware parsing to extract structured data from every format. No document is rejected for format incompatibility. Handles low-resolution scans, rubber-stamp overlays, and handwritten annotations.
3. Per-Criterion Verdict with Source-Page Anchoring — For every eligibility criterion and every bidder, the AI produces a verdict with a confidence score. Each verdict hyperlinks to the exact page number and bounding box region in the original bidder PDF that contains the supporting or contradicting evidence. Officers click through to the source, verify in seconds, and override if needed. The override is logged with the officer's justification.
4. Bid-Rigging Detection via Temporal Graph Neural Networks — Beyond document evaluation, TenderEval builds a continuously growing knowledge graph of all bidding entities across historical tenders: registered directors, shared addresses, IP submission metadata, bid amount patterns, and win/loss sequences. A Temporal GNN detects shell company clusters, round-robin bidding rings, and coordinated price inflation. Every bidder receives a forensic risk score visible to evaluation officers.
5. Live Statutory Cross-Verification — The AI cross-references bidder claims against live government databases: GST registration status, MSME Udyam registration, blacklisting records on GeM, and active labor law compliance. Discrepancies between claimed and actual status are flagged automatically with the exact conflicting data source cited.
6. Comparative Bid Matrix with Override Audit — Officers see a side-by-side comparison of all bidders across all criteria — color-coded pass/fail with drill-down into every supporting document. When an officer overrides the AI's verdict, the system records the override reason, the officer's identity, and the timestamp. The full override history is available to audit.

Who Benefits
CRPF and Defense procurement get tamper-proof, explainable evaluations with source-linked verdicts. All central and state procurement bodies achieve 10x faster evaluation with zero silent disqualifications. Anti-corruption agencies (CBI, CVC) get automated cartel detection across historical tenders. Genuine MSME bidders receive fair evaluation regardless of document formatting quality. RTI applicants and investigative journalists get a complete audit trail for every procurement decision including officer overrides.

Key References
Central Vigilance Commission — Annual Reports

Government e-Marketplace (GeM)

MSME Udyam Registration Portal

GST Taxpayer Search

Temporal Graph Networks — Research Paper

LLaVA Vision-Language Model — Research Paper

solutions.solution_text:

A amancodit2004_8f47
TEAM SUBMISSION [10]
NYAYA SAKSHAM AI: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 05:25 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
NYAYA SAKSHAM AI: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans
Government departments handling court cases often rely on the Court Case Monitoring System (CCMS), which provides access to High Court judgments in PDF format. These documents are typically long, unstructured, and written in complex legal language. Critical directives such as compliance requirements, appeal decisions, responsible authorities, and timelines are embedded within dense text, making manual interpretation time-consuming and error-prone.The core challenge lies not in accessing judgments, but in accurately extracting actionable insights and transforming them into structured, verifiable decision support.We propose NYAYA SAKSHAM AI, an AI-powered system that converts court judgment PDFs into structured, human-verified action plans. The system combines document intelligence, legal understanding, and human-in-the-loop validation to ensure that all outputs are accurate, explainable, and decision-ready.




Technical Architecture Overview


The system follows a modular pipeline consisting of document processing, information extraction, action plan generation, and human verification layers.


1. Document Ingestion and Preprocessing
The system accepts court judgment PDFs (both scanned and digitally generated). Preprocessing includes:


PDF parsing using tools such as PyMuPDF or PDFPlumber

OCR for scanned documents using models such as Tesseract or transformer-based OCR (e.g., TrOCR)

Layout detection using document AI models (e.g., LayoutLM) to preserve structural elements like paragraphs, headings, and tables
This step converts raw PDFs into structured text with positional metadata.


2. Information Extraction Layer
The extracted text is processed using NLP and document understanding models to identify key information:


Case details (case number, court name)

Date of order

Parties involved (petitioner/respondent)

Key directions and judicial orders

Explicit timelines and deadlines
Models and Techniques:


Transformer-based models (Legal-BERT / LayoutLM / fine-tuned LLMs)

Named Entity Recognition (NER) for legal entities

Rule-based + ML hybrid extraction for dates and case references
The output is structured data linked to source text spans for traceability.


3. Action Plan Generation (Core Differentiator)
This module transforms extracted legal information into a structured administrative action plan.Using fine-tuned large language models (e.g., LLaMA, Mistral via Hugging Face), the system generates:



Required action (compliance / appeal consideration)

Responsible department(s)

Nature of action (administrative, legal, financial, etc.)

Key timelines (explicit or inferred using legal rules)

Priority level
The system uses prompt engineering combined with structured extraction to ensure outputs remain grounded in the source document.


4. Explainability and Source Mapping
To ensure transparency and trust:


Each extracted field is linked to its exact location in the PDF

Key directives are highlighted within the original document

Users can trace every output back to source evidence
This ensures the system remains explainable and auditable, which is critical for government workflows.


5. Confidence Scoring Engine
A confidence score is generated for each extracted field and action item based on:


OCR confidence

Extraction model confidence

Consistency checks across document sections
This helps prioritize human review and identify uncertain outputs.


6. Human-in-the-Loop Verification (Mandatory Layer)
Before any data is used, it passes through a verification interface.Interface Capabilities:



Display extracted data alongside highlighted source text

Show confidence scores

Allow user actions:

Approve

Edit

Reject
Only verified records are allowed to proceed to the dashboard.The interface is implemented using:



Frontend: React.js

Backend: FastAPI / Node.js


7. Dashboard and Decision Support System
A clean and structured dashboard presents only approved action plans.Features:



Department-wise filtering

Case-wise summaries

Key actions required

Important dates and deadlines
The dashboard ensures that decision-makers interact only with trusted, validated information, reducing ambiguity and delays.


8. Learning and Feedback System
The system continuously improves by capturing:


Human corrections

Approved vs rejected outputs

Common extraction errors
Technologies:


Vector databases (FAISS / Pinecone) for semantic storage

Fine-tuning pipelines for model improvement
This enables domain adaptation to legal language and evolving case patterns.


End-to-End Flow

Court judgment PDF is ingested into the system

OCR and parsing extract structured text with layout information

NLP models identify key entities and directives

Action plan is generated using LLM-based reasoning

Outputs are linked to source text for explainability

Confidence scores are assigned

Human reviewer verifies, edits, or rejects outputs

Only approved data is displayed in the dashboard

Feedback is stored to improve future performance


Key Features

Automated extraction from complex legal PDFs

AI-assisted structured action plan generation

Source-linked explainability for all outputs

Mandatory human verification workflow

Confidence-aware decision support

Clean and intuitive dashboard for officials


Unique Value Proposition
Unlike conventional document processing systems that focus only on extraction, NYAYA SAKSHAM AI provides:Extract → Interpret → Plan → Verify → ActThe system goes beyond reading judgments to delivering actionable, verified insights that directly support administrative decision-making.




Impact

Reduces manual effort in reading lengthy legal documents

Improves accuracy and timeliness of decisions

Minimizes risk of missing critical directives

Enhances efficiency in government legal workflows

Provides a transparent and auditable decision-support system


Future Scope

Integration with CCMS and other e-governance platforms

Advanced legal reasoning for appeal recommendation

Automated alerts for deadlines and compliance tracking

Expansion to other legal and administrative document types

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


C CORx4
TEAM SUBMISSION [10]
Verdict→Action — Legal Entailment Engine for Court Judgment Compliance
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 05:41 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
The Problem
Indian courts issue thousands of judgments annually directing government departments to act — build infrastructure, compensate victims, shut down polluting factories. These orders are buried in dense 50–200 page PDFs written in complex legal prose with layered conditional directives. Departments miss deadlines, misinterpret obligations, and face contempt of court proceedings. According to National Judicial Data Grid statistics, lakhs of cases involve pending compliance. Judges require a system that extracts structured data (Case ID, Date, Parties, specific orders/directions), generates action plans, and provides a side-by-side dashboard for human verification with links back to the original judgment text.

What Verdict→Action Does
Verdict→Action ingests court judgment PDFs (typed and scanned), extracts every specific obligation — Case ID, judgment date, petitioner/respondent names, specific directions, deadlines, and responsible authorities — using Legal-BERT with Chain-of-Thought reasoning. Every extracted obligation is hyperlinked to the exact paragraph and page in the original PDF so officers can verify the AI's interpretation against the source text in a side-by-side view. Before execution, the platform cross-references obligations against existing state laws to flag statutory contradictions. Officers verify, assign, and track compliance through a structured escalation workflow with real-time contempt risk scoring.

Core Features
1. Structured Extraction: Case ID, Parties, Orders, Deadlines — The AI extracts every structured field the judges require: Case ID/citation, judgment date, bench composition, petitioner names, respondent departments, specific directions/orders, compliance deadlines, and named responsible authorities. Each field is extracted as a typed, structured object — not free text. Conditional and layered directives ("if the State fails to comply within 90 days, the Commissioner shall...") are decomposed into separate obligation objects with trigger conditions.
2. Side-by-Side Verification Dashboard with Source Linking — Officers see the extracted action plan on the left and the original judgment PDF on the right. Every extracted obligation is hyperlinked to the exact paragraph and page in the source PDF. Clicking an obligation scrolls the PDF viewer to the corresponding text, highlighted in context. Officers verify, approve, or correct each extraction before it enters the compliance workflow. This is the core UX requirement from the judges — human verification with full source traceability.
3. Legal-BERT + Chain-of-Thought Reasoning — Uses fine-tuned Legal-BERT with explicit Chain-of-Thought prompting. The AI traces its reasoning step-by-step: "Paragraph 42 states 'the respondent State shall ensure...', which identifies the respondent as [Department X]. The phrase 'within six months from the date of this order' sets the deadline at [Date Y]." Every extraction ships with the reasoning chain as an auditable artifact. Officers see how the AI reached its interpretation, not just the result.
4. Statutory Contradiction Detection — Before a department executes a court-ordered action, the platform cross-references it against a vector database of existing state legislative acts, departmental circulars, and SOPs. If the court order directs an action that contradicts an existing statute (e.g., "demolish structure X" vs. heritage protection act), the system flags the exact conflicting provisions with citations from both the judgment and the statute.
5. Multi-Party Compliance Tracker with Automated Escalation — Each judgment spawns a compliance project. Every obligation becomes a task card assigned to a specific officer with a hard deadline. Overdue tasks trigger automated escalation: assigned officer → department secretary → chief secretary → Advocate General's office. The escalation chain is configurable per department and per urgency level.
6. Contempt Risk Scoring — Continuously calculates a real-time Contempt Risk Score for every active judgment based on: (a) number of overdue obligations, (b) days past deadline, (c) historical compliance rate of the assigned department, (d) severity of the original order. Departments with rising contempt risk scores are surfaced on a priority dashboard for the Chief Secretary's office.

Who Benefits
State law departments and the Advocate General get automated extraction that eliminates manual reading of thousands of pages with full source verification. Department secretaries receive clear, deadline-bound task assignments from every judgment. The Chief Secretary's office gets a real-time contempt risk dashboard across all departments. High Court and Supreme Court registries receive structured compliance reporting from the executive branch. Citizens and PIL petitioners get verifiable tracking of whether court-ordered relief is executed.

Key References
National Judicial Data Grid (NJDG)

Indian Kanoon — Free Indian Law

SCC Online — Supreme Court Cases

Legal-BERT — Research Paper

Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 05:43 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
NyayaEval — AI-Powered Explainable Tender Evaluation Engine
NyayaEval is a production-grade, AI-driven procurement intelligence system designed to transform how government tenders are evaluated. Today, tender evaluation is slow, inconsistent, and opaque—taking 3–5 days of manual effort, often leading to silent bidder disqualifications with no explanation. NyayaEval solves this by introducing a fully automated yet legally defensible pipeline that extracts tender criteria, parses multi-format bidder documents (PDFs, scans, images), and evaluates eligibility using a hybrid AI + rule-based decision engine.
At its core is a 3-Layer Confidence Cascade that ensures no bidder is unfairly rejected—every decision is backed by document-level evidence, extraction confidence, and matching validation. High-confidence cases are auto-approved, while ambiguous ones are routed to a human reviewer through an intelligent dashboard with source-highlighted proofs. The system generates a complete audit-ready eligibility report, where every decision is traceable, explainable, and compliant with government standards.
Built entirely on secure, on-premise AI (LLaMA/Mistral, OCR, NER, semantic search) with zero PII exposure, NyayaEval is scalable, auditable, and deployable across central and state procurement systems. It reduces evaluation time from days to hours while bringing transparency, consistency, and trust into a ₹20 lakh crore public procurement ecosystem.

TENDERWISE AI: Intelligent Eligibility Evaluation & Explainable Procurement Analysis Platform
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 06:14 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Description
Government procurement processes, such as those conducted by the Central Reserve Police Force (CRPF), involve evaluating complex tender documents and bidder submissions. These documents contain detailed eligibility criteria including technical specifications, financial thresholds, compliance requirements, and mandatory certifications.Currently, this evaluation is manual, time-consuming, and inconsistent. Procurement officers must review hundreds of pages across multiple formats—typed PDFs, scanned documents, Word files, and even images. The same information is often presented differently across bidders, increasing the risk of oversight, inconsistency, and lack of auditability.The core challenge is not just extracting information, but building a system that can understand legal and technical language, match bidder evidence to eligibility criteria, ensure explainability, and maintain audit-grade reliability.We propose TENDERWISE AI, an AI-powered document intelligence platform that automates tender understanding, bidder evaluation, and eligibility analysis with full explainability and auditability. The system transforms unstructured procurement documents into structured insights, enabling faster, consistent, and transparent decision-making.





Technical Architecture Overview


The system is designed as an end-to-end document intelligence and decision-support pipeline, integrating document processing, NLP-based understanding, rule evaluation, and explainable reporting.


1. Document Ingestion Layer
Supports multiple input formats:


Tender documents (PDF, Word)

Bidder submissions (PDF, scanned docs, images)
Capabilities:

Format detection (text vs scanned vs image)

Secure storage and indexing

Layout-aware models (LayoutLM)


3. Tender Understanding Engine

Technical requirements

Financial thresholds

Certification requirements
Capabilities:


Distinguish mandatory vs optional criteria

Convert criteria into structured, machine-readable format
Approach:


LLM-based semantic extraction

Rule-based classification for critical conditions


4. Bidder Document Understanding Engine
Parses and extracts relevant data from submissions:


Financial data (turnover, balance sheets)

Experience records

Certifications (GST, ISO, etc.)
Handles:


Variations in document formats

Inconsistent terminology

Partial or missing information


5. Information Mapping & Matching Engine
Maps extracted bidder data to tender criteria:


Semantic matching between criteria and evidence

Normalization of values (currency, dates, units)
Handles:


Ambiguity and incomplete data

Multiple representations of the same information


6. Evaluation & Decision Engine

Eligible

Not Eligible

Needs Manual Review
Logic:

7. Explainability Engine

Source document reference

Reason for pass/fail/review
Features:

Confidence scores

Clear justification


8. Human-in-the-Loop Review Layer

9. Audit & Traceability Layer

10. Storage & Data Layer

Exportable reports

Consolidated eligibility report

Audit-ready documentation

Continuously enhances system performance:


Learns from manual corrections

Improves extraction accuracy

Refines matching and evaluation models

Bidder data extracted and structured

TenderTrust - AI Driven Tender Eligibility & Evaluation for CRPF Procurement
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 06:43 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Prototype :
The prototype is designed to take a tender document along with multiple bidder submissions as input. It first processes the tender using a language model to identify and structure the eligibility requirements, clearly distinguishing between mandatory and optional conditions. Next, a document processing pipeline analyzes each bidder’s files—whether they are digital PDFs, scanned documents, or images—and extracts the relevant information needed for evaluation. This extracted data is then evaluated against the defined criteria using a decision engine, which classifies each bidder as Eligible, Not Eligible, or requiring Manual Review. Each outcome is supported with clear evidence, including the source document and the specific values used. Finally, the system compiles all results into a comprehensive report that can be reviewed and verified by a procurement officer.

Automatically reads tender documents and finds eligibility rules

Extracts required details from bidder files (PDFs, scans, images, etc.)

Checks each bidder against each rule and gives a clear result

Marks unclear or low-confidence cases for human review

Creates a final report for officers

Saves time and reduces manual effort

Gives consistent and fair evaluation

Shows proof for every decision (transparent)

Works with real-world messy documents

Keeps a proper record for audit and verification

Implementation :1. Extracting Rules form Tender Document
Extract text → PyMuPDF / pdfplumber (typed PDFs), Tesseract (scanned)

Process text → NLP (spaCy / regex for patterns like numbers, dates)

Extract criteria → LLM (OpenAI / Gemini) using prompt-based extraction

Structure output → JSON format (name, type, value, mandatory/optional)

Validate → map each rule back to source sentence in document

2.Understanding Bidder Document Pipeline:
Input types → PDF, images, scanned docs, Word files

Text extraction → PyMuPDF / OCR (Tesseract / EasyOCR / Vision API)

Store data → vector DB (FAISS / Pinecone) for easy retrieval

Process → LLM to extract required values (turnover, GST, certificates, etc.)

Track source → store document name + page/section for each value

3.Evaluation and Decision Making Rule-based + AI approach:
Compare extracted values with criteria (numbers, keywords, dates)

Assign result:

Eligible → clear match

Not Eligible → clear fail

Needs Review → missing / unclear data

Generate report → include value, rule, source document, and reason

4.Human-in-Loop System marks cases as Needs Review when:
Data is missing

Extracted value is unclear or unreadable

Multiple possible values are found

Highlighted document section

Extracted value and related criterion

Accept the result

Correct the value

Change the final decision

Show the officer: Officer can: Store corrections to improve future processing
5.Audit and Transparency : Store all decisions with source references Keep logs of extraction, matching, and changes Allow trace: criterion → value → document → decision Lock final report after approval
6.Tech stack:
Backend → FastAPI

Frontend → Streamlit (dashboard + review UI)

OCR → Tesseract / EasyOCR / Google Vision

LLM → OpenAI / Gemini

Database → PostgreSQL / MongoDB

Vector DB → FAISS / Pinecone

Risk and Mitigations :
OCR errors → Use preprocessing + manual review for unclear text

Wrong rule extraction → Map rules to source text + allow officer validation

Incorrect evaluation → Use rule-based checks + avoid auto-rejection

Missing/ambiguous data → Mark as Needs Review

Low trust in system → Provide clear evidence + audit trail

NyayaFlow: AI-Driven Judicial Compliance & Action Plan Engine
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 06:57 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
1. Problem Statement
The "Last Mile" of the Indian judicial system often fails due to the complexity of legal documents. High Court and District Court judgments are frequently 50+ pages of complex legal jargon. Local administrative officers (Tehsildars, Police, Municipal Heads) struggle to:
Identify specific mandatory actions buried in the text.

Track inter-departmental responsibilities.

Ensure 100% compliance with judicial deadlines, leading to contempt of court cases.

2. Proposed Solution

NyayaFlow is a forensic intelligence platform that translates judicial "Orders" into "Operations." It uses Generative AI (Gemini 2.5 Flash) to analyze the operative part of court judgments and generate a Verified Action Plan.
The system operates in three distinct layers:
Directive Extraction: Automatically identifies "Who" must do "What" by "When."

Responsibility Matrix: Segregates tasks by department (e.g., Police vs. Revenue) to prevent administrative overlap.

Compliance Verification: A forensic ledger where ground-level officers must upload proof of action (photos/documents) against the specific court clause.

3. Key Features

Automated Action Itemization: Converts legal paragraphs into a clear, numbered checklist.

Department-Wise Tagging: Automatically routes directives to the relevant authority (Municipality, PWD, Police).

Contempt Risk Alert: Highlights specific deadlines and legal penalties mentioned in the judgment to prioritize urgent actions.

Vernacular Translation: Translates English action plans into local languages (Hindi/Kannada) for ground-level execution without misinterpretation.

Evidence-Linked Audit Trail: Every action taken is linked back to the specific page and paragraph of the court order.

4. Impact on Bharat

Reduces Judicial Backlog: Ensures that court orders are executed promptly, reducing the need for follow-up litigation.

Transparency: Provides a clear dashboard for District Collectors to monitor the execution of all pending court orders in their jurisdiction.

Administrative Efficiency: Saves thousands of man-hours spent manually summarizing legal documents across various government departments.

solutions.solution_text:

B BhartiyaNagrik
TEAM SUBMISSION [2]
NyayaPradata: An AI-Powered Court Judgment Intelligence & Compliance Tracker for CCMS
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 08:27 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Problem Karnataka's Court Case Monitoring System (CCMS) automatically fetches High Court judgment PDFs via API, but everything after that is manual. Legal officers spend 2–3 days reading 50–200 page judgments to find 2–3 buried directives. Deadlines are missed. Compliance ownership is unclear. No dashboard reflects ground-level execution status.
The Insight The ingestion pipeline already exists. The failure is post-ingestion. No system today distinguishes binding directives ("the State shall ensure...") from judicial observations ("the court notes with concern...") — the two are mixed across hundreds of pages of dense legal prose.
Our Solution: NyayaSetu An AI layer that plugs directly into CCMS after PDF ingestion and performs four steps:
Extract — Parses judgment PDFs (digital and scanned), classifies sentences as directives vs observations, and extracts: case details, date of order, key directions, parties involved, and explicit/inferred timelines.

Generate Action Plan — Structures each directive into: What action is required | Compliance or appeal decision | Responsible department | Deadline (explicit or inferred from statutory periods) | Nature of action.

Human Verification (Mandatory) — Extracted data is shown alongside source-highlighted PDF passages with confidence scores. Reviewers approve, edit, or reject each item. Only verified records proceed.

Trusted Dashboard — Department-wise view of verified action plans only. Key actions, deadlines, and responsible authorities; clean and reliable for decision-makers.

Why AI Here Is Non-Negotiable Karnataka's High Court disposes thousands of cases annually. Manual extraction cannot scale. AI reads every judgment in seconds, applies consistent extraction logic, and flags uncertainty rather than guessing — critical for formal government compliance use.
Tech Stack PyMuPDF + Tesseract OCR (digital + scanned PDFs), LLM-based two-stage pipeline (directive classifier → structured extractor), confidence-scored verification UI with PDF source highlighting, React dashboard with department-wise filtering.
Data Public Karnataka High Court judgments are freely available, no proprietary data required for prototyping.
Feasibility Plugs into existing CCMS architecture without modifying any source system. Prototype demonstrable with real public judgment PDFs within the hackathon timeline.
Impact Compliance action time reduced from days to hours. Zero missed directives. Full audit trail. Scalable across all government departments using CCMS with no additional integration effort per department.

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


B bbsarada07_b3ff
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
ProcureAI: Multi-Tenant Tender Evaluation & Compliance Command Centre
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 08:43 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
1. Problem Relevance & Depth of Understanding
Government procurement for defence organisations like the CRPF is strictly bound by the General Financial Rules (GFR). The core operational bottleneck is not merely "reading PDFs"—it is evaluating hundreds of heterogeneous, noisy pages (scanned documents, Indic languages, stamped certificates) with zero margin for protocol violation. Current manual evaluation is severely delayed and highly vulnerable to sophisticated fraud, such as cartel ring-bidding and suspended GSTNs. Conversely, deploying standard "black-box" AI introduces the catastrophic risk of silent disqualifications—rejecting legitimate vendors due to OCR hallucinations. This triggers massive legal disputes and RTI (Right to Information) inquiries. A viable solution must handle "Bharat" edge-cases, guarantee legal transparency, and keep bureaucratic authority intact.
2.Technical Implementation & Innovation
ProcureAI is a deterministic, event-driven execution engine built for enterprise deployment, completely moving away from subjective "AI rule extraction."
Tender DNA Compiler: The FastAPI backend utilises Google Gemini 2.0 Flash (Structured Output mode) to ingest complex 500+ page ZIP files. It compiles dense legal paragraphs into strict, binary Logic-Gates (e.g., [MANDATORY] Turnover > ₹5Cr), automatically flagging restrictive clauses to prevent vendor bias.

Asynchronous "No-Timeout" Architecture: Heavy extraction tasks use background workers. The Next.js 14 frontend leverages WebSockets to stream evaluation logs cell-by-cell in real time, preventing browser timeouts on massive document loads.

Visual Decision Trees: We eliminate "black-box" fear. Every rejection is backed by an auto-generated visual flowchart mapping the exact logic path, linking extracted values directly to bounding boxes in the source document.

3. Real-World Deployability & Government Feasibility
ProcureAI is engineered strictly around Indian bureaucratic realities and GFR compliance, ensuring it can be deployed in high-stakes environments today.
Precedent-Aware HITL (Human-in-the-Loop): We enforce a "Zero Silent Disqualification" policy. Ambiguous scans trigger a "Needs Review" state, citing relevant GFR statutes and historical precedents to help the officer make a legally defensible ruling.

Cryptographic Maker-Checker Ledger: The system natively enforces hierarchy. A Junior Evaluator (Maker) initiates the matrix, but a Procurement Director (Checker) must digitally sign and lock the ledger. This generates a SHA-256 cryptographic hash to guarantee a tamper-proof, court-admissible audit trail.

Zero-Trust Live Oracles: ProcureAI verifies reality via mock live API integrations. It cross-references extracted GST numbers against the GSTN Portal to detect suspended accounts, and compares quotes against a Live Market Pricing Index to immediately flag predatory pricing.

4.Demo Quality & Presentation
The platform features a highly polished "Bureaucratic Light" UI designed for data density and extended use, mirroring a high-security intelligence terminal.
Cartel & Collusion Topology: Instead of basic risk scoring, the UI features a dynamic network graph that connects bidder data points, exposing "ring-bidding" if supposedly competing vendors share identical IP addresses, Chartered Accountants, or overlapping DINs.

Bhashini-Aligned Localisation: Features a seamless English/Hindi localisation toggle for pan-India usability.

Graceful Degradation: The architecture includes a secure local cache fallback. In the event of an API rate limit or network drop, the system gracefully loads offline data without crashing, ensuring uninterrupted operational continuity.

5. Scalability & Long-Term Impact
While initially modelled for the CRPF, ProcureAI is architected as a Multi-Tenant B2G SaaS platform capable of standardising procurement across all Ministries, State Governments, and PSUs.
National Command Centre: Features a macro-analytics executive dashboard aggregating cross-department efficiency, tracking administrative hours saved, and calculating total public funds secured from fraud.

Automated Bureaucracy: Post-evaluation, ProcureAI automatically generates formal Official Memorandums (OM) and auto-drafts legally sound RTI responses using the cryptographic audit trail.

By expanding this architecture nationwide, ProcureAI has the potential to secure billions in public funds, reduce evaluation timelines from weeks to hours, and create an immutable standard for Indian Government Procurement.

solutions.team_name: Veridict Evaluation System
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


B banerjeesangram666_a6fc
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
Veridict Evaluation System
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 09:17 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Government procurement is a tedious and difficult process which involves many employees and their undivided attention to each and every detail in each tender which can be notoriously painstaking sometimes. Additionally, a wrongful termination might result in a legal dispute which in turn may cost both the organizations time and credibility. We decided to automate this manual process. Veridict Evaluation System aims to compare the requirements mentioned in the Government tender with the various bidder submissions and aims to find which bidders are legit and which are suspicious. It shall be noted that we are not replacing human judgement completely as any ambiguity in the report shall be addressed by an officer and the system will not reject any submission on its own. As for document scanning, our system handles it in the following way There are mainly two types of documents that we deal with-

Digital PDF/ Word documents - These are the easiest to scan. We use pdfplumber to extract the text and tables directly and then we perform Semantic classification to categorize the texts into various important criteria.

Scanned PDF/ Photographs - We initially check whether there are text layers or not using pdfplumber. After the check we use PyMuPDF to convert each PDF page into a high resolution image. After this process we use OpenCV to perform deskewing using Hough line detection. This is done to correct page rotation and adapt to uneven lighting and shadows. After this preprocessing step is over we run two OCR engines in parallel. Azure Document Intelligence serves as the primary engine as it is the best at handling rubber stamps, Hindi and English text and embedded tables while returning per word confidence scores. Tesseract 5 in LSTM mode runs as the fallback.

This scanner works for both tender and the bidder submissions and produces a JSON output in which the most important criteria of the documents are stored.
The system then evaluates every bidder submission against the tender document based on the JSON output produced from the scanners. It compares them field by field, item descriptions using fuzzy token matching to handle wording variations, quantities using direct numeric comparison, ISO certifications by normalized identifier matching and financial values after full currency normalization across all Indian formats. Every single check produces an explicit result - Pass or Flagged, along with the reason documented. The overall verdict is derived strictly from the criterion results. If all the checks pass then the submission is marked as eligible. Else, any flag marks the submission for manual review. As you can see the system never autonomously rejects a bidder. The system outputs a report regarding each bidder submission which a designated officer can read and then make decision. Therefore, what once took a committee days of checking and cross referencing is now reduced to a few minutes.
Github Repo for our project - https://github.com/sangram528/Veridict_Evaluation_System.git
