solutions.team_name: Dyuti — A Criterion Ontology Engine for Explainable, Vigilance-Aware Bid Evaluation in Government Procurement
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


V vaanya2708_e83e
TEAM SUBMISSION [6]
Dyuti — A Criterion Ontology Engine for Explainable, Vigilance-Aware Bid Evaluation in Government Procurement
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 10:09 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
What we build: A three-stage AI pipeline that transforms heterogeneous tender and bidder documents — typed PDFs, scanned certificates, photographs, Word files, and Kannada-language certificates — into a structured, criterion-by-criterion evaluation with confidence scores, source citations, a collusion detection layer, and a tamper-proof audit trail for formal government procurement decisions. Core insight: The hardest problem is not reading documents. It is recognising that "annual turnover ≥ ₹5 crore," "yearly revenue above 50 lakhs," and a scanned CA-certified balance sheet answer the same criterion — expressed completely differently. Dyuti solves this through a Criterion Ontology: a normalised schema of what each criterion actually means, so matching happens at the semantic level, not surface text. Problem Understanding Government procurement evaluation is slow, inconsistent, and legally fragile. A single CRPF tender spans 80 pages of formal legal language; ten bidders each submit 200+ pages in mixed formats. One missed criterion means a wrongful disqualification challengeable in a tribunal. IAS officers and Lokayukta vigilance divisions require not just correct decisions but fully traceable ones. Three realities most AI solutions ignore:
Language inconsistency: Tender criteria use formal legal phrasing; bidder documents use accounting language and regional terminology — often in Kannada or other Indian languages.

Format chaos: Scanned PDFs, faded rubber stamps, handwritten CA notations, Word tables — a digital-text-only system fails on 40-60% of real Indian government submissions.

Silent disqualification trap: Systems that reject bidders without surfacing uncertainty are legally unusable. Every ambiguous case must reach a human officer with a full explanation.

Stage 1 — Tender Understanding: The Criterion Ontology Dyuti builds a Criterion Ontology — each criterion normalised into: type (financial / technical / compliance / documentation), mandatory or optional flag, normalised condition (e.g. annual_turnover >= 5_crore_INR), accepted evidence types, and an ambiguity flag for vague language. Mistral-7B (on-premises) performs initial extraction. For financial thresholds, deterministic Regex parsing is primary — the LLM is confirmation-only, never inferring a numeric figure. This eliminates hallucination risk on the criteria that matter most. A rule-based post-processor normalises units and resolves relative dates ("last 5 years" → absolute range). The ontology is shown to the procurement officer for sign-off before any bidder evaluation begins. If criterion extraction is wrong, every downstream evaluation is wrong — this step eliminates that systemic risk. Stage 2 — Bidder Document Parsing
Typed PDFs and Word files: Apache Tika with layout-aware parsing preserving table and financial statement structure.

Scanned PDFs and photographs: Two-pass OCR — Tesseract for extraction, LayoutLM for structure. Confidence scored per field, not per document. Fields below 85% confidence flagged individually — never used to reject an entire submission.

Dirty documents (faded stamps, smudged ink, overlapping seals): Adaptive thresholding, deskewing, contrast normalisation before OCR. Stamps unreadable after pre-processing escalated with raw image attached — never silently discarded.

Kannada and regional language documents: Bhashini API translates Kannada bank guarantees, local government certificates, and state registrations, mapping them to the English Criterion Ontology. Original-language document retained as authoritative audit source.

Document Integrity Check: Dyuti detects copy-pasted CA stamps via pixel-level metadata comparison across bidder PDFs and cloned signatures via bounding-box artefact analysis — a common fraud vector, surfaced as a forensic flag, not auto-disqualification.

Every extracted value stored with: document name, page number, bounding box, OCR confidence, language of origin. Stage 3 — Matching, Verdicts & Collusion Detection Three matching layers:
Exact match: Financial figures and registration numbers matched deterministically. LLM never used for numeric thresholds.

Semantic match: BGE-M3 multilingual embeddings on narrative criteria. Above 0.85 → match; below 0.65 → escalate; 0.65-0.85 → uncertain, flagged.

LLM reasoning: Mistral-7B generates a structured reasoning trace for ambiguous cases — what was found, what is missing, why it cannot decide.

Eligible: criterion met, evidence cited, confidence above threshold.

Not Eligible: criterion not met, document and value cited, reason stated.

Needs Manual Review: reason explicitly stated — OCR low / similarity borderline / language ambiguous / date unclear.

Three verdicts only — never silent rejection: Cross-Bidder Vigilance Layer The layer most AI procurement systems omit — and the one IAS and Lokayukta divisions care most about. After individual evaluation, Dyuti runs a cross-bidder forensic scan:
PDF metadata: Flags bidders sharing identical author metadata, creation timestamps, or Word template IDs.

Template fingerprinting: Detects identical sentence structures, punctuation patterns, or typographic fingerprints — including identical typos across supposedly independent CA certificates.

Financial clustering: Turnover figures suspiciously similar across bidders surfaced as coordination alerts.

Submission metadata: Identical upload IP addresses across competing bidders flagged where available.

All collusion flags are advisory, not disqualifying — surfaced as a separate Vigilance Report. The officer decides. Explainability & Reports Each bidder report covers every criterion: normalised condition, verdict, evidence with document name and page, plain-English reasoning, confidence score. A consolidated table covers all bidders. A separate Vigilance Report covers cross-bidder anomalies. Officer exports a cryptographically hashed signed PDF — the formal evaluation order basis. Audit Trail & Feasibility Every action logged to a tamper-proof append-only ledger: upload, ontology sign-off, every extraction, every verdict, every override, every vigilance flag. Full chain of evidence for tribunal challenges. Runs on NIC cloud or on-premises — no document leaves the government network. Bhashini is the sole external integration. Architecture
Ingestion: Apache Tika + Tesseract + LayoutLM + image pre-processing

Language: Bhashini API for Kannada and regional translation

Extraction: Mistral-7B on-premises + Regex deterministic parser

Matching: BGE-M3 multilingual embeddings

Integrity: Pixel-level stamp forensics + PDF metadata analysis

Collusion: Template fingerprinting + metadata clustering

Deployment: NIC cloud or on-premises; fully sovereign

Risk Register
Dirty documents: Two-pass OCR + pre-processing; unreadable fields escalated with raw image.

LLM hallucination: Regex primary; LLM confirmation-only for all numeric thresholds.

Criterion extraction errors: Officer sign-off on ontology before evaluation runs.

Regional language gaps: Bhashini integration; original document retained as authoritative source.

Document forgery: Stamp authenticity and PDF metadata forensics.

Missed collusion: Cross-bidder forensic layer with advisory vigilance flags.

Evaluation Approach
Criterion extraction: Precision and recall vs. annotated mock tender ground truth.

Matching accuracy: F1 across all three verdict types on synthetic bidder submissions.

OCR reliability: Field-level accuracy on 500+ heterogeneous scanned documents — faded, multilingual, smudged.

Escalation calibration: Genuine ambiguous cases correctly routed vs. false escalations.

Collusion detection: True positive rate on synthetic coordinated-bid scenarios.

Audit completeness: 100% of decisions traceable to source document, page, and extracted value.

Implementation Plan — Round 2
Week 1: OCR calibration on 500+ mock documents (scanned, handwritten, Kannada, faded stamps). Criterion Ontology builder with officer sign-off UI on sample CRPF tender.

Week 2: BGE-M3 + Mistral-7B matching engine; three-verdict taxonomy; Bhashini integration; stamp forensics.

Week 3: Cross-bidder collusion layer + audit ledger + React dashboard — end-to-end demo on mock CRPF tender with 5 synthetic bidders.

Policy Alignment & Scalability The Criterion Ontology is standardisable across procurement categories — enabling a shared national library of normalised criteria any ministry can adopt, aligned with the GeM digitalisation agenda and DPDPA 2023 sovereignty requirements. Dyuti does not replace the procurement officer. It gives them a defensible, vigilance-aware, fully explained basis for every decision they sign — eliminating 60-70% of evaluation time spent on manual document cross-checking.

R riyachauhan2608_3cb3
TEAM SUBMISSION [2]
Quorix – AI Decision Intelligence for Transparent and Defensible Procurement
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 11:35 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
While existing AI systems for tender evaluation focus on automating document parsing and eligibility checks, they largely overlook the core challenge of government procurement: making high-stakes decisions that are transparent, auditable, and defensible. Instead of asking “who is eligible,” Quorix addresses a more critical question: “what is the best decision, and why?”
Quorix is a decision intelligence system that augments traditional tender evaluation with reasoning, simulation, and risk-aware analysis. It uses Natural Language Processing (NLP) to semantically extract eligibility criteria and Optical Character Recognition (OCR) to process heterogeneous bidder documents, including scanned and unstructured data. A structured evaluation engine produces criterion-level decisions with clear, evidence-linked explanations.
The key innovation is a scenario-aware decision layer that enables what-if simulation of policy and threshold changes. This allows procurement officers to understand how decisions impact competition, fairness, and risk before finalization. The system also integrates fraud detection, price anomaly analysis, and bias identification to uncover hidden patterns.
Each decision is supported by confidence scores, traceable evidence, and audit-ready justifications, ensuring accountability and legal defensibility. By shifting from static evaluation to simulation-driven decision support, Quorix transforms procurement into an intelligent, transparent, and policy-aware process.

A amancodit2004_8f47
TEAM SUBMISSION [10]
TENDERWISE AI: Intelligent Eligibility Evaluation & Explainable Procurement Analysis Platform
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 06:14 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Description
Government procurement processes, such as those conducted by the Central Reserve Police Force (CRPF), involve evaluating complex tender documents and bidder submissions. These documents contain detailed eligibility criteria including technical specifications, financial thresholds, compliance requirements, and mandatory certifications.Currently, this evaluation is manual, time-consuming, and inconsistent. Procurement officers must review hundreds of pages across multiple formats—typed PDFs, scanned documents, Word files, and even images. The same information is often presented differently across bidders, increasing the risk of oversight, inconsistency, and lack of auditability.The core challenge is not just extracting information, but building a system that can understand legal and technical language, match bidder evidence to eligibility criteria, ensure explainability, and maintain audit-grade reliability.We propose TENDERWISE AI, an AI-powered document intelligence platform that automates tender understanding, bidder evaluation, and eligibility analysis with full explainability and auditability. The system transforms unstructured procurement documents into structured insights, enabling faster, consistent, and transparent decision-making.





Technical Architecture Overview


The system is designed as an end-to-end document intelligence and decision-support pipeline, integrating document processing, NLP-based understanding, rule evaluation, and explainable reporting.


1. Document Ingestion Layer
Supports multiple input formats:


Tender documents (PDF, Word)

Bidder submissions (PDF, scanned docs, images)
Capabilities:


Bulk upload and document classification

Format detection (text vs scanned vs image)

Secure storage and indexing


2. Document Processing & OCR Layer
Extracts text from diverse formats:


OCR for scanned documents and images

Table extraction for structured data

Layout-aware parsing
Technologies:


OCR engines (Tesseract / Transformer-based OCR)

Layout-aware models (LayoutLM)


3. Tender Understanding Engine
Extracts eligibility criteria from tender documents:


Technical requirements

Financial thresholds

Compliance conditions

Certification requirements
Capabilities:


Distinguish mandatory vs optional criteria

Convert criteria into structured, machine-readable format
Approach:


LLM-based semantic extraction

Rule-based classification for critical conditions


4. Bidder Document Understanding Engine
Parses and extracts relevant data from submissions:


Financial data (turnover, balance sheets)

Experience records

Certifications (GST, ISO, etc.)
Handles:


Variations in document formats

Inconsistent terminology

Partial or missing information


5. Information Mapping & Matching Engine
Maps extracted bidder data to tender criteria:


Semantic matching between criteria and evidence

Normalization of values (currency, dates, units)
Handles:


Ambiguity and incomplete data

Multiple representations of the same information


6. Evaluation & Decision Engine
Determines eligibility for each bidder:Outputs:



Eligible

Not Eligible

Needs Manual Review
Logic:


Rule-based evaluation for strict conditions

AI-assisted reasoning for complex cases


7. Explainability Engine
Ensures transparency for every decision:For each criterion:



Criterion description

Source document reference

Extracted value

Reason for pass/fail/review
Features:


Highlighted evidence from documents

Confidence scores

Clear justification


8. Human-in-the-Loop Review Layer
Handles uncertain or ambiguous cases:


Flags low-confidence decisions

Provides reasoning for ambiguity

Allows manual override and annotation
Ensures no bidder is silently disqualified.


9. Audit & Traceability Layer
Maintains a complete audit trail:


Logs every extraction and decision

Tracks document sources and versions

Records user actions and overrides
Ensures compliance with government standards.


10. Storage & Data Layer
Stores all processed data:


Raw documents

Extracted structured data

Evaluation results
Supports:


Fast retrieval

Historical analysis


11. Dashboard & Reporting Layer
Provides actionable insights for procurement officers:Features:



Criterion-wise evaluation view

Bidder comparison

Flagged cases for review

Exportable reports
Outputs:


Consolidated eligibility report

Audit-ready documentation


12. Learning & Improvement Layer
Continuously enhances system performance:


Learns from manual corrections

Improves extraction accuracy

Refines matching and evaluation models


End-to-End Flow

Procurement officer uploads tender and bidder documents

System processes documents using OCR and parsing

Eligibility criteria extracted from tender

Bidder data extracted and structured

Information mapped to criteria

Eligibility evaluated for each bidder

Explainable results generated with evidence

Ambiguous cases flagged for review

Final report generated and exported

Audit trail maintained for all decisions


Key Features

Multi-format document processing (PDF, scans, images)

Automated eligibility criteria extraction

Intelligent bidder data parsing

Explainable AI-driven evaluation

Human-in-the-loop for ambiguous cases

Full auditability and traceability

Scalable processing for large tenders


Unique Value Proposition
Unlike manual procurement workflows, TENDERWISE AI enables:Understand → Extract → Match → Evaluate → Explain → AuditIt transforms tender evaluation into a transparent, consistent, and AI-assisted decision-making process.




Impact

Reduces evaluation time from days to hours

Improves consistency across evaluators

Minimizes human error and oversight

Ensures audit-ready procurement decisions

Enhances transparency and trust


Future Scope

Integration with government e-procurement portals

Advanced legal language understanding models

Cross-tender analytics and benchmarking

Multilingual document support

Automated compliance monitoring

S shivanishingole_6bd7
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
TenderTrust - AI Driven Tender Eligibility & Evaluation for CRPF Procurement
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 06:43 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Prototype :
The prototype is designed to take a tender document along with multiple bidder submissions as input. It first processes the tender using a language model to identify and structure the eligibility requirements, clearly distinguishing between mandatory and optional conditions. Next, a document processing pipeline analyzes each bidder’s files—whether they are digital PDFs, scanned documents, or images—and extracts the relevant information needed for evaluation. This extracted data is then evaluated against the defined criteria using a decision engine, which classifies each bidder as Eligible, Not Eligible, or requiring Manual Review. Each outcome is supported with clear evidence, including the source document and the specific values used. Finally, the system compiles all results into a comprehensive report that can be reviewed and verified by a procurement officer.

Automatically reads tender documents and finds eligibility rules

Extracts required details from bidder files (PDFs, scans, images, etc.)

Checks each bidder against each rule and gives a clear result

Marks unclear or low-confidence cases for human review

Creates a final report for officers

Saves time and reduces manual effort

Gives consistent and fair evaluation

Shows proof for every decision (transparent)

Works with real-world messy documents

Keeps a proper record for audit and verification

Implementation :1. Extracting Rules form Tender Document
Extract text → PyMuPDF / pdfplumber (typed PDFs), Tesseract (scanned)

Process text → NLP (spaCy / regex for patterns like numbers, dates)

Extract criteria → LLM (OpenAI / Gemini) using prompt-based extraction

Structure output → JSON format (name, type, value, mandatory/optional)

Validate → map each rule back to source sentence in document

2.Understanding Bidder Document Pipeline:
Input types → PDF, images, scanned docs, Word files

Text extraction → PyMuPDF / OCR (Tesseract / EasyOCR / Vision API)

Store data → vector DB (FAISS / Pinecone) for easy retrieval

Process → LLM to extract required values (turnover, GST, certificates, etc.)

Track source → store document name + page/section for each value

3.Evaluation and Decision Making Rule-based + AI approach:
Compare extracted values with criteria (numbers, keywords, dates)

Assign result:

Eligible → clear match

Not Eligible → clear fail

Needs Review → missing / unclear data

Generate report → include value, rule, source document, and reason

4.Human-in-Loop System marks cases as Needs Review when:
Data is missing

Extracted value is unclear or unreadable

Multiple possible values are found

Highlighted document section

Extracted value and related criterion

Accept the result

Correct the value

Change the final decision

Show the officer: Officer can: Store corrections to improve future processing
5.Audit and Transparency : Store all decisions with source references Keep logs of extraction, matching, and changes Allow trace: criterion → value → document → decision Lock final report after approval
6.Tech stack:
Backend → FastAPI

Frontend → Streamlit (dashboard + review UI)

OCR → Tesseract / EasyOCR / Google Vision

LLM → OpenAI / Gemini

Database → PostgreSQL / MongoDB

Vector DB → FAISS / Pinecone

Risk and Mitigations :
OCR errors → Use preprocessing + manual review for unclear text

Wrong rule extraction → Map rules to source text + allow officer validation

Incorrect evaluation → Use rule-based checks + avoid auto-rejection

Missing/ambiguous data → Mark as Needs Review

Low trust in system → Provide clear evidence + audit trail

Project Description
Problem: Terrorist Activity Hidden in Web Data
Today, a massive amount of information is generated on the web-social media posts, forums, blogs, and messaging platforms. Terrorist groups often exploit these platforms to:


Communicate secretly

Spread propaganda

Recruit members

Plan and coordinate attacks
The challenge is that this data is:


Huge (Big Data scale)

Unstructured (text, images, videos)

Hidden in plain sight using coded language or slang
Traditional surveillance methods struggle to keep up with the volume, speed, and complexity of online data, making early detection difficult.

Solution: Smart Surveillance Using Web Mining
Smart surveillance systems use web mining and AI techniques to automatically detect suspicious patterns and activities online.

How it works:

Data Collection: Gather public data from websites, forums, and social media

Text Analysis (NLP): Analyze keywords, sentiments, and hidden meanings in messages

Pattern Detection: Identify unusual behaviors or connections between users

Machine Learning Models: Learn from past data to predict potential threats

Alert System: Notify authorities when suspicious activity is detected

Key Benefits:

Faster and automated monitoring

Early detection of threats

Ability to analyze massive datasets

Improved national security response

Summary
The problem is that terrorist activities are increasingly hidden within vast online data, making them hard to detect manually. The solution is to use smart surveillance systems powered by web mining and AI to automatically analyze and identify suspicious patterns in real time.

TEAM SUBMISSION [2]
NyayaFlow: AI-Driven Judicial Compliance & Action Plan Engine
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 06:57 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
1. Problem Statement
The "Last Mile" of the Indian judicial system often fails due to the complexity of legal documents. High Court and District Court judgments are frequently 50+ pages of complex legal jargon. Local administrative officers (Tehsildars, Police, Municipal Heads) struggle to:
Identify specific mandatory actions buried in the text.

Track inter-departmental responsibilities.

Ensure 100% compliance with judicial deadlines, leading to contempt of court cases.

2. Proposed Solution

NyayaFlow is a forensic intelligence platform that translates judicial "Orders" into "Operations." It uses Generative AI (Gemini 2.5 Flash) to analyze the operative part of court judgments and generate a Verified Action Plan.
The system operates in three distinct layers:
Directive Extraction: Automatically identifies "Who" must do "What" by "When."

Responsibility Matrix: Segregates tasks by department (e.g., Police vs. Revenue) to prevent administrative overlap.

Compliance Verification: A forensic ledger where ground-level officers must upload proof of action (photos/documents) against the specific court clause.

3. Key Features

Automated Action Itemization: Converts legal paragraphs into a clear, numbered checklist.

Department-Wise Tagging: Automatically routes directives to the relevant authority (Municipality, PWD, Police).

Contempt Risk Alert: Highlights specific deadlines and legal penalties mentioned in the judgment to prioritize urgent actions.

Vernacular Translation: Translates English action plans into local languages (Hindi/Kannada) for ground-level execution without misinterpretation.

Evidence-Linked Audit Trail: Every action taken is linked back to the specific page and paragraph of the court order.

4. Impact on Bharat

Reduces Judicial Backlog: Ensures that court orders are executed promptly, reducing the need for follow-up litigation.

Transparency: Provides a clear dashboard for District Collectors to monitor the execution of all pending court orders in their jurisdiction.

Administrative Efficiency: Saves thousands of man-hours spent manually summarizing legal documents across various government departments.

solutions.solution_text:

B BhartiyaNagrik
TEAM SUBMISSION [2]
NyayaPradata: An AI-Powered Court Judgment Intelligence & Compliance Tracker for CCMS
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 08:27 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Problem Karnataka's Court Case Monitoring System (CCMS) automatically fetches High Court judgment PDFs via API, but everything after that is manual. Legal officers spend 2–3 days reading 50–200 page judgments to find 2–3 buried directives. Deadlines are missed. Compliance ownership is unclear. No dashboard reflects ground-level execution status.
The Insight The ingestion pipeline already exists. The failure is post-ingestion. No system today distinguishes binding directives ("the State shall ensure...") from judicial observations ("the court notes with concern...") — the two are mixed across hundreds of pages of dense legal prose.
Our Solution: NyayaSetu An AI layer that plugs directly into CCMS after PDF ingestion and performs four steps:
Extract — Parses judgment PDFs (digital and scanned), classifies sentences as directives vs observations, and extracts: case details, date of order, key directions, parties involved, and explicit/inferred timelines.

Generate Action Plan — Structures each directive into: What action is required | Compliance or appeal decision | Responsible department | Deadline (explicit or inferred from statutory periods) | Nature of action.

Human Verification (Mandatory) — Extracted data is shown alongside source-highlighted PDF passages with confidence scores. Reviewers approve, edit, or reject each item. Only verified records proceed.

Trusted Dashboard — Department-wise view of verified action plans only. Key actions, deadlines, and responsible authorities; clean and reliable for decision-makers.

Why AI Here Is Non-Negotiable Karnataka's High Court disposes thousands of cases annually. Manual extraction cannot scale. AI reads every judgment in seconds, applies consistent extraction logic, and flags uncertainty rather than guessing — critical for formal government compliance use.
Tech Stack PyMuPDF + Tesseract OCR (digital + scanned PDFs), LLM-based two-stage pipeline (directive classifier → structured extractor), confidence-scored verification UI with PDF source highlighting, React dashboard with department-wise filtering.
Data Public Karnataka High Court judgments are freely available, no proprietary data required for prototyping.
Feasibility Plugs into existing CCMS architecture without modifying any source system. Prototype demonstrable with real public judgment PDFs within the hackathon timeline.
Impact Compliance action time reduced from days to hours. Zero missed directives. Full audit trail. Scalable across all government departments using CCMS with no additional integration effort per department.

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE

B bbsarada07_b3ff
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
ProcureAI: Multi-Tenant Tender Evaluation & Compliance Command Centre
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 08:43 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
1. Problem Relevance & Depth of Understanding
Government procurement for defence organisations like the CRPF is strictly bound by the General Financial Rules (GFR). The core operational bottleneck is not merely "reading PDFs"—it is evaluating hundreds of heterogeneous, noisy pages (scanned documents, Indic languages, stamped certificates) with zero margin for protocol violation. Current manual evaluation is severely delayed and highly vulnerable to sophisticated fraud, such as cartel ring-bidding and suspended GSTNs. Conversely, deploying standard "black-box" AI introduces the catastrophic risk of silent disqualifications—rejecting legitimate vendors due to OCR hallucinations. This triggers massive legal disputes and RTI (Right to Information) inquiries. A viable solution must handle "Bharat" edge-cases, guarantee legal transparency, and keep bureaucratic authority intact.
2.Technical Implementation & Innovation
ProcureAI is a deterministic, event-driven execution engine built for enterprise deployment, completely moving away from subjective "AI rule extraction."
Tender DNA Compiler: The FastAPI backend utilises Google Gemini 2.0 Flash (Structured Output mode) to ingest complex 500+ page ZIP files. It compiles dense legal paragraphs into strict, binary Logic-Gates (e.g., [MANDATORY] Turnover > ₹5Cr), automatically flagging restrictive clauses to prevent vendor bias.

Asynchronous "No-Timeout" Architecture: Heavy extraction tasks use background workers. The Next.js 14 frontend leverages WebSockets to stream evaluation logs cell-by-cell in real time, preventing browser timeouts on massive document loads.

Visual Decision Trees: We eliminate "black-box" fear. Every rejection is backed by an auto-generated visual flowchart mapping the exact logic path, linking extracted values directly to bounding boxes in the source document.

3. Real-World Deployability & Government Feasibility
ProcureAI is engineered strictly around Indian bureaucratic realities and GFR compliance, ensuring it can be deployed in high-stakes environments today.
Precedent-Aware HITL (Human-in-the-Loop): We enforce a "Zero Silent Disqualification" policy. Ambiguous scans trigger a "Needs Review" state, citing relevant GFR statutes and historical precedents to help the officer make a legally defensible ruling.

Cryptographic Maker-Checker Ledger: The system natively enforces hierarchy. A Junior Evaluator (Maker) initiates the matrix, but a Procurement Director (Checker) must digitally sign and lock the ledger. This generates a SHA-256 cryptographic hash to guarantee a tamper-proof, court-admissible audit trail.

Zero-Trust Live Oracles: ProcureAI verifies reality via mock live API integrations. It cross-references extracted GST numbers against the GSTN Portal to detect suspended accounts, and compares quotes against a Live Market Pricing Index to immediately flag predatory pricing.

4.Demo Quality & Presentation
The platform features a highly polished "Bureaucratic Light" UI designed for data density and extended use, mirroring a high-security intelligence terminal.
Cartel & Collusion Topology: Instead of basic risk scoring, the UI features a dynamic network graph that connects bidder data points, exposing "ring-bidding" if supposedly competing vendors share identical IP addresses, Chartered Accountants, or overlapping DINs.

Bhashini-Aligned Localisation: Features a seamless English/Hindi localisation toggle for pan-India usability.

Graceful Degradation: The architecture includes a secure local cache fallback. In the event of an API rate limit or network drop, the system gracefully loads offline data without crashing, ensuring uninterrupted operational continuity.

5. Scalability & Long-Term Impact
While initially modelled for the CRPF, ProcureAI is architected as a Multi-Tenant B2G SaaS platform capable of standardising procurement across all Ministries, State Governments, and PSUs.
National Command Centre: Features a macro-analytics executive dashboard aggregating cross-department efficiency, tracking administrative hours saved, and calculating total public funds secured from fraud.

Automated Bureaucracy: Post-evaluation, ProcureAI automatically generates formal Official Memorandums (OM) and auto-drafts legally sound RTI responses using the cryptographic audit trail.

By expanding this architecture nationwide, ProcureAI has the potential to secure billions in public funds, reduce evaluation timelines from weeks to hours, and create an immutable standard for Indian Government Procurement.

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


B banerjeesangram666_a6fc
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
Veridict Evaluation System
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 09:17 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Government procurement is a tedious and difficult process which involves many employees and their undivided attention to each and every detail in each tender which can be notoriously painstaking sometimes. Additionally, a wrongful termination might result in a legal dispute which in turn may cost both the organizations time and credibility. We decided to automate this manual process. Veridict Evaluation System aims to compare the requirements mentioned in the Government tender with the various bidder submissions and aims to find which bidders are legit and which are suspicious. It shall be noted that we are not replacing human judgement completely as any ambiguity in the report shall be addressed by an officer and the system will not reject any submission on its own. As for document scanning, our system handles it in the following way There are mainly two types of documents that we deal with-

Digital PDF/ Word documents - These are the easiest to scan. We use pdfplumber to extract the text and tables directly and then we perform Semantic classification to categorize the texts into various important criteria.

Scanned PDF/ Photographs - We initially check whether there are text layers or not using pdfplumber. After the check we use PyMuPDF to convert each PDF page into a high resolution image. After this process we use OpenCV to perform deskewing using Hough line detection. This is done to correct page rotation and adapt to uneven lighting and shadows. After this preprocessing step is over we run two OCR engines in parallel. Azure Document Intelligence serves as the primary engine as it is the best at handling rubber stamps, Hindi and English text and embedded tables while returning per word confidence scores. Tesseract 5 in LSTM mode runs as the fallback.

This scanner works for both tender and the bidder submissions and produces a JSON output in which the most important criteria of the documents are stored.
The system then evaluates every bidder submission against the tender document based on the JSON output produced from the scanners. It compares them field by field, item descriptions using fuzzy token matching to handle wording variations, quantities using direct numeric comparison, ISO certifications by normalized identifier matching and financial values after full currency normalization across all Indian formats. Every single check produces an explicit result - Pass or Flagged, along with the reason documented. The overall verdict is derived strictly from the criterion results. If all the checks pass then the submission is marked as eligible. Else, any flag marks the submission for manual review. As you can see the system never autonomously rejects a bidder. The system outputs a report regarding each bidder submission which a designated officer can read and then make decision. Therefore, what once took a committee days of checking and cross referencing is now reduced to a few minutes.
Github Repo for our project - https://github.com/sangram528/Veridict_Evaluation_System.git

solutions.team_name: Dyuti — A Criterion Ontology Engine for Explainable, Vigilance-Aware Bid Evaluation in Government Procurement
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


V vaanya2708_e83e
TEAM SUBMISSION [6]
Dyuti — A Criterion Ontology Engine for Explainable, Vigilance-Aware Bid Evaluation in Government Procurement
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 10:09 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
What we build: A three-stage AI pipeline that transforms heterogeneous tender and bidder documents — typed PDFs, scanned certificates, photographs, Word files, and Kannada-language certificates — into a structured, criterion-by-criterion evaluation with confidence scores, source citations, a collusion detection layer, and a tamper-proof audit trail for formal government procurement decisions. Core insight: The hardest problem is not reading documents. It is recognising that "annual turnover ≥ ₹5 crore," "yearly revenue above 50 lakhs," and a scanned CA-certified balance sheet answer the same criterion — expressed completely differently. Dyuti solves this through a Criterion Ontology: a normalised schema of what each criterion actually means, so matching happens at the semantic level, not surface text. Problem Understanding Government procurement evaluation is slow, inconsistent, and legally fragile. A single CRPF tender spans 80 pages of formal legal language; ten bidders each submit 200+ pages in mixed formats. One missed criterion means a wrongful disqualification challengeable in a tribunal. IAS officers and Lokayukta vigilance divisions require not just correct decisions but fully traceable ones. Three realities most AI solutions ignore:
Language inconsistency: Tender criteria use formal legal phrasing; bidder documents use accounting language and regional terminology — often in Kannada or other Indian languages.

Format chaos: Scanned PDFs, faded rubber stamps, handwritten CA notations, Word tables — a digital-text-only system fails on 40-60% of real Indian government submissions.

Silent disqualification trap: Systems that reject bidders without surfacing uncertainty are legally unusable. Every ambiguous case must reach a human officer with a full explanation.

Stage 1 — Tender Understanding: The Criterion Ontology Dyuti builds a Criterion Ontology — each criterion normalised into: type (financial / technical / compliance / documentation), mandatory or optional flag, normalised condition (e.g. annual_turnover >= 5_crore_INR), accepted evidence types, and an ambiguity flag for vague language. Mistral-7B (on-premises) performs initial extraction. For financial thresholds, deterministic Regex parsing is primary — the LLM is confirmation-only, never inferring a numeric figure. This eliminates hallucination risk on the criteria that matter most. A rule-based post-processor normalises units and resolves relative dates ("last 5 years" → absolute range). The ontology is shown to the procurement officer for sign-off before any bidder evaluation begins. If criterion extraction is wrong, every downstream evaluation is wrong — this step eliminates that systemic risk. Stage 2 — Bidder Document Parsing
Typed PDFs and Word files: Apache Tika with layout-aware parsing preserving table and financial statement structure.

Scanned PDFs and photographs: Two-pass OCR — Tesseract for extraction, LayoutLM for structure. Confidence scored per field, not per document. Fields below 85% confidence flagged individually — never used to reject an entire submission.

Dirty documents (faded stamps, smudged ink, overlapping seals): Adaptive thresholding, deskewing, contrast normalisation before OCR. Stamps unreadable after pre-processing escalated with raw image attached — never silently discarded.

Kannada and regional language documents: Bhashini API translates Kannada bank guarantees, local government certificates, and state registrations, mapping them to the English Criterion Ontology. Original-language document retained as authoritative audit source.

Document Integrity Check: Dyuti detects copy-pasted CA stamps via pixel-level metadata comparison across bidder PDFs and cloned signatures via bounding-box artefact analysis — a common fraud vector, surfaced as a forensic flag, not auto-disqualification.

Every extracted value stored with: document name, page number, bounding box, OCR confidence, language of origin. Stage 3 — Matching, Verdicts & Collusion Detection Three matching layers:
Exact match: Financial figures and registration numbers matched deterministically. LLM never used for numeric thresholds.

Semantic match: BGE-M3 multilingual embeddings on narrative criteria. Above 0.85 → match; below 0.65 → escalate; 0.65-0.85 → uncertain, flagged.

LLM reasoning: Mistral-7B generates a structured reasoning trace for ambiguous cases — what was found, what is missing, why it cannot decide.

Eligible: criterion met, evidence cited, confidence above threshold.

Not Eligible: criterion not met, document and value cited, reason stated.

Needs Manual Review: reason explicitly stated — OCR low / similarity borderline / language ambiguous / date unclear.

Three verdicts only — never silent rejection: Cross-Bidder Vigilance Layer The layer most AI procurement systems omit — and the one IAS and Lokayukta divisions care most about. After individual evaluation, Dyuti runs a cross-bidder forensic scan:
PDF metadata: Flags bidders sharing identical author metadata, creation timestamps, or Word template IDs.

Template fingerprinting: Detects identical sentence structures, punctuation patterns, or typographic fingerprints — including identical typos across supposedly independent CA certificates.

Financial clustering: Turnover figures suspiciously similar across bidders surfaced as coordination alerts.

Submission metadata: Identical upload IP addresses across competing bidders flagged where available.

All collusion flags are advisory, not disqualifying — surfaced as a separate Vigilance Report. The officer decides. Explainability & Reports Each bidder report covers every criterion: normalised condition, verdict, evidence with document name and page, plain-English reasoning, confidence score. A consolidated table covers all bidders. A separate Vigilance Report covers cross-bidder anomalies. Officer exports a cryptographically hashed signed PDF — the formal evaluation order basis. Audit Trail & Feasibility Every action logged to a tamper-proof append-only ledger: upload, ontology sign-off, every extraction, every verdict, every override, every vigilance flag. Full chain of evidence for tribunal challenges. Runs on NIC cloud or on-premises — no document leaves the government network. Bhashini is the sole external integration. Architecture
Ingestion: Apache Tika + Tesseract + LayoutLM + image pre-processing

Language: Bhashini API for Kannada and regional translation

Extraction: Mistral-7B on-premises + Regex deterministic parser

Matching: BGE-M3 multilingual embeddings

Integrity: Pixel-level stamp forensics + PDF metadata analysis

Collusion: Template fingerprinting + metadata clustering

Deployment: NIC cloud or on-premises; fully sovereign

Risk Register
Dirty documents: Two-pass OCR + pre-processing; unreadable fields escalated with raw image.

LLM hallucination: Regex primary; LLM confirmation-only for all numeric thresholds.

Criterion extraction errors: Officer sign-off on ontology before evaluation runs.

Regional language gaps: Bhashini integration; original document retained as authoritative source.

Document forgery: Stamp authenticity and PDF metadata forensics.

Missed collusion: Cross-bidder forensic layer with advisory vigilance flags.

Evaluation Approach
Criterion extraction: Precision and recall vs. annotated mock tender ground truth.

Matching accuracy: F1 across all three verdict types on synthetic bidder submissions.

OCR reliability: Field-level accuracy on 500+ heterogeneous scanned documents — faded, multilingual, smudged.

Escalation calibration: Genuine ambiguous cases correctly routed vs. false escalations.

Collusion detection: True positive rate on synthetic coordinated-bid scenarios.

Audit completeness: 100% of decisions traceable to source document, page, and extracted value.

Implementation Plan — Round 2
Week 1: OCR calibration on 500+ mock documents (scanned, handwritten, Kannada, faded stamps). Criterion Ontology builder with officer sign-off UI on sample CRPF tender.

Week 2: BGE-M3 + Mistral-7B matching engine; three-verdict taxonomy; Bhashini integration; stamp forensics.

Week 3: Cross-bidder collusion layer + audit ledger + React dashboard — end-to-end demo on mock CRPF tender with 5 synthetic bidders.

Policy Alignment & Scalability The Criterion Ontology is standardisable across procurement categories — enabling a shared national library of normalised criteria any ministry can adopt, aligned with the GeM digitalisation agenda and DPDPA 2023 sovereignty requirements. Dyuti does not replace the procurement officer. It gives them a defensible, vigilance-aware, fully explained basis for every decision they sign — eliminating 60-70% of evaluation time spent on manual document cross-checking.

TEAM SUBMISSION [2]
Quorix – AI Decision Intelligence for Transparent and Defensible Procurement
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 26, 2026 11:35 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
While existing AI systems for tender evaluation focus on automating document parsing and eligibility checks, they largely overlook the core challenge of government procurement: making high-stakes decisions that are transparent, auditable, and defensible. Instead of asking “who is eligible,” Quorix addresses a more critical question: “what is the best decision, and why?”
Quorix is a decision intelligence system that augments traditional tender evaluation with reasoning, simulation, and risk-aware analysis. It uses Natural Language Processing (NLP) to semantically extract eligibility criteria and Optical Character Recognition (OCR) to process heterogeneous bidder documents, including scanned and unstructured data. A structured evaluation engine produces criterion-level decisions with clear, evidence-linked explanations.
The key innovation is a scenario-aware decision layer that enables what-if simulation of policy and threshold changes. This allows procurement officers to understand how decisions impact competition, fairness, and risk before finalization. The system also integrates fraud detection, price anomaly analysis, and bias identification to uncover hidden patterns.
Each decision is supported by confidence scores, traceable evidence, and audit-ready justifications, ensuring accountability and legal defensibility. By shifting from static evaluation to simulation-driven decision support, Quorix transforms procurement into an intelligent, transparent, and policy-aware process.

Project Description
Project Title: DocuMind
This project is a smart reading and annotation mobile application designed to support students, teachers, and researchers by providing a seamless and distraction-free study environment. The application works in both offline and online modes, ensuring uninterrupted access to learning materials anytime, anywhere.
The app supports multiple document formats, including PDF, images, and Word files, allowing users to read, analyze, and interact with content efficiently within a single platform.
Key Features

1. Offline + Online Accessibility The application is fully functional offline, enabling users to access documents and use core features without internet connectivity. Online mode enhances the experience by allowing deeper research and web navigation when required.
2. Smart Text Selection & Instant Meaning Users can select any word or sentence within the document to trigger a contextual pop-up menu.
“Meaning” option displays definitions instantly using an offline dictionary, ensuring zero distraction from notifications or internet dependency.

“Search” option allows users to navigate to relevant web resources for deeper understanding.

3. Floating Sticky Notes for Meaning Display Selected word meanings are shown in a semi-transparent floating sticky note UI, allowing users to view definitions without losing focus on the original content. This enhances readability and multitasking.
4. Distraction-Free Offline Dictionary An integrated offline dictionary engine ensures that students can learn meanings without needing internet access, promoting focused and uninterrupted study sessions.
5. Smart Annotation & Note-Taking System Users can annotate directly on PDFs, images, or documents by simply long-pressing (3 seconds) anywhere on the screen.
Notes can be typed using the keypad.

The app converts typed text into natural cursive handwriting style, making annotations appear personalized and realistic.

6. Flexible Saving Options Users can:
Save edits within the same file

Or create a new version of the document, preserving the original content

? Additional Innovative Features

7. Multi-Layer Annotation System Users can add multiple notes without clutter, toggle visibility, or organize annotations for better readability.
8. Highlighting & Bookmarking Important sections can be highlighted and bookmarked for quick revision.
9. Cross-Format Learning Hub A unified interface to handle PDFs, Word documents, and images eliminates the need for multiple apps.
10. Focus Mode (Distraction-Free Study) Offline-first design ensures minimal interruptions, helping users maintain deep focus while studying.

TEAM SUBMISSION [2]
Verdict-2-Action
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 27, 2026 05:58 AM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description

Verdict2Action – From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans

Government departments handle thousands of court cases where every judgment carries critical directives—compliance orders, deadlines, or decisions on whether to appeal. However, these judgments are long, complex PDF documents with unstructured legal language, making it difficult for officials to quickly identify what actions are required. This leads to delays, missed deadlines, and inefficient decision-making. Verdict2Action is an AI-powered decision support system that transforms court judgments into clear, actionable insights—enabling faster, more reliable governance.
The system begins by ingesting court judgment PDFs (both scanned and digital) and intelligently extracting key information such as case details, parties involved, dates, and most importantly, court directives. What sets Verdict2Action apart is how it presents this information through two powerful, user-driven modes:
Smart Highlight View: The original judgment is displayed with AI-generated highlights directly on the PDF. Critical directives are marked in red, important instructions in orange, and contextual information in yellow—allowing users to instantly identify what matters without reading the entire document.


Executive Brief: For quick decision-making, the system generates a concise 2–3 page simplified document summarizing the case, key directions, timelines, and recommended actions in clear, non-legal language.

Building on this understanding, Verdict2Action’s Action Engine converts extracted insights into structured, decision-ready action plans. These include whether compliance is required or an appeal should be considered, the responsible department, key deadlines (explicit or inferred), and the reasoning behind each recommendation—all linked back to the original judgment for full transparency.
To ensure trust and accuracy, the system incorporates a human-in-the-loop verification layer. Officials can review extracted data and action plans alongside highlighted source text, with confidence scores provided for each element. They can approve, edit, or reject outputs—ensuring that only verified information is used for decision-making.
Once approved, actions are tracked through a Compliance Tracker and Dashboard, offering a clean, department-wise view of responsibilities, upcoming deadlines, and priority tasks. Smart notifications and reminders (e.g., days before deadlines) ensure that no critical directive is missed.
Verdict2Action is not just an extraction tool—it is a complete pipeline that bridges the gap between legal complexity and administrative action. By combining visual explainability, AI-driven insights, and human validation, it empowers government officials to move from lengthy judgments to timely, informed decisions with confidence.

Project Description
1. Problem Understanding
Government tender evaluation is slow and inconsistent due to:


Manual review of lengthy, complex documents

Criteria scattered across technical, financial, and compliance sections

Ambiguous legal language ("must," "should," conditional clauses)

Bidder submissions in mixed formats (PDFs, scans, images)

Lack of standard evaluation → errors, delays, low transparency


2. Proposed Solution
TenderAI Assist – a decision-support platform for automated, explainable evaluation.Workflow:



Upload tender document

Extract and classify criteria (mandatory/optional)

Convert clauses into structured logic

Upload bidder documents

Extract structured data

Match against criteria

Output: Eligible / Not Eligible / Needs Review

Generate explainable reports + risk score

Compare bidders via dashboard


3. AI & Technology Usage

OCR: Extract text from scanned docs (Tesseract/PaddleOCR)

NLP Models: Identify eligibility clauses, classify conditions

Clause Logic Engine: Convert text → logical expressions

Example: (Projects ≥ 3) OR (Turnover ≥ ₹5Cr)

Semantic Matching: Match bidder content with criteria

Confidence Scoring: Detect uncertainty

Multilingual AI: Support Hindi + regional languages

User → Upload → OCR → NLP Processing → Clause Logic Engine → Data Extraction → Matching Engine → Risk Scoring → Explainability Layer → Dashboard + Audit Logs

Full logs (who, when, what)

Document version tracking

Exportable compliance reports


6. Edge Cases

Low-quality scans: Image preprocessing + OCR confidence

Missing data: Flagged for review

Ambiguous clauses: Sent to human validation

Format variation: Standardized parsing layer

Low-confidence cases → flagged

An officer reviews, edits, overrides

Ensures accountability and trust

Frontend: React

Easily integrates with existing e-procurement systems.

70%+ reduction in evaluation time

Improved consistency and accuracy

Transparent, auditable decisions

Reduced manual workload

OCR errors → confidence scoring + review

Legal ambiguity → human validation

OCR + document ingestion

Rule-based criteria extraction

Clause logic engine (basic)

Matching + eligibility output

Dashboard with explainability

Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 27, 2026 08:18 AM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
1. Problem Understanding
Government departments receive thousands of court judgments, but the real bottleneck is not access—it is actionability.Current gaps:



Judgments are long, complex, and often scanned PDFs.

Critical directives are buried in legal language.

No standardized way to track compliance or appeal timelines

Manual interpretation leads to delays, errors, and missed deadlines.
Core problem: Converting court judgments into verified, department-ready action plans with accountability and timelines.


2. Proposed Solution (Step-by-Step Flow)
Step 1: Ingestion


Upload PDF (scanned or digital) via CCMS

OCR applied if needed
Step 2: Intelligent Extraction


Extract: case details, parties, order date, directives, timelines
Step 3: Directive-to-Action Engine


Convert legal text into structured tasks Example: “Respondent shall reinstate within 30 days” →

Action: Reinstate employee

Deadline: +30 days

Department: HR/Admin
Step 4: Timeline Intelligence


Extract explicit deadlines

Infer missing ones (e.g., appeal windows based on case type)

Flag urgency
Step 5: Risk Priority Scoring

&
High: Immediate compliance / expiring appeal window

Medium: Defined timelines

Low: Informational or long-term
Step 6: Human Verification Layer


Show extracted fields with PDF highlights

Allow approval/editing/rejection

Display confidence scores
Step 7: Verified Dashboard


Only approved data is visible.

Department-wise actionable view

Alerts for deadlines and risks


3. AI & Technology Usage

OCR (Tesseract / PaddleOCR) Handles scanned judgments reliably

LLM-based Legal Extraction (fine-tuned) Identifies legal entities, directives, and obligations

NER + Rule-based Hybrid Models Ensures precision for dates, parties, case numbers

Directive-to-Action Transformer Converts unstructured legal text → structured tasks

Timeline Engine (Rules + ML) Infers deadlines using legal norms (e.g., appeal limits)

Confidence Scoring Model Combines extraction probability + text clarity


4. System Architecture (Pipeline)

Input Layer (PDF Upload via CCMS)

Preprocessing (OCR + cleaning)

Extraction Engine (LLM + NER)

Directive-to-Action Engine

Timeline & Risk Engine

Verification Interface (Human-in-loop)

Storage (Verified vs Raw Data Separation)

Dashboard & Alerts System


5. Explainability & Auditability
Strong focus to build trust in government workflows:


Evidence Highlighting Every extracted field is linked to exact PDF text.

Click-to-View Context Officers can see original sentence instantly.

Who verified

What changes were made

When decisions were taken

6. Handling Edge Cases

Poor scan quality → fallback OCR + manual flag

Ambiguous directives → marked “Needs Review”

Multiple conflicting timelines → highlight all + flag risk

Missing deadlines → inferred + labeled as “estimated”

Complex multi-department cases → split into sub-actions


7. Human-in-the-Loop Design
Not automation—decision support:

Inline editing of fields

Approve / Reject / Modify actions

Mandatory verification for high-risk cases

Feedback loop improves model accuracy over time


8. Feasibility & Deployment

Integrates directly with existing CCMS

Cloud or NIC-hosted deployment

Works with existing PDF archives

Scalable across departments and states

Zero missed deadlines via alerts

2–3x faster compliance execution

Improved legal outcomes (timely appeals)

Standardized decision-making across departments


10. Risks & Trade-offs

Extraction errors → mitigated via human verification

Over-reliance on AI → prevented by approval workflow

Legal variability → handled via hybrid rules + learning

Initial training effort → improves rapidly with usage


11. Implementation Plan (MVP)
Week 1–2:


OCR + basic extraction (case details, dates)
Week 3–4:


Directive-to-Action Engine (rule-based + LLM prompts)
Week 5:


Timeline inference + risk scoring
Week 6:


Verification UI with PDF highlighting

Dashboard + alerts
MVP Output:


End-to-end pipeline from PDF → verified action plan

S sunflower
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
PramanAI: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 27, 2026 12:23 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
PramanAI: Court Judgment to Action Plan System
Problem Statement

India’s court system processes a very large number of cases, and government departments are frequently involved in compliance, appeal, and follow-up work. The main challenge is not only volume, but the format of the judgments. Most judgments arrive as long PDF files with dense legal language, scattered directions, and no clear action summary. Officials must read the full document to find what needs to be done, who is responsible, and what deadline applies. This creates delay, increases manual workload, and raises the risk of missed directives.

Proposed Solution

PramanAI is an AI system that reads court judgment PDFs and turns them into structured, human-verified action plans. It is designed for government use, where accuracy, explainability, and accountability matter more than speed alone. The system does not try to make legal decisions on its own. Instead, it helps officials quickly understand the judgment, identify the action required, and verify the output before it is used.

Architecture


Step 1: Judgment Ingestion

The system receives a court judgment PDF through upload or integration with court systems (API). Metadata such as case ID and department ID is captured at this stage.

Step 2: Document Parsing

OCR Model extracts text, tables, and layout from the PDF while preserving the reading order. This helps the system understand the structure of the judgment instead of treating it as plain text.

Step 3: Legal Entity Extraction

A legal NER(Named Entity Recognition) model identifies important entities such as parties, dates, statutes, and case references. This creates a cleaner and more precise document understanding layer.
Step 4: Key Direction Detection

The system extracts the important parts of the judgment, including the final order, required compliance, appeal-related signals, and timelines mentioned in the text.

Step 5: Structured Action Plan Generation

LangChain and Groq convert the extracted information into a structured action plan. The output is constrained to a fixed schema so the data stays consistent across cases.

Step 6: Source Highlighting

Each extracted field is linked to the relevant text snippet from the judgment. This gives reviewers direct evidence for verification.

Step 7: Human Verification

A legal or administrative reviewer checks the AI output, edits incorrect fields if needed, and approves or rejects the record.

Step 8: Trusted Dashboard

Only verified action plans are displayed on the dashboard for department-level monitoring, filtering, and follow-up.



Tech Stack


Layer
Technologies
Frontend
Next.js, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui, React Query
Backend
Python, FastAPI, LangChain, Groq, MinerU, HuggingFace
Database
MongoDB
Authentication
OAuth 2.0, JWT


Feasibility and Viability


Technical feasibility is high because the system uses proven components for each stage, including FastAPI for APIs, MinerU for PDF parsing, legal NER for entity extraction, and Groq for structured generation.

Integration feasibility is strong because the input format is standard PDF and the system can connect to existing court or department workflows through APIs.

Accuracy can be controlled through layout-aware extraction, legal domain NER, strict structured output, and human verification before publication.

Explainability is built in because every field in the action plan is linked to source text from the original judgment.

Cost viability is reasonable because the stack uses open-source and efficient tools, with cloud scaling used only when needed.


Impact and Benefits


Faster decision-making by reducing the time needed to understand a judgment from hours to minutes.

Lower manual effort for officials, with expected time savings of around 60% to 70% in document review and action extraction.

Better compliance tracking because every action, deadline, and responsible department is stored in a structured format.

Trusted outputs because no record reaches the dashboard without human verification.

Better inter-department coordination because the action plan gives a shared and consistent view of what must happen next.

Improved transparency because every output can be traced back to the exact judgment text that generated it.


Closing Summary

PramanAI turns long and complex court judgments into verified action plans that government teams can actually use. It combines document understanding, legal extraction, structured generation, and human review into one practical workflow. The result is faster compliance, stronger accountability, and a more reliable way to manage post-judgment action.

M mukesh482_0d62
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
TenderAI: An AI-Powered Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis Platform for Government Procurement.
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 27, 2026 12:44 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
TenderAI: An AI-Powered Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis Platform for Government Procurement — A Document Intelligence System for Automated Criterion Extraction, Multi-Format Bid Parsing, and Explainable Audit-Ready Decision Support in CRPF Procurement Workflows.
01 — Problem Understanding
The real challenge

Procurement under GFR and CVC guidelines is high-stakes — every disqualification can be legally challenged and every oversight can trigger re-tendering. The problem is structured reasoning over unstructured data with full auditability, not simply automation.
Tenders run 60–200 pages; eligibility criteria are buried in legal prose, not neatly listed.

Bidder documents arrive as typed PDFs, scanned certificates, phone photographs, Word files, and embedded tables — each requiring a different extraction approach.

Vague language like "similar projects" creates evaluator inconsistency that can be exploited in appeals.

Every decision must trace back to a specific criterion, document, and piece of evidence.

Success means consistent, explainable, human-verifiable decisions — and honest surfacing of uncertainty. A confident wrong answer is worse than "needs review."

02 — Criterion Extraction
From tender prose to structured rules

Ingestion: Typed PDFs → PyMuPDF; scanned PDFs → Google Document AI or AWS Textract (trained on Indian government layouts, bilingual Hindi/English); DOCX → python-docx. All outputs preserve page-level provenance.
LLM extraction: Claude Sonnet, with a structured system prompt, identifies every bidder requirement and returns a strict JSON object per criterion — never free text. Each object includes: criterion ID, type (Technical / Financial / Compliance / Documentation / Certification), mandatory flag ("shall"/"must" = mandatory; "preferred"/"desirable" = optional), operator, threshold, and source page.
Officer sign-off: Before any evaluation runs, the officer reviews and approves the criterion list. This creates a signed, version-controlled criterion manifest — the audit anchor for the entire evaluation. Ambiguous criteria (e.g., undefined "similar projects") are flagged for officer clarification at this stage.

03 — Bidder Document Parsing
Handling every document format

Every uploaded file is classified and routed to the appropriate pipeline. Photographs receive OpenCV pre-processing (perspective correction, CLAHE contrast normalisation, Sauvola binarisation) before OCR. If confidence falls below 70%, the document is flagged for manual review — never silently processed.
Extraction is criterion-targeted via RAG: each criterion queries the bidder's embedded document corpus (pgvector), and retrieved chunks are passed to the LLM with an explicit instruction to return null with a reason rather than guess when information is unclear.
A wrong low-confidence extraction is worse than an honest "not found" — it creates an invisible error. The system is designed to fail loudly.

04 — Matching & Evaluation
Verdict logic

A deterministic rules engine — not LLM judgement — applies this decision table to each bidder–criterion pair:
Condition
Verdict
Value found, meets threshold, confidence ≥ 80%
Eligible
Value found, fails threshold, confidence ≥ 80%
Not eligible
Value found, confidence 50–80%
Needs manual review
Value not found in any document
Needs manual review — "not evidenced"
Document unreadable
Needs manual review — "document quality"
Optional criterion, not submitted
Noted — no disqualification

Language variation ("civil works" vs "construction services") is handled via sentence-embedding semantic similarity and a procurement synonym map. Any semantic match is flagged for officer confirmation.

05 — Explainability & Human Review
Criterion-level verdicts

Every verdict states: which criterion, which document and page, what was extracted, what was required, and why. No silent decisions.
C-003 — EligibleCriterionAnnual turnover ≥ ₹5,00,00,000 (mandatory)SourceBidder A / Balance_Sheet_FY2024.pdf, p.3 Table 2Extracted₹7,23,45,000 — 96% confidence (digital text)Decision₹7.23 cr ≥ ₹5.00 cr — threshold met.C-003 — Needs manual reviewCriterionAnnual turnover ≥ ₹5,00,00,000 (mandatory)SourceBidder F / CA_Certificate.pdf, p.1Extracted₹[UNCLEAR] — 42% confidence, low print qualityActionOfficer to verify turnover figure in original document.
Three principles: never silently reject — every uncertain case is surfaced with a reason. Minimal reviewer burden — officer sees exactly what was found and where to look. Officer decision is final — recorded with name, reason, and timestamp; system never overrides.

06 — Auditability
Immutable audit trail

An append-only, hash-chained PostgreSQL log captures: tender document hash; signed criterion manifest; per bidder–criterion — document used, extracted text, confidence, verdict; and per manual review — officer identity, decision, reason, timestamp. Records cannot be modified, only annotated. The final report exports as a signed PDF with a cryptographic hash of all inputs.

07 — Architecture & Technology
Stack and justification

Component
Choice
Why
LLM
Claude Sonnet
Best structured JSON output; low hallucination; bilingual
OCR — scanned
Google Document AI
Indian govt format accuracy; per-character confidence
OCR — photos
Textract + OpenCV
Handles distortion; bounding-box provenance
RAG / embeddings
text-embedding-3-large + pgvector
Strong semantic retrieval; co-located with audit DB
PDF/table extraction
PyMuPDF + Camelot
Handles bordered and borderless tables
Backend / DB
FastAPI + PostgreSQL
Async, type-safe, ACID-compliant, row-level versioning

Fully containerised (Docker/Kubernetes). Deployable on NIC Cloud, fully on-premise (open-weight model for air-gapped environments), or hybrid with data residency controls.

08 — Risks & Trade-offs
Key risks and mitigations

Risk
Mitigation
LLM hallucination in criterion extraction
Officer signs criterion manifest before any evaluation; extraction cross-checked against source page
OCR failure on poor photographs
Confidence threshold forces manual review — never silent disqualification
Semantic mismatch (false negative)
Synonym map + semantic similarity; every semantic match flagged for officer confirmation
Legal challenge to AI decision
Decision-support only; mandatory officer sign-off; full audit trail makes decisions defensible
Model version drift
Runs pinned to model version; version recorded in audit log


09 — Round 2 Plan
Implementation roadmap

Sprint
Deliverable
When
1
Ingestion pipeline — typed PDF, DOCX, OCR for scanned docs
Wk 1–2
2
Criterion extractor + officer review UI
Wk 2–3
3
Bidder RAG pipeline — embed, retrieve, extract
Wk 3–4
4
Matching engine + manual review queue
Wk 4–5
5
Audit log + signed PDF report
Wk 5–6
6
End-to-end integration + edge case testing
Wk 6–7
7
Polish, optimisation, demo
Wk 7–8

Target metrics: criterion extraction precision ≥ 95%; evidence recall ≥ 90% typed / ≥ 80% scanned; zero silent disqualifications; 10-bidder evaluation in under 5 minutes.
TenderAI assists procurement officers — it never replaces them. Every decision is consistent, explainable, and auditable.

solutions.team_name: OrderDesk: Turning court orders into timely government action!!!!
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


P priyadevraj006_16b3
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
OrderDesk: Turning court orders into timely government action!!!!
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 27, 2026 01:27 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
1) Problem we are trying to solve:
Government departments in Karnataka receive court judgment PDFs through CCMS daily. Each document runs 30 to 80 pages of complex legal language. No tool currently surfaces what action is needed, by which department, and by when forcing officials to read every word manually. Missing a deadline or misreading an order can have serious legal and administrative consequences.

This causes 4 crtical failures: 1) Compliance deadlines are missed because directives are buried in text. 2) Appeal limitation periods expire before a decision is even made. 3) Accountability gaps arise because no one is formally assigned to act. 4) Scale of the problem: Karnataka High Court disposes approximately 'N' no.of cases per month involving government departments. Each requires manual review by a designated officer with no tracking system

2 to 3 hours: Manual read time per judgement --> ~< 10 minutes: with OrderDesk review time --> 0 risk of missed directives.

2) My solution: OrderDesk
It is a 4 stage pipeline that plugs directly into CCMS. It reads judgment PDFs, pulls out every critical directive, builds a structured action plan, puts a human reviewer in control before anything is acted upon, and then displays only verified records on a clean government dashboard.

The core design principle: AI does the reading, humans make the decisions. Nothing reaches the dashboard without a Section Officer or Law Officer in the department's legal cell approving it first, the exact people currently responsible for manually reading and acting on judgment copies received through CCMS. ie: purpose-built for government use with human oversight at every step.

3) The Idea:
i) Extract -> Read & understand the judgement
Judgment PDFs arrive from CCMS via API. Scanned pages go through PaddleOCR; digital PDFs are parsed with PyMuPDF. A legal-tuned LLM then extracts:

Case number, court name, bench composition

Date of order and disposal status

Parties involved - petitioner, respondent, department name

Key directions and operative clauses (the "shall", "directed to", "ordered" language)

Explicit timelines mentioned in the judgment

Prompt engineering approach: ie: how to make the LLM extract legal clauses accurately? The LLM is prompted using a structured JSON output format with role-based legal instructions like the model is told it is a legal analyst reviewing a High Court judgment, and must return only structured fields (case number, parties, directives, timelines) with a confidence score per field. Directive detection uses keyword-anchored prompting, the model specifically looks for operative legal language such as "directed to", "shall forthwith", "ordered to pay", "liberty to apply", reducing hallucination on non-directive paragraphs.

Multi language PDF handling: Karnataka judgments can be in Kannada or English. For English PDFs, PyMuPDF handles parsing directly. For Kannada or mixed-language documents, PaddleOCR's multilingual model (which supports Kannada script) handles OCR before passing text to the LLM. If the detected language is Kannada, the extracted text is passed through a translation step (Google Translate API - free tier) before LLM processing, ensuring the action plan is always generated and reviewed in English for consistency across departments.

ii) Generate -> Build a structured action plan
This is the key differentiator. The AI does not just summarize, it reasons about what needs to happen next:

Comply or Appeal? - classified from directive language patterns

Deadline inference - explicit dates extracted directly; implicit ones derived from standard limitation periods (90 days for HC appeal under CPC, 30 days for compliance orders, etc.)

Department mapping - responsible authority identified from respondent details and subject matter (land, tax, service, tender)

Action type - Comply / File SLP / Pay compensation / Submit status report / Obtain stay.

Sample output --> Action: Comply | Department: Revenue | Deadline: within 60 days of order | Inferred date: [dates accordingly] | Confidence: High

iii) Verify -> Human in the loop (HITL), mandatory before anything moves
Every extracted field must be approved by the designated Section Officer or Law Officer assigned to that case in CCMS before it becomes an action record. The verification screen shows:

PDF viewer on the left each extracted field highlighted at its exact source location

Extracted data panel on the right, with confidence score per field (High / Medium / Low) [tbh, scores won't be perfectly calibrated. But for demo it works fine]

Per-field controls: Approve ✓ | Edit ✎ | Reject ✗

Full audit trail - who reviewed, when, what was changed

Record only moves to dashboard after full approval A low-confidence field (e.g., inferred deadline) is flagged in amber and requires mandatory manual confirmation before it can be approved.


iv) Dashboard -> Clean & structured view for decision makers
Only verified records appear here. The dashboard shows:

Department-wise filtered action cards

Each card: Case ID · Action required · Deadline · Assigned officer

Red / Amber / Green status based on days remaining to deadline

Role-based access - Viewer, Reviewer, Admin

One-click PDF export for offline circulation and file noting [low priority feature]

Escalation alerts when a deadline crosses into the red zone


4) Tech stacks:
PDF parsing & OCR for scanned pages --> PyMuPDF + PaddleOCR

AI Extraction & Action Plan --> Groq API - Llama 3.1 70B with structured JSON prompting for legal clause extraction. Gemini 1.5 Flash as fallback [Free tier - as of now planned]

Backend --> FastAPI (Python)

Frontend --> React + TailWindCSS

Database --> SQLite for demo (zero setup, fully local, built into Python). PostgreSQL at deployment stage.

PDF highlight viewer --> PDF.js

Auth --> Role based (Viewer / Reviewer / Admin) via JWT authentication - LDAP and SSO compatible for government network integration at deployment stage [ie: demo uses JWT (free), and when the government actually deploys it on their network, it plugs into their existing LDAP/AD system which they already own], Login system - username & password, Session management - FastAPI

Hosting demo --> localhost or render.com free tier

Deployment (if needed) --> Docker + Ngnix

IMPORTANT NOTE: Data Privacy: Court judgments are public record under the Right to Information Act and are already accessible via CCMS, but the action plans & approval records are sensitive government data. How is that protected? These are stored exclusively in the department's local PostgreSQL instance, never sent to any external server. Only the judgment text (already public) is sent to the LLM API for extraction. All reviewer activity is logged locally with timestamps and officer ID for audit purpose.

5) What makes OrderDesk different:
Capability
Existing Tools
OrderDesk
OCR + digital PDF handling
✓
✓
Legal text summarization
✓
✓
Comply/appeal classification
✗
✓
Appeal deadline inference from limitation law
✗
✓
Per-field confidence scoring
✗
✓
Source-highlighted PDF review UI
✗
✓
Field-level approve / edit / reject controls
✗
✓
Verified-only government dashboard
✗
✓

6) Handling the hard constraints:
Inconsistent legal PDFs --> Dual-path ingestion - OCR for scanned, direct parse for digital.

Outputs must be explainable --> Every extracted field links back to the exact PDF paragraph it came from.

Outputs must be verifiable --> Human reviewer must approve each field individually.

Decision support, not full automation --> A government officer owns every final record on the dashboard


Github Link: https://github.com/priya-devaraj/AI-for-Bharat-hackathon---OrderDesk
