solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


P pratikbavche2005_ee46
TEAM SUBMISSION [3]
AI-Powered Tender Intelligence System for Automated Eligibility Evaluation and Explainable Procurement Decisions
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 27, 2026 02:20 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Government procurement processes involve evaluating complex tender documents and multiple bidder submissions, often spanning hundreds of pages across structured and unstructured formats such as PDFs, scanned documents, images, and spreadsheets. Currently, this evaluation is largely manual, time-consuming, inconsistent, and difficult to audit.
Our solution proposes an AI-powered Tender Intelligence Platform that automates the end-to-end process of tender understanding, bidder analysis, and eligibility evaluation while ensuring full explainability and human oversight.
The system begins by ingesting tender documents and automatically extracting key eligibility criteria, including technical specifications, financial thresholds, compliance requirements, certifications, and mandatory documentation. Using advanced Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Large Language Models (LLMs), the platform classifies criteria into mandatory and optional categories and structures them into machine-readable formats.
Simultaneously, the platform processes bidder submissions across multiple formats, including scanned PDFs and images using OCR (Optical Character Recognition). It extracts relevant data such as turnover values, project experience, certifications, and registration details, even when presented in inconsistent formats.
The core of the system is an intelligent evaluation engine that maps extracted bidder data against tender criteria. Each bidder is classified as:
Eligible

Not Eligible

Requires Manual Review

Unlike traditional automation systems, our platform emphasizes explainable AI. Every decision is accompanied by:
The specific criterion evaluated

The document used as evidence

Extracted values

Reasoning behind the decision

Ambiguous or low-confidence cases are never automatically rejected; instead, they are routed to a human-in-the-loop verification system, where reviewers can approve, edit, or reject AI outputs. This ensures compliance with government standards and builds trust in the system.
The platform also generates a comprehensive evaluation report, providing procurement officers with a clear, auditable, and structured summary of all bidders. This significantly reduces evaluation time from days to minutes while improving consistency and transparency.
Key Features

AI-based extraction of tender eligibility criteria

Multi-format document parsing (PDF, scanned, images)

Automated bidder eligibility evaluation

Explainable decision-making with evidence linking

Human-in-the-loop validation workflow

Audit-ready consolidated reports

Technology Stack (Proposed)

OCR: Tesseract / AWS Textract

NLP/LLM: OpenAI / HuggingFace models

Backend: Node.js / Python (FastAPI)

Frontend: React.js dashboard

Database: MongoDB / PostgreSQL

Impact

This solution transforms government procurement by making it:
Faster

Transparent

Consistent

Fully auditable

It reduces manual effort, minimizes human error, and ensures fair and data-driven decision-making in public sector tenders.

P pratikbavche2005_ee46
TEAM SUBMISSION [3]
AI-Powered Court Judgment Intelligence System for Automated Action Plan Generation and Verified Decision Support
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 27, 2026 02:23 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Government departments frequently deal with large volumes of court judgments that require timely analysis and action. These judgments are typically delivered as lengthy, unstructured PDF documents containing complex legal language. Critical directives—such as compliance requirements, deadlines, and decisions regarding appeals—are often buried within dense text, making manual interpretation slow, error-prone, and inconsistent.
Our solution is an AI-powered Court Judgment Intelligence Platform that transforms unstructured legal documents into clear, structured, and actionable plans, enabling faster and more reliable decision-making while ensuring human verification.
The system begins by ingesting court judgment PDFs, including scanned and image-based documents. Using OCR (Optical Character Recognition) and advanced Natural Language Processing (NLP), it extracts key information such as:
Case details (case number, parties involved)

Date of judgment

Key directives and orders

Important timelines and deadlines

Once the core information is extracted, the platform generates a structured action plan, which is the key differentiator of the system. The AI analyzes the judgment and suggests:
Whether compliance is required

Whether an appeal should be considered

Relevant deadlines (explicit or inferred)

Responsible department or authority

Nature and urgency of required actions

To ensure reliability and trust, the system incorporates a mandatory human-in-the-loop verification layer. All extracted data and generated action plans are presented with:
Highlighted source references from the original document

Confidence scores for each extracted element

Reviewers can:
Approve

Edit

Reject

Only verified information is moved forward, ensuring that the system acts as a decision-support tool rather than full automation, aligning with government requirements.
The final output is displayed through an intuitive dashboard that provides:
Department-wise action items

Pending tasks and deadlines

Verified and clean structured data for decision-makers

This eliminates the need for officials to manually read entire judgments, significantly reducing processing time and minimizing the risk of missing critical directives.
Key Features

Automated extraction from complex legal PDFs (including scanned documents)

AI-generated structured action plans from judgments

Explainable outputs with source highlighting

Human-in-the-loop verification system

Confidence scoring for extracted data

Clean and reliable dashboard for decision-making

Technology Stack (Proposed)

OCR: Tesseract / AWS Textract

NLP/LLM: OpenAI / Legal NLP models

Backend: Python (FastAPI) / Node.js

Frontend: React.js dashboard

Database: PostgreSQL / MongoDB

Impact

This solution modernizes legal decision workflows in government by making them:
Faster

More accurate

Transparent

Action-oriented

It reduces manual workload, improves compliance tracking, and ensures that no critical legal directive is overlooked, ultimately enabling timely and informed administrative decisions.

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


T tanishkatrivedi.20_9ea5
TEAM SUBMISSION [3]
CourtAction AI: Verified Judgment-to-Action Intelligence
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 27, 2026 03:58 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
CourtAction AI is an AI-assisted decision-support layer for the Court Case Monitoring System that converts High Court judgment PDFs into human-verified, department-ready action plans.
Today, CCMS can fetch judgment PDFs after case disposal, but officials still need to manually read lengthy legal documents to identify key directions, compliance requirements, appeal considerations, responsible authorities, and deadlines. This creates delays and increases the risk of missed court directions.
Our solution uses a custom legal RAG pipeline, judgment knowledge graph, confidence scoring, and mandatory human verification to extract, structure, and validate actionable information from court judgments.
The system works in four stages:
Extract judgment information The platform reads both digital and scanned PDFs using PDF parsing, OCR, and layout-aware document processing. It extracts case details, order date, parties, departments involved, final directions, timelines, and important source paragraphs.

Generate an action plan A custom Legal RAG engine retrieves only the most relevant parts of the judgment, especially final order and direction sections, and generates a structured administrative action plan. The action plan identifies whether compliance is required, whether appeal review may be needed, the likely responsible department or authority, key deadlines, and the nature of action required.

Build a judgment knowledge graph Each case is converted into a structured graph linking: Case → Parties → Respondent Department → Court Direction → Required Action → Timeline → Source Paragraph → Verification Status. This helps handle judgments with multiple parties, multiple departments, and multiple directions without losing responsibility mapping.

Verify before dashboard use Every extracted field and AI-generated action is shown to a human reviewer with source highlights from the PDF, confidence levels, and editable fields. The reviewer can approve, edit, reject, or escalate the record. Only verified records move to the dashboard.

The dashboard shows a clean, trusted view for decision-makers:
Department-wise pending actions

Compliance-required cases

Cases needing appeal review

Upcoming and overdue deadlines

Responsible authority

Verified source paragraph from the judgment

Reviewer approval status

The system is designed for decision support, not full automation. It does not make final legal or administrative decisions. Instead, it reduces manual reading effort, highlights critical directions, and ensures that officials act on verified information.
Key technical methods include:
OCR and layout-aware PDF parsing for scanned and inconsistent judgments

Legal-aware chunking to preserve judgment structure

Hybrid retrieval using keyword and semantic search

Cross-encoder reranking for better passage selection

Custom RAG with source-grounded outputs

Named entity and timeline extraction

Judgment knowledge graph for responsibility and action mapping

Confidence scoring for every extracted field

Human-in-the-loop verification with full audit trail

This approach directly addresses the evaluation criteria. It improves extraction accuracy by grounding every field in source text, improves action plan quality by converting legal directions into administrative tasks, strengthens human verification through editable source-backed review, and provides a clear dashboard that shows only approved, reliable action plans.
For Round 2, the prototype can demonstrate the full flow: upload or fetch a judgment PDF, extract case details and directions, generate an action plan, show source highlights and confidence scores, verify through a reviewer screen, and publish the approved action plan to a department-wise dashboard.
The long-term value is faster compliance, fewer missed deadlines, better appeal tracking, reduced manual workload, and a trusted workflow for converting court judgments into timely government action.

Project Description
Project Title: DocuMind
This project is a smart reading and annotation mobile application designed to support students, teachers, and researchers by providing a seamless and distraction-free study environment. The application works in both offline and online modes, ensuring uninterrupted access to learning materials anytime, anywhere.
The app supports multiple document formats, including PDF, images, and Word files, allowing users to read, analyze, and interact with content efficiently within a single platform.
Key Features

1. Offline + Online Accessibility The application is fully functional offline, enabling users to access documents and use core features without internet connectivity. Online mode enhances the experience by allowing deeper research and web navigation when required.
2. Smart Text Selection & Instant Meaning Users can select any word or sentence within the document to trigger a contextual pop-up menu.
“Meaning” option displays definitions instantly using an offline dictionary, ensuring zero distraction from notifications or internet dependency.

“Search” option allows users to navigate to relevant web resources for deeper understanding.

3. Floating Sticky Notes for Meaning Display Selected word meanings are shown in a semi-transparent floating sticky note UI, allowing users to view definitions without losing focus on the original content. This enhances readability and multitasking.
4. Distraction-Free Offline Dictionary An integrated offline dictionary engine ensures that students can learn meanings without needing internet access, promoting focused and uninterrupted study sessions.
5. Smart Annotation & Note-Taking System Users can annotate directly on PDFs, images, or documents by simply long-pressing (3 seconds) anywhere on the screen.
Notes can be typed using the keypad.

The app converts typed text into natural cursive handwriting style, making annotations appear personalized and realistic.

6. Flexible Saving Options Users can:
Save edits within the same file

Or create a new version of the document, preserving the original content

? Additional Innovative Features

7. Multi-Layer Annotation System Users can add multiple notes without clutter, toggle visibility, or organize annotations for better readability.
8. Highlighting & Bookmarking Important sections can be highlighted and bookmarked for quick revision.
9. Cross-Format Learning Hub A unified interface to handle PDFs, Word documents, and images eliminates the need for multiple apps.
10. Focus Mode (Distraction-Free Study) Offline-first design ensures minimal interruptions, helping users maintain deep focus while studying.

Project Description
Our project proposes an AI-powered decision-support system that transforms unstructured court judgment PDFs into structured, actionable insights for government departments.
Currently, court judgments are available only as lengthy PDF documents, making it difficult for officials to quickly identify key directives, deadlines, and required actions. This leads to delays in compliance, inefficient decision-making, and risk of missing critical instructions.
? Solution Overview

We design a system that integrates with existing platforms like CCMS and processes court judgment PDFs through the following pipeline:

1. Intelligent Data Extraction

Case details (case number, parties involved)

TEAM SUBMISSION [2]
Verdict-2-Action
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 27, 2026 05:58 AM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description

Verdict2Action – From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans

Government departments handle thousands of court cases where every judgment carries critical directives—compliance orders, deadlines, or decisions on whether to appeal. However, these judgments are long, complex PDF documents with unstructured legal language, making it difficult for officials to quickly identify what actions are required. This leads to delays, missed deadlines, and inefficient decision-making. Verdict2Action is an AI-powered decision support system that transforms court judgments into clear, actionable insights—enabling faster, more reliable governance.
The system begins by ingesting court judgment PDFs (both scanned and digital) and intelligently extracting key information such as case details, parties involved, dates, and most importantly, court directives. What sets Verdict2Action apart is how it presents this information through two powerful, user-driven modes:
Smart Highlight View: The original judgment is displayed with AI-generated highlights directly on the PDF. Critical directives are marked in red, important instructions in orange, and contextual information in yellow—allowing users to instantly identify what matters without reading the entire document.


Executive Brief: For quick decision-making, the system generates a concise 2–3 page simplified document summarizing the case, key directions, timelines, and recommended actions in clear, non-legal language.

Building on this understanding, Verdict2Action’s Action Engine converts extracted insights into structured, decision-ready action plans. These include whether compliance is required or an appeal should be considered, the responsible department, key deadlines (explicit or inferred), and the reasoning behind each recommendation—all linked back to the original judgment for full transparency.
To ensure trust and accuracy, the system incorporates a human-in-the-loop verification layer. Officials can review extracted data and action plans alongside highlighted source text, with confidence scores provided for each element. They can approve, edit, or reject outputs—ensuring that only verified information is used for decision-making.
Once approved, actions are tracked through a Compliance Tracker and Dashboard, offering a clean, department-wise view of responsibilities, upcoming deadlines, and priority tasks. Smart notifications and reminders (e.g., days before deadlines) ensure that no critical directive is missed.
Verdict2Action is not just an extraction tool—it is a complete pipeline that bridges the gap between legal complexity and administrative action. By combining visual explainability, AI-driven insights, and human validation, it empowers government officials to move from lengthy judgments to timely, informed decisions with confidence.

1. Problem Understanding
Government tender evaluation is slow and inconsistent due to:


Manual review of lengthy, complex documents

TenderAI Assist – a decision-support platform for automated, explainable evaluation.Workflow:

Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 27, 2026 08:18 AM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
1. Problem Understanding
Government departments receive thousands of court judgments, but the real bottleneck is not access—it is actionability.Current gaps:



Judgments are long, complex, and often scanned PDFs.

Convert legal text into structured tasks Example: “Respondent shall reinstate within 30 days” →

&

NER + Rule-based Hybrid Models Ensures precision for dates, parties, case numbers

Confidence Scoring Model Combines extraction probability + text clarity


4. System Architecture (Pipeline)

Input Layer (PDF Upload via CCMS)

Extraction Engine (LLM + NER)

Directive-to-Action Engine

Timeline & Risk Engine

5. Explainability & Auditability
Strong focus to build trust in government workflows:

6. Handling Edge Cases

Poor scan quality → fallback OCR + manual flag

Ambiguous directives → marked “Needs Review”

Multiple conflicting timelines → highlight all + flag risk

Not automation—decision support:

11. Implementation Plan (MVP)

S sunflower
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
PramanAI: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 27, 2026 12:23 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
PramanAI: Court Judgment to Action Plan System
Problem Statement

India’s court system processes a very large number of cases, and government departments are frequently involved in compliance, appeal, and follow-up work. The main challenge is not only volume, but the format of the judgments. Most judgments arrive as long PDF files with dense legal language, scattered directions, and no clear action summary. Officials must read the full document to find what needs to be done, who is responsible, and what deadline applies. This creates delay, increases manual workload, and raises the risk of missed directives.

Proposed Solution

PramanAI is an AI system that reads court judgment PDFs and turns them into structured, human-verified action plans. It is designed for government use, where accuracy, explainability, and accountability matter more than speed alone. The system does not try to make legal decisions on its own. Instead, it helps officials quickly understand the judgment, identify the action required, and verify the output before it is used.

Architecture


Step 1: Judgment Ingestion

The system receives a court judgment PDF through upload or integration with court systems (API). Metadata such as case ID and department ID is captured at this stage.

Step 2: Document Parsing

OCR Model extracts text, tables, and layout from the PDF while preserving the reading order. This helps the system understand the structure of the judgment instead of treating it as plain text.

Step 3: Legal Entity Extraction

A legal NER(Named Entity Recognition) model identifies important entities such as parties, dates, statutes, and case references. This creates a cleaner and more precise document understanding layer.
Step 4: Key Direction Detection

The system extracts the important parts of the judgment, including the final order, required compliance, appeal-related signals, and timelines mentioned in the text.

Step 5: Structured Action Plan Generation

LangChain and Groq convert the extracted information into a structured action plan. The output is constrained to a fixed schema so the data stays consistent across cases.

Step 6: Source Highlighting

Each extracted field is linked to the relevant text snippet from the judgment. This gives reviewers direct evidence for verification.

Step 7: Human Verification

A legal or administrative reviewer checks the AI output, edits incorrect fields if needed, and approves or rejects the record.

Step 8: Trusted Dashboard

Only verified action plans are displayed on the dashboard for department-level monitoring, filtering, and follow-up.



Tech Stack


Layer
Technologies
Frontend
Next.js, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui, React Query
Backend
Python, FastAPI, LangChain, Groq, MinerU, HuggingFace
Database
MongoDB
Authentication
OAuth 2.0, JWT


Feasibility and Viability


Technical feasibility is high because the system uses proven components for each stage, including FastAPI for APIs, MinerU for PDF parsing, legal NER for entity extraction, and Groq for structured generation.

Integration feasibility is strong because the input format is standard PDF and the system can connect to existing court or department workflows through APIs.

Accuracy can be controlled through layout-aware extraction, legal domain NER, strict structured output, and human verification before publication.

Explainability is built in because every field in the action plan is linked to source text from the original judgment.

Cost viability is reasonable because the stack uses open-source and efficient tools, with cloud scaling used only when needed.


Impact and Benefits


Faster decision-making by reducing the time needed to understand a judgment from hours to minutes.

Lower manual effort for officials, with expected time savings of around 60% to 70% in document review and action extraction.

Better compliance tracking because every action, deadline, and responsible department is stored in a structured format.

Trusted outputs because no record reaches the dashboard without human verification.

Better inter-department coordination because the action plan gives a shared and consistent view of what must happen next.

Improved transparency because every output can be traced back to the exact judgment text that generated it.


Closing Summary

PramanAI turns long and complex court judgments into verified action plans that government teams can actually use. It combines document understanding, legal extraction, structured generation, and human review into one practical workflow. The result is faster compliance, stronger accountability, and a more reliable way to manage post-judgment action.

solutions.team_name: TenderAI: An AI-Powered Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis Platform for Government Procurement.
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


M mukesh482_0d62
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
TenderAI: An AI-Powered Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis Platform for Government Procurement.
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 27, 2026 12:44 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
TenderAI: An AI-Powered Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis Platform for Government Procurement — A Document Intelligence System for Automated Criterion Extraction, Multi-Format Bid Parsing, and Explainable Audit-Ready Decision Support in CRPF Procurement Workflows.
01 — Problem Understanding
The real challenge

Procurement under GFR and CVC guidelines is high-stakes — every disqualification can be legally challenged and every oversight can trigger re-tendering. The problem is structured reasoning over unstructured data with full auditability, not simply automation.
Tenders run 60–200 pages; eligibility criteria are buried in legal prose, not neatly listed.

Bidder documents arrive as typed PDFs, scanned certificates, phone photographs, Word files, and embedded tables — each requiring a different extraction approach.

Vague language like "similar projects" creates evaluator inconsistency that can be exploited in appeals.

Every decision must trace back to a specific criterion, document, and piece of evidence.

Success means consistent, explainable, human-verifiable decisions — and honest surfacing of uncertainty. A confident wrong answer is worse than "needs review."

02 — Criterion Extraction
From tender prose to structured rules

Ingestion: Typed PDFs → PyMuPDF; scanned PDFs → Google Document AI or AWS Textract (trained on Indian government layouts, bilingual Hindi/English); DOCX → python-docx. All outputs preserve page-level provenance.
LLM extraction: Claude Sonnet, with a structured system prompt, identifies every bidder requirement and returns a strict JSON object per criterion — never free text. Each object includes: criterion ID, type (Technical / Financial / Compliance / Documentation / Certification), mandatory flag ("shall"/"must" = mandatory; "preferred"/"desirable" = optional), operator, threshold, and source page.
Officer sign-off: Before any evaluation runs, the officer reviews and approves the criterion list. This creates a signed, version-controlled criterion manifest — the audit anchor for the entire evaluation. Ambiguous criteria (e.g., undefined "similar projects") are flagged for officer clarification at this stage.

03 — Bidder Document Parsing
Handling every document format

Every uploaded file is classified and routed to the appropriate pipeline. Photographs receive OpenCV pre-processing (perspective correction, CLAHE contrast normalisation, Sauvola binarisation) before OCR. If confidence falls below 70%, the document is flagged for manual review — never silently processed.
Extraction is criterion-targeted via RAG: each criterion queries the bidder's embedded document corpus (pgvector), and retrieved chunks are passed to the LLM with an explicit instruction to return null with a reason rather than guess when information is unclear.
A wrong low-confidence extraction is worse than an honest "not found" — it creates an invisible error. The system is designed to fail loudly.

04 — Matching & Evaluation
Verdict logic

A deterministic rules engine — not LLM judgement — applies this decision table to each bidder–criterion pair:
Condition
Verdict
Value found, meets threshold, confidence ≥ 80%
Eligible
Value found, fails threshold, confidence ≥ 80%
Not eligible
Value found, confidence 50–80%
Needs manual review
Value not found in any document
Needs manual review — "not evidenced"
Document unreadable
Needs manual review — "document quality"
Optional criterion, not submitted
Noted — no disqualification

Language variation ("civil works" vs "construction services") is handled via sentence-embedding semantic similarity and a procurement synonym map. Any semantic match is flagged for officer confirmation.

05 — Explainability & Human Review
Criterion-level verdicts

Every verdict states: which criterion, which document and page, what was extracted, what was required, and why. No silent decisions.
C-003 — EligibleCriterionAnnual turnover ≥ ₹5,00,00,000 (mandatory)SourceBidder A / Balance_Sheet_FY2024.pdf, p.3 Table 2Extracted₹7,23,45,000 — 96% confidence (digital text)Decision₹7.23 cr ≥ ₹5.00 cr — threshold met.C-003 — Needs manual reviewCriterionAnnual turnover ≥ ₹5,00,00,000 (mandatory)SourceBidder F / CA_Certificate.pdf, p.1Extracted₹[UNCLEAR] — 42% confidence, low print qualityActionOfficer to verify turnover figure in original document.
Three principles: never silently reject — every uncertain case is surfaced with a reason. Minimal reviewer burden — officer sees exactly what was found and where to look. Officer decision is final — recorded with name, reason, and timestamp; system never overrides.

06 — Auditability
Immutable audit trail

An append-only, hash-chained PostgreSQL log captures: tender document hash; signed criterion manifest; per bidder–criterion — document used, extracted text, confidence, verdict; and per manual review — officer identity, decision, reason, timestamp. Records cannot be modified, only annotated. The final report exports as a signed PDF with a cryptographic hash of all inputs.

07 — Architecture & Technology
Stack and justification

Component
Choice
Why
LLM
Claude Sonnet
Best structured JSON output; low hallucination; bilingual
OCR — scanned
Google Document AI
Indian govt format accuracy; per-character confidence
OCR — photos
Textract + OpenCV
Handles distortion; bounding-box provenance
RAG / embeddings
text-embedding-3-large + pgvector
Strong semantic retrieval; co-located with audit DB
PDF/table extraction
PyMuPDF + Camelot
Handles bordered and borderless tables
Backend / DB
FastAPI + PostgreSQL
Async, type-safe, ACID-compliant, row-level versioning

Fully containerised (Docker/Kubernetes). Deployable on NIC Cloud, fully on-premise (open-weight model for air-gapped environments), or hybrid with data residency controls.

08 — Risks & Trade-offs
Key risks and mitigations

Risk
Mitigation
LLM hallucination in criterion extraction
Officer signs criterion manifest before any evaluation; extraction cross-checked against source page
OCR failure on poor photographs
Confidence threshold forces manual review — never silent disqualification
Semantic mismatch (false negative)
Synonym map + semantic similarity; every semantic match flagged for officer confirmation
Legal challenge to AI decision
Decision-support only; mandatory officer sign-off; full audit trail makes decisions defensible
Model version drift
Runs pinned to model version; version recorded in audit log


09 — Round 2 Plan
Implementation roadmap

Sprint
Deliverable
When
1
Ingestion pipeline — typed PDF, DOCX, OCR for scanned docs
Wk 1–2
2
Criterion extractor + officer review UI
Wk 2–3
3
Bidder RAG pipeline — embed, retrieve, extract
Wk 3–4
4
Matching engine + manual review queue
Wk 4–5
5
Audit log + signed PDF report
Wk 5–6
6
End-to-end integration + edge case testing
Wk 6–7
7
Polish, optimisation, demo
Wk 7–8

Target metrics: criterion extraction precision ≥ 95%; evidence recall ≥ 90% typed / ≥ 80% scanned; zero silent disqualifications; 10-bidder evaluation in under 5 minutes.
TenderAI assists procurement officers — it never replaces them. Every decision is consistent, explainable, and auditable.

solutions.team_name: OrderDesk: Turning court orders into timely government action!!!!
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


P priyadevraj006_16b3
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
OrderDesk: Turning court orders into timely government action!!!!
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 27, 2026 01:27 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
1) Problem we are trying to solve:
Government departments in Karnataka receive court judgment PDFs through CCMS daily. Each document runs 30 to 80 pages of complex legal language. No tool currently surfaces what action is needed, by which department, and by when forcing officials to read every word manually. Missing a deadline or misreading an order can have serious legal and administrative consequences.

This causes 4 crtical failures: 1) Compliance deadlines are missed because directives are buried in text. 2) Appeal limitation periods expire before a decision is even made. 3) Accountability gaps arise because no one is formally assigned to act. 4) Scale of the problem: Karnataka High Court disposes approximately 'N' no.of cases per month involving government departments. Each requires manual review by a designated officer with no tracking system

2 to 3 hours: Manual read time per judgement --> ~< 10 minutes: with OrderDesk review time --> 0 risk of missed directives.

2) My solution: OrderDesk
It is a 4 stage pipeline that plugs directly into CCMS. It reads judgment PDFs, pulls out every critical directive, builds a structured action plan, puts a human reviewer in control before anything is acted upon, and then displays only verified records on a clean government dashboard.

The core design principle: AI does the reading, humans make the decisions. Nothing reaches the dashboard without a Section Officer or Law Officer in the department's legal cell approving it first, the exact people currently responsible for manually reading and acting on judgment copies received through CCMS. ie: purpose-built for government use with human oversight at every step.

3) The Idea:
i) Extract -> Read & understand the judgement
Judgment PDFs arrive from CCMS via API. Scanned pages go through PaddleOCR; digital PDFs are parsed with PyMuPDF. A legal-tuned LLM then extracts:

Case number, court name, bench composition

Date of order and disposal status

Parties involved - petitioner, respondent, department name

Key directions and operative clauses (the "shall", "directed to", "ordered" language)

Explicit timelines mentioned in the judgment

Prompt engineering approach: ie: how to make the LLM extract legal clauses accurately? The LLM is prompted using a structured JSON output format with role-based legal instructions like the model is told it is a legal analyst reviewing a High Court judgment, and must return only structured fields (case number, parties, directives, timelines) with a confidence score per field. Directive detection uses keyword-anchored prompting, the model specifically looks for operative legal language such as "directed to", "shall forthwith", "ordered to pay", "liberty to apply", reducing hallucination on non-directive paragraphs.

Multi language PDF handling: Karnataka judgments can be in Kannada or English. For English PDFs, PyMuPDF handles parsing directly. For Kannada or mixed-language documents, PaddleOCR's multilingual model (which supports Kannada script) handles OCR before passing text to the LLM. If the detected language is Kannada, the extracted text is passed through a translation step (Google Translate API - free tier) before LLM processing, ensuring the action plan is always generated and reviewed in English for consistency across departments.

ii) Generate -> Build a structured action plan
This is the key differentiator. The AI does not just summarize, it reasons about what needs to happen next:

Comply or Appeal? - classified from directive language patterns

Deadline inference - explicit dates extracted directly; implicit ones derived from standard limitation periods (90 days for HC appeal under CPC, 30 days for compliance orders, etc.)

Department mapping - responsible authority identified from respondent details and subject matter (land, tax, service, tender)

Action type - Comply / File SLP / Pay compensation / Submit status report / Obtain stay.

Sample output --> Action: Comply | Department: Revenue | Deadline: within 60 days of order | Inferred date: [dates accordingly] | Confidence: High

iii) Verify -> Human in the loop (HITL), mandatory before anything moves
Every extracted field must be approved by the designated Section Officer or Law Officer assigned to that case in CCMS before it becomes an action record. The verification screen shows:

PDF viewer on the left each extracted field highlighted at its exact source location

Extracted data panel on the right, with confidence score per field (High / Medium / Low) [tbh, scores won't be perfectly calibrated. But for demo it works fine]

Per-field controls: Approve ✓ | Edit ✎ | Reject ✗

Full audit trail - who reviewed, when, what was changed

Record only moves to dashboard after full approval A low-confidence field (e.g., inferred deadline) is flagged in amber and requires mandatory manual confirmation before it can be approved.


iv) Dashboard -> Clean & structured view for decision makers
Only verified records appear here. The dashboard shows:

Department-wise filtered action cards

Each card: Case ID · Action required · Deadline · Assigned officer

Red / Amber / Green status based on days remaining to deadline

Role-based access - Viewer, Reviewer, Admin

One-click PDF export for offline circulation and file noting [low priority feature]

Escalation alerts when a deadline crosses into the red zone


4) Tech stacks:
PDF parsing & OCR for scanned pages --> PyMuPDF + PaddleOCR

AI Extraction & Action Plan --> Groq API - Llama 3.1 70B with structured JSON prompting for legal clause extraction. Gemini 1.5 Flash as fallback [Free tier - as of now planned]

Backend --> FastAPI (Python)

Frontend --> React + TailWindCSS

Database --> SQLite for demo (zero setup, fully local, built into Python). PostgreSQL at deployment stage.

PDF highlight viewer --> PDF.js

Auth --> Role based (Viewer / Reviewer / Admin) via JWT authentication - LDAP and SSO compatible for government network integration at deployment stage [ie: demo uses JWT (free), and when the government actually deploys it on their network, it plugs into their existing LDAP/AD system which they already own], Login system - username & password, Session management - FastAPI

Hosting demo --> localhost or render.com free tier

Deployment (if needed) --> Docker + Ngnix

IMPORTANT NOTE: Data Privacy: Court judgments are public record under the Right to Information Act and are already accessible via CCMS, but the action plans & approval records are sensitive government data. How is that protected? These are stored exclusively in the department's local PostgreSQL instance, never sent to any external server. Only the judgment text (already public) is sent to the LLM API for extraction. All reviewer activity is logged locally with timestamps and officer ID for audit purpose.

5) What makes OrderDesk different:
Capability
Existing Tools
OrderDesk
OCR + digital PDF handling
✓
✓
Legal text summarization
✓
✓
Comply/appeal classification
✗
✓
Appeal deadline inference from limitation law
✗
✓
Per-field confidence scoring
✗
✓
Source-highlighted PDF review UI
✗
✓
Field-level approve / edit / reject controls
✗
✓
Verified-only government dashboard
✗
✓

6) Handling the hard constraints:
Inconsistent legal PDFs --> Dual-path ingestion - OCR for scanned, direct parse for digital.

Outputs must be explainable --> Every extracted field links back to the exact PDF paragraph it came from.

Outputs must be verifiable --> Human reviewer must approve each field individually.

Decision support, not full automation --> A government officer owns every final record on the dashboard


Github Link: https://github.com/priya-devaraj/AI-for-Bharat-hackathon---OrderDesk

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


P pratikbavche2005_ee46
TEAM SUBMISSION [3]
AI-Powered Tender Intelligence System for Automated Eligibility Evaluation and Explainable Procurement Decisions
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 27, 2026 02:20 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Government procurement processes involve evaluating complex tender documents and multiple bidder submissions, often spanning hundreds of pages across structured and unstructured formats such as PDFs, scanned documents, images, and spreadsheets. Currently, this evaluation is largely manual, time-consuming, inconsistent, and difficult to audit.
Our solution proposes an AI-powered Tender Intelligence Platform that automates the end-to-end process of tender understanding, bidder analysis, and eligibility evaluation while ensuring full explainability and human oversight.
The system begins by ingesting tender documents and automatically extracting key eligibility criteria, including technical specifications, financial thresholds, compliance requirements, certifications, and mandatory documentation. Using advanced Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Large Language Models (LLMs), the platform classifies criteria into mandatory and optional categories and structures them into machine-readable formats.
Simultaneously, the platform processes bidder submissions across multiple formats, including scanned PDFs and images using OCR (Optical Character Recognition). It extracts relevant data such as turnover values, project experience, certifications, and registration details, even when presented in inconsistent formats.
The core of the system is an intelligent evaluation engine that maps extracted bidder data against tender criteria. Each bidder is classified as:
Eligible

Not Eligible

Requires Manual Review

Unlike traditional automation systems, our platform emphasizes explainable AI. Every decision is accompanied by:
The specific criterion evaluated

The document used as evidence

Extracted values

Reasoning behind the decision

Ambiguous or low-confidence cases are never automatically rejected; instead, they are routed to a human-in-the-loop verification system, where reviewers can approve, edit, or reject AI outputs. This ensures compliance with government standards and builds trust in the system.
The platform also generates a comprehensive evaluation report, providing procurement officers with a clear, auditable, and structured summary of all bidders. This significantly reduces evaluation time from days to minutes while improving consistency and transparency.
Key Features

AI-based extraction of tender eligibility criteria

Multi-format document parsing (PDF, scanned, images)

Automated bidder eligibility evaluation

Explainable decision-making with evidence linking

Human-in-the-loop validation workflow

Audit-ready consolidated reports

Technology Stack (Proposed)

OCR: Tesseract / AWS Textract

NLP/LLM: OpenAI / HuggingFace models

Backend: Node.js / Python (FastAPI)

Frontend: React.js dashboard

Database: MongoDB / PostgreSQL

Impact

This solution transforms government procurement by making it:
Faster

Transparent

Consistent

Fully auditable

It reduces manual effort, minimizes human error, and ensures fair and data-driven decision-making in public sector tenders.

TEAM SUBMISSION [3]
AI-Powered Court Judgment Intelligence System for Automated Action Plan Generation and Verified Decision Support
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 27, 2026 02:23 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Government departments frequently deal with large volumes of court judgments that require timely analysis and action. These judgments are typically delivered as lengthy, unstructured PDF documents containing complex legal language. Critical directives—such as compliance requirements, deadlines, and decisions regarding appeals—are often buried within dense text, making manual interpretation slow, error-prone, and inconsistent.
Our solution is an AI-powered Court Judgment Intelligence Platform that transforms unstructured legal documents into clear, structured, and actionable plans, enabling faster and more reliable decision-making while ensuring human verification.
The system begins by ingesting court judgment PDFs, including scanned and image-based documents. Using OCR (Optical Character Recognition) and advanced Natural Language Processing (NLP), it extracts key information such as:
Case details (case number, parties involved)

Date of judgment

Key directives and orders

Important timelines and deadlines

Once the core information is extracted, the platform generates a structured action plan, which is the key differentiator of the system. The AI analyzes the judgment and suggests:
Whether compliance is required

Whether an appeal should be considered

Relevant deadlines (explicit or inferred)

Responsible department or authority

Nature and urgency of required actions

To ensure reliability and trust, the system incorporates a mandatory human-in-the-loop verification layer. All extracted data and generated action plans are presented with:
Highlighted source references from the original document

Confidence scores for each extracted element

Reviewers can:
Approve

Edit

Reject

Only verified information is moved forward, ensuring that the system acts as a decision-support tool rather than full automation, aligning with government requirements.
The final output is displayed through an intuitive dashboard that provides:
Department-wise action items

Pending tasks and deadlines

Verified and clean structured data for decision-makers

This eliminates the need for officials to manually read entire judgments, significantly reducing processing time and minimizing the risk of missing critical directives.
Key Features

Automated extraction from complex legal PDFs (including scanned documents)

AI-generated structured action plans from judgments

Explainable outputs with source highlighting

Human-in-the-loop verification system

Confidence scoring for extracted data

Clean and reliable dashboard for decision-making

Technology Stack (Proposed)

OCR: Tesseract / AWS Textract

NLP/LLM: OpenAI / Legal NLP models

Backend: Python (FastAPI) / Node.js

Frontend: React.js dashboard

Database: PostgreSQL / MongoDB

Impact

This solution modernizes legal decision workflows in government by making them:
Faster

More accurate

Transparent

Action-oriented

It reduces manual workload, improves compliance tracking, and ensures that no critical legal directive is overlooked, ultimately enabling timely and informed administrative decisions.

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


T tanishkatrivedi.20_9ea5
TEAM SUBMISSION [3]
CourtAction AI: Verified Judgment-to-Action Intelligence
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 27, 2026 03:58 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
CourtAction AI is an AI-assisted decision-support layer for the Court Case Monitoring System that converts High Court judgment PDFs into human-verified, department-ready action plans.
Today, CCMS can fetch judgment PDFs after case disposal, but officials still need to manually read lengthy legal documents to identify key directions, compliance requirements, appeal considerations, responsible authorities, and deadlines. This creates delays and increases the risk of missed court directions.
Our solution uses a custom legal RAG pipeline, judgment knowledge graph, confidence scoring, and mandatory human verification to extract, structure, and validate actionable information from court judgments.
The system works in four stages:
Extract judgment information The platform reads both digital and scanned PDFs using PDF parsing, OCR, and layout-aware document processing. It extracts case details, order date, parties, departments involved, final directions, timelines, and important source paragraphs.

Generate an action plan A custom Legal RAG engine retrieves only the most relevant parts of the judgment, especially final order and direction sections, and generates a structured administrative action plan. The action plan identifies whether compliance is required, whether appeal review may be needed, the likely responsible department or authority, key deadlines, and the nature of action required.

Build a judgment knowledge graph Each case is converted into a structured graph linking: Case → Parties → Respondent Department → Court Direction → Required Action → Timeline → Source Paragraph → Verification Status. This helps handle judgments with multiple parties, multiple departments, and multiple directions without losing responsibility mapping.

Verify before dashboard use Every extracted field and AI-generated action is shown to a human reviewer with source highlights from the PDF, confidence levels, and editable fields. The reviewer can approve, edit, reject, or escalate the record. Only verified records move to the dashboard.

The dashboard shows a clean, trusted view for decision-makers:
Department-wise pending actions

Compliance-required cases

Cases needing appeal review

Upcoming and overdue deadlines

Responsible authority

Verified source paragraph from the judgment

Reviewer approval status

The system is designed for decision support, not full automation. It does not make final legal or administrative decisions. Instead, it reduces manual reading effort, highlights critical directions, and ensures that officials act on verified information.
Key technical methods include:
OCR and layout-aware PDF parsing for scanned and inconsistent judgments

Legal-aware chunking to preserve judgment structure

Hybrid retrieval using keyword and semantic search

Cross-encoder reranking for better passage selection

Custom RAG with source-grounded outputs

Named entity and timeline extraction

Judgment knowledge graph for responsibility and action mapping

Confidence scoring for every extracted field

Human-in-the-loop verification with full audit trail

This approach directly addresses the evaluation criteria. It improves extraction accuracy by grounding every field in source text, improves action plan quality by converting legal directions into administrative tasks, strengthens human verification through editable source-backed review, and provides a clear dashboard that shows only approved, reliable action plans.
For Round 2, the prototype can demonstrate the full flow: upload or fetch a judgment PDF, extract case details and directions, generate an action plan, show source highlights and confidence scores, verify through a reviewer screen, and publish the approved action plan to a department-wise dashboard.
The long-term value is faster compliance, fewer missed deadlines, better appeal tracking, reduced manual workload, and a trusted workflow for converting court judgments into timely government action.

solutions.team_name: PRATIDHVANI: Conflict-Free Bidirectional Interoperability Fabric for Karnataka's Single Window System
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


T TenderLens AI
TEAM SUBMISSION [4]
PRATIDHVANI: Conflict-Free Bidirectional Interoperability Fabric for Karnataka's Single Window System
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 14, 2026 10:13 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
PRATIDHVANI (Sanskrit: echo) is a UBID-keyed interoperability fabric that propagates service requests between Karnataka's SWS and 40+ department systems using event sourcing and operation-based CRDTs — guaranteeing idempotency, at-least-once delivery, and mathematically provable conflict convergence, without modifying a single line of code in SWS or any department system.1. Problem UnderstandingSWS is Karnataka's forward-looking canonical front door; 40+ legacy department systems remain authoritative for businesses already on their books. The estate is permanently heterogeneous — different schemas, identifiers, APIs, and operational tempos. The GST rollout is the reference case for why a single-day cutover fails at this scale. The result is split brain: the same service request (e.g., change of registered address) can originate on either side, and today it does not propagate.Four realities drive our architecture: (a) neither side can be changed; (b) UBID — produced by the Theme-1 system — is the only trustworthy join key; (c) "configurable last-write-wins" is not a real conflict strategy; (d) every byte of propagated state must be auditable to a formal government standard.﻿2. Technical Approach2.1 Event-sourced backboneEvery service request — from SWS or a department system — becomes an immutable event in a UBID-partitioned Kafka log with log compaction. SWS and department systems are projections of this log. Replay, reconciliation, and audit reduce to well-understood operations on the log. This single design choice makes idempotency, retries, and audit trails correct by construction rather than bolted on.﻿2.2 CRDTs for conflict resolution — the defining differentiatorEach field of a business record is typed as a CRDT: LWW-Register with hybrid logical clocks for scalars (address, PIN); OR-Set for multi-value fields (authorised signatories, registered categories); PN-Counter for monotonic accumulators. When concurrent writes for the same UBID arrive from SWS and a department system, they converge by the CRDT's algebraic properties — not by a guessed rule. The resolution is explainable directly from the CRDT semantics, which is a stronger guarantee than any "policy engine" a competitor is likely to propose.﻿2.3 Tiered change detection for heterogeneous source systemsDepartment systems vary widely in observability. We handle all three tiers:Tier A — Native webhooks / event emission. Subscribe directly.Tier B — DB access available. Change Data Capture via binlog/WAL tailing (Debezium-class).Tier C — Neither. Periodic snapshot with Merkle-tree diffing: hash each table in O(n), compare roots in O(1), descend only into subtrees that differ. Detects "something changed here" without reading the whole table. This is the realistic path for the oldest department systems and is what most competitors will not have.﻿2.4 Schema translationA locally-hosted small LLM proposes field mappings between heterogeneous schemas (e.g., Factories.signatory_name → SWS.authorised_representative.full_name) with confidence scores. Every mapping is human-approved, versioned, and stored as an executable transform in a mapping registry. New-department onboarding drops from weeks of manual integration to roughly a day. The LLM operates only on schema metadata and synthetic payloads — never on real PII — honouring the non-negotiable.﻿2.5 Idempotency & delivery guaranteesEvery event carries a deterministic ID: event_id = hash(ubid, source_system, logical_timestamp, canonical_payload). Duplicate events are detected and dropped at the write boundary. Writes to target systems use saga-pattern orchestration with compensating transactions on partial failure; failed writes move to a retry queue with exponential backoff and ultimately to a dead-letter queue with an alert.﻿2.6 Audit & reconciliationThe audit store answers, for any UBID: what changed, when, from where, to where, with what retry history, and — on conflicts — which CRDT merge applied. A nightly reconciliation job re-hashes department snapshots and compares to the event log to detect silent drift, surfacing any divergence to operators.﻿3. Architecture Overview & Technology ChoicesLayerTechnologyWhyIngress adaptersTiered (webhook / Debezium CDC / Merkle-diff poller)Matches each department's actual observabilityUBID resolverThin client to Theme-1 SAKSHI serviceSingle source of identity truthEvent logApache Kafka, UBID-partitioned, compactedIndustry-standard, replayable, auditableCRDT state storeRust service, per-UBID materialised stateProvable convergence, O(1) mergeSchema mappingLocal Llama-3-8B + versioned mapping registryFast onboarding, PII-safeSaga orchestratorTemporalReliable multi-step workflowsEgress adaptersPer-target idempotent writers with DLQNo duplicates, no lost updatesAudit storeAppend-only Postgres with BRIN indicesQueryable, cost-efficient at scale4. Non-Negotiables — how we satisfy eachNeither SWS nor department systems modified: integration is purely via existing APIs, DB read access, or file exports — no code changes requested from either side.UBID as the only join key: the fabric refuses to propagate any event whose UBID cannot be resolved above threshold; such events go to a quarantine queue, never silently joined.Idempotency & full audit: deterministic event IDs + append-only log + reconciliation job.Scrambled/synthetic data only in Round 2: mock SWS and department endpoints stand in for real systems.No hosted-LLM on raw PII: schema-mapping LLM sees only metadata and synthetic examples, hosted locally.﻿5. Risks & Trade-offs (named explicitly)Departments with zero observable change surface (no API, no DB access, no export). Mitigation: request a scheduled read-only flat-file export as the minimum onboarding contract; offer a lightweight sidecar exporter as a last resort.Clock skew between systems breaking LWW semantics. Mitigation: hybrid logical clocks rather than wall-clock timestamps.Schema-mapping errors. Mitigation: every mapping is human-approved before activation; mappings are versioned and reversible.Throughput at full 40-department scale. Mitigation: per-UBID partitioning scales horizontally; Kafka + Temporal are proven at this scale.﻿6. Round 2 Implementation PlanWeek 1: Event log, two mock adapters (one API-based, one CDC-based), UBID resolver stub, deterministic event IDs, idempotent writes.Week 2: CRDT state store, saga orchestrator, audit store, end-to-end SWS → Factories propagation demo.Week 3: Conflict scenario (simultaneous SWS + department update) with CRDT convergence demo; reconciliation job.Week 4: Live onboarding of a third mock department during the demo — from connector registration to first successful round-trip propagation — proving the "add a new department in a day" claim to the jury.﻿7. What Success Looks Like for Karnataka C&IAn address change raised in SWS reaches every matching department for the same UBID, with a complete audit trail. A signatory change raised inside a department reaches SWS and is visibly reflected. Near-simultaneous conflicting updates converge deterministically and the audit explains why. Any individual request is traceable end-to-end across retries and reconciliations. Adding a new department to the fabric is a configuration task, not a rewrite.

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


V VSquare
TEAM SUBMISSION [3]
BridgeSync : A Two-Way Interoperability Fabric for Karnataka's Single Window System
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 19, 2026 10:40 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Problem Statement and Need Karnataka’s business registration ecosystem faces a classic “split-brain” challenge: the modern Single Window System (SWS) coexists with 40+ legacy department systems, each acting as its own source of truth with different schemas, identifiers, and integration capabilities. Updates made in one system often don’t propagate to others, leading to inconsistent records, conflicting decisions across departments, and confusion for citizens. A full-scale migration into SWS is impractical and high-risk, as seen in large rollouts like GST, due to heterogeneous data formats, limited or absent APIs, and fragile legacy infrastructure. What’s fundamentally missing is a non-intrusive interoperability layer that continuously synchronizes, normalizes, and reconciles data across systems without requiring disruptive changes.
Solution Overview BridgeSync is a standalone interoperability fabric deployed independently of both SWS and department systems, integrating only through existing APIs, webhooks, or accessible data surfaces without requiring internal changes. Direction 1 — SWS to Department Systems: When SWS generates a service request, BridgeSync captures the event, identifies the UBID, and uses a Translation Registry to map SWS actions to each department’s schema and APIs. It dispatches requests with idempotency keys, ensures reliable delivery via retries with backoff, and records outcomes for consistency and traceability. Direction 2 — Department Systems to SWS:BridgeSync handles heterogeneous systems using three strategies: webhooks (push-based), API polling (incremental fetch), and snapshot comparison (for systems with no change signals). Detected changes are normalized via the Translation Registry, mapped to UBIDs, and synchronized back to SWS. Conflict Detection and Resolution: For near-simultaneous updates on the same UBID, BridgeSync enforces a configurable lock window to detect conflicts. Resolution follows ownership-based policies (e.g., SWS vs department authority), while unresolved cases are escalated for human review with complete audit context. Audit Trail: Every synchronization event—success, failure, retry, or conflict—is logged in an append-only, queryable audit store capturing source, target systems, payload transformations, timestamps, and final outcomes, enabling full end-to-end traceability and accountability.
Comprehensive Feature Set 1. Bidirectional Event Router — smart routing of events to relevant systems, 2. Three-Mode Change Capture — webhook, polling, and diff-based update detection, 3. Translation Registry — configurable schema and payload mapping, 4. UBID Resolver — identifies systems holding business records, 5. Idempotent Dispatch Engine — prevents duplicate processing on retries, 6. Conflict Detection & Resolution Engine — detects and resolves concurrent updates, 7. Retry Queue with Dead-Letter — retries failures and isolates unresolved cases, 8. Append-Only Audit Ledger — immutable logging for full traceability, 9. Operations Dashboard — real-time monitoring of sync and system health, 10. Connector SDK — standardised framework for adding new integrations
Technology Stack 1. Apache Kafka — event streaming, ordering, 2. Python — flexible data transformation, 3. PostgreSQL — transactional state, audit, 4. Redis — fast idempotency, locking, 5. Apache Airflow — scheduled polling orchestration, 6. FastAPI — async APIs, webhooks, 7. Docker & Kubernetes — scalable deployment
System Architecture and Workflow Tier 1 — Source Systems (Untouched): SWS and 40+ legacy department systems operate exactly as they do today with zero modifications. They remain purely as producers and consumers of data through their existing surfaces. Tier 2 — Change Capture Layer: Dedicated per-department connectors detect changes via webhook listeners, scheduled API polling, or snapshot diffing depending on the system's capability. This ensures no department system, however legacy, is left out of the sync loop. Tier 3 — Event Backbone (Kafka): Every captured change is published to a Kafka topic partitioned by UBID, guaranteeing ordered, durable, and replayable delivery. This fully decouples detection from processing and acts as the system's single source of event truth. Tier 4 — Core Processing Engine: The Event Router resolves target systems via UBID, transforms payloads through the Translation Registry, and enforces conflict detection and idempotency before any write is dispatched. This is where all the intelligence — translation, conflict resolution, deduplication — lives. Tier 5 — Dispatch and Delivery Layer: Translated and verified requests are pushed to target systems via their APIs, with a Retry Queue handling transient failures through exponential backoff. Exhausted retries land in a Dead-Letter Queue with full diagnostics and operator alerts. Tier 6 — Persistence and Audit Layer: PostgreSQL maintains the State Store for delivery tracking and snapshot baselines, alongside an append-only Audit Ledger capturing every event, translation, conflict, and resolution permanently. Redis handles ephemeral idempotency keys and UBID lock windows at low latency. Tier 7 — Operations and Observability Layer: A FastAPI-powered dashboard gives operators real-time visibility into sync status, conflict queues, failed propagations, and end-to-end traceability for any service request. This transforms silent failures into visible, actionable, self-healing operational events.
Unique Differentiators 1. Configuration-driven extensibility — no code-heavy integrations needed, 2. Change-capture without source modification — works with any legacy system, 3. UBID as core primitive — unified identity across systems, 4. Field-level conflict resolution — explainable, auditable decisions, 5. Designed for progressive migration — avoids big-bang migration risk
Challenges And Impacts 1. Challenge — Schema heterogeneity, Strategy — automated versioned mappings, Impact — consistent integrations, 2. Challenge — No change emission systems, Strategy — timestamps, faster polling, Impact — improved freshness, 3. Challenge — Non-idempotent APIs, Strategy — read-before-write checks, Impact — avoids duplicates, 4. Challenge — Conflict resolution trust, Strategy — review, notifications, Impact — user confidence, 5. Challenge — PII in LLM features, Strategy — data anonymisation layer, Impact — privacy compliance
Scenario After Project Adoption Before BridgeSync, address updates made in SWS don’t reach systems like Factories or Shop Establishment, forcing businesses to resubmit changes across portals and leading to inconsistent records and repeated notices. After BridgeSync, a single update on SWS is automatically propagated to all relevant departments within minutes, with confirmations and a complete audit trail visible end-to-end. Department-side updates also sync back to SWS seamlessly, eliminating silent data divergence and removing the need for manual reconciliation. Operationally, failures become visible via retry queues and dashboards, enabling faster resolution instead of relying on citizen complaints. Overall, it enables a gradual, low-risk transition where SWS becomes the single source of truth with full consistency, auditability, and trust across the ecosystem.
For More Understanding of the Idea and Visual Representation :https://drive.google.com/file/d/1qKSKwP70UwG06or82br8hWt_Q0jK9e-V/view A simple prototype to demonstrate the proposed solution :https://bridge-truth-sync.lovable.app

“From system silos to a single sync — BridgeSync turns fragmented truths into one trusted truth.”

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


P priyanshu23
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
The "Smart Bridge": A Two-Way Interoperability Layer for Karnataka's E-Governance Ecosystem
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 20, 2026 08:36 AM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Solution Proposal: Two-Way Interoperability Layer for Karnataka's Single Window System
Problem Understanding & The "Split-Brain" Dilemma Karnataka currently operates a modern Single Window System (SWS) alongside 40+ legacy department systems (e.g., Factories, Shop Establishment). Because a "big-bang" migration is unviable due to the risk of losing decades of operational data and disrupting active businesses, both systems run concurrently. This creates a "split-brain" problem: a business updating its registered address in the SWS will not see this change reflected in the legacy systems, and vice versa. To resolve this without modifying the source systems, we propose an anti-fragile, bidirectional interoperability layer that relies strictly on the Unique Business Identifier (UBID) as the absolute semantic anchor to join business records across all disparate databases.
2. Architectural Overview To avoid the exponential technical debt of point-to-point connections, the proposed solution operates as a centralized, decoupled, event-driven middleware hub. Grounded in Enterprise Integration Patterns, this layer acts as a Message Router, Schema Translator, and SAGA Orchestrator. It guarantees asynchronous, at-least-once delivery using Apache Kafka as its distributed event broker.
Direction 1: Propagating from SWS to Department Systems When a service request is raised in the SWS, the change is captured via an outbound webhook or the Transactional Outbox pattern. UBID Routing: A Redis-based UBID Registry dictates exactly which legacy departments hold records for the specific business entity. Metadata-Driven Schema Translation: To integrate legacy schemas without hardcoding rules, we deploy a Metadata-Driven Schema Mapping Engine (using tools like Apache Camel or Jolt). Translation rules are stored as declarative YAML files, meaning new departments can be onboarded seamlessly without recompiling the core application. Idempotent Proxy (No Lost Updates or Duplicates):Because older APIs lack native duplicate detection, we protect them using an Idempotent Proxy backed by a Redis cluster. Before making an API call, it generates a SHA-256 hash of the payload and checks the cache; if a network timeout forces a retry, the proxy intercepts it and returns the cached success response, preventing silent data overwrites.
Direction 2: Discovering and Propagating from Departments to SWS Detecting manual changes in legacy systems without altering their code requires non-intrusive data extraction methodologies. Our architecture utilizes a tiered approach: *Log-Based Change Data Capture (CDC): For databases with Write-Ahead Logs (WAL) like Oracle, MySQL, or PostgreSQL, we deploy Debezium. It silently reads the database transaction logs at the filesystem level to capture INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE events in real-time, completely bypassing the database query engine. Hash-Based Snapshot Differential Algorithms: For legacy flat-file data stores or highly locked-down systems without WAL access, we deploy advanced snapshot comparison algorithms, such as the Partitioned Hash Algorithm and the Hash-Based Window Algorithm. By hashing keys and compressing non-key attributes into cryptographic signatures in memory, these algorithms rapidly identify exact deltas between periodic data snapshots without causing server overloads. Detected changes are then reverse-translated and pushed to the SWS.
Cross-Cutting Concern: Conflict Detection & Resolution Concurrent modifications are inevitable. If a citizen updates their address in SWS and an officer updates a different field for the same UBID in a legacy system simultaneously, the system employs a Distributed State Arbiter powered by Apache Flink. Configurable Policy Engine: A time-windowed ledger flags concurrent updates and routes them to a policy engine. Resolution policies are field-specific and include strategies like Last-Write-Wins (LWW), Source-of-Truth Priority (e.g., SWS always dictates addresses), Custom Hybrid Merges (e.g., combining license arrays), or Human-in-the-Loop queues for high-stakes legal anomalies. Only mathematically resolved payloads are committed, entirely preventing data corruption.
Cross-Cutting Concern: Auditability & Distributed State Management Immutable Audit Trail: To satisfy strict government compliance, every transaction, translation rule applied, conflict resolution decision, and final payload delivery is logged in a cryptographically secure, append-only Merkle Tree audit chain (stored in TimescaleDB). This ensures all state changes are mathematically tamper-evident, fully explainable, and securely reversible. SAGA Orchestrator: If a multi-department update partially fails (e.g., 4 out of 5 legacy systems succeed, but the 5th crashes), a Temporal.io SAGA Orchestrator automatically halts the workflow and issues Compensating Transactions to roll back the successful systems. This guarantees that the UBID state is consistent across the entire government ecosystem.
Risks and Constraints Eventual Consistency: By using message brokers, the system trades strict, real-time ACID consistency for high availability, meaning systems may be out of sync for a few seconds during processing. Constraint Compliance: Our solution uses strictly synthetic data and zero raw PII is exposed to LLMs. LLMs are strictly limited to offline tasks such as suggesting schema mapping rules based on obfuscated payload structures.
