solutions.team_name: SyncBridge — A Two-Way Interoperability Layer for Karnataka's SWS and Department Systems
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


F FutureMinds
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
SyncBridge — A Two-Way Interoperability Layer for Karnataka's SWS and Department Systems
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 20, 2026 12:29 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
What We Understood From the ProblemKarnataka has a classic "too many systems, no single truth" problem. The Single Window System exists as the intended front door for businesses, but 40+ legacy department systems — Factories, Shop Establishment, Labour, KSPCB, and others — are still the actual source of truth for most registered businesses. These systems were built independently, each with different schemas and different identifiers, and they have no way to talk to each other.The real pain is simple: if a business updates its address on SWS, none of the department systems know about it. If the same business renews a licence directly inside the Factories system, SWS never finds out. Both sides drift. Officers see inconsistent data. Citizens have to make the same update in multiple places. And there's no audit trail of what happened where.The obvious fix — migrating everything into one system — doesn't work. The GST rollout showed what happens when you try a big-bang cutover at government scale. Our solution doesn't touch either side. It sits in the middle, listens to both, and keeps them in sync.One important dependency we're acknowledging upfront: this solution requires the UBID (Unique Business Identifier) from Theme 1 as a precondition. Without a common join key across systems, two-way sync is impossible. We treat UBID as an input to our layer, not something we re-solve here.﻿How SyncBridge WorksAt its core, SyncBridge is an event-driven middleware layer. When something changes on either side, we capture it, normalize it into a common format, translate it for the target system, and write it — with a complete audit trail at every step.We call this common format a Canonical Change Event (CCE). It looks like this:json{ "event_id": "EVT-20240415-0042", "ubid": "KA-2024-001892", "source": "SWS", "field": "registered_address", "new_value": "14, MG Road, Bengaluru - 560001", "timestamp": "2024-04-15T10:32:00Z"}Every change, regardless of where it originated, becomes a CCE. This means our Translation Engine only has to solve one problem: converting between a CCE and a system-specific payload, rather than mapping every system to every other system directly.﻿Direction 1: SWS → Department SystemsWhen a service request is raised on SWS, our SWS adapter picks it up (via webhook if SWS supports it, polling otherwise). It converts the request into a CCE and publishes it to our internal Kafka event bus.The Translation Engine reads the CCE, looks up which department systems have a record for that UBID, fetches the appropriate field mapping from our Schema Registry, and sends a translated write request to each target system.We handle idempotency by generating a deterministic request_id from the event ID + target system name. If a write fails and retries, the same request_id goes again — the target system won't apply it twice if it supports idempotency keys. If it doesn't, we do a pre-write read to check if the value already matches before writing.Failed writes after all retries go to a dead letter queue — never silently dropped.﻿Direction 2: Department Systems → SWSThis direction is harder because most legacy systems don't broadcast changes. We handle three different situations:Webhook mode — for systems that can push notifications. We register a callback URL and process incoming events as they arrive.Polling mode — for systems with a queryable API. We poll on a schedule (every 5 minutes by default), compare the response to our last known snapshot per UBID, and generate a CCE for any detected delta.Snapshot diff mode — for systems that only provide periodic data exports. We ingest the export, compute a hash per UBID, diff against the previous run, and generate CCEs for anything that changed.In all three cases, before we generate a CCE, we resolve the department's native record ID to a UBID using the UBID registry. If we can't make a confident match, the event goes to a human reviewer queue — not silently dropped, not silently assigned.﻿Conflict HandlingConflicts happen when two updates for the same UBID and field arrive from different sources within a short time window (we default to 60 seconds, configurable).We detect this using a Redis-based in-flight tracker — when a second CCE arrives for a (UBID, field) that already has an unresolved write in progress, we pause both and apply the configured conflict policy:SWS-wins — for fields where SWS is meant to be authoritative (e.g., GST number, business name)Last-write-wins — for operational fields like phone numbers, using timestamp + source priority as tiebreakerHold for human review — for high-stakes fields like registered address or authorised signatoryThe conflict policy is configured per field type in the Schema Registry YAML. A reviewer sees both values, both sources, both timestamps, and clicks to resolve. That decision is logged.﻿Schema Registry — How We Handle 40+ Different SystemsEvery department's field mappings are stored as a versioned YAML file:yamlsystem: shop_establishmentubid_field: pan_linked_idmappings: registered_address: target_field: estb_address transform: "concat(addr1, ', ', addr2, ' - ', pincode)" conflict_policy: HOLD_FOR_REVIEWAdding a new department means writing a new YAML file. No code changes. No restarts. The Translation Engine loads mappings dynamically at runtime. This is important because onboarding a 41st department shouldn't require a deployment.﻿Audit TrailEvery event gets a row in an append-only PostgreSQL table. No UPDATEs, no DELETEs — ever. Each row records what changed, which system it came from, which system it was written to, what the outcome was, and whether a conflict was detected and how it was resolved.We store value hashes, not raw values, in the audit table — this satisfies the PII constraint. A reviewer can retrieve the actual value from the source system using the event metadata.You can reconstruct the full history of any service request with one query:sqlSELECT * FROM audit_ledger WHERE ubid = 'KA-2024-001892' ORDER BY processed_at;﻿Architecture OverviewSWS API ◄──► SWS Adapter ──┐ │Dept Systems ◄──► Dept ├──► Kafka Event Bus Adapters ────────┘ │(webhook / ▼ polling / Translation Engine snapshot) + Schema Registry (YAML) │ Conflict Detector (Redis) │ Audit Ledger (PostgreSQL) │ Reviewer Dashboard (React)Why these choices:Kafka over a simple queue because we need replay capability — if something goes wrong, we can reprocess events from any point in timePostgreSQL append-only table for audit because ACID guarantees matter and we never want a row modified or deletedRedis for conflict window tracking because it's fast and TTL-based keys clean themselves up automaticallyYAML + Git for schema mappings because version control gives us change history and rollback for freeSpring Boot for the engine because we have existing experience and its Kafka integration is mature﻿Risks We've Thought AboutWhat if a department has no usable API at all? We'd fall back to authenticated scraping of the department portal as a last resort, flagged as low-confidence. Events from this source always go through human review before being propagated. This is an edge case but it exists.What if UBID resolution fails for a department record? The event goes to a reviewer pending tray. It never propagates with a wrong UBID. As the UBID registry matures (Theme 1's output improves over time), these failures will decrease — it's an expected bootstrapping cost.What if a department changes its API schema without notice? Our adapters validate every response against the expected schema definition. Any unexpected field structure raises an alert immediately. We fail loudly, not silently.What if a department does a bulk offline update and comes back online? We'd see a conflict storm — thousands of events all flagged as conflicts. We handle this with a "bulk-update mode" flag in the Schema Registry: when set, that department's events skip the conflict detector, write with department-wins policy, and are bulk-flagged in the audit log for retrospective review.﻿Round 2 PlanGiven a sandbox with mock SWS and 3–4 department endpoints:Week 1 — Set up Kafka, PostgreSQL, Redis. Build SWS adapter and one department adapter (polling). Get a single end-to-end address change propagating with audit trail.Week 2 — Add remaining department adapters (webhook + snapshot diff). Build Translation Engine with Schema Registry. Demonstrate Direction 2.Week 3 — Build Conflict Detector, all three resolution policies, and the Reviewer Dashboard. Demo the conflict scenario from the problem statement.Week 4 — Idempotency stress test (replay same events 10x, verify no duplicates). Bulk-update scenario test. Final audit trail demo. Record walkthrough video.Final deliverable: Docker Compose stack with all components running, scripted demo reproducing all three scenarios from the problem statement.﻿Team FutureMinds — PIEMR, Indore

solutions.team_name: UBID Fabric — Deterministic Interoperability Layer for Karnataka's SWS and Department Systems
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


R rasikarakhewar30102004_1a04
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
UBID Fabric — Deterministic Interoperability Layer for Karnataka's SWS and Department Systems
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 19, 2026 01:31 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
The full technical architecture document, including 31 production-grade Mermaid diagrams covering every component, every data flow, every conflict scenario, and every failure/recovery path, is available at:  https://github.com/Omnifex-Agency/UBID-Fabric/blob/main/UBID_Fabric_Document.md#executive-summary  — this document serves as both the design specification and the implementation blueprint for UBID Fabric, demonstrating that the solution is not just theoretically sound but practically deployable on Karnataka's infrastructure today.
Karnataka's Single Window System (SWS) was designed as a unified portal for business registrations, approvals, and compliance — a single place where businesses interact with the government. In practice, however, SWS operates alongside 40+ independent department systems — Factories and Boilers, Shop and Commercial Establishments, Commercial Taxes (GST), Labour Department, Excise Department, Pollution Control Board, Fire Services, and dozens more — each maintaining its own database, its own schema, its own data formats, and its own version of the truth about the same business entity. When a business updates its registered address on SWS, that change does not automatically propagate to the Factories system, the Shop Establishment system, or any other department database. When a department officer corrects a signatory name or employee count in their local system based on a field verification, SWS never learns about it. Over time, the same business entity — the same physical company with the same PAN, the same registration certificate — exists in 5, 10, or even 15 different systems with conflicting addresses, different signatory lists, mismatched employee counts, and inconsistent registration dates. This is what we call the "split-brain" problem, and it is not a bug in any individual system — it is a structural gap in how Karnataka's e-governance infrastructure was architected. Each department system was built independently, often by different vendors (NIC, Wipro, TCS, Infosys, and smaller state-level contractors), at different times (some in 2005, some in 2023), using different technologies (Java, .NET, PHP, Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL), and critically, none of them were designed to synchronise with each other. There is no shared event bus, no common identity layer, no conflict resolution protocol, and no audit mechanism that spans across systems. The consequences are severe: businesses receive contradictory notices from different departments, compliance officers make decisions based on stale data, RTI queries cannot be answered because no authoritative source of truth exists, and the government's own digital infrastructure undermines its goal of ease of doing business.UBID Fabric is our solution to this structural problem. It is a deterministic, non-invasive interoperability layer that sits between all 40+ systems and ensures that every change made in any system is detected, canonicalised into a uniform format, conflict-resolved using mathematically proven algorithms, and propagated to all other relevant systems — automatically, reliably, and with a complete, legally auditable trail of every decision made along the way. The foundational design constraint is that UBID Fabric achieves all of this without modifying a single line of code in any source system. No department system needs to change its database schema, its API, its UI, or its deployment. UBID Fabric connects to each system using the best available integration method for that system's maturity level. For modern systems that support webhooks (Tier 1), UBID Fabric deploys webhook listeners that receive push events in real-time. For systems that expose REST APIs but lack push capability (Tier 2), UBID Fabric uses API polling with cursor-based pagination or Debezium-based CDC (Change Data Capture) that reads the database transaction log with zero performance impact on the source system. For legacy systems that offer only bulk data exports or opaque APIs (Tier 3), UBID Fabric uses periodic snapshot connectors that capture full state exports and compute field-level diffs against the previous snapshot. Regardless of the integration tier, every detected change is transformed into a uniform RawChange format and published to an Apache Kafka event backbone, partitioned by a Universal Business Identity (UBID) — a cross-system identity that links the same business entity across all 40+ department systems.The core innovation of UBID Fabric lies in three areas. First, the Conflict Convergence Engine uses CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types) — mathematically proven data structures that guarantee deterministic state convergence regardless of the order in which events are processed. For single-value fields like registered_address, we use Last-Writer-Wins (LWW) Registers ordered by Lamport timestamps. For multi-value fields like authorised_signatories, we use OR-Sets (Observed-Remove Sets) that correctly handle concurrent additions and removals. For fields that should only increase, like cumulative_investment, we use Monotonic registers. When two systems update the same field for the same business entity simultaneously, the system resolves the conflict through a four-level ladder: Level 1 applies the CRDT merge rule, Level 2 checks configurable source-priority policies (e.g., SWS is authoritative for registration fields, Factories is authoritative for factory-specific compliance data), Level 3 applies domain-ownership rules maintained by governance administrators, and Level 4, if all automated levels are insufficient, escalates to a human reviewer through the Manual Review Console with full context. Every resolution — whether automated at Level 1 or manually decided at Level 4 — is recorded as a node in the Evidence Graph with causal links to all input events, policies consulted, and actions taken.Second, the UBID identity model explicitly represents uncertainty. Instead of treating cross-system identity as binary (matched or not), UBID Fabric uses a three-state confidence model: High Confidence (score ≥ 0.90) means full propagation is allowed, Probation (0.70–0.89) means propagation proceeds but a background verification task is queued and a human reviewer is notified, and Quarantine (Third, the Evidence Graph is not a flat log — it is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) stored in PostgreSQL where every event, every translation, every conflict resolution, every propagation write, every retry, and every failure is a node, and the causal relationships between them are edges. This means that for any piece of data in any system, a reviewer or auditor can trace the complete causal chain: "This value in SWS was set because of this propagation, which was triggered by this conflict resolution, which was caused by these two competing events from these two source systems, detected at these timestamps, through these connectors." This level of traceability satisfies government audit requirements under the IT Act 2000 and enables efficient responses to RTI queries about data processing decisions.AI is integrated into UBID Fabric in five strictly controlled, offline-only use cases, each with mandatory human approval and zero access to raw PII. Use Case 1: Schema Mapping Suggestion — when onboarding a new department, an LLM (Gemini or Claude) analyses the department's schema structure alongside the SWS canonical schema using only synthetic sample data and suggests initial field correspondences with confidence scores, transformation types, and reasoning. This reduces schema mapping time from 2–3 days of manual work to 2–3 hours of human review. Use Case 2: Reconciliation Anomaly Explanation — the reconciliation engine sends aggregate drift statistics (field names, counts, temporal patterns — no actual values) to AI, which generates plain-language hypotheses about probable causes, displayed on the reviewer dashboard with explicit "AI-generated — verify before acting" labels. Use Case 3: Conflict Context Summarisation — when a conflict reaches Level 4 manual escalation, AI generates a context summary describing what happened, why automated resolution failed, and what similar past conflicts were resolved to, reducing reviewer context-gathering time from 15–30 minutes to 2–3 minutes. Use Case 4: DLQ Root Cause Analysis — when the dead-letter queue exceeds warning thresholds, AI analyses error codes, HTTP status codes, and temporal patterns (no payloads) to suggest probable root causes and investigation steps. Use Case 5: Onboarding Readiness Assessment — AI analyses department API documentation and schema exports to produce first-pass integration assessments including recommended connector tier, mapping complexity estimates, and risk flags. All AI data passes through a mandatory PII scrubbing pipeline that replaces field values with synthetic placeholders, scrambles identifiers, shifts dates, and removes all contact information. AI never touches the runtime processing path, never makes autonomous decisions, and every AI output is recorded in the evidence graph with full audit trail including model version, prompt hash, all suggestions, human decisions, and shadow mode verification results.The technology stack is production-grade and fully justified: Apache Kafka for durable, replayable event streaming with exactly-once semantics; PostgreSQL for the immutable event log and evidence graph with ACID guarantees; Redis for atomic idempotency checks (SET NX) ensuring exactly-once processing; Temporal.io for saga-based orchestration with automatic retry, timeout management, and compensation workflows; Debezium for zero-impact CDC; Kubernetes for container orchestration; Prometheus and Grafana for comprehensive monitoring with 30+ custom metrics across five categories (connector health, Kafka throughput, pipeline latency, saga execution, and governance); and HashiCorp Vault for centralised credential management with automatic rotation.

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


V VSquare
TEAM SUBMISSION [3]
BridgeSync : A Two-Way Interoperability Fabric for Karnataka's Single Window System
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 19, 2026 10:40 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Problem Statement and Need Karnataka’s business registration ecosystem faces a classic “split-brain” challenge: the modern Single Window System (SWS) coexists with 40+ legacy department systems, each acting as its own source of truth with different schemas, identifiers, and integration capabilities. Updates made in one system often don’t propagate to others, leading to inconsistent records, conflicting decisions across departments, and confusion for citizens. A full-scale migration into SWS is impractical and high-risk, as seen in large rollouts like GST, due to heterogeneous data formats, limited or absent APIs, and fragile legacy infrastructure. What’s fundamentally missing is a non-intrusive interoperability layer that continuously synchronizes, normalizes, and reconciles data across systems without requiring disruptive changes.
Solution Overview BridgeSync is a standalone interoperability fabric deployed independently of both SWS and department systems, integrating only through existing APIs, webhooks, or accessible data surfaces without requiring internal changes. Direction 1 — SWS to Department Systems: When SWS generates a service request, BridgeSync captures the event, identifies the UBID, and uses a Translation Registry to map SWS actions to each department’s schema and APIs. It dispatches requests with idempotency keys, ensures reliable delivery via retries with backoff, and records outcomes for consistency and traceability. Direction 2 — Department Systems to SWS:BridgeSync handles heterogeneous systems using three strategies: webhooks (push-based), API polling (incremental fetch), and snapshot comparison (for systems with no change signals). Detected changes are normalized via the Translation Registry, mapped to UBIDs, and synchronized back to SWS. Conflict Detection and Resolution: For near-simultaneous updates on the same UBID, BridgeSync enforces a configurable lock window to detect conflicts. Resolution follows ownership-based policies (e.g., SWS vs department authority), while unresolved cases are escalated for human review with complete audit context. Audit Trail: Every synchronization event—success, failure, retry, or conflict—is logged in an append-only, queryable audit store capturing source, target systems, payload transformations, timestamps, and final outcomes, enabling full end-to-end traceability and accountability.
Comprehensive Feature Set 1. Bidirectional Event Router — smart routing of events to relevant systems, 2. Three-Mode Change Capture — webhook, polling, and diff-based update detection, 3. Translation Registry — configurable schema and payload mapping, 4. UBID Resolver — identifies systems holding business records, 5. Idempotent Dispatch Engine — prevents duplicate processing on retries, 6. Conflict Detection & Resolution Engine — detects and resolves concurrent updates, 7. Retry Queue with Dead-Letter — retries failures and isolates unresolved cases, 8. Append-Only Audit Ledger — immutable logging for full traceability, 9. Operations Dashboard — real-time monitoring of sync and system health, 10. Connector SDK — standardised framework for adding new integrations
Technology Stack 1. Apache Kafka — event streaming, ordering, 2. Python — flexible data transformation, 3. PostgreSQL — transactional state, audit, 4. Redis — fast idempotency, locking, 5. Apache Airflow — scheduled polling orchestration, 6. FastAPI — async APIs, webhooks, 7. Docker & Kubernetes — scalable deployment
System Architecture and Workflow Tier 1 — Source Systems (Untouched): SWS and 40+ legacy department systems operate exactly as they do today with zero modifications. They remain purely as producers and consumers of data through their existing surfaces. Tier 2 — Change Capture Layer: Dedicated per-department connectors detect changes via webhook listeners, scheduled API polling, or snapshot diffing depending on the system's capability. This ensures no department system, however legacy, is left out of the sync loop. Tier 3 — Event Backbone (Kafka): Every captured change is published to a Kafka topic partitioned by UBID, guaranteeing ordered, durable, and replayable delivery. This fully decouples detection from processing and acts as the system's single source of event truth. Tier 4 — Core Processing Engine: The Event Router resolves target systems via UBID, transforms payloads through the Translation Registry, and enforces conflict detection and idempotency before any write is dispatched. This is where all the intelligence — translation, conflict resolution, deduplication — lives. Tier 5 — Dispatch and Delivery Layer: Translated and verified requests are pushed to target systems via their APIs, with a Retry Queue handling transient failures through exponential backoff. Exhausted retries land in a Dead-Letter Queue with full diagnostics and operator alerts. Tier 6 — Persistence and Audit Layer: PostgreSQL maintains the State Store for delivery tracking and snapshot baselines, alongside an append-only Audit Ledger capturing every event, translation, conflict, and resolution permanently. Redis handles ephemeral idempotency keys and UBID lock windows at low latency. Tier 7 — Operations and Observability Layer: A FastAPI-powered dashboard gives operators real-time visibility into sync status, conflict queues, failed propagations, and end-to-end traceability for any service request. This transforms silent failures into visible, actionable, self-healing operational events.
Unique Differentiators 1. Configuration-driven extensibility — no code-heavy integrations needed, 2. Change-capture without source modification — works with any legacy system, 3. UBID as core primitive — unified identity across systems, 4. Field-level conflict resolution — explainable, auditable decisions, 5. Designed for progressive migration — avoids big-bang migration risk
Challenges And Impacts 1. Challenge — Schema heterogeneity, Strategy — automated versioned mappings, Impact — consistent integrations, 2. Challenge — No change emission systems, Strategy — timestamps, faster polling, Impact — improved freshness, 3. Challenge — Non-idempotent APIs, Strategy — read-before-write checks, Impact — avoids duplicates, 4. Challenge — Conflict resolution trust, Strategy — review, notifications, Impact — user confidence, 5. Challenge — PII in LLM features, Strategy — data anonymisation layer, Impact — privacy compliance
Scenario After Project Adoption Before BridgeSync, address updates made in SWS don’t reach systems like Factories or Shop Establishment, forcing businesses to resubmit changes across portals and leading to inconsistent records and repeated notices. After BridgeSync, a single update on SWS is automatically propagated to all relevant departments within minutes, with confirmations and a complete audit trail visible end-to-end. Department-side updates also sync back to SWS seamlessly, eliminating silent data divergence and removing the need for manual reconciliation. Operationally, failures become visible via retry queues and dashboards, enabling faster resolution instead of relying on citizen complaints. Overall, it enables a gradual, low-risk transition where SWS becomes the single source of truth with full consistency, auditability, and trust across the ecosystem.
For More Understanding of the Idea and Visual Representation :https://drive.google.com/file/d/1qKSKwP70UwG06or82br8hWt_Q0jK9e-V/view A simple prototype to demonstrate the proposed solution :https://bridge-truth-sync.lovable.app

“From system silos to a single sync — BridgeSync turns fragmented truths into one trusted truth.”

solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


P priyanshu23
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
The "Smart Bridge": A Two-Way Interoperability Layer for Karnataka's E-Governance Ecosystem
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 20, 2026 08:36 AM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Solution Proposal: Two-Way Interoperability Layer for Karnataka's Single Window System
Problem Understanding & The "Split-Brain" Dilemma Karnataka currently operates a modern Single Window System (SWS) alongside 40+ legacy department systems (e.g., Factories, Shop Establishment). Because a "big-bang" migration is unviable due to the risk of losing decades of operational data and disrupting active businesses, both systems run concurrently. This creates a "split-brain" problem: a business updating its registered address in the SWS will not see this change reflected in the legacy systems, and vice versa. To resolve this without modifying the source systems, we propose an anti-fragile, bidirectional interoperability layer that relies strictly on the Unique Business Identifier (UBID) as the absolute semantic anchor to join business records across all disparate databases.
2. Architectural Overview To avoid the exponential technical debt of point-to-point connections, the proposed solution operates as a centralized, decoupled, event-driven middleware hub. Grounded in Enterprise Integration Patterns, this layer acts as a Message Router, Schema Translator, and SAGA Orchestrator. It guarantees asynchronous, at-least-once delivery using Apache Kafka as its distributed event broker.
Direction 1: Propagating from SWS to Department Systems When a service request is raised in the SWS, the change is captured via an outbound webhook or the Transactional Outbox pattern. UBID Routing: A Redis-based UBID Registry dictates exactly which legacy departments hold records for the specific business entity. Metadata-Driven Schema Translation: To integrate legacy schemas without hardcoding rules, we deploy a Metadata-Driven Schema Mapping Engine (using tools like Apache Camel or Jolt). Translation rules are stored as declarative YAML files, meaning new departments can be onboarded seamlessly without recompiling the core application. Idempotent Proxy (No Lost Updates or Duplicates):Because older APIs lack native duplicate detection, we protect them using an Idempotent Proxy backed by a Redis cluster. Before making an API call, it generates a SHA-256 hash of the payload and checks the cache; if a network timeout forces a retry, the proxy intercepts it and returns the cached success response, preventing silent data overwrites.
Direction 2: Discovering and Propagating from Departments to SWS Detecting manual changes in legacy systems without altering their code requires non-intrusive data extraction methodologies. Our architecture utilizes a tiered approach: *Log-Based Change Data Capture (CDC): For databases with Write-Ahead Logs (WAL) like Oracle, MySQL, or PostgreSQL, we deploy Debezium. It silently reads the database transaction logs at the filesystem level to capture INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE events in real-time, completely bypassing the database query engine. Hash-Based Snapshot Differential Algorithms: For legacy flat-file data stores or highly locked-down systems without WAL access, we deploy advanced snapshot comparison algorithms, such as the Partitioned Hash Algorithm and the Hash-Based Window Algorithm. By hashing keys and compressing non-key attributes into cryptographic signatures in memory, these algorithms rapidly identify exact deltas between periodic data snapshots without causing server overloads. Detected changes are then reverse-translated and pushed to the SWS.
Cross-Cutting Concern: Conflict Detection & Resolution Concurrent modifications are inevitable. If a citizen updates their address in SWS and an officer updates a different field for the same UBID in a legacy system simultaneously, the system employs a Distributed State Arbiter powered by Apache Flink. Configurable Policy Engine: A time-windowed ledger flags concurrent updates and routes them to a policy engine. Resolution policies are field-specific and include strategies like Last-Write-Wins (LWW), Source-of-Truth Priority (e.g., SWS always dictates addresses), Custom Hybrid Merges (e.g., combining license arrays), or Human-in-the-Loop queues for high-stakes legal anomalies. Only mathematically resolved payloads are committed, entirely preventing data corruption.
Cross-Cutting Concern: Auditability & Distributed State Management Immutable Audit Trail: To satisfy strict government compliance, every transaction, translation rule applied, conflict resolution decision, and final payload delivery is logged in a cryptographically secure, append-only Merkle Tree audit chain (stored in TimescaleDB). This ensures all state changes are mathematically tamper-evident, fully explainable, and securely reversible. SAGA Orchestrator: If a multi-department update partially fails (e.g., 4 out of 5 legacy systems succeed, but the 5th crashes), a Temporal.io SAGA Orchestrator automatically halts the workflow and issues Compensating Transactions to roll back the successful systems. This guarantees that the UBID state is consistent across the entire government ecosystem.
Risks and Constraints Eventual Consistency: By using message brokers, the system trades strict, real-time ACID consistency for high availability, meaning systems may be out of sync for a few seconds during processing. Constraint Compliance: Our solution uses strictly synthetic data and zero raw PII is exposed to LLMs. LLMs are strictly limited to offline tasks such as suggesting schema mapping rules based on obfuscated payload structures.

solutions.team_name: SyncBridge — A Two-Way Interoperability Layer for Karnataka's SWS and Department Systems
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


F FutureMinds
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
SyncBridge — A Two-Way Interoperability Layer for Karnataka's SWS and Department Systems
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 20, 2026 12:29 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
What We Understood From the ProblemKarnataka has a classic "too many systems, no single truth" problem. The Single Window System exists as the intended front door for businesses, but 40+ legacy department systems — Factories, Shop Establishment, Labour, KSPCB, and others — are still the actual source of truth for most registered businesses. These systems were built independently, each with different schemas and different identifiers, and they have no way to talk to each other.The real pain is simple: if a business updates its address on SWS, none of the department systems know about it. If the same business renews a licence directly inside the Factories system, SWS never finds out. Both sides drift. Officers see inconsistent data. Citizens have to make the same update in multiple places. And there's no audit trail of what happened where.The obvious fix — migrating everything into one system — doesn't work. The GST rollout showed what happens when you try a big-bang cutover at government scale. Our solution doesn't touch either side. It sits in the middle, listens to both, and keeps them in sync.One important dependency we're acknowledging upfront: this solution requires the UBID (Unique Business Identifier) from Theme 1 as a precondition. Without a common join key across systems, two-way sync is impossible. We treat UBID as an input to our layer, not something we re-solve here.﻿How SyncBridge WorksAt its core, SyncBridge is an event-driven middleware layer. When something changes on either side, we capture it, normalize it into a common format, translate it for the target system, and write it — with a complete audit trail at every step.We call this common format a Canonical Change Event (CCE). It looks like this:json{ "event_id": "EVT-20240415-0042", "ubid": "KA-2024-001892", "source": "SWS", "field": "registered_address", "new_value": "14, MG Road, Bengaluru - 560001", "timestamp": "2024-04-15T10:32:00Z"}Every change, regardless of where it originated, becomes a CCE. This means our Translation Engine only has to solve one problem: converting between a CCE and a system-specific payload, rather than mapping every system to every other system directly.﻿Direction 1: SWS → Department SystemsWhen a service request is raised on SWS, our SWS adapter picks it up (via webhook if SWS supports it, polling otherwise). It converts the request into a CCE and publishes it to our internal Kafka event bus.The Translation Engine reads the CCE, looks up which department systems have a record for that UBID, fetches the appropriate field mapping from our Schema Registry, and sends a translated write request to each target system.We handle idempotency by generating a deterministic request_id from the event ID + target system name. If a write fails and retries, the same request_id goes again — the target system won't apply it twice if it supports idempotency keys. If it doesn't, we do a pre-write read to check if the value already matches before writing.Failed writes after all retries go to a dead letter queue — never silently dropped.﻿Direction 2: Department Systems → SWSThis direction is harder because most legacy systems don't broadcast changes. We handle three different situations:Webhook mode — for systems that can push notifications. We register a callback URL and process incoming events as they arrive.Polling mode — for systems with a queryable API. We poll on a schedule (every 5 minutes by default), compare the response to our last known snapshot per UBID, and generate a CCE for any detected delta.Snapshot diff mode — for systems that only provide periodic data exports. We ingest the export, compute a hash per UBID, diff against the previous run, and generate CCEs for anything that changed.In all three cases, before we generate a CCE, we resolve the department's native record ID to a UBID using the UBID registry. If we can't make a confident match, the event goes to a human reviewer queue — not silently dropped, not silently assigned.﻿Conflict HandlingConflicts happen when two updates for the same UBID and field arrive from different sources within a short time window (we default to 60 seconds, configurable).We detect this using a Redis-based in-flight tracker — when a second CCE arrives for a (UBID, field) that already has an unresolved write in progress, we pause both and apply the configured conflict policy:SWS-wins — for fields where SWS is meant to be authoritative (e.g., GST number, business name)Last-write-wins — for operational fields like phone numbers, using timestamp + source priority as tiebreakerHold for human review — for high-stakes fields like registered address or authorised signatoryThe conflict policy is configured per field type in the Schema Registry YAML. A reviewer sees both values, both sources, both timestamps, and clicks to resolve. That decision is logged.﻿Schema Registry — How We Handle 40+ Different SystemsEvery department's field mappings are stored as a versioned YAML file:yamlsystem: shop_establishmentubid_field: pan_linked_idmappings: registered_address: target_field: estb_address transform: "concat(addr1, ', ', addr2, ' - ', pincode)" conflict_policy: HOLD_FOR_REVIEWAdding a new department means writing a new YAML file. No code changes. No restarts. The Translation Engine loads mappings dynamically at runtime. This is important because onboarding a 41st department shouldn't require a deployment.﻿Audit TrailEvery event gets a row in an append-only PostgreSQL table. No UPDATEs, no DELETEs — ever. Each row records what changed, which system it came from, which system it was written to, what the outcome was, and whether a conflict was detected and how it was resolved.We store value hashes, not raw values, in the audit table — this satisfies the PII constraint. A reviewer can retrieve the actual value from the source system using the event metadata.You can reconstruct the full history of any service request with one query:sqlSELECT * FROM audit_ledger WHERE ubid = 'KA-2024-001892' ORDER BY processed_at;﻿Architecture OverviewSWS API ◄──► SWS Adapter ──┐ │Dept Systems ◄──► Dept ├──► Kafka Event Bus Adapters ────────┘ │(webhook / ▼ polling / Translation Engine snapshot) + Schema Registry (YAML) │ Conflict Detector (Redis) │ Audit Ledger (PostgreSQL) │ Reviewer Dashboard (React)Why these choices:Kafka over a simple queue because we need replay capability — if something goes wrong, we can reprocess events from any point in timePostgreSQL append-only table for audit because ACID guarantees matter and we never want a row modified or deletedRedis for conflict window tracking because it's fast and TTL-based keys clean themselves up automaticallyYAML + Git for schema mappings because version control gives us change history and rollback for freeSpring Boot for the engine because we have existing experience and its Kafka integration is mature﻿Risks We've Thought AboutWhat if a department has no usable API at all? We'd fall back to authenticated scraping of the department portal as a last resort, flagged as low-confidence. Events from this source always go through human review before being propagated. This is an edge case but it exists.What if UBID resolution fails for a department record? The event goes to a reviewer pending tray. It never propagates with a wrong UBID. As the UBID registry matures (Theme 1's output improves over time), these failures will decrease — it's an expected bootstrapping cost.What if a department changes its API schema without notice? Our adapters validate every response against the expected schema definition. Any unexpected field structure raises an alert immediately. We fail loudly, not silently.What if a department does a bulk offline update and comes back online? We'd see a conflict storm — thousands of events all flagged as conflicts. We handle this with a "bulk-update mode" flag in the Schema Registry: when set, that department's events skip the conflict detector, write with department-wins policy, and are bulk-flagged in the audit log for retrospective review.﻿Round 2 PlanGiven a sandbox with mock SWS and 3–4 department endpoints:Week 1 — Set up Kafka, PostgreSQL, Redis. Build SWS adapter and one department adapter (polling). Get a single end-to-end address change propagating with audit trail.Week 2 — Add remaining department adapters (webhook + snapshot diff). Build Translation Engine with Schema Registry. Demonstrate Direction 2.Week 3 — Build Conflict Detector, all three resolution policies, and the Reviewer Dashboard. Demo the conflict scenario from the problem statement.Week 4 — Idempotency stress test (replay same events 10x, verify no duplicates). Bulk-update scenario test. Final audit trail demo. Record walkthrough video.Final deliverable: Docker Compose stack with all components running, scripted demo reproducing all three scenarios from the problem statement.﻿Team FutureMinds — PIEMR, Indore

solutions.team_name: IML: A Non-Invasive Interoperability Layer for SWS and Karnataka's 40+ Department Systems
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


M mohitkumar001700_7bd8
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
IML: A Non-Invasive Interoperability Layer for SWS and Karnataka's 40+ Department Systems
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 22, 2026 02:45 AM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
The core problem we are solvingKarnataka's Single Window System and 40+ legacy department systems (Shop Establishment, Factories, Labour, KSPCB, BESCOM, BWSSB, Fire, Food Safety, ULB/RLB) run as independent silos with no shared identifier and no change propagation. A business updating its address in SWS sees that update silently ignored by Factories. A signatory change made directly in Factories never reaches SWS. Citizens and officers see contradictory state. The gap is not digitisation — it is the missing coordination layer between systems that cannot be modified.Our solution: Interoperability Middleware Layer (IML)An event-driven propagation engine that sits entirely outside SWS and every department system, integrates only through existing APIs, and guarantees bidirectional synchronisation with full auditability.Five-layer architectureChange Detection Layer — Three connector strategies cover every legacy system: webhook listener (for event-emitting systems), API polling with snapshot hashing (for REST-queryable systems with no event emission), and diff-based file comparison (for batch-export-only systems). No source system is modified.Normalisation Layer — Every inbound event is translated into a canonical IML format using a versioned Schema Registry (PostgreSQL + Flyway). Field mappings are stored as configuration, not code. Adding a new department is a registry entry, not a release.Routing & Conflict Engine — UBID (from Theme 1) is the canonical join key. When two events for the same UBID and field arrive inside a configurable window (default: 30 seconds), one of four policies applies: SWS-wins, last-write-wins, source-priority, or human-review-required. Every automated resolution is reversible.Write & Confirmation Layer — Idempotency is guaranteed via a SHA-256 dedup key (UBID + event_type + source + field + new_value + timestamp). At-least-once delivery runs on Kafka with 5-attempt exponential backoff. Exhausted retries land in a Dead Letter Queue visible to reviewers — nothing is silently dropped.Audit & Review Layer — Append-only log of every event: detection, normalisation, routing decision, conflict, write attempt, confirmation, and human override. Immutable and suitable for formal compliance review. A Flutter Web dashboard lets reviewers inspect conflict pairs, trace any propagation end-to-end, and work the DLQ.Why we are the right team for this problemWe previously built Samay Sarathi — a real-time coordination platform linking ambulances, hospitals, and traffic systems with no shared identifier, incompatible schemas, and no common API contract. IML is architecturally the same problem class: multiple authoritative systems, heterogeneous APIs, no source modification permitted. This is a direct application of what we've already solved, not a new bet.Non-negotiables complianceNo source system (SWS or any department system) is modified. External-facing APIs only.UBID is the only join key. IML does not invent its own identifier.No hosted LLM is called on any raw or scrambled PII. Schema translation is deterministic rule-based mapping only.All propagation is idempotent (dedup key) and fully auditable (append-only log).No silent overwrites. Conflicts surface for review. Failures surface in the DLQ.Tech stack: Spring Boot microservices, Apache Kafka, Redis (dedup store), PostgreSQL (Schema Registry + Audit log), Flutter Web (reviewer dashboard). Deployable on NIC Cloud / NeSDA government infrastructure.Round 2 deliverable: A working sandbox demo of a single business going through five scenarios — SWS→dept propagation, dept→SWS propagation, concurrent conflict with human resolution, write retry on failure, and end-to-end audit trace query — all on scrambled synthetic data.

TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
IML: A Non-Invasive Interoperability Layer for SWS and Karnataka's 40+ Department Systems
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 22, 2026 02:45 AM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
The core problem we are solvingKarnataka's Single Window System and 40+ legacy department systems (Shop Establishment, Factories, Labour, KSPCB, BESCOM, BWSSB, Fire, Food Safety, ULB/RLB) run as independent silos with no shared identifier and no change propagation. A business updating its address in SWS sees that update silently ignored by Factories. A signatory change made directly in Factories never reaches SWS. Citizens and officers see contradictory state. The gap is not digitisation — it is the missing coordination layer between systems that cannot be modified.Our solution: Interoperability Middleware Layer (IML)An event-driven propagation engine that sits entirely outside SWS and every department system, integrates only through existing APIs, and guarantees bidirectional synchronisation with full auditability.Five-layer architectureChange Detection Layer — Three connector strategies cover every legacy system: webhook listener (for event-emitting systems), API polling with snapshot hashing (for REST-queryable systems with no event emission), and diff-based file comparison (for batch-export-only systems). No source system is modified.Normalisation Layer — Every inbound event is translated into a canonical IML format using a versioned Schema Registry (PostgreSQL + Flyway). Field mappings are stored as configuration, not code. Adding a new department is a registry entry, not a release.Routing & Conflict Engine — UBID (from Theme 1) is the canonical join key. When two events for the same UBID and field arrive inside a configurable window (default: 30 seconds), one of four policies applies: SWS-wins, last-write-wins, source-priority, or human-review-required. Every automated resolution is reversible.Write & Confirmation Layer — Idempotency is guaranteed via a SHA-256 dedup key (UBID + event_type + source + field + new_value + timestamp). At-least-once delivery runs on Kafka with 5-attempt exponential backoff. Exhausted retries land in a Dead Letter Queue visible to reviewers — nothing is silently dropped.Audit & Review Layer — Append-only log of every event: detection, normalisation, routing decision, conflict, write attempt, confirmation, and human override. Immutable and suitable for formal compliance review. A Flutter Web dashboard lets reviewers inspect conflict pairs, trace any propagation end-to-end, and work the DLQ.Why we are the right team for this problemWe previously built Samay Sarathi — a real-time coordination platform linking ambulances, hospitals, and traffic systems with no shared identifier, incompatible schemas, and no common API contract. IML is architecturally the same problem class: multiple authoritative systems, heterogeneous APIs, no source modification permitted. This is a direct application of what we've already solved, not a new bet.Non-negotiables complianceNo source system (SWS or any department system) is modified. External-facing APIs only.UBID is the only join key. IML does not invent its own identifier.No hosted LLM is called on any raw or scrambled PII. Schema translation is deterministic rule-based mapping only.All propagation is idempotent (dedup key) and fully auditable (append-only log).No silent overwrites. Conflicts surface for review. Failures surface in the DLQ.Tech stack: Spring Boot microservices, Apache Kafka, Redis (dedup store), PostgreSQL (Schema Registry + Audit log), Flutter Web (reviewer dashboard). Deployable on NIC Cloud / NeSDA government infrastructure.Round 2 deliverable: A working sandbox demo of a single business going through five scenarios — SWS→dept propagation, dept→SWS propagation, concurrent conflict with human resolution, write retry on failure, and end-to-end audit trace query — all on scrambled synthetic data.

COMPETE

D DEVELOPERS
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
SyncBridge A Two-Way Interoperability Layer for Karnataka's SWS and Legacy Department Systems
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 23, 2026 02:38 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Karnataka's businesses live in two worlds simultaneously. They exist in the modern Single Window System (SWS) and also in 40+ old department systems Factories, Shop Establishment, Labour, KSPCB and more. When a business updates its address on SWS, the department systems never find out. When something changes inside a department system, SWS stays in the dark. Two systems, two versions of truth, zero sync.We are building SyncBridge a middleware layer that sits between SWS and all legacy department systems without modifying either side. It watches for changes on both ends, translates data between different formats (since every system stores the same information differently), and delivers updates reliably in both directions using UBID as the common join key.Our approach handles three core challenges:Change Detection — For modern systems we use webhooks. For older systems that don't emit events, we use scheduled polling. For completely silent legacy systems, we do periodic snapshot comparisons to catch what changed.Conflict Resolution — When two updates for the same business arrive from different systems within a short window, we detect the conflict, apply a configurable resolution policy (SWS-priority by default), and log exactly why the final value was chosen. Nothing is silently overwritten.Full Audit Trail — Every propagation is logged end to end — what changed, where it came from, where it was delivered, whether a conflict occurred and how it was resolved. Any officer can trace any update back to its origin.Every delivery is idempotent , if a network failure causes a retry, the same update reaching a system twice produces the same result, no duplicates.Tech stack: FastAPI for the middleware layer, Kafka for reliable event queuing, MongoDB for the audit log, Python config-driven schema translation maps for each department, Docker and GitHub Actions for deployment.Our team brings direct relevant experience API integration across multiple services, async background job pipelines, and ETL data translation work making this a problem we are confident we can actually build, not just design on paper.

solutions.team_name: JudgeAct — AI-Powered Court Compliance Intelligence for Karnataka's CCMS
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


S samikshakhaire05_cc62
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
JudgeAct — AI-Powered Court Compliance Intelligence for Karnataka's CCMS
Theme: Theme 11: From Court Judgments to Verified Action Plans Submitted On: Apr 23, 2026 05:49 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Problem Karnataka's CCMS automatically receives judgment PDFs from the High Court but stops there. Officials manually read 20–200 page documents to identify compliance obligations, responsible departments, and appeal windows. Critical directives get buried, deadlines are missed, and missed deadlines expose senior officers to personal contempt-of-court liability. No automated system currently tracks these deadlines across Karnataka.
Solution — JudgeAct (4-Stage Verified Pipeline)
Stage 1 — Intelligent Extraction JudgeAct passively reads the existing CCMS PDF feed with zero modification to the source system. PyMuPDF + Tesseract 5 (Kannada/Hindi OCR) parse each judgment. GPT-4o in JSON mode extracts: case details, operative directions, compliance type, parties, deadlines, appeal windows, and per-field confidence scores.
Stage 2 — Action Plan Generation For every extraction, JudgeAct auto-generates a structured action plan covering all four mandatory decision points: compliance required → responsible department (mapped to Karnataka's org structure) → whether to appeal → deadline with live countdown (Karnataka holiday-aware calendar).
Stage 3 — Mandatory Human Verification No AI output reaches any officer without explicit human sign-off. Reviewers see the source PDF with relevant sentences highlighted, extracted fields with confidence bars, and full source traceability. Actions: Approve / Edit & Approve / Reject. Every action is logged to an immutable, exportable audit trail.
Stage 4 — Trusted Dashboard Only human-approved records appear. Department-wise compliance views, deadline calendars with automated alerts at T−30, T−14, T−7, T−3, and T−0 (Email + SMS), priority queues (CRITICAL → LOW), and appeal trackers give decision-makers reliable, accountable visibility.
Key Features • Zero modification to existing CCMS infrastructure • Full explainability — every field traceable to its source sentence in the original PDF • Confidence-based routing — ambiguous extractions always go to human review, never silent commitment • Immutable audit log exportable for contempt proceedings • Scalable from Karnataka to all 18 lakh+ High Court cases/year across India
Tech Stack: PyMuPDF · Tesseract 5 (Indic) · GPT-4o (JSON mode) · FastAPI · PostgreSQL · Celery + Redis · React + TypeScript · Docker Compose
Impact: Zero missed court directives. Faster government response. Accountable, auditable compliance tracking — at scale across all Karnataka departments.

solutions.team_name: Zero-Disruption Bidirectional Sync Layer for Karnataka's Single Window System
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


B bvsaisujith27_bfcc
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
Zero-Disruption Bidirectional Sync Layer for Karnataka's Single Window System
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 23, 2026 08:04 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Zero Disruption Bidirectional Sync Layer for Karnatakas Single Window SystemUnderstanding the Problem and Real World ConstraintsKarnatakas system has a big problem. The state cannot suddenly move all 40 plus department systems to the Single Window System because it is too risky. We cannot change the code or database structures of the Single Window System or the old systems. So we need to build a middle layer that works with the Unique Business Identifier to recognize the same business across different databases and make changes without disrupting anything.﻿2.Two Way Propagation and Change DiscoveryTo capture changes in data without changing the code we use Change Data Capture.Log Based Change Data Capture: For databases that support it we use Debezium to read transaction logs. This captures changes in time without slowing down the original databases.Snapshot Based Change Data Capture: For systems that do not support change data capture we compare snapshots of the data at different times to find changes.3.Translating Database SchemasWhen a change is captured it is sent as an event. A middle layer intercepts this event uses the Unique Business Identifier to find the target record. Applies a translation dictionary. As new departments are added administrators can simply add a mapping configuration without changing the core synchronization logic.4.Resolving Conflicts and Keeping a RecordWhen changes are made to the Unique Business Identifier from both the Single Window System and an old system the middle layer does not automatically overwrite the data. Instead it applies a set of rules.Source of Truth Rules: Certain systems are designated as the authority for fields.Timestamp Rules: If the authority is the same it uses a Last Writer Wins approach based on the timestamp.Record of Changes: Every change is recorded in a ledger that cannot be changed including the old state, the new state, the source of the change and the conflict resolution rule applied.5.Guaranteeing Delivery and IdempotencyTo ensure that no changes are lost if the system fails we use an event streaming platform that guarantees at one delivery. Because this can sometimes result in events the target databases must be able to handle duplicate writes. We achieve this by converting all writes into operations that update if the record exists and insert if it does not.6.Risks and Trade OffsThere is a risk that old systems may experience delays in updates. We trade off updates for system stability accepting a delay of 5 to 15 minutes for older systems to fulfill the requirement of not modifying the original code.7.Implementation PlanWe will build a prototype using data.Days 1 and 2: We will generate 500 business records to mock the Single Window System and other endpoints.Days 3 and 4: We will build a middle layer script to simulate changes and schema translation, between these mock endpoints.Days 5 and 6: We will implement the idempotency logic. Build a simple user interface to display the record of changes and demonstrate conflict resolution.

solutions.team_name: Samanvay — A Causal Semantic Interoperability Fabric Between Karnataka's Single Window System and 40+ Department Systems
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


A Arjuna
TEAM SUBMISSION [9]
Samanvay — A Causal Semantic Interoperability Fabric Between Karnataka's Single Window System and 40+ Department Systems
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 23, 2026 10:17 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Samanvay is Sanskrit for coordination, harmony, integration — which is exactly what we are building. It is a causal-semantic interoperability fabric that sits between Karnataka's Single Window System (SWS) and the 40+ legacy department systems that must stay in sync with it. Samanvay never modifies either side. It observes changes at both ends, understands what they mean, translates between their schemas, reasons about what each change should trigger elsewhere, propagates it in both directions, detects and resolves conflicts by policy, and records every action on a tamper-evident audit ledger so that any service request — and any retry, any conflict, any reconciliation — can be traced end-to-end by UBID.The problem, stated plainly. SWS is the State's forward-looking front door for new business registrations and cross-department service requests. In parallel, 40+ department systems remain the authoritative record for businesses already on their books, and will continue to be for years. A single-day cutover is not realistic at this scale; the GST rollout is a permanent reminder of why big-bang migrations of this scope fail. The consequence today is a split-brain problem — a change of registered address raised on SWS does not reach Factories, a change of authorised signatory raised inside Shop Establishment does not reach SWS, and the two sides drift out of sync. Citizens and officers see inconsistent state depending on which window they walk up to. Any working solution must use UBID as the common join key and must not touch SWS or any department system.The baseline, done rigorously. Samanvay is built on five components, each doing one job well. A UBID Registry maps every business's UBID to its local IDs across all 40+ systems — every propagation starts with a lookup here, and this is the only new state Samanvay holds about businesses. A Schema Mapping Registry is one declarative YAML per department: when department #41 onboards, we add one file and redeploy — no core code changes, which is the single biggest lever for long-term maintainability. Change Detection is tiered: Tier A for departments with native webhooks, Tier B polling for those with "changes-since" APIs, Tier C nightly snapshot diffing for read-only systems, and Tier D Debezium log-tailing where permitted. No department can block the bus by refusing to emit events — there is always a fallback tier. The Propagation Engine is idempotent by construction: every event gets a deterministic ID, at-least-once delivery from Kafka combined with idempotent writes produces an exactly-once effect, and permanent failures move to a dead-letter queue. The Conflict Resolution Engine applies per-field policies — not per-system — because authority over "registered address" and authority over "factory safety certificate" live with different departments. Under all of it, an append-only PostgreSQL Audit Ledger with hash-chained rows records every event_id, UBID, field, old_value, new_value, source_system, target_system, and resolution reason, producing the tamper-evident end-to-end traceability the problem statement demands.Where Samanvay earns its keep, beyond the baseline. Four additions layer on top of the proven interoperability pattern — together they form an architecture that no conventional event-bus-plus-mapping approach can match.(1) The Causal Interoperability Graph (CIG). Every propagation is treated as a causal event in a Neo4j-backed knowledge structure. Over months of operation, the CIG learns which changes in which department tend to produce which downstream changes elsewhere — a registered-address change typically cascading to Factories, Shop Establishment, and Pollution Control forms a causal cluster. When a new change arrives, Samanvay does not just propagate it mechanically; it predicts which downstream updates are likely, runs the predicted cascade inside a Digital Twin Shadow before committing, and only commits once the cascade passes pre-flight validation. This turns reactive integration into predictive integration, catches silently-failing departments before a citizen complaint does, and turns the bus itself into the State's real-time causal model of its own business fabric.(2) Zero-Trust PII with Partial Homomorphic Encryption. Samanvay sits on the juiciest dataset in the State — so we built it so the bus itself cannot read the most sensitive fields. PAN, bank account, Aadhaar, and authorised signatory never leave encrypted form end to end. They move through Samanvay under partial homomorphic encryption using OpenFHE (the open-source successor to Microsoft SEAL and PALISADE), so that field-level equality matching, format conversion, and routing happen on ciphertext. The bus never sees plaintext PII. Audit rows over these fields are anchored with zero-knowledge proofs, so an auditor or a CAG officer can verify that "the value written to Factories equals the value raised from SWS" without either system or the auditor learning the underlying value. This is not a theoretical nice-to-have — it is what allows a bus of this scope to exist inside a government perimeter in the first place.(3) Neuro-Symbolic Conflict Arbitration with Explanations in Kannada. Conflicts are resolved by a hybrid engine. The symbolic half is Open Policy Agent: declarative, Git-versioned, reviewable rules that decide authority per field. The neural half is a locally-hosted small language model — Llama 3.1 8B fine-tuned on Karnataka administrative language, running on inference nodes that never leave Indian jurisdiction — that produces plain-language explanations of every resolution, in Kannada and English. An officer reviewing a flag does not see "policy 47B applied"; they read "the registered address raised on SWS at 14:03 overrode the Factories address last updated 11 February 2024, because SWS is authoritative for identity fields; the earlier Factories value is retained in audit row 18772 and is restorable." Every resolution is reversible, every explanation is auditable, every policy change is a Git pull request reviewed like code.(4) CRDT Consistency Layer and Self-Healing Schema Drift Detection. For the handful of fields where either side can legitimately be authoritative — typically contact metadata — Samanvay uses Conflict-free Replicated Data Types (Last-Write-Wins registers with vector clocks, OR-Sets for multi-valued attributes). This gives a mathematical convergence guarantee: regardless of the order in which updates arrive across Kafka partitions, all replicas reach the same final state. In parallel, a drift detector continuously computes embedding-space signatures of incoming payloads per department using sentence-transformers and alerts when a department's distribution shifts beyond a learned threshold — a field renamed in a quiet API update, a date format silently changing, a new enum value appearing. At that point Samanvay either auto-regenerates the mapping YAML using a locally hosted model and flags it for human merge, or holds writes to that department until an operator confirms. This is the feature that keeps the bus alive in year three without a platform team quietly growing to thirty engineers.Why this is deployable inside a government. Every technology choice is open source, Indian-cloud-deployable, and familiar to the engineers who already run state digital infrastructure: Apache Kafka (or Redpanda where operational simplicity matters more) for the event bus; PostgreSQL with hash-chained rows for the registry and audit; Debezium for log-tailing where permitted; Open Policy Agent for declarative policies; Neo4j for the causal graph; Apache Flink for stream processing; Kubernetes on MeghRaj, the State Data Centre, or any Indian sovereign cloud; Prometheus, Grafana, and Loki for observability; HashiCorp Vault for secret management; Istio for the service mesh; eBPF-based tooling for deep telemetry; OpenFHE for homomorphic operations; locally-hosted Llama 3.1 8B and sentence-transformers models for the AI-assisted pieces. No vendor lock-in. No closed-source critical path. No data ever leaves Indian jurisdiction. Mappings, policies, and conflict rules all live in Git — reviewable by officers, versioned, auditable by the CAG if ever required. The rollout is gradual: two departments in shadow mode first, live writes only once the audit ledger runs clean for a chosen window, then department-by-department expansion. There is never a big-bang moment. That is the opposite of the GST rollout pattern, and it is deliberate.Scaling and long-term impact. The entire architecture is designed around a single principle: the marginal cost of adding a new department should be one YAML mapping file, one CDC tier classification, and one row in the registry schema. No core code changes. No downtime. No re-architecture. That is why the five-year version of Samanvay — same core, 200+ departments across sectors, other states adopting the pattern — is not a different system. It is the same system with more YAML files, a denser causal graph, and years of audit history. The long-term dividend is more interesting than the immediate one: once every department is live on the bus and the ledger has accumulated years of propagation data, Samanvay stops being plumbing and starts being the State's real-time view of its own business fabric — which departments are slow to apply updates, where conflicts cluster, which fields drift most, which businesses have not been touched in years. The same ledger that guarantees auditability today becomes an analytical asset tomorrow. That is the quiet reason a bus designed for reliability becomes the most valuable piece of state-level business infrastructure five years from now.Samanvay — the State stays in sync, one change at a time, and learns from every one.

We cannot change the code or database structures of the Single Window System or the old systems. So we need to build a middle layer that works with the Unique Business Identifier to recognize the same business across different databases and make changes without disrupting anything.﻿2.Two Way Propagation and Change DiscoveryTo capture changes in data without changing the code we use Change Data Capture.Log Based Change Data Capture: For databases that support it we use Debezium to read transaction logs. This captures changes in time without slowing down the original databases.Snapshot Based Change Data Capture: For systems that do not support change data capture we compare snapshots of the data at different times to find changes.3.Translating Database SchemasWhen a change is captured it is sent as an event. A middle layer intercepts this event uses the Unique Business Identifier to find the target record. Applies a translation dictionary. As new departments are added administrators can simply add a mapping configuration without changing the core synchronization logic.4.Resolving Conflicts and Keeping a RecordWhen changes are made to the Unique Business Identifier from both the Single Window System and an old system the middle layer does not automatically overwrite the data. Instead it applies a set of rules.Source of Truth Rules: Certain systems are designated as the authority for fields.Timestamp Rules: If the authority is the same it uses a Last Writer Wins approach based on the timestamp.Record of Changes: Every change is recorded in a ledger that cannot be changed including the old state, the new state, the source of the change and the conflict resolution rule applied.5.Guaranteeing Delivery and IdempotencyTo ensure that no changes are lost if the system fails we use an event streaming platform that guarantees at one delivery. Because this can sometimes result in events the target databases must be able to handle duplicate writes. We achieve this by converting all writes into operations that update if the record exists and insert if it does not.6.Risks and Trade OffsThere is a risk that old systems may experience delays in updates. We trade off updates for system stability accepting a delay of 5 to 15 minutes for older systems to fulfill the requirement of not modifying the original code.7.Implementation PlanWe will build a prototype using data.Days 1 and 2: We will generate 500 business records to mock the Single Window System and other endpoints.Days 3 and 4: We will build a middle layer script to simulate changes and schema translation, between these mock endpoints.Days 5 and 6: We will implement the idempotency logic. Build a simple user interface to display the record of changes and demonstrate conflict resolution.
