solutions.team_name: Samanvay — A Causal Semantic Interoperability Fabric Between Karnataka's Single Window System and 40+ Department Systems
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


A Arjuna
TEAM SUBMISSION [9]
Samanvay — A Causal Semantic Interoperability Fabric Between Karnataka's Single Window System and 40+ Department Systems
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 23, 2026 10:17 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
Samanvay is Sanskrit for coordination, harmony, integration — which is exactly what we are building. It is a causal-semantic interoperability fabric that sits between Karnataka's Single Window System (SWS) and the 40+ legacy department systems that must stay in sync with it. Samanvay never modifies either side. It observes changes at both ends, understands what they mean, translates between their schemas, reasons about what each change should trigger elsewhere, propagates it in both directions, detects and resolves conflicts by policy, and records every action on a tamper-evident audit ledger so that any service request — and any retry, any conflict, any reconciliation — can be traced end-to-end by UBID.The problem, stated plainly. SWS is the State's forward-looking front door for new business registrations and cross-department service requests. In parallel, 40+ department systems remain the authoritative record for businesses already on their books, and will continue to be for years. A single-day cutover is not realistic at this scale; the GST rollout is a permanent reminder of why big-bang migrations of this scope fail. The consequence today is a split-brain problem — a change of registered address raised on SWS does not reach Factories, a change of authorised signatory raised inside Shop Establishment does not reach SWS, and the two sides drift out of sync. Citizens and officers see inconsistent state depending on which window they walk up to. Any working solution must use UBID as the common join key and must not touch SWS or any department system.The baseline, done rigorously. Samanvay is built on five components, each doing one job well. A UBID Registry maps every business's UBID to its local IDs across all 40+ systems — every propagation starts with a lookup here, and this is the only new state Samanvay holds about businesses. A Schema Mapping Registry is one declarative YAML per department: when department #41 onboards, we add one file and redeploy — no core code changes, which is the single biggest lever for long-term maintainability. Change Detection is tiered: Tier A for departments with native webhooks, Tier B polling for those with "changes-since" APIs, Tier C nightly snapshot diffing for read-only systems, and Tier D Debezium log-tailing where permitted. No department can block the bus by refusing to emit events — there is always a fallback tier. The Propagation Engine is idempotent by construction: every event gets a deterministic ID, at-least-once delivery from Kafka combined with idempotent writes produces an exactly-once effect, and permanent failures move to a dead-letter queue. The Conflict Resolution Engine applies per-field policies — not per-system — because authority over "registered address" and authority over "factory safety certificate" live with different departments. Under all of it, an append-only PostgreSQL Audit Ledger with hash-chained rows records every event_id, UBID, field, old_value, new_value, source_system, target_system, and resolution reason, producing the tamper-evident end-to-end traceability the problem statement demands.Where Samanvay earns its keep, beyond the baseline. Four additions layer on top of the proven interoperability pattern — together they form an architecture that no conventional event-bus-plus-mapping approach can match.(1) The Causal Interoperability Graph (CIG). Every propagation is treated as a causal event in a Neo4j-backed knowledge structure. Over months of operation, the CIG learns which changes in which department tend to produce which downstream changes elsewhere — a registered-address change typically cascading to Factories, Shop Establishment, and Pollution Control forms a causal cluster. When a new change arrives, Samanvay does not just propagate it mechanically; it predicts which downstream updates are likely, runs the predicted cascade inside a Digital Twin Shadow before committing, and only commits once the cascade passes pre-flight validation. This turns reactive integration into predictive integration, catches silently-failing departments before a citizen complaint does, and turns the bus itself into the State's real-time causal model of its own business fabric.(2) Zero-Trust PII with Partial Homomorphic Encryption. Samanvay sits on the juiciest dataset in the State — so we built it so the bus itself cannot read the most sensitive fields. PAN, bank account, Aadhaar, and authorised signatory never leave encrypted form end to end. They move through Samanvay under partial homomorphic encryption using OpenFHE (the open-source successor to Microsoft SEAL and PALISADE), so that field-level equality matching, format conversion, and routing happen on ciphertext. The bus never sees plaintext PII. Audit rows over these fields are anchored with zero-knowledge proofs, so an auditor or a CAG officer can verify that "the value written to Factories equals the value raised from SWS" without either system or the auditor learning the underlying value. This is not a theoretical nice-to-have — it is what allows a bus of this scope to exist inside a government perimeter in the first place.(3) Neuro-Symbolic Conflict Arbitration with Explanations in Kannada. Conflicts are resolved by a hybrid engine. The symbolic half is Open Policy Agent: declarative, Git-versioned, reviewable rules that decide authority per field. The neural half is a locally-hosted small language model — Llama 3.1 8B fine-tuned on Karnataka administrative language, running on inference nodes that never leave Indian jurisdiction — that produces plain-language explanations of every resolution, in Kannada and English. An officer reviewing a flag does not see "policy 47B applied"; they read "the registered address raised on SWS at 14:03 overrode the Factories address last updated 11 February 2024, because SWS is authoritative for identity fields; the earlier Factories value is retained in audit row 18772 and is restorable." Every resolution is reversible, every explanation is auditable, every policy change is a Git pull request reviewed like code.(4) CRDT Consistency Layer and Self-Healing Schema Drift Detection. For the handful of fields where either side can legitimately be authoritative — typically contact metadata — Samanvay uses Conflict-free Replicated Data Types (Last-Write-Wins registers with vector clocks, OR-Sets for multi-valued attributes). This gives a mathematical convergence guarantee: regardless of the order in which updates arrive across Kafka partitions, all replicas reach the same final state. In parallel, a drift detector continuously computes embedding-space signatures of incoming payloads per department using sentence-transformers and alerts when a department's distribution shifts beyond a learned threshold — a field renamed in a quiet API update, a date format silently changing, a new enum value appearing. At that point Samanvay either auto-regenerates the mapping YAML using a locally hosted model and flags it for human merge, or holds writes to that department until an operator confirms. This is the feature that keeps the bus alive in year three without a platform team quietly growing to thirty engineers.Why this is deployable inside a government. Every technology choice is open source, Indian-cloud-deployable, and familiar to the engineers who already run state digital infrastructure: Apache Kafka (or Redpanda where operational simplicity matters more) for the event bus; PostgreSQL with hash-chained rows for the registry and audit; Debezium for log-tailing where permitted; Open Policy Agent for declarative policies; Neo4j for the causal graph; Apache Flink for stream processing; Kubernetes on MeghRaj, the State Data Centre, or any Indian sovereign cloud; Prometheus, Grafana, and Loki for observability; HashiCorp Vault for secret management; Istio for the service mesh; eBPF-based tooling for deep telemetry; OpenFHE for homomorphic operations; locally-hosted Llama 3.1 8B and sentence-transformers models for the AI-assisted pieces. No vendor lock-in. No closed-source critical path. No data ever leaves Indian jurisdiction. Mappings, policies, and conflict rules all live in Git — reviewable by officers, versioned, auditable by the CAG if ever required. The rollout is gradual: two departments in shadow mode first, live writes only once the audit ledger runs clean for a chosen window, then department-by-department expansion. There is never a big-bang moment. That is the opposite of the GST rollout pattern, and it is deliberate.Scaling and long-term impact. The entire architecture is designed around a single principle: the marginal cost of adding a new department should be one YAML mapping file, one CDC tier classification, and one row in the registry schema. No core code changes. No downtime. No re-architecture. That is why the five-year version of Samanvay — same core, 200+ departments across sectors, other states adopting the pattern — is not a different system. It is the same system with more YAML files, a denser causal graph, and years of audit history. The long-term dividend is more interesting than the immediate one: once every department is live on the bus and the ledger has accumulated years of propagation data, Samanvay stops being plumbing and starts being the State's real-time view of its own business fabric — which departments are slow to apply updates, where conflicts cluster, which fields drift most, which businesses have not been touched in years. The same ledger that guarantees auditability today becomes an analytical asset tomorrow. That is the quiet reason a bus designed for reliability becomes the most valuable piece of state-level business infrastructure five years from now.Samanvay — the State stays in sync, one change at a time, and learns from every one.

solutions.team_name: CloudGlance AI Evaluator's Workbench: AI-Assisted Tender Evaluation for CRPF (L1 and QCBS)
solutions.solution_text:
PRACTICE
COMPETE
JOBS
LEADERBOARD


Y yash.tiwari3565_3c86
TEAM SUBMISSION [1]
CloudGlance AI Evaluator's Workbench: AI-Assisted Tender Evaluation for CRPF (L1 and QCBS)
Theme: Theme 3: AI-Based Tender Evaluation and Eligibility Analysis for Government Procurement by CRPF Submitted On: Apr 24, 2026 06:04 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
CRPF tender evaluation committees currently spend five to seven working days manually cross-checking hundreds of pages of bidder documents against tender eligibility criteria. Different evaluators extract different values from the same scanned certificates. The audit record captures the final sign-off but not the reasoning that led to each individual inclusion or exclusion decision. When a disqualified bidder files a complaint under RTI, reconstructing the specific evidence trail is manual and error-prone. This pain exists across both L1 (lowest-cost selection) and QCBS (quality-and-cost-based selection) evaluations, which together cover essentially every CRPF tender.Our submission is an Evaluator's Workbench that collapses this exercise into a single-day review, in both L1 and QCBS modes, on the same pipeline. The procurement officer uploads the tender (single PDF or a ZIP of tender, corrigenda, and annexures). The system extracts the eligibility rubric as a structured list of queries, classifies each as non-negotiable or optional, auto-detects the evaluation method and the bidding mode (single-packet or two-packet), and presents everything to the Tender Evaluation Committee (TEC) for review and explicit sign-off. Only after the TEC confirms the rubric does bidder evaluation proceed. For each bidder, the platform parses every submission format that appears in real procurement - typed PDFs, scanned PDFs, DOCX, XLSX, photographed certificates, bilingual documents - and runs the confirmed rubric against the bidder corpus. The primary output is a criterion-by-bidder comparison matrix. Every cell carries a verdict (Eligible / Not Eligible / Needs Manual Review), the extracted answer value, and a source citation at page-and-bounding-box precision. The secondary output is a per-bidder archive report in the format typically maintained in the departmental file.Three design commitments drive every decision in this submission. First, verdicts are grounded in procurement rules, not invented scales. In L1 mode the technical evaluation is a qualifying gate and we refuse to invent numeric scores where the rules do not require them. In QCBS mode we score only when the tender itself defines a rubric with weights, and we honour the tender's specific weight structure. Second, any missing mandatory document is immediate disqualification in both modes, regardless of how strong the bidder looks on other criteria; this is enforced as a hard binary gate, not a threshold on an aggregate score. A bidder missing a GST certificate is out, even with an otherwise perfect submission. Third, the system never silently disqualifies a bidder. Ambiguous cases route to a mandatory officer review step with plain-English context, a clickable source citation, and a remarks field the officer must fill in before the case can be resolved. These remarks become part of the audit record and appear in the exported report.The evaluation runs as three gates. Gate 1 applies the non-negotiable document checklist: present, missing, or needs review per bidder per item. Gate 2 produces the per-criterion verdict using hybrid retrieval (semantic + keyword + page-summary search, fused with RRF, reranked) against each bidder's indexed corpus; the answer generation model is multimodal, reads the rendered source page alongside the extracted text, and can only claim a value by pointing to a specific source element with its bounding box. Gate 3 handles financial comparison: in L1 mode, ranks qualified bidders by lowest normalised quote; in QCBS mode, combines the Gate 2 technical score with the normalised financial bid per the tender's weight ratio.Role separation mirrors the CRPF evaluation hierarchy. A dealing officer can upload documents, trigger evaluation runs, watch a live progress stream during the run (Gate 1: 10/10 documents parsed per bidder; Gate 2: 12/18 queries resolved, 67%; overall 43% complete, estimated 8 minutes remaining), and navigate the matrix. The two actions that carry personal accountability - Confirm Rubric and Final Sign-off - are restricted to TEC members and are executed via their Class-III DSC per IT Act Section 35. The signed document hash and the signing certificate's serial number are written to the audit log. This is enforced at the API layer, not just the UI; a dealing officer attempting to sign off is rejected with the specific reason.The audit design assumes CVC, CAG, and RTI scrutiny. The audit log is append-only and tamper-evident; records cannot be modified, and any correction to a past verdict is a new record referencing the superseded one. Rubric versions are preserved across edits and corrigenda, so an auditor asking "what was the rubric when this specific verdict was made" can always answer precisely. Retention follows CVC guidelines (seven years minimum, configurable upward). The export includes every criterion, every verdict, every citation, every officer remarks entry, and the committee's DSC sign-off, suitable for the departmental file, for an RTI response (with RTI Act Section 8 exemptions applied automatically), and for CAG submission. Post sign-off the report is locked; any post-award correction is a new versioned record.Architecture and model choices, with alternatives named. Azure Document Intelligence for OCR and layout (MeitY-empanelled, Indian-region endpoints, better on stamped Indian government documents than Tesseract or Textract in our tests). Gemini-family multimodal models for page enrichment, query extraction, answer generation, L1 verdict, and QCBS rubric scoring (priced aggressively per token, India-region availability in Mumbai and Delhi, native Hindi and major Indic-script support). The architecture is model-agnostic through a router, so a newer or cheaper model swaps in without workflow changes. PostgreSQL with pgvector for both vector and full-text indexing (one database simplifies operations and backups, production-proven in our pilots). Temporal for workflow orchestration (durable execution survives worker restarts, native support for parallel fan-out with bounded concurrency). Python workers, Go API, TypeScript/React UI. Deployable on any MeitY-empanelled cloud (Azure, AWS, GCP India-region) or on-premise Kubernetes - pilot-department residency rule dictates the choice.Regulatory alignment is specific. GFR 2017 Rules 163 (two-packet), 173 (L1), 192 (QCBS). MHA Procurement Guidelines and Manual for Procurement of Goods 2024, which govern CRPF procurement. Public Procurement Policy for MSEs 2012 (EMD waiver, turnover and experience relaxation where proportionate, 15% price preference, 25% reservation with 4% SC/ST sub-quota) applied per-tender on the basis of Udyam certificate and the tender's exemption language. DPIIT startup recognition similarly per-tender. CVC Manual 2017 for audit expectations. RTI Act 2005 Section 8 exemptions applied on exports. DPDP Act 2023 and MeghRaj cloud guidelines for data residency.What we bring to this hackathon is not a proof-of-concept. The RAG indexing pipeline, query extraction capability, and grounded-citation answer format are running today in pilot deployments at IIT Hyderabad and Welspun, with ongoing pilot discussions at Oil India and NHPC. The NHPC conversations specifically shaped our commitment to the matrix output format and the per-bidder archive report - that's how real committees consume results. The hackathon delta is a well-scoped set of CRPF-specific additions: L1 binary verdicts and QCBS rubric-scored verdicts on the same Gate 2 pipeline, non-negotiable document gating, matrix output with per-bidder archive, DSC-gated TEC sign-off, corrigendum mid-flight handling, and MSME exemption handling per tender language.Round 2 plan. Six days between Round 1 shortlist and Finale. Day 1 stands up the repo, ingestion, and Azure DI integration on sample PDFs. Day 2 implements criterion extraction with L1-versus-QCBS detection, the mandatory document checklist, and the rubric review and Confirm Rubric UI. Day 3 implements hybrid retrieval, per-query answer extraction, Gate 1, and three document types end-to-end. Day 4 implements Gate 2 L1 verdicts with cell summaries and bolding, plus the matrix UI and per-bidder archive. Day 5 implements Gate 2 QCBS scoring, Gate 3 for both modes, audit log with rubric versioning, manual review queue with mandatory remarks, export to Excel and PDF, and DSC sign-off. Day 6 is end-to-end testing against whichever mock or redacted sandbox dataset the organisers provide, including edge cases like corrigendum mid-flight, rubric edits with re-evaluation, and multi-officer review reconciliation. Team of three to four engineers.What we would ask a CRPF pilot sponsor for Round 2. Three or four redacted real past tender evaluation files would let us calibrate the non-negotiable-versus-optional detection against CRPF's specific procurement language. A typical department-standard checklist would let us preload a baseline query library so officers spend their first hour editing rather than writing from scratch. Neither is required for the Finale but both would meaningfully improve the pilot.Full written submission (approximately 14 pages, 11 sections) is attached as a PDF covering detailed problem understanding, full architecture with rejected alternatives, audit design, risks and mitigations, and the ten-minute Finale demo script. here is link of pdf:  https://drive.google.com/file/d/1S3QBquZ6oDEpbaKlYmu5VSwB5VYPhJ5o/view?usp=sharing

A abhinav.24bce10992_5984
TEAM SUBMISSION [3]
SyncBridge — AI-Assisted Two-Way Interoperability Layer for Karnataka's Single Window System.
Theme: Theme 2: Two-Way Interoperability between the Single Window System and Department Systems by Karnataka Commerce & Industry Submitted On: Apr 25, 2026 07:07 PM IST (Asia/Kolkata)
Project Description
SyncBridge is a non-invasive layer of interoperability that sends service requests bi-directionally between Karnataka’s Single Window System and 40+ legacy department systems – without changing anything on either side – by leveraging the Unique Business Identifier (UBID) as the only joining key for all systems. The tool works bi-directionally. On one side, all service requests raised on the SWS platform, e.g., change in the registered address, get automatically discovered, transformed from the SWS schema into a representation for all target departments, and successfully pushed to all department systems with that UBID. There will be no duplicate pushes nor data loss due to retries when needed. On the other side, any changes generated within legacy departments' systems independently get picked up by SyncBridge regardless of the interface exposed (webhook, polling endpoint, database snapshots, or diffs), transformed into the SWS representation, joined by the UBID, and pushed back to SWS. At the heart of the system lies the Conflict Resolution Engine. In case of a conflict between two updates of the same UBID coming from two separate sources, SyncBridge identifies the conflict, uses a configurable conflict resolution strategy – last write wins, source priority, or officer escalation review, and captures the exact reason behind the resultant state. All propagation, translation, retries, and conflict resolution activities are captured in an immutable audit trail, which can answer four key questions about each service request: What has been changed? Where has it come from? Where has it been recorded? And, Has there been any conflict resolution activity associated with it?
